The colleague who’s been writing excellent client reports for twelve years might hear “let’s use AI for reports” as “your reports aren’t good enough.” That’s not what you mean. But it can be what they hear.

The antidote is leading with curiosity instead of efficiency. Not “AI will make our reports faster” but “I’ve been experimenting with using Claude on my own writing and I’m finding it does some interesting things. Want to try it on something together?” That’s a completely different conversation. One feels like a judgment. The other feels like an invitation.

“The goal is to demystify, right? Like, hey, this is just a chat window and it’s just doing stuff on your computer.” — Matt Hastings

That framing, “just a chat window,” is a gift, not a simplification. It gives people permission to interact with AI like they’d interact with a capable colleague: expecting to direct it, push back on it, and evaluate its work, rather than treating it as a magic box that either works or doesn’t.

That managerial framing is what turns occasional use into real fluency. People stop waiting for the perfect prompt and start having conversations. They stop being disappointed by imperfect outputs and start iterating. The skill compounds from there.

Where to start

Pick one thing from Phase 1 and do it this week. Not Phase 2. Phase 1.

Use Claude for thirty minutes on something real: a report, a meeting summary, a proposal section. Not as a test of the tool. As a genuine attempt to get useful work done. Notice what worked, what didn’t, and what you’d try differently.

That’s it. That’s the start.

A month from now you’ll have real examples to share. Two months from now, you’ll have early movers. Six months from now, you may have a business case. The roadmap isn’t a sprint. It’s a sequence of small bets, each one building on the last.

The professionals making it real at their organizations right now didn’t start with a strategy deck. They started with a Tuesday afternoon and a task they’d been putting off. You can do the same thing this week.

For more on where to focus your own practice as you go, The AI Learning Path: What to Focus on First, Second, and Third is a useful companion to this roadmap.

The professionals who are figuring this out fastest are doing it alongside peers who are navigating the same organizational questions. If you want to see how other leaders in MVP Club are doing it on their teams, and share what you’re learning along the way, that’s exactly what our weekly sessions are for. Come find us at mvpclub.ai/community.
Phase 3: Run a small pilot with real stakes (weeks 6-12)
Phase 4: Make it easy for AI use to spread (ongoing)
Phase 5: Translate what’s working into language leadership understands
The identity question underneath all of this
AI as a Thinking Tool: How to Reason Through Complex Problems with AI
Learn how to use Claude or ChatGPT as a thinking partner to reason through complex problems, not just generate output.

The first time I used Claude to think through a decision instead of just asking it for an answer, something clicked. I stopped typing questions. I started typing context.

Most people bring AI a finished question: "What should I do about X?" They get a polished, generic answer. They shrug and move on. That's not wrong, exactly. But it's using maybe 20% of what's possible.

The professionals getting the most from tools like Claude and ChatGPT have figured out a different mode. They're not just asking for output. They're using AI to think out loud, pressure-test ideas, and see their own thinking more clearly. They're using AI as a thinking tool, not just a task tool.

## What a Thinking Tool Actually Does

AI works like a whiteboard more than a calculator. A calculator takes your input and gives you an answer. A whiteboard gives you a surface where thinking can happen more clearly: you make ideas visible, move things around, find connections you couldn't see in your head. Most people use AI like a calculator. The ones getting the best results are using it like a whiteboard.

When you talk through a problem with a smart colleague, you don't just get their answer. The conversation itself changes how you see the problem. You articulate things you'd only been feeling. You consider angles you hadn't thought of. You leave with better clarity even if you never take their advice.

Claude can do something similar. Not because it has lived experience or genuine judgment, but because it gives you something to think with. You throw an idea at it, it responds, that response shifts your thinking. You refine. You push back. The loop produces clarity you couldn't have generated alone.

## The Difference Between Asking and Thinking

Say you're trying to decide whether to take on a new project at work.

**Asking mode:** "Should I take on this new project?"

Claude gives you a generic framework: consider your workload, assess the opportunity cost, think about career alignment. You've heard this before. Not that useful.

**Thinking mode:** "I'm trying to decide whether to take on a new project. Here's my situation: I'm already stretched on two other deliverables, the new project has high visibility with senior leadership, I've been feeling underchallenged in my current role, and my manager seems to want me to step up. I'm not sure if I'm excited about it or just flattered that I was asked. Help me think through this."

Now Claude has enough to actually engage. It might reflect back what it hears in tension with each other. It might ask what "underchallenged" means to you specifically. It might point out that you named two different things, feeling underchallenged and being flattered, and ask if you know which one is doing more work in this decision.

That's not Claude having better answers. That's Claude having enough context to be a real thinking partner.

## The Research Behind This (and What It Says About How You Use AI)

A 2025 Microsoft Research study surveyed 319 knowledge workers on how generative AI affects critical thinking [NEEDS SOURCE: link to Microsoft Research 2025 study on knowledge workers and critical thinking]. One of the most striking findings: higher confidence in AI is associated with less critical thinking, while higher self-confidence is associated with more. People who lean on AI hardest often engage with it least critically.

Professionals who use AI as a reasoning partner, not just an answer machine, actually sharpen their thinking rather than outsourcing it. They're verifying, questioning, integrating. They're in a dialogue, not just receiving a dispatch. Using AI in a way that turns off your judgment instead of engaging it is where things go sideways. The thinking-tool approach keeps your judgment on.

## Four Ways to Use AI as a Thinking Tool

### 1. Let It Interview You

One technique that works well, especially when you're stuck or at a starting point: ask Claude to interview you before it gives you anything.

"I'm trying to work through a decision about [X]. Before you give me any suggestions, I want you to ask me questions. Interview me about this until you have enough to actually be useful."

I said something like this in a recent Getting Unstuck session with one of our members:

> "Have Claude interview you around your goals for this, then make a plan to achieve them. It's a nice move to have Claude interview you whenever you're at a turning point or a starting point, just so it gets really crisp. Sometimes you might not even know what you want, but having that conversation can help get you clear."

This works because the questions themselves are often more valuable than any answer. When Claude asks "What does success actually look like for you here?", and you realize you can't answer it cleanly, that's data. That's the thinking you needed to do.

### 2. Think Out Loud, Then Ask for Reflection

Instead of bringing Claude a clean question, bring it a messy thought. Open a chat and just type what you're actually thinking, including the contradictions and uncertainties.

"I've been thinking about this for a few days and I can't get clear. On one hand, [X]. On the other hand, [Y]. Part of me thinks the answer is [Z] but I'm not sure if that's what I actually believe or just the path of least resistance. What do you notice about how I'm framing this?"

Asking "what do you notice about how I'm framing this?" is one of the most productive questions you can put to Claude. It invites the AI to comment on your thinking process, not just your content. Often the framing is where the real problem lives.

### 3. Pressure-Test a Decision You've Already Made

You've decided something, but you're not fully confident. Before committing, run it through Claude with explicit permission to push back.

"I've decided to [X]. I want you to argue against this decision. Not devil's advocate for its own sake, but genuinely: what are the best reasons this might be wrong? What am I probably not seeing?"

This works because it's much easier to generate counterarguments when you're not defending a position yourself. Claude has no stake in your decision. It can lay out the strongest version of the opposing case without the emotional cost of you having to do it yourself.

Then you can decide which objections are real and which aren't. That's judgment. Claude doesn't replace it. It surfaces the material your judgment needs.

### 4. Use Plan Mode for Complex Problems

If you're using Claude for a project or a bigger decision, plan mode (in Claude Code) keeps AI in conversation mode without taking action. But even in regular Claude chat, you can set this up yourself.

"I'm going to describe a complex situation. I want you to ask me clarifying questions and help me think through it before either of us gets to any conclusions or recommendations. Just think with me for now."

This slows the interaction down in a productive way. You're not rushing toward an answer. You're building a shared understanding of the problem first. The output from that process is usually much better because the inputs are cleaner.

## What This Looks Like Day-to-Day

You don't need to reserve this for big decisions. The thinking-tool approach works for everyday professional moments.

Stuck on how to frame a difficult message to your team? Open Claude and type out what you actually want to say and why it's hard. Ask it to reflect what it hears. You'll often find the real problem in the first response.

Preparing for a performance review conversation and not sure how to approach it? Tell Claude the full situation, including the parts that make you feel uncomfortable. Ask it to help you see the conversation from your employee's perspective. Then decide how you want to show up.

About to make a resource decision and feeling uncertain? Walk Claude through your constraints and what you're weighing. Ask it what you're not considering. Use its response to sharpen your own thinking before the meeting.

None of this replaces your judgment. All of it informs it.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: a member describing a specific moment where talking through a decision with Claude changed how they saw the problem]

## The Managerial Relationship

One frame that helps: using AI is a managerial relationship. You manage what you give it, what you ask it to do, and how you evaluate what comes back. You're not a passive recipient. You're an active director.

In thinking mode, that relationship becomes more dynamic. You're not just managing inputs and outputs. You're running a real-time reasoning process with AI as a collaborator. You set the direction. You ask the questions. You decide what to do with what you find. AI just helps you see more clearly along the way.

This is different from delegating your thinking. You're not asking Claude to think for you. You're asking it to think with you. The distinction matters, because the value you get from AI as a thinking tool depends entirely on how much of your own thinking you bring to the conversation.

The more context you give, the more clearly you articulate what you're uncertain about, the more honest you are about the contradictions in your thinking, the better Claude can function as a partner. Garbage in, generic out. Real thinking in, real thinking support out.

## Where to Go From Here

If you've been using AI mostly for task execution, the thinking-tool approach is a different gear. It takes a little practice to shift into. The key is to stop polishing your questions before you bring them to Claude. Start messier. Bring the problem as it actually lives in your head, not the cleaned-up version you'd put in a report.

And if you want to understand where AI judgment ends and yours begins, [When AI Gets It Wrong: The Human Judgment Layer](/learn/when-ai-gives-bad-output) is a good next read.

The blog post [AI Is a Thinking Tool: How to Build Scaffolds That Expand What You Can Do](/blog/ai-thinking-tool-build-scaffolds-expand-capability) goes deeper on building custom thinking surfaces if you want to take this further.

How to Build AI into Your Daily Workflow (The 30-Minute Habit)
Build an AI daily workflow in 30 minutes a day. A practical routine for busy professionals who want consistent results, not occasional experiments.

Carmella Thompson, a real estate agent in our community, spent three months using Claude occasionally before anything changed. Then she built one repeatable workflow: every morning, she pastes the day's property data into Claude and gets a market report draft. That's it. Within two weeks, she had cut her report prep from 45 minutes to under 10.

The shift wasn't a new tool or a better prompt. It was doing the same thing, the same way, every day.

That's what this guide is about. Not more tips. A specific 30-minute structure you can follow starting tomorrow.

## Why consistency beats intensity

Most professionals try AI the same way they try a new diet: aggressively for a week, then barely at all. They spend two hours exploring ChatGPT on a Saturday, get some interesting results, close the tab, and open it again three weeks later. Nothing sticks.

The reason is simple: you're not building a habit, you're having an experience. And experiences don't compound the way habits do.

Research on habit formation from the [European Journal of Social Psychology](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/ejsp.674) found that behaviors become automatic through consistent repetition in the same context, averaging 66 days. The key word is consistent. Not intensive. Not hours. Repeated contact with the same task, in the same context, at the same time.

Fifteen minutes of Claude every morning beats a two-hour AI session on Sunday. Every time.

## What 30 minutes actually looks like

The structure below divides your AI time into three short blocks that fit inside your existing workday. You don't need to carve out a dedicated learning session. You weave it into things you're already doing.

**Block 1: Morning prep (10 minutes)**

At the start of your workday, before you open email or Slack, open Claude or ChatGPT and paste in your to-do list or meeting schedule for the day. Ask it to help you think through your priorities, flag anything that needs prep, or draft a quick agenda for your first meeting.

This is your context-setting block. You're not asking Claude to do your job. You're thinking out loud with a capable thought partner before the day's noise kicks in.

A simple prompt that works: "Here's my schedule for today: [paste it]. What should I prep for, and is there anything I'm likely to underestimate in terms of time or complexity?"

**Block 2: One real task (15 minutes)**

Pick one task from your actual work and do it with Claude instead of without it. Not a test task. Something real.

This is where the habit gets built. You're creating a track record of use cases that are specific to your job, your relationships, and your writing style. Over time, Claude gets more useful because you get better at directing it.

Good candidates for this block: a follow-up email that needs a careful tone, a document summary for a meeting you haven't had time to read, a rough outline for a report you've been putting off, or a few talking points for a conversation that's been on your mind.

The key is picking ONE thing and actually finishing it. A completed draft that you send is worth more than five half-started experiments.

**Block 3: End-of-day reflection (5 minutes)**

Before you close out for the day, take five minutes to note what worked and what didn't. You don't need a special system. A running note in your phone or a sticky note works fine. Something like: "Used Claude for the board summary today. The first draft was too formal. Asked it to rewrite in plain language and it nailed it."

This reflection step is often skipped, and skipping it is why people plateau. The professionals in our community who improve fastest are the ones who treat AI like an ongoing experiment, not a static tool.

## The one rule that makes this work

Build your AI practice on top of something you already do, not in addition to it.

This is called habit stacking, and it's the most reliable way to make any new behavior stick. Instead of carving out AI time from scratch, you attach it to an existing anchor. Morning coffee and email becomes morning coffee, Claude, then email. Your pre-meeting prep ritual gets one new step. Your end-of-day wind-down picks up five minutes of reflection.

If you try to find new time for this, you will fail. Not because you lack willpower, but because your schedule is real and already full. The only time that's reliably available is inside existing routines.

> **Matt:** "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude how you want it done. The next time, you can just say: here's the input, here's the approach, go do it. That's how a good tool becomes a great one."

## Common mistakes that break the habit

**Expecting every output to be perfect.** It won't be. The first draft Claude gives you is the beginning of the conversation, not the end. Expect to push back, clarify, and iterate. That back-and-forth is the practice.

**Using a different tool every week.** Pick one: Claude or ChatGPT. Use it daily for 30 days before exploring others. The value of AI comes partly from the tool learning your patterns and you learning how to direct it. Switching constantly resets both.

**Treating it as a research tool instead of a work tool.** "Tell me about AI trends" is a research prompt. "Help me write a response to this client complaint in a tone that's firm but not defensive" is a work prompt. The second category is where the habit pays off.

**Saving the complicated tasks for AI and doing the simple ones yourself.** This is backwards. Start with small, simple tasks where you can see the output clearly and build a feedback loop. The complicated tasks come later, once you know how to steer.

## From the Practice

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what members say about building a consistent AI workflow and what changed when they stopped treating it as an occasional tool]

One pattern we see consistently in coaching sessions: the people who make the fastest progress aren't the ones who use the most tools. They're the ones who use one tool, on the same tasks, every day. The variety comes later, once the habit is there.

Ryan describes it this way: Claude's value multiplies when you stop treating it like a vending machine and start treating it like someone you're onboarding. "It's very much like onboarding a person," he's said in sessions. "You walk through it very slowly the first time. Then the next day you say, do that thing we did yesterday. And sure enough, it will."

## Choosing Claude or ChatGPT for your daily routine

For professional writing, meeting prep, and document work, Claude tends to give more careful, nuanced output. For research, summarization, and broader exploration, ChatGPT is equally solid. Both offer paid plans around $20/month, and both are meaningfully better on the paid tier than the free version.

Pick one. Use it every day. You'll know within three weeks whether it fits your work.

If you want a deeper comparison between the two, both have different strengths for different kinds of work (Claude leans better for writing; ChatGPT is solid for research and summarization), and the best way to find out is to use the same task in both on the same day.

For the mechanics of getting better output, the most important skill is learning to give Claude the context it needs: who you're talking to, what the situation is, what a good response looks like, and what you want it NOT to do. [How to use AI at work: a practical guide for busy professionals](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) covers this in detail.

If the bigger challenge is time rather than technique, [How to find time for AI when you're already busy](/learn/finding-time-for-ai) has a practical framework for weaving AI into a packed schedule without adding to it.

## Your starting point

This week, pick one task you do every day and run it through Claude instead of alone. Same task, same time, every day for five days.

By Friday, you'll have a track record to evaluate. You'll know whether it saved time, what the friction points were, and whether you want to keep it. That's more useful than any amount of reading about AI productivity.

Pick one task from your actual work and do it with Claude instead of without it. Not a test task. Something real.

This is where the habit gets built. You’re creating a track record of use cases that are specific to your job, your relationships, and your writing style. Over time, Claude gets more useful because you get better at directing it.

Good candidates for this block: a follow-up email that needs a careful tone, a document summary for a meeting you haven’t had time to read, a rough outline for a report you’ve been putting off, or a few talking points for a conversation that’s been on your mind.

The key is picking ONE thing and actually finishing it. A completed draft that you send is worth more than five half-started experiments.

Block 3: End-of-day reflection (5 minutes)

Before you close out for the day, take five minutes to note what worked and what didn’t. You don’t need a special system. A running note in your phone or a sticky note works fine. Something like: “Used Claude for the board summary today. The first draft was too formal. Asked it to rewrite in plain language and it nailed it.”

This reflection step is often skipped, and skipping it is why people plateau. The professionals in our community who improve fastest are the ones who treat AI like an ongoing experiment, not a static tool.

The one rule that makes this work

Build your AI practice on top of something you already do, not in addition to it.

This is called habit stacking, and it’s the most reliable way to make any new behavior stick. Instead of carving out AI time from scratch, you attach it to an existing anchor. Morning coffee and email becomes morning coffee, Claude, then email. Your pre-meeting prep ritual gets one new step. Your end-of-day wind-down picks up five minutes of reflection.

If you try to find new time for this, you will fail. Not because you lack willpower, but because your schedule is real and already full. The only time that’s reliably available is inside existing routines.

Matt: “Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude how you want it done. The next time, you can just say: here’s the input, here’s the approach, go do it. That’s how a good tool becomes a great one.”

Common mistakes that break the habit

Expecting every output to be perfect. It won’t be. The first draft Claude gives you is the beginning of the conversation, not the end. Expect to push back, clarify, and iterate. That back-and-forth is the practice.

Using a different tool every week. Pick one: Claude or ChatGPT. Use it daily for 30 days before exploring others. The value of AI comes partly from the tool learning your patterns and you learning how to direct it. Switching constantly resets both.

Treating it as a research tool instead of a work tool. “Tell me about AI trends” is a research prompt. “Help me write a response to this client complaint in a tone that’s firm but not defensive” is a work prompt. The second category is where the habit pays off.

Saving the complicated tasks for AI and doing the simple ones yourself. This is backwards. Start with small, simple tasks where you can see the output clearly and build a feedback loop. The complicated tasks come later, once you know how to steer.

From the Practice

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what members say about building a consistent AI workflow and what changed when they stopped treating it as an occasional tool]

One pattern we see consistently in coaching sessions: the people who make the fastest progress aren’t the ones who use the most tools. They’re the ones who use one tool, on the same tasks, every day. The variety comes later, once the habit is there.

Ryan describes it this way: Claude’s value multiplies when you stop treating it like a vending machine and start treating it like someone you’re onboarding. “It’s very much like onboarding a person,” he’s said in sessions. “You walk through it very slowly the first time. Then the next day you say, do that thing we did yesterday. And sure enough, it will.”

Choosing Claude or ChatGPT for your daily routine

For professional writing, meeting prep, and document work, Claude tends to give more careful, nuanced output. For research, summarization, and broader exploration, ChatGPT is equally solid. Both offer paid plans around $20/month, and both are meaningfully better on the paid tier than the free version.

Pick one. Use it every day. You’ll know within three weeks whether it fits your work.

If you want a deeper comparison between the two, both have different strengths for different kinds of work (Claude leans better for writing; ChatGPT is solid for research and summarization), and the best way to find out is to use the same task in both on the same day.

For the mechanics of getting better output, the most important skill is learning to give Claude the context it needs: who you’re talking to, what the situation is, what a good response looks like, and what you want it NOT to do. How to use AI at work: a practical guide for busy professionals covers this in detail.

If the bigger challenge is time rather than technique, How to find time for AI when you’re already busy has a practical framework for weaving AI into a packed schedule without adding to it.

Your starting point

This week, pick one task you do every day and run it through Claude instead of alone. Same task, same time, every day for five days.

By Friday, you’ll have a track record to evaluate. You’ll know whether it saved time, what the friction points were, and whether you want to keep it. That’s more useful than any amount of reading about AI productivity.

If you want to see how other professionals are building these routines and share what’s actually working in your role, that’s exactly what we do in MVP Club’s weekly sessions. Come practice alongside people navigating the same question. Join the community at /community.

The Biggest Mistakes People Make When Getting Started with AI
Most AI beginners stall for the same reasons. A career coach breaks down the 5 mistakes that derail real progress, and what to do instead.

The most common thing I hear from people who've been "trying to get into AI" is some version of: "I've opened it a few times, played around, didn't get much out of it, and kind of gave up." They're not behind. They didn't fail. They just ran into the same walls almost everyone hits when getting started with AI. And nobody warned them. I've been in some form of education for more than a decade, and watching professionals navigate AI adoption has been one of the most fascinating (and occasionally heartbreaking) things I've observed. The technical part? Not the problem. The psychological and behavioral patterns? Those are what derail people, consistently and predictably.

Five mistakes, five fixes

The patterns that stall AI beginners, and what to do instead.

1 Waiting until you feel "ready" → Start before you're ready
2 Starting with your most important work → Pick something low-stakes first
3 Treating the first response as final → Iterate. The third exchange is where it lands
4 Giving no context → Brief it like a new hire, or ask it to ask you
5 Going it alone → Find others figuring it out too
## Mistake 1: Waiting until you feel "ready" You don't have to feel ready, just start. That's the first thing I want you to hear. Most mid-career professionals are used to preparing before they act. You study, you take the training, you get certified, and then you do the thing. That pattern works for most professional development. It does not work for AI. AI is a do-first, understand-later tool. The understanding literally cannot come through preparation. It comes through use. Every coaching conversation I've had confirms this: the people who waited until they understood AI well enough to start are still waiting. They can keep up with the latest buzzwords as they rotate through LinkedIn, but they still haven't become AI-first practitioners. The people who just opened Claude or ChatGPT and tried something with real work came back the next week with actual questions. Give yourself permission to be a beginner. You don't have to have it all figured out. You know who DOES know about what AI can do? The AI itself! Just keep asking while you work, at any fricton point, just ask the LLM itself what's going on. ## Mistake 2: Starting with your most important work This one is counterintuitive, but bear with me. When people finally commit to trying AI, they tend to bring it their biggest, most high-stakes task. A client proposal. A presentation for the executive team. A performance review for someone they're about to put on a PIP. Then the output isn't quite right (because it never is on the first try), and they conclude that AI doesn't work for their kind of work. Start with something that doesn't matter much. An internal update nobody will scrutinize. A quick recap email to a team member. A brainstorm you'd throw away half of anyway. I started applying it at work with articles written for a Knowledge Base that hadn't even been released to customers yet. We were translating our outdated 80-page "how-to" guide with a more searchable, updated knowledge base site filled with articles addressing specific questions. This was, in hindsight, a perfect project to get better at using AI: low stakes, plenty of articles to write, plenty of opportunities to improve the process. The goal in week one is not to get better output. The goal is to get comfortable with the back-and-forth. Because AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT almost never nail it on the first try. That's not a bug. The first output is a starting point. The real work (and the real skill) is in the conversation that follows, where you shape and redirect and push back until you get something useful. Low-stakes practice builds the iteration muscle. Build that first. ## Mistake 3: Treating the first response like a finished product This is probably the single most common reason people decide AI "doesn't work." They type something in, read what comes back, go "that's not right" or "that sounds like AI wrote it," and close the tab. What they missed: the first response was never supposed to be the final answer. When you get output that's almost right, that's the moment to lean in, not give up. Try: "That's close, but the tone is too formal. Make it more direct." Or: "Good structure, but I need you to cut the second paragraph and sharpen the opening." Each round gets you closer. Most professionals find that by the third exchange, they have something genuinely useful. Claude and ChatGPT are not vending machines. They're more like a new colleague who needs direction. You give direction. They try. You refine the direction. They get better. ## Mistake 4: Giving no context (and then being surprised by the generic output) "Write me a status update that the new project is rolling out next week." I understand the instinct: you want AI to just handle it so you don't have to think about it. The problem is that Claude or ChatGPT has no idea who you are, who you're writing to, what the project is, what's actually going on, or what "a status update" means in your organization. The output you get back reflects exactly how much context you gave: almost none. Think about it this way. If you were onboarding a smart new hire and you handed them a task, you'd give them background. Who the audience is. What you're trying to achieve. What tone is appropriate. What to leave out. AI needs the same thing. The more specific and grounded you get with context, the more useful the result. A better version of that status update prompt might look like: "I'm a project manager at a mid-size consulting firm. I need to write a Friday status update for my VP. The project is two weeks behind due to a data migration issue that wasn't in scope. My VP doesn't like excuses, just wants to know what's happening and what's next. Can you draft this for me in a direct, professional tone that's under 200 words?" That's not harder. It just requires you to think for 90 seconds before you type. The output difference is significant. ### Context hack: ask it to ask you Here's the even better version of giving context: don't try to front-load it all yourself. Have Claude pull it out of you. State your goal, then tell the LLM to ask you clarifying questions before it writes anything. "I need a Friday status update for my VP. Before you draft it, ask me 3–5 questions you need answered to write it well." Now Claude names what it doesn't know. You answer. Then it drafts, grounded in context it actively collected rather than context you tried to predict. This works because the LLM knows what's missing better than you do. It's spent millions of tokens drafting status updates. It knows what makes one land or flop. Let it use that judgment to interview you. Don't rush to output. Have a conversation first. The draft gets dramatically better.

Ask AI to Ask You

Let Claude pull the context out of you. Don't rush to output.

1 YOU START

State the goal, ask for questions

"I need a status update for my VP. Before you draft it, ask me 3–5 clarifying questions."

2 CLAUDE ASKS

It names what's missing

"Who's the audience? What's the key update? What tone? What should I leave out?"

3 THEN IT DRAFTS

Now the output is grounded

A draft built on context Claude actively collected, not context you tried to predict.

Have a conversation before the output. The draft gets dramatically better.

## Mistake 5: Going it alone This one is more subtle than the others, but I think it might be the most important. When you're getting started with AI on your own, you have no reference point for whether what you're experiencing is normal. Is this output good? Is it taking me too long? Is everyone else getting better results than this? Am I doing something wrong? That uncertainty compounds over time. And without community or feedback, most people quietly stop. Not because AI failed them, but because the isolation made the friction feel like personal failure. The most useful advice I keep giving to new users is painfully obvious, but they all need to hear it a few more times: "Just ask Claude." Getting that reminder, and that reassurance that there is no secret power move the more experienced users are doing, they are also just asking Claude along the way, is a powerful unblocker. Without it, you might feel "bad at it" or that the tool just isn't mature enough to get the job done. And it's easy to walk away from it at that point. The professionals who are getting the most from AI right now are not doing it alone. They're showing each other what works. They're normalizing the friction. They're bouncing prompts off each other and stealing approaches that would never have occurred to them solo. You don't have to figure this out by yourself. ## The underlying pattern These five mistakes are all variations on the same thing: AI adoption is not a knowledge problem. It's a behavior and psychology problem. You don't need a course. You need low-stakes practice with real work, realistic expectations about the first response, a habit of giving context, and people to figure it out alongside. Start there. You don't have to be perfect about it. ## Where to practice this alongside other professionals The patterns above are what I see week after week in our community sessions. And watching people break through them in real time, with each other's support, is one of my favorite things about this work. If you want to practice getting started with AI alongside other professionals who are navigating the same questions, that's exactly what we do at [MVP Club](/community). No pressure, no performance. Just real work, real iteration, and people who get it.

Building an AI Champions Network at Your Company
How to identify, activate, and support peer AI champions who drive real adoption across your organization. A practical guide for non-technical leaders.

Most organizations that try to expand AI company-wide hit the same wall about three months in. The pilot was promising. A few people loved it. Leadership signed off. And then... nothing spread. The folks who were curious stayed curious. Everyone else went back to doing things the way they always had.

The tool wasn't the problem. The approach was.

What changes outcomes has nothing to do with a better platform or a bigger launch announcement. It comes down to people. Specifically, having the right people inside departments, embedded in real workflows, who are already trusted by their colleagues and willing to say, "Here's what I've been doing with this. Want to try it?"

That's the case for building an AI champions network.

## What an AI champions network actually is (and isn't)

An AI champion is a peer: someone in finance, operations, HR, or marketing who has figured out how to use Claude or ChatGPT in ways that actually help with the work they and their teammates do every day. They live inside the normal organizational structure. They're not a trainer, and they're not a dedicated "AI lead" set apart from the rest of the team.

They're the person who shows their colleague how they use Claude to draft the first pass at a weekly status update. The one who saves a template prompt they've been using and shares it in a Slack channel. The one who troubleshoots when someone says "I tried it and it gave me something weird."

What makes champions effective is proximity and trust, not technical expertise.

A champions network is a structured way to identify those people, give them light support, and help their knowledge travel across the organization instead of staying siloed in one person's head.

## Why peer-led beats top-down

In organizations where AI adoption stalls, leadership mandates it, IT provides access, and then HR sends an email with a link to an online resource. Adoption rates hover around 15-20%. People click through the content but don't change how they work.

In organizations where it actually spreads, one person in accounting figures out a genuinely useful workflow. Their manager notices they're finishing things faster. A few colleagues ask what they're doing. A month later, half the team has tried it.

The difference is social proof from a trusted peer, not instruction from an authority.

[NEEDS CITATION: Deloitte finding on leadership-driven AI adoption] But leadership setting direction is different from leadership driving adoption. Champions close that last mile.

[NEEDS CITATION: Citi champions network figures] The ratio holds: roughly one champion per 50-75 people creates enough density for knowledge to actually move.

## Who makes a good champion (it's not who you think)

The instinct is to find your most technically curious employees. The ones who've already been experimenting, who ask IT questions, who read about new tools on their own time. And yes, early adopters often make good champions.

But the most effective champions are the people who are already trusted, regardless of their technical background. They may not have been the first to try AI. What they have is credibility with their team. When they say something is worth trying, people actually try it.

Look for people who:

- Are known as problem-solvers and "go-to" people in their departments
- Communicate clearly and don't condescend
- Are genuinely curious (not performatively enthusiastic)
- Have some flexibility in how they structure their workday
- Are comfortable saying "I don't know" alongside "let me find out"

The non-technical background is often a feature (and I say that as someone who came to Claude without a coding background). Someone who figured out how to use it for real work without technical scaffolding has already done the translation work. They're more relatable, not less.

The average person is not going to push through friction. They just won't. That applies to would-be champions just as much as it applies to the colleagues they're trying to bring along. If you put the wrong people in the role, or overburden the right ones, the network goes quiet.

[NEEDS PERSONAL JILL EXAMPLE]

## How to structure the network without making it a second job

The biggest risk with a champions program is asking too much. Champions have real jobs. If you're expecting them to lead formal training sessions, create content, attend weekly committee meetings, and respond to every ad-hoc question in Slack, you'll burn them out or they'll quietly step back.

A practical structure that works:

**A steering layer (small):** 2-3 people who hold the overall strategy, maintain the resource library, and run the monthly champions sync. This is typically an internal comms or ops person plus whoever owns AI adoption at the leadership level. Their job is coordination, not content creation.

**A champions layer (distributed):** One champion per department or team of 50-75 people. Their minimum viable commitment is 30-60 minutes per week, doing what they'd probably do anyway: using AI in their work, sharing what they're learning with colleagues, and showing up when someone gets stuck.

**Informal community:** An internal Slack or Teams channel where champions and their teammates can share prompts, ask questions, and celebrate small wins. Low overhead, high value.

The key framing for recruiting champions is that this is optional and additive, not compulsory. "We'd love for you to be someone who shares what you're learning with your team" lands very differently than "we're adding this to your role description." (That distinction matters more than it sounds. One feels like an invitation. The other feels like surveillance.)

## What champions actually do week to week

This is where most programs get vague. Here's what it actually looks like in practice:

**Share one thing per week.** Not a formal presentation. A message in the team channel: "I've been using Claude to draft my talking points before 1:1s. Here's the prompt I've been using." That's it.

**Be available for short side-by-side moments.** When a colleague is curious but stuck, the champion spends 15 minutes with them, not to teach a class but to show them what they actually do. This is where adoption happens.

**Collect friction.** Champions are the best early warning system for what's not working. When a workflow breaks, when policies are unclear, when colleagues have the same question repeatedly, they surface it to the steering layer. This feedback loop is genuinely valuable for leadership.

**Connect people across teams.** One of the best things a champions network enables is cross-departmental learning. The champion in marketing who figured out a research workflow might share it with the operations champion who has the same underlying need.

If you want to see what this looks like at the peer level, [how to explain AI to your boss, team, or skeptical colleague](/learn/explain-ai-to-your-team) covers the communication side of these conversations in detail.

## The governance piece you can't skip

Champions need cover. This is one of the things that gets left out of most champions playbooks and then bites organizations six months later.

When a department head pushes back on a champion who's spending time experimenting with Claude instead of "real work," or when someone raises a concern about data privacy and the champion doesn't know the policy, the program unravels fast.

Before you launch a champions network, make sure:

**There's a clear policy on approved tools.** Champions can't confidently share workflows if they don't know what's allowed. If your organization doesn't have an AI policy yet, the champions program actually becomes a good reason to create one. [AI workplace policies: what you need to know](/learn/ai-workplace-policies) breaks down what most workplace policies actually say and where the gaps usually are.

**Champions have explicit top cover.** They should be able to point to a leadership statement or an internal document that says: "This work is encouraged. Time spent on this is time well spent." Without that, champions operate in a gray zone and will hedge.

**There's a clear escalation path.** When questions come up that champions can't answer (data security, vendor contracts, policy exceptions), they need to know who to direct people to. Champions should never have to guess on governance questions.

## Getting the first cohort started

You don't need a perfect program design before you start. You need five people who are already doing the work informally and a reason to bring them together.

**Week 1-2:** Identify 3-5 people across different departments who are already using AI tools in their daily work. Talk to them individually. Ask what they've figured out. Don't pitch the program yet. Just learn.

**Week 3-4:** Bring them together for an informal 60-minute conversation. Share what you heard from each of them. Ask what they'd find helpful in being more deliberate about sharing what they know. This group should design the program, not just receive it.

**Month 2:** Soft launch. Give the group a shared space (Slack channel, shared doc library), a light cadence (monthly check-in, no more), and one simple expectation: share something useful with your team once a week.

**Month 3:** Evaluate and expand. What's working? Who in the organization has been most receptive? Where are the natural next champions?

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what a member discovered when they started sharing AI workflows with their team]

The first cohort teaches you what the program needs to be. Don't over-engineer it before you have that learning.

## The mistake that kills most programs before they start

Announcing it too big.

The instinct when you're excited about an initiative is to brief leadership, send an all-staff email, and create a formal nomination process for champions. That sequence adds pressure before value has been demonstrated and puts champions in a spotlight they didn't sign up for.

The programs that work tend to start quietly, inside one or two teams, until they have enough real wins that people start asking why their department doesn't have something like it. Grassroots momentum is more sustainable than top-down mandate.

As one of our community members put it when she started sharing Claude workflows with her HR team: people don't need to be convinced that AI is important. They need to see that someone just like them has figured out something useful and is willing to show them.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific moment where a champion showed a colleague one thing and it changed how that colleague thinks about AI at work]

## The career angle: why being this person matters

If you're reading this, you may be thinking about whether you want to be this person at your organization rather than how to build the program from above. That's a legitimate position, and it's probably the more common one. (You don't have to wait for someone to hand you the title.)

Being an informal champion before any formal structure exists is actually the most valuable version of this. You're doing the real work, building real credibility, and positioning yourself as the person who helped your organization figure this out.

That kind of reputation doesn't show up on a job description. It shows up in who leadership turns to when they need someone who understands how AI actually fits into the work people do. [How to become the "AI person" on your team](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) covers this positioning in more depth.

## Where community comes in

The challenge with being a champion inside your organization is that you're often working through it alone. There's no internal peer group with the same mandate. There's no one to ask when you get stuck or when you're not sure whether what you're seeing is normal.

That's exactly what brings a lot of people to MVP Club. The working sessions, the Getting Unstuck calls, the community channels: they function as the external champions network for people who are supporting AI adoption at their organizations without a lot of internal backup.

If that's where you are, come find your people at [mvpclub.ai/community](/community). You don't have to have it all figured out to show up.

Most organizations that try to expand AI company-wide hit the same wall about three months in. The pilot was promising. A few people loved it. Leadership signed off. And then… nothing spread. The folks who were curious stayed curious. Everyone else went back to doing things the way they always had.

The tool wasn’t the problem. The approach was.

What changes outcomes has nothing to do with a better platform or a bigger launch announcement. It comes down to people. Specifically, having the right people inside departments, embedded in real workflows, who are already trusted by their colleagues and willing to say, “Here’s what I’ve been doing with this. Want to try it?”

That’s the case for building an AI champions network.

What an AI champions network actually is (and isn’t)

An AI champion is a peer: someone in finance, operations, HR, or marketing who has figured out how to use Claude or ChatGPT in ways that actually help with the work they and their teammates do every day. They live inside the normal organizational structure. They’re not a trainer, and they’re not a dedicated “AI lead” set apart from the rest of the team.

They’re the person who shows their colleague how they use Claude to draft the first pass at a weekly status update. The one who saves a template prompt they’ve been using and shares it in a Slack channel. The one who troubleshoots when someone says “I tried it and it gave me something weird.”

What makes champions effective is proximity and trust, not technical expertise.

A champions network is a structured way to identify those people, give them light support, and help their knowledge travel across the organization instead of staying siloed in one person’s head.

Why peer-led beats top-down

In organizations where AI adoption stalls, leadership mandates it, IT provides access, and then HR sends an email with a link to an online resource. Adoption rates hover around 15-20%. People click through the content but don’t change how they work.

In organizations where it actually spreads, one person in accounting figures out a genuinely useful workflow. Their manager notices they’re finishing things faster. A few colleagues ask what they’re doing. A month later, half the team has tried it.

The difference is social proof from a trusted peer, not instruction from an authority.

[NEEDS CITATION: Deloitte finding on leadership-driven AI adoption] But leadership setting direction is different from leadership driving adoption. Champions close that last mile.

[NEEDS CITATION: Citi champions network figures] The ratio holds: roughly one champion per 50-75 people creates enough density for knowledge to actually move.

Who makes a good champion (it’s not who you think)

The instinct is to find your most technically curious employees. The ones who’ve already been experimenting, who ask IT questions, who read about new tools on their own time. And yes, early adopters often make good champions.

But the most effective champions are the people who are already trusted, regardless of their technical background. They may not have been the first to try AI. What they have is credibility with their team. When they say something is worth trying, people actually try it.

Look for people who:

  • Are known as problem-solvers and “go-to” people in their departments
  • Communicate clearly and don’t condescend
  • Are genuinely curious (not performatively enthusiastic)
  • Have some flexibility in how they structure their workday
  • Are comfortable saying “I don’t know” alongside “let me find out”

The non-technical background is often a feature (and I say that as someone who came to Claude without a coding background). Someone who figured out how to use it for real work without technical scaffolding has already done the translation work. They’re more relatable, not less.

The average person is not going to push through friction. They just won’t. That applies to would-be champions just as much as it applies to the colleagues they’re trying to bring along. If you put the wrong people in the role, or overburden the right ones, the network goes quiet.

[NEEDS PERSONAL JILL EXAMPLE]

How to structure the network without making it a second job

The biggest risk with a champions program is asking too much. Champions have real jobs. If you’re expecting them to lead formal training sessions, create content, attend weekly committee meetings, and respond to every ad-hoc question in Slack, you’ll burn them out or they’ll quietly step back.

A practical structure that works:

A steering layer (small): 2-3 people who hold the overall strategy, maintain the resource library, and run the monthly champions sync. This is typically an internal comms or ops person plus whoever owns AI adoption at the leadership level. Their job is coordination, not content creation.

A champions layer (distributed): One champion per department or team of 50-75 people. Their minimum viable commitment is 30-60 minutes per week, doing what they’d probably do anyway: using AI in their work, sharing what they’re learning with colleagues, and showing up when someone gets stuck.

Informal community: An internal Slack or Teams channel where champions and their teammates can share prompts, ask questions, and celebrate small wins. Low overhead, high value.

The key framing for recruiting champions is that this is optional and additive, not compulsory. “We’d love for you to be someone who shares what you’re learning with your team” lands very differently than “we’re adding this to your role description.” (That distinction matters more than it sounds. One feels like an invitation. The other feels like surveillance.)

What champions actually do week to week

This is where most programs get vague. Here’s what it actually looks like in practice:

Share one thing per week. Not a formal presentation. A message in the team channel: “I’ve been using Claude to draft my talking points before 1:1s. Here’s the prompt I’ve been using.” That’s it.

Be available for short side-by-side moments. When a colleague is curious but stuck, the champion spends 15 minutes with them, not to teach a class but to show them what they actually do. This is where adoption happens.

Collect friction. Champions are the best early warning system for what’s not working. When a workflow breaks, when policies are unclear, when colleagues have the same question repeatedly, they surface it to the steering layer. This feedback loop is genuinely valuable for leadership.

Connect people across teams. One of the best things a champions network enables is cross-departmental learning. The champion in marketing who figured out a research workflow might share it with the operations champion who has the same underlying need.

If you want to see what this looks like at the peer level, how to explain AI to your boss, team, or skeptical colleague covers the communication side of these conversations in detail.

The governance piece you can’t skip

Champions need cover. This is one of the things that gets left out of most champions playbooks and then bites organizations six months later.

When a department head pushes back on a champion who’s spending time experimenting with Claude instead of “real work,” or when someone raises a concern about data privacy and the champion doesn’t know the policy, the program unravels fast.

Before you launch a champions network, make sure:

There’s a clear policy on approved tools. Champions can’t confidently share workflows if they don’t know what’s allowed. If your organization doesn’t have an AI policy yet, the champions program actually becomes a good reason to create one. AI workplace policies: what you need to know breaks down what most workplace policies actually say and where the gaps usually are.

Champions have explicit top cover. They should be able to point to a leadership statement or an internal document that says: “This work is encouraged. Time spent on this is time well spent.” Without that, champions operate in a gray zone and will hedge.

There’s a clear escalation path. When questions come up that champions can’t answer (data security, vendor contracts, policy exceptions), they need to know who to direct people to. Champions should never have to guess on governance questions.

Getting the first cohort started

You don’t need a perfect program design before you start. You need five people who are already doing the work informally and a reason to bring them together.

Week 1-2: Identify 3-5 people across different departments who are already using AI tools in their daily work. Talk to them individually. Ask what they’ve figured out. Don’t pitch the program yet. Just learn.

Week 3-4: Bring them together for an informal 60-minute conversation. Share what you heard from each of them. Ask what they’d find helpful in being more deliberate about sharing what they know. This group should design the program, not just receive it.

Month 2: Soft launch. Give the group a shared space (Slack channel, shared doc library), a light cadence (monthly check-in, no more), and one simple expectation: share something useful with your team once a week.

Month 3: Evaluate and expand. What’s working? Who in the organization has been most receptive? Where are the natural next champions?

AI for Consultants: Research, Deliverables, and Client Communication
How consultants are using Claude and ChatGPT to do better research, write sharper deliverables, and communicate more clearly with clients.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A consultant community member's before/after on client kickoff prep time, specifically research synthesis and pre-read drafting. The pattern we're seeing: 4-6 hours of prep time compressing to 60-90 minutes. Confirm with a named member before publishing.]

A consultant in MVP Club's community was spending several hours preparing for every client kickoff: pulling background research, synthesizing industry context, drafting the agenda, prepping the pre-read. With Claude handling the synthesis, she's doing the same prep in a fraction of the time, with her attention on the judgment calls instead of the assembly work.

That's not automation. The work is still hers. The thinking is still hers. Claude is handling the parts of the prep that used to eat her afternoon without actually requiring her expertise to execute.

Consulting is one of the roles where AI is making the biggest practical difference right now, and not in the ways people usually expect. It's not about writing reports faster. It's about the quality of thinking you can bring to a client when you're not buried in the scaffolding work around that thinking.

## The three highest-impact use cases for consultants

Before diving into specifics, here's the honest picture of where Claude and ChatGPT are actually earning their keep in consulting work right now.

**Research synthesis:** Taking large volumes of source material (industry reports, interview notes, competitive analyses, earnings calls) and pulling out what's relevant to a specific question. This is where AI saves the most time for consultants, because synthesis is time-intensive and doesn't require the kind of judgment your clients are paying for.

**Deliverable drafting:** Getting from "notes and ideas" to "structured document" faster. AI is good at first drafts, outlines, executive summaries, and translating messy thinking into clean structure. The insight still comes from you. The scaffolding comes from Claude.

**Client communication:** Drafting emails, preparing talking points, anticipating objections, and sharpening messaging for different audiences. This is one of the most underused use cases in consulting, and one of the highest-leverage ones.

We'll go deep on each of these. But start with whichever one costs you the most time right now.

## Use case 1: Research synthesis (the fastest win)

A lot of consulting research follows the same basic pattern: gather sources, read everything, pull out what's relevant, synthesize into a point of view. The gathering and reading are yours. The synthesis is where AI can dramatically compress your time.

Here's what this looks like in practice. You're preparing for a client in the healthcare technology space. You have a 60-page industry report, three competitor annual reports, and a handful of analyst articles. You need to understand the competitive field and identify where your client has differentiated positioning.

Instead of reading every page front-to-back, you paste the content into Claude (or upload the documents) and ask a specific question: "Read this material and identify the three most significant competitive pressures facing mid-sized health tech companies right now, with evidence from the sources."

What comes back is not a replacement for your analysis. It's a starting point that would have taken you two hours to produce manually. You now spend forty-five minutes evaluating and refining Claude's synthesis rather than generating it from scratch.

The critical shift is how you ask. "Summarize this report" gets you a generic summary. "Tell me the three biggest risks to our client's current positioning based on this competitive landscape" gets you something you can actually use in client conversation.

### A prompt worth keeping

For research synthesis, this framing reliably produces useful output:

*"You are helping me prepare for a client engagement in [industry]. Here is the background material: [paste content]. My client's key question is [specific question]. Based only on the material I've provided, identify [specific deliverable: risks, opportunities, gaps, competitive factors]. Flag anything where the evidence is thin or where I should gather more information."*

The last instruction matters: asking Claude to flag weak evidence keeps you honest and keeps the synthesis trustworthy.

## Use case 2: Deliverable drafting

The blank page problem is real in consulting. You have the insight from your analysis. You have the client's situation in your head. You know what you want to say. Getting it onto the page in a structure that works for a senior audience is a separate skill that takes time even when the thinking is done.

This is where Claude earns its $20/month.

The approach that works: instead of asking Claude to "write the executive summary," give it the raw inputs and let it do the structural heavy lifting. Here's what that looks like.

You paste in your notes from a discovery workshop, your analysis of the data, and any specific conclusions you've reached. Then you ask: "Draft an executive summary for a CFO audience. The main finding is [X]. The supporting evidence includes [Y and Z]. The recommended action is [A]. Keep it under 400 words, lead with the finding, and assume the CFO has ten minutes to read this and will forward it to her leadership team."

That last sentence, about the CFO forwarding it, is the kind of audience context that makes the difference between a generic draft and one that's actually useful. The more specific you are about who's reading it and what they'll do with it, the better the draft.

What comes back still needs editing. The language may be too formal, or not formal enough. The structure may not match your firm's conventions. Certain points may be mis-weighted. That's all fixable in twenty minutes of editing. The alternative is starting from a blank page and spending two hours getting to the same place.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after from a consultant's deliverable, showing how a raw-notes prompt produced a first draft and what edits were made to make it client-ready]

### For slide decks

The same approach works for presentation outlines. Give Claude the story you're trying to tell and the audience it's for, and ask for a slide-by-slide outline with one-sentence talking points for each slide. You'll still build the slides yourself (or use your firm's templates), but the outline gives you a structure to push back against rather than a blank canvas to fill.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A consultant describing the first time they used Claude for a deliverable outline, and what surprised them about the output]

## Use case 3: Client communication

This is the underrated one. Consulting involves constant written communication with clients: emails that need to strike exactly the right tone, updates that need to be brief without feeling dismissive, meeting follow-ups that capture decisions without being bureaucratic, scope discussions that are firm without being defensive.

Getting this communication right takes time and attention that many consultants underinvest in because they're focused on the analysis work. AI can help close that gap.

Here's an example. You've just finished a discovery workshop. The client asked three questions that your team doesn't have answers to yet. You need to send a same-day follow-up that acknowledges the open questions without making the client nervous, summarizes what was decided, and frames the next two weeks of work.

That email might take you thirty minutes to draft well. You could paste your notes from the workshop into Claude with this prompt: "Draft a follow-up email to a client after a discovery workshop. The tone should be confident and organized. We need to acknowledge three open questions [list them] without signaling that we're behind. We covered the following decisions [list them]. The next steps are [list them]. The audience is the VP of Operations and her chief of staff."

You'll still edit it. But you'll edit it in ten minutes, not write it in thirty.

The same applies to scope conversations, which are among the most delicate communications in consulting. Before you send any email that involves scope, timeline, or budget, you can run your draft through Claude with a simple prompt: "Read this email. Is there any language that could be read as defensive, ambiguous about our commitments, or that might make the client nervous? Suggest specific edits."

That's not outsourcing judgment. That's using a thinking partner to pressure-test your communication before it reaches the client.

## Tool recommendations

For most consulting work, Claude is currently the better choice over ChatGPT. The reasons are practical: Claude handles longer documents better (important for pasting in research material), its default writing style is closer to professional business communication, and it tends to stay on-task with complex, multi-part instructions.

For research synthesis specifically, Claude's ability to process long documents without losing track of the question is noticeably stronger than ChatGPT's free tier.

That said, both tools are worth trying for your specific workflow. Some consultants find ChatGPT's conversational style better for thinking out loud during the analysis phase. If you're already paying for ChatGPT Plus, try it for three tasks before buying a second subscription.

The paid tier is worth it for consulting work. The difference between Claude Pro ($20/month) and the free tier is meaningful when you're pasting in 60-page reports or asking for long-form drafts. Think of it as a monthly retainer for a research assistant who works at midnight and never complains about scope creep.

## The career angle

Here's what this is actually about for consultants, beyond the efficiency gains.

The consultants who are building real AI capability right now are not just doing their current work faster. They're changing what they can promise a client. When research synthesis takes ninety minutes instead of six hours, you can do more synthesis per engagement. When deliverables come together in half the time, you can iterate more. When client communication is sharper, you get fewer follow-up questions and more trust.

That compounds over time. Clients notice when they work with someone who is consistently well-prepared, whose documents are clean and clear, whose communication is timely and precisely calibrated. They don't know you're using Claude. They just know the work is good.

There's also the internal credibility angle. In a lot of consulting firms, the people who are figuring out how to use AI in their practice are becoming the people others come to for advice. That's a career position worth occupying. It doesn't require being technical. It requires being curious and willing to try things.

## Your first week: a five-day micro-practice plan

The fastest way to build AI capability as a consultant is to use it on real client work, not test cases. If you want a broader framework for building the daily habit, the [first week with AI guide](/learn/first-week-with-ai) covers the fundamentals. Here's the consulting-specific version.

**Monday:** Take a research document you already have. Paste it into Claude. Ask one specific question about it (not "summarize this," but a real question relevant to a current engagement). Spend fifteen minutes evaluating and editing the response.

**Tuesday:** Draft one client-facing email with Claude's help. Use the prompt structure above: give it the context, the audience, the tone, and the goal. Compare the draft to what you would have written. What did it get right? What needed fixing?

**Wednesday:** Pull your notes from a recent client meeting. Ask Claude to draft a one-page meeting summary that would make sense to someone who wasn't in the room. Edit it until it's accurate and sounds like you.

**Thursday:** Use Claude as a thinking partner on a current client challenge. Describe the situation, what you know, what you don't know, and where you're stuck. Ask it to generate the three most important questions you should be asking. Push back on the ones that aren't useful.

**Friday:** Reflect. Which task saved you the most time? Which felt most like useful output and which felt like generic output? That reflection tells you where to focus in week two.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can I paste client documents into Claude or ChatGPT?**

Check your firm's policies before you do. Most major consulting firms have guidelines about client data and AI tools. Some have enterprise agreements with Anthropic or OpenAI that include data privacy protections. Others prohibit pasting client information into consumer AI products. When in doubt, anonymize the content before pasting: replace client names, remove identifying details, use placeholders for proprietary numbers.

**What if the AI output sounds too polished or corporate?**

Edit it. The most common issue consultants report is that Claude's default register is slightly too formal, or uses phrasing that doesn't match their firm's voice. That's a normal editing task, not a failure of the tool. Read the draft aloud. Every sentence that sounds like a press release needs rewriting. Every sentence that sounds like you can stay.

**How do I know when to trust the synthesis and when to check the sources?**

When it matters, always check the sources. For research synthesis, Claude can miss nuance, misweight evidence, or draw on its general training rather than the document you provided. The prompt "Flag anything where the evidence is thin or where I should gather more information" helps surface the uncertain parts. But for client-facing work, you should be reading the underlying sources on the points that matter most.

**How long does it take to get good at this?**

A week of daily practice. Not weeks. The learning curve for using Claude effectively on consulting work is short because the core skill is giving clear instructions, which is something consultants already know how to do. The prompts in this article are starting points, not final answers. Try them, adjust them for your work, and build from there. If you've hit early frustration with AI not producing useful output, the [common beginner mistakes](/learn/ai-beginner-mistakes) article covers the patterns that derail most people in the first few weeks.

## Practice alongside people in the same situation

A lot of consultants are figuring this out alone, which is slower than it needs to be. Seeing how someone else uses Claude for a deliverable, or hearing what prompt worked for a difficult client email, accelerates the process more than reading about it does.

That's the kind of peer learning that happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions: consultants, project managers, analysts, and coaches sharing what's working in their actual practice. If you want to see that in action, come visit the [MVP Club community](/community).
