[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A consultant community member’s before/after on client kickoff prep time, specifically research synthesis and pre-read drafting. The pattern we’re seeing: 4-6 hours of prep time compressing to 60-90 minutes. Confirm with a named member before publishing.]

A consultant in MVP Club’s community was spending several hours preparing for every client kickoff: pulling background research, synthesizing industry context, drafting the agenda, prepping the pre-read. With Claude handling the synthesis, she’s doing the same prep in a fraction of the time, with her attention on the judgment calls instead of the assembly work.

That’s not automation. The work is still hers. The thinking is still hers. Claude is handling the parts of the prep that used to eat her afternoon without actually requiring her expertise to execute.

Consulting is one of the roles where AI is making the biggest practical difference right now, and not in the ways people usually expect. It’s not about writing reports faster. It’s about the quality of thinking you can bring to a client when you’re not buried in the scaffolding work around that thinking.

The three highest-impact use cases for consultants

Before diving into specifics, here’s the honest picture of where Claude and ChatGPT are actually earning their keep in consulting work right now.

Research synthesis: Taking large volumes of source material (industry reports, interview notes, competitive analyses, earnings calls) and pulling out what’s relevant to a specific question. This is where AI saves the most time for consultants, because synthesis is time-intensive and doesn’t require the kind of judgment your clients are paying for.

Deliverable drafting: Getting from “notes and ideas” to “structured document” faster. AI is good at first drafts, outlines, executive summaries, and translating messy thinking into clean structure. The insight still comes from you. The scaffolding comes from Claude.

Client communication: Drafting emails, preparing talking points, anticipating objections, and sharpening messaging for different audiences. This is one of the most underused use cases in consulting, and one of the highest-leverage ones.

We’ll go deep on each of these. But start with whichever one costs you the most time right now.

Use case 1: Research synthesis (the fastest win)

A lot of consulting research follows the same basic pattern: gather sources, read everything, pull out what’s relevant, synthesize into a point of view. The gathering and reading are yours. The synthesis is where AI can dramatically compress your time.

Here’s what this looks like in practice. You’re preparing for a client in the healthcare technology space. You have a 60-page industry report, three competitor annual reports, and a handful of analyst articles. You need to understand the competitive field and identify where your client has differentiated positioning.

Instead of reading every page front-to-back, you paste the content into Claude (or upload the documents) and ask a specific question: “Read this material and identify the three most significant competitive pressures facing mid-sized health tech companies right now, with evidence from the sources.”

What comes back is not a replacement for your analysis. It’s a starting point that would have taken you two hours to produce manually. You now spend forty-five minutes evaluating and refining Claude’s synthesis rather than generating it from scratch.

The critical shift is how you ask. “Summarize this report” gets you a generic summary. “Tell me the three biggest risks to our client’s current positioning based on this competitive landscape” gets you something you can actually use in client conversation.

A prompt worth keeping

For research synthesis, this framing reliably produces useful output:

“You are helping me prepare for a client engagement in [industry]. Here is the background material: [paste content]. My client’s key question is [specific question]. Based only on the material I’ve provided, identify [specific deliverable: risks, opportunities, gaps, competitive factors]. Flag anything where the evidence is thin or where I should gather more information.”

The last instruction matters: asking Claude to flag weak evidence keeps you honest and keeps the synthesis trustworthy.

Use case 2: Deliverable drafting

The blank page problem is real in consulting. You have the insight from your analysis. You have the client’s situation in your head. You know what you want to say. Getting it onto the page in a structure that works for a senior audience is a separate skill that takes time even when the thinking is done.

This is where Claude earns its $20/month.

The approach that works: instead of asking Claude to “write the executive summary,” give it the raw inputs and let it do the structural heavy lifting. Here’s what that looks like.

You paste in your notes from a discovery workshop, your analysis of the data, and any specific conclusions you’ve reached. Then you ask: “Draft an executive summary for a CFO audience. The main finding is [X]. The supporting evidence includes [Y and Z]. The recommended action is [A]. Keep it under 400 words, lead with the finding, and assume the CFO has ten minutes to read this and will forward it to her leadership team.”

That last sentence, about the CFO forwarding it, is the kind of audience context that makes the difference between a generic draft and one that’s actually useful. The more specific you are about who’s reading it and what they’ll do with it, the better the draft.

What comes back still needs editing. The language may be too formal, or not formal enough. The structure may not match your firm’s conventions. Certain points may be mis-weighted. That’s all fixable in twenty minutes of editing. The alternative is starting from a blank page and spending two hours getting to the same place.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after from a consultant’s deliverable, showing how a raw-notes prompt produced a first draft and what edits were made to make it client-ready]

For slide decks

The same approach works for presentation outlines. Give Claude the story you’re trying to tell and the audience it’s for, and ask for a slide-by-slide outline with one-sentence talking points for each slide. You’ll still build the slides yourself (or use your firm’s templates), but the outline gives you a structure to push back against rather than a blank canvas to fill.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A consultant describing the first time they used Claude for a deliverable outline, and what surprised them about the output]

Use case 3: Client communication

This is the underrated one. Consulting involves constant written communication with clients: emails that need to strike exactly the right tone, updates that need to be brief without feeling dismissive, meeting follow-ups that capture decisions without being bureaucratic, scope discussions that are firm without being defensive.

Getting this communication right takes time and attention that many consultants underinvest in because they’re focused on the analysis work. AI can help close that gap.

Here’s an example. You’ve just finished a discovery workshop. The client asked three questions that your team doesn’t have answers to yet. You need to send a same-day follow-up that acknowledges the open questions without making the client nervous, summarizes what was decided, and frames the next two weeks of work.

That email might take you thirty minutes to draft well. You could paste your notes from the workshop into Claude with this prompt: “Draft a follow-up email to a client after a discovery workshop. The tone should be confident and organized. We need to acknowledge three open questions [list them] without signaling that we’re behind. We covered the following decisions [list them]. The next steps are [list them]. The audience is the VP of Operations and her chief of staff.”

You’ll still edit it. But you’ll edit it in ten minutes, not write it in thirty.

The same applies to scope conversations, which are among the most delicate communications in consulting. Before you send any email that involves scope, timeline, or budget, you can run your draft through Claude with a simple prompt: “Read this email. Is there any language that could be read as defensive, ambiguous about our commitments, or that might make the client nervous? Suggest specific edits.”

That’s not outsourcing judgment. That’s using a thinking partner to pressure-test your communication before it reaches the client.

Tool recommendations

For most consulting work, Claude is currently the better choice over ChatGPT. The reasons are practical: Claude handles longer documents better (important for pasting in research material), its default writing style is closer to professional business communication, and it tends to stay on-task with complex, multi-part instructions.

For research synthesis specifically, Claude’s ability to process long documents without losing track of the question is noticeably stronger than ChatGPT’s free tier.

That said, both tools are worth trying for your specific workflow. Some consultants find ChatGPT’s conversational style better for thinking out loud during the analysis phase. If you’re already paying for ChatGPT Plus, try it for three tasks before buying a second subscription.

The paid tier is worth it for consulting work. The difference between Claude Pro ($20/month) and the free tier is meaningful when you’re pasting in 60-page reports or asking for long-form drafts. Think of it as a monthly retainer for a research assistant who works at midnight and never complains about scope creep.

The career angle

Here’s what this is actually about for consultants, beyond the efficiency gains.

The consultants who are building real AI capability right now are not just doing their current work faster. They’re changing what they can promise a client. When research synthesis takes ninety minutes instead of six hours, you can do more synthesis per engagement. When deliverables come together in half the time, you can iterate more. When client communication is sharper, you get fewer follow-up questions and more trust.

That compounds over time. Clients notice when they work with someone who is consistently well-prepared, whose documents are clean and clear, whose communication is timely and precisely calibrated. They don’t know you’re using Claude. They just know the work is good.

There’s also the internal credibility angle. In a lot of consulting firms, the people who are figuring out how to use AI in their practice are becoming the people others come to for advice. That’s a career position worth occupying. It doesn’t require being technical. It requires being curious and willing to try things.

Your first week: a five-day micro-practice plan

The fastest way to build AI capability as a consultant is to use it on real client work, not test cases. If you want a broader framework for building the daily habit, the first week with AI guide covers the fundamentals. Here’s the consulting-specific version.

Monday: Take a research document you already have. Paste it into Claude. Ask one specific question about it (not “summarize this,” but a real question relevant to a current engagement). Spend fifteen minutes evaluating and editing the response.

Tuesday: Draft one client-facing email with Claude’s help. Use the prompt structure above: give it the context, the audience, the tone, and the goal. Compare the draft to what you would have written. What did it get right? What needed fixing?

Wednesday: Pull your notes from a recent client meeting. Ask Claude to draft a one-page meeting summary that would make sense to someone who wasn’t in the room. Edit it until it’s accurate and sounds like you.

Thursday: Use Claude as a thinking partner on a current client challenge. Describe the situation, what you know, what you don’t know, and where you’re stuck. Ask it to generate the three most important questions you should be asking. Push back on the ones that aren’t useful.

Friday: Reflect. Which task saved you the most time? Which felt most like useful output and which felt like generic output? That reflection tells you where to focus in week two.

Frequently asked questions

Can I paste client documents into Claude or ChatGPT?

Check your firm’s policies before you do. Most major consulting firms have guidelines about client data and AI tools. Some have enterprise agreements with Anthropic or OpenAI that include data privacy protections. Others prohibit pasting client information into consumer AI products. When in doubt, anonymize the content before pasting: replace client names, remove identifying details, use placeholders for proprietary numbers.

What if the AI output sounds too polished or corporate?

Edit it. The most common issue consultants report is that Claude’s default register is slightly too formal, or uses phrasing that doesn’t match their firm’s voice. That’s a normal editing task, not a failure of the tool. Read the draft aloud. Every sentence that sounds like a press release needs rewriting. Every sentence that sounds like you can stay.

How do I know when to trust the synthesis and when to check the sources?

When it matters, always check the sources. For research synthesis, Claude can miss nuance, misweight evidence, or draw on its general training rather than the document you provided. The prompt “Flag anything where the evidence is thin or where I should gather more information” helps surface the uncertain parts. But for client-facing work, you should be reading the underlying sources on the points that matter most.

How long does it take to get good at this?

A week of daily practice. Not weeks. The learning curve for using Claude effectively on consulting work is short because the core skill is giving clear instructions, which is something consultants already know how to do. The prompts in this article are starting points, not final answers. Try them, adjust them for your work, and build from there. If you’ve hit early frustration with AI not producing useful output, the common beginner mistakes article covers the patterns that derail most people in the first few weeks.

Practice alongside people in the same situation

A lot of consultants are figuring this out alone, which is slower than it needs to be. Seeing how someone else uses Claude for a deliverable, or hearing what prompt worked for a difficult client email, accelerates the process more than reading about it does.

That’s the kind of peer learning that happens in MVP Club’s weekly sessions: consultants, project managers, analysts, and coaches sharing what’s working in their actual practice. If you want to see that in action, come visit the MVP Club community.

AI for Executive Assistants: Scheduling, Communications, and Research
How executive assistants are using Claude and ChatGPT to handle scheduling, communications, and research faster without losing the human touch.

Your job is already about managing complexity on behalf of someone else. You context-switch faster than anyone in the building, you draft communications that sound like another person, and you do research that makes your executive look prepared for rooms they barely have time to show up to. That's not a small thing. That's a set of skills that maps almost perfectly onto how AI tools work best.

Executive assistants who are picking up Claude and ChatGPT right now aren't replacing their judgment. They're giving themselves a capable collaborator for the parts of the job that eat time without requiring the relationship intelligence only they have.

Here's where the biggest gains are, and how to get started.

## The three areas where AI for executive assistants pays off fastest

Not every task is worth rerouting through an AI tool. But three areas of the EA role are particularly well-suited to working with Claude or ChatGPT: drafting communications, preparing for meetings and research, and handling the information synthesis that used to mean reading everything yourself.

### 1. Communications: drafting in someone else's voice

Writing emails, memos, and messages that sound like your executive, not you, is one of the hardest parts of the job. You've spent months learning how they phrase things, what they care about, what they don't say directly but expect people to understand. That institutional knowledge is yours. AI can't replace it. But it can take that knowledge and use it to produce faster first drafts.

The key is giving Claude or ChatGPT enough context upfront. Not "write an email declining this meeting" but something more like this:

> "Draft an email from Sarah (VP of Ops, direct but warm, hates long emails) to her direct report declining a project proposal. The proposal is well-researched but the timing doesn't work with Q3 priorities. Sarah wants to encourage the person, leave the door open for Q4, and not get into specifics about why. Keep it under 100 words."

That level of context takes 45 seconds to write and produces a first draft that needs minor adjustments rather than a full rewrite. If you do this once a day for a week, the time savings compound.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An EA or Chief of Staff describing how they feed their executive's communication style into Claude to get usable first drafts]

### 2. Meeting prep and research: brief faster, better

An hour of research used to mean reading five articles and hoping you caught the important parts. With Claude, you can paste in a document, a news article, a company earnings call transcript, or a collection of meeting notes and ask specific questions about it.

The move isn't "summarize this for me." The move is: "My executive is meeting with the CFO of [company] tomorrow to discuss a potential partnership. Read this and tell me the three things she should know before walking in, the one number she should have memorized, and the most likely question she'll be asked that she won't expect."

That specificity is what separates useful AI output from generic output. [What AI can and can't do](/learn/what-ai-can-and-cant-do) matters here: Claude is good at pulling out what you tell it to look for. It's not good at knowing what matters to your executive unless you tell it.

Matt Hastings, co-founder of MVP Club, described his own version of this in a recent community session: "My current workflow checks our meeting transcripts, goes and opens our transcript website, navigates to the transcripts from the past week, reads through them, and gives me a summary. The web page navigation, scheduled automation, task workflow automation is very useful for getting information that's otherwise in a different context. You just say, go find it."

That's the EA mindset applied to AI: you're not doing less work, you're redirecting your effort toward the parts that require your judgment.

### 3. Scheduling logistics: reducing the back-and-forth

Scheduling coordination isn't intellectually demanding, but it is cognitively taxing because of the constant context-switching it requires. AI tools can help here in two ways.

First, drafting scheduling emails. Most scheduling emails follow predictable patterns: proposing times, confirming details, sending reminders, following up on no-responses. Claude can draft these in seconds if you describe the situation. You edit for accuracy and send. The drafting is the part that eats attention.

Second, building reusable templates. If your executive has a recurring set of meeting types (board prep, 1:1s, client dinners, media calls), you can build a set of prompts in Claude that handle the standard scheduling communications for each. Set it up once. Reuse it every week.

Angela Peat, an MVP Club community member, put it well in a community session: "If you had transcripts of your meetings and they were a cadence meeting you had on the regular, you could have AI send everyone an email with the updates from the last meeting. Or some really nice ways of automating keeping people's context put together when people jump in and out." Jill Ozovek, MVP Club co-founder, added: "You could have like a recap with who said they were doing what, and schedule it so 48 hours before the next meeting, it resends a shorter recap with who said they'd do what. How many times have we all been to a meeting when someone's like, oh, I didn't get to do that."

## A detailed walkthrough: drafting communications in Claude

This is the quickest win for most EAs, so here's exactly how to do it.

**Step 1: Build a context block for your executive.**

Before your first session with Claude or ChatGPT, spend 15 minutes writing a brief description of your executive: their communication style, their typical concerns, the audiences they write to most often, phrases they use and phrases they'd never use, and the general tone of their external vs. internal communications.

Save this as a document. You'll paste it into the beginning of any new Claude chat where you're drafting on their behalf.

**Step 2: Give Claude the situation, not just the task.**

Instead of "write an email declining this meeting," give Claude the situation:

- Who is this person to your executive?
- What is the subtext of the situation?
- What outcome does your executive actually want from this email?
- What should the tone be?
- Are there things that should NOT be said?

The more situation, the better the draft.

**Step 3: Edit for voice, not content.**

The first Claude draft will probably get the content right and the voice partially right. Your job is to edit for the places where it doesn't sound like your executive. Make a note of the phrases you changed. Over time, you can add those corrections back into your context block so future drafts need less editing.

**Step 4: Build your prompt library.**

After a month of doing this, you'll have a set of situation types you handle regularly. Write a standardized prompt for each one. Keep them somewhere you can grab them quickly. This is the stage where the time savings become significant.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after showing a generic EA email prompt vs. a context-rich prompt and the resulting difference in output quality]

## Tool recommendations

For most EAs, **Claude** (claude.ai) is the better starting point for communication drafting because its outputs tend to read more naturally and it handles longer context well. The $20/month Claude Pro subscription is worth it if you're using it daily: faster responses, longer conversations, and access to the best available model.

**ChatGPT** (chat.openai.com) is a solid alternative and slightly more familiar to most people. For research synthesis, both tools perform well. Try both for a week with the same types of tasks and see which output requires less editing for your specific use cases.

Neither tool connects directly to your calendar or email unless you're using a tool like Microsoft Copilot (if your organization has it) or a third-party integration. For most EAs, the workflow is: complete a task or get information, open Claude, draft or synthesize, copy to your actual tool. It's a two-window workflow, not a one-click solution. That's worth knowing upfront.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An EA who switched from ChatGPT to Claude (or vice versa) for communications and what they noticed]

## How AI positions you as an EA

There's a career angle here worth naming directly. Executive assistants who are using AI tools effectively right now are doing something that gets noticed.

The pattern Matt Hastings described at dinner with two senior colleagues captures it: one was a Chief of Staff "still very much at the beginning of her journey," and the other was a high-level ops person who was nearly keeping up with the engineers. The difference wasn't technical background. It was attitude: "She's non-technical, but she's just like, I'll learn this. I have to."

EAs who develop fluency with AI tools have a natural pathway to expanding their scope: AI project coordination, internal tool adoption, workflow design. These are roles that didn't exist two years ago and that organizations are actively trying to fill with people who already understand how they work.

That's not pressure to become something you're not. It's context for why the time you put into learning these tools compounds in your favor. [The career case for building AI into your daily practice](/learn/ai-learning-path) is straightforward: you're developing a skill that most people at your level are not yet developing.

## Your first week: a 5-day micro-practice schedule

You don't need to overhaul how you work. You need five small experiments.

**Monday:** Draft one email in Claude that you'd normally write yourself. Give it real context (situation, audience, tone, constraints). Compare the draft to what you would have written. Note what you changed.

**Tuesday:** Take meeting notes or a document you need to read and paste it into Claude. Ask three specific questions about it instead of reading the whole thing. See if you got what you needed in less time.

**Wednesday:** Write a 10-sentence description of your executive's communication style. Save it. Use it as the opening context block for a drafting session.

**Thursday:** Build one reusable prompt for a scheduling email type you send regularly. Test it. Refine it.

**Friday:** Reflect: which task felt most useful? Which felt awkward? That tells you where to focus next week.

The goal in week one isn't to be efficient. It's to find the one or two use cases where you naturally reach for Claude instead of having to remind yourself to try it. Those are the ones that will stick.

## Frequently asked questions

**Will my organization's IT policy allow me to use Claude or ChatGPT?**

Many organizations have guidelines about what information can be pasted into external AI tools. If you're unsure, check with IT before pasting anything confidential. A good rule: treat Claude like a public email. Don't paste internal financial data, personal information about employees, or anything marked confidential. For most communications drafting and research synthesis, this isn't a barrier.

**What if the draft doesn't sound like my executive?**

That's the editing step. The draft is a starting point, not a finished product. Over time, your context block gets better and your drafts need less editing. [The first week usually feels awkward for everyone](/learn/ai-beginner-mistakes), not just EAs. Stick with it.

**Do I need the paid version of Claude or ChatGPT?**

The free versions are a fine starting point. If you're using it daily and running into limits or slow responses, the $20/month paid tier is worth it. Think of it as the cost of a few hours of your time per month, which it will save you in the first week.

**Can Claude access my calendar or email directly?**

Not without additional integrations. The core workflow is copy, paste, draft, copy back. That sounds like friction, but most EAs find the drafting speed more than makes up for it.

## Working on this alongside people who get it

The EAs and Chiefs of Staff who are getting the most from these tools aren't figuring it out in isolation. They're comparing notes with peers, sharing the prompts that work, and adapting what others have discovered to their own situations.

That's exactly what happens in [MVP Club's weekly sessions](/community). If you want a community of professionals who are actively practicing this, not just reading about it, that's where people like you are doing it in real time.

Your job is already about managing complexity on behalf of someone else. You context-switch faster than anyone in the building, you draft communications that sound like another person, and you do research that makes your executive look prepared for rooms they barely have time to show up to. That’s not a small thing. That’s a set of skills that maps almost perfectly onto how AI tools work best.

Executive assistants who are picking up Claude and ChatGPT right now aren’t replacing their judgment. They’re giving themselves a capable collaborator for the parts of the job that eat time without requiring the relationship intelligence only they have.

Here’s where the biggest gains are, and how to get started.

The three areas where AI for executive assistants pays off fastest

Not every task is worth rerouting through an AI tool. But three areas of the EA role are particularly well-suited to working with Claude or ChatGPT: drafting communications, preparing for meetings and research, and handling the information synthesis that used to mean reading everything yourself.

1. Communications: drafting in someone else’s voice

Writing emails, memos, and messages that sound like your executive, not you, is one of the hardest parts of the job. You’ve spent months learning how they phrase things, what they care about, what they don’t say directly but expect people to understand. That institutional knowledge is yours. AI can’t replace it. But it can take that knowledge and use it to produce faster first drafts.

The key is giving Claude or ChatGPT enough context upfront. Not “write an email declining this meeting” but something more like this:

“Draft an email from Sarah (VP of Ops, direct but warm, hates long emails) to her direct report declining a project proposal. The proposal is well-researched but the timing doesn’t work with Q3 priorities. Sarah wants to encourage the person, leave the door open for Q4, and not get into specifics about why. Keep it under 100 words.”

That level of context takes 45 seconds to write and produces a first draft that needs minor adjustments rather than a full rewrite. If you do this once a day for a week, the time savings compound.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An EA or Chief of Staff describing how they feed their executive’s communication style into Claude to get usable first drafts]

2. Meeting prep and research: brief faster, better

An hour of research used to mean reading five articles and hoping you caught the important parts. With Claude, you can paste in a document, a news article, a company earnings call transcript, or a collection of meeting notes and ask specific questions about it.

The move isn’t “summarize this for me.” The move is: “My executive is meeting with the CFO of [company] tomorrow to discuss a potential partnership. Read this and tell me the three things she should know before walking in, the one number she should have memorized, and the most likely question she’ll be asked that she won’t expect.”

That specificity is what separates useful AI output from generic output. What AI can and can’t do matters here: Claude is good at pulling out what you tell it to look for. It’s not good at knowing what matters to your executive unless you tell it.

Matt Hastings, co-founder of MVP Club, described his own version of this in a recent community session: “My current workflow checks our meeting transcripts, goes and opens our transcript website, navigates to the transcripts from the past week, reads through them, and gives me a summary. The web page navigation, scheduled automation, task workflow automation is very useful for getting information that’s otherwise in a different context. You just say, go find it.”

That’s the EA mindset applied to AI: you’re not doing less work, you’re redirecting your effort toward the parts that require your judgment.

3. Scheduling logistics: reducing the back-and-forth

Scheduling coordination isn’t intellectually demanding, but it is cognitively taxing because of the constant context-switching it requires. AI tools can help here in two ways.

First, drafting scheduling emails. Most scheduling emails follow predictable patterns: proposing times, confirming details, sending reminders, following up on no-responses. Claude can draft these in seconds if you describe the situation. You edit for accuracy and send. The drafting is the part that eats attention.

Second, building reusable templates. If your executive has a recurring set of meeting types (board prep, 1:1s, client dinners, media calls), you can build a set of prompts in Claude that handle the standard scheduling communications for each. Set it up once. Reuse it every week.

Angela Peat, an MVP Club community member, put it well in a community session: “If you had transcripts of your meetings and they were a cadence meeting you had on the regular, you could have AI send everyone an email with the updates from the last meeting. Or some really nice ways of automating keeping people’s context put together when people jump in and out.” Jill Ozovek, MVP Club co-founder, added: “You could have like a recap with who said they were doing what, and schedule it so 48 hours before the next meeting, it resends a shorter recap with who said they’d do what. How many times have we all been to a meeting when someone’s like, oh, I didn’t get to do that.”

A detailed walkthrough: drafting communications in Claude

This is the quickest win for most EAs, so here’s exactly how to do it.

Step 1: Build a context block for your executive.

Before your first session with Claude or ChatGPT, spend 15 minutes writing a brief description of your executive: their communication style, their typical concerns, the audiences they write to most often, phrases they use and phrases they’d never use, and the general tone of their external vs. internal communications.

Save this as a document. You’ll paste it into the beginning of any new Claude chat where you’re drafting on their behalf.

Step 2: Give Claude the situation, not just the task.

Instead of “write an email declining this meeting,” give Claude the situation:

  • Who is this person to your executive?
  • What is the subtext of the situation?
  • What outcome does your executive actually want from this email?
  • What should the tone be?
  • Are there things that should NOT be said?

The more situation, the better the draft.

Step 3: Edit for voice, not content.

The first Claude draft will probably get the content right and the voice partially right. Your job is to edit for the places where it doesn’t sound like your executive. Make a note of the phrases you changed. Over time, you can add those corrections back into your context block so future drafts need less editing.

Step 4: Build your prompt library.

After a month of doing this, you’ll have a set of situation types you handle regularly. Write a standardized prompt for each one. Keep them somewhere you can grab them quickly. This is the stage where the time savings become significant.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after showing a generic EA email prompt vs. a context-rich prompt and the resulting difference in output quality]

Tool recommendations

For most EAs, Claude (claude.ai) is the better starting point for communication drafting because its outputs tend to read more naturally and it handles longer context well. The $20/month Claude Pro subscription is worth it if you’re using it daily: faster responses, longer conversations, and access to the best available model.

ChatGPT (chat.openai.com) is a solid alternative and slightly more familiar to most people. For research synthesis, both tools perform well. Try both for a week with the same types of tasks and see which output requires less editing for your specific use cases.

Neither tool connects directly to your calendar or email unless you’re using a tool like Microsoft Copilot (if your organization has it) or a third-party integration. For most EAs, the workflow is: complete a task or get information, open Claude, draft or synthesize, copy to your actual tool. It’s a two-window workflow, not a one-click solution. That’s worth knowing upfront.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An EA who switched from ChatGPT to Claude (or vice versa) for communications and what they noticed]

How AI positions you as an EA

There’s a career angle here worth naming directly. Executive assistants who are using AI tools effectively right now are doing something that gets noticed.

The pattern Matt Hastings described at dinner with two senior colleagues captures it: one was a Chief of Staff “still very much at the beginning of her journey,” and the other was a high-level ops person who was nearly keeping up with the engineers. The difference wasn’t technical background. It was attitude: “She’s non-technical, but she’s just like, I’ll learn this. I have to.”

EAs who develop fluency with AI tools have a natural pathway to expanding their scope: AI project coordination, internal tool adoption, workflow design. These are roles that didn’t exist two years ago and that organizations are actively trying to fill with people who already understand how they work.

That’s not pressure to become something you’re not. It’s context for why the time you put into learning these tools compounds in your favor. The career case for building AI into your daily practice is straightforward: you’re developing a skill that most people at your level are not yet developing.

Your first week: a 5-day micro-practice schedule

You don’t need to overhaul how you work. You need five small experiments.

Monday: Draft one email in Claude that you’d normally write yourself. Give it real context (situation, audience, tone, constraints). Compare the draft to what you would have written. Note what you changed.

Tuesday: Take meeting notes or a document you need to read and paste it into Claude. Ask three specific questions about it instead of reading the whole thing. See if you got what you needed in less time.

Wednesday: Write a 10-sentence description of your executive’s communication style. Save it. Use it as the opening context block for a drafting session.

Thursday: Build one reusable prompt for a scheduling email type you send regularly. Test it. Refine it.

Friday: Reflect: which task felt most useful? Which felt awkward? That tells you where to focus next week.

The goal in week one isn’t to be efficient. It’s to find the one or two use cases where you naturally reach for Claude instead of having to remind yourself to try it. Those are the ones that will stick.

Frequently asked questions

Will my organization’s IT policy allow me to use Claude or ChatGPT?

Many organizations have guidelines about what information can be pasted into external AI tools. If you’re unsure, check with IT before pasting anything confidential. A good rule: treat Claude like a public email. Don’t paste internal financial data, personal information about employees, or anything marked confidential. For most communications drafting and research synthesis, this isn’t a barrier.

What if the draft doesn’t sound like my executive?

That’s the editing step. The draft is a starting point, not a finished product. Over time, your context block gets better and your drafts need less editing. The first week usually feels awkward for everyone, not just EAs. Stick with it.

Do I need the paid version of Claude or ChatGPT?

The free versions are a fine starting point. If you’re using it daily and running into limits or slow responses, the $20/month paid tier is worth it. Think of it as the cost of a few hours of your time per month, which it will save you in the first week.

Can Claude access my calendar or email directly?

Not without additional integrations. The core workflow is copy, paste, draft, copy back. That sounds like friction, but most EAs find the drafting speed more than makes up for it.

Working on this alongside people who get it

The EAs and Chiefs of Staff who are getting the most from these tools aren’t figuring it out in isolation. They’re comparing notes with peers, sharing the prompts that work, and adapting what others have discovered to their own situations.

That’s exactly what happens in MVP Club’s weekly sessions. If you want a community of professionals who are actively practicing this, not just reading about it, that’s where people like you are doing it in real time.

AI for Email Writing: Drafting, Summarizing, and Managing Your Inbox
How to use AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT for email writing, inbox summarizing, and triage. Step-by-step workflows for busy professionals.

Open your inbox right now and count how many emails need a real response. Not a quick "thanks," but a message that requires you to think, draft, and revise. For most professionals, that list is longer than they want it to be, and it refills faster than they can clear it.

AI for email writing is one of the highest-return places to start. Not because it takes over your inbox, but because it removes the friction from the two hardest parts: starting a message from scratch and processing a pile of threads you haven't touched in three days.

This guide walks through exactly how to use Claude and ChatGPT for email, with specific prompts you can copy, common mistakes to skip, and a clear picture of where AI helps versus where your own judgment still drives the work.

## Why email is the ideal first AI habit

If you've been meaning to build AI into your daily work but haven't found a foothold, email is the right place to start. You already do it every day. The feedback loop is fast. And the stakes on most emails are low enough that a mediocre first draft is still useful.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing the moment AI email drafting clicked for them, with a specific time savings or workflow detail]

One pattern we see consistently in our coaching practice: people who start with email drafting build the skill of giving AI good context much faster than people who start with more complex tasks. Writing a good email prompt teaches you the same thing as writing a good prompt for anything else. You learn to describe your audience, your goal, and your constraints. That skill transfers everywhere.

## Step 1: Draft emails with context, not just a request

The most common AI email mistake is this prompt: "Write me an email to my boss about the project delay."

Claude and ChatGPT are capable enough to produce something from that. But it will be generic, formal, and probably wrong for your specific relationship and situation. The output improves dramatically when you give the model something to work with.

Here's a prompt structure that works:

```
I need to write an email to [recipient and relationship]. The situation is: [2-3 sentences of context]. My goal with this email is [what you want to happen]. The tone should be [professional/direct/warm/etc.]. Draft the email for me, then tell me if there's anything important I might be missing.
```

A real example: "I need to write an email to my project sponsor, who I talk to about once a month. The situation is: we're behind on the Q2 deliverable by about two weeks because a key vendor pushed their timeline. My goal is to flag this proactively before she hears it elsewhere, and propose a revised schedule. The tone should be direct and solutions-focused, not apologetic. Draft the email, then tell me if there's anything I might be missing."

That prompt gives Claude a recipient relationship, a specific situation, a goal, and a tone. The result will be significantly closer to what you'd actually send.

Your job is still to edit. AI is a starting point, not a finisher. Read what it gives you, shape it until it sounds like you, and send the version you'd be proud of.

## Step 2: Summarize long threads before you reply

Here's a use case that saves time before you even write a word: paste a long email thread into Claude and ask it to tell you what's actually going on.

Most email threads are inefficient. People reply to the wrong part of a message, loop in people late, or let a three-sentence question turn into a twelve-email chain. By the time you join or return to a thread, you're re-reading context you shouldn't have to.

Try this prompt:

```
Here is an email thread. Please tell me: (1) what the core question or decision is, (2) where it currently stands, (3) what action, if any, is being asked of me. Keep it to 3-5 sentences.

[paste the thread]
```

You can also ask Claude to pull out specific information: "What did Sarah agree to in this thread?" or "Is there anything unresolved that I need to address?" This is where AI for email writing crosses into something more powerful: using AI as a reading assistant, not just a writing assistant.

According to [Microsoft's New Future of Work Report 2025](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/new-future-of-work-report-2025/), Copilot usage in Microsoft 365 is showing consistent time savings on communication tasks, with one enterprise deploying it seeing a 10% reduction in email time. The gains are real, but they depend on knowing how to prompt. Generic summarization requests give generic summaries. Specific questions give useful answers.

## Step 3: Triage your inbox with a clear system

AI for inbox management works differently than drafting or summarizing. You can't paste 200 emails into a chat window. But you can build a simple triage system that makes the decisions faster.

The most useful thing AI does here is help you clarify your own criteria. Most professionals have a vague sense of what email deserves immediate attention, but they never write it down. That means every email becomes a small decision under pressure.

Try this exercise once, outside your inbox:

```
I manage email for [your role]. Help me write a simple triage rule for my inbox. The types of email I get are: [list 4-5 types]. The things that are actually urgent for me are: [list]. What I can batch and respond to at the end of the day: [list]. What I can safely delay or ignore: [list]. Help me turn this into a short decision framework I can keep open next to my inbox.
```

You write this once. Then you use the output as a reference every morning. It sounds simple, and it is. But having your own criteria written down is different from having them in your head. The process of writing the prompt forces clarity you didn't know you were missing.

For built-in AI inbox tools: if you're using Gmail, Google's Gemini features include summary cards that automatically surface the key point of long threads. In Outlook, Microsoft Copilot can summarize thread context before you reply. These work best as starting points for your own reading, not as replacements for it.

## Step 4: Write templates for the emails you send every week

If you find yourself writing variations of the same email repeatedly, that's a clear signal to build a reusable template with Claude.

Some examples: weekly status updates to your manager, meeting follow-up notes, project kickoff messages, request-for-information emails to vendors, check-ins with your team.

The prompt:

```
I write this type of email about once a week: [describe the type]. The audience is usually [recipient type]. The goal is always [purpose]. Can you write a template with [X] blanks I can fill in each time? Make it sound like a real person wrote it, not a form letter.
```

Once you have a template you like, keep it somewhere accessible. Google Docs, a Notion page, a text file. You'll be surprised how fast you can process recurring email when you're starting from a solid template instead of a blank page.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific member who built a weekly status update template with Claude and the time it saved them each week]

## Common mistakes with AI for email

**Sending the first draft.** AI generates. You edit. Every time. The first draft from Claude or ChatGPT is a starting point. Reading it back will almost always reveal something that sounds off, too formal, too long, or missing a nuance only you know. That editing is not extra work. It's the work.

**Asking for tone without showing an example.** "Make this more casual" is hard for AI to act on without knowing what casual means to you. If you have a past email you think struck the right tone, paste it in and say: "Write the new email in a similar style to this example." You'll get much closer results.

**Using AI for high-stakes emails without review.** For an email to your skip-level manager, a difficult conversation with a report, or anything that could affect a relationship, don't just clean up the AI draft. Write the core yourself, then use AI to review and sharpen it. The judgment layer on important communication should stay with you.

**Expecting AI to know your relationships.** Claude and ChatGPT have no idea who your boss is, what your history with a vendor looks like, or what language your team uses internally. The more relationship context you give, the better. Think of it as briefing a capable temp on your team dynamics.

## From the practice: what shifts when email stops being friction

One thing that comes up regularly in our community sessions is how much cognitive energy professionals spend on email they haven't started yet. The draft sitting in your head while you're in a meeting, the reply you're mentally composing while you're trying to focus on something else.

Using AI for email writing doesn't just save time on the email itself. It reduces the mental load of having emails pending. When you know you can sit down, give Claude a paragraph of context, and have a solid draft in 90 seconds, you stop carrying those drafts around in the background.

That shift, from email as something you brace for to email as something you process efficiently, is what professionals who've built this habit describe most often. It's not about the words on the screen. It's about getting the drafts out of your head.

See also the section on [drafting work in our How to Use AI at Work guide](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work), which covers this as one of four categories of AI-friendly tasks across your full workday.

## Where to start today

Pick one email you need to write in the next hour. Something real, not a test. Give Claude the full context: who you're writing to, what the situation is, what you want to happen, what tone fits. Read what comes back. Edit until it sounds like you. Send it.

That's it. One email. The point is not to get it perfect. The point is to build the habit of giving AI enough context to actually help.

If you want to practice these workflows with other professionals and see how they're building AI into their daily routines, that's exactly what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions. Come see what people are building at [mvpclub.ai/community](/community).

Sending the first draft. AI generates. You edit. Every time. The first draft from Claude or ChatGPT is a starting point. Reading it back will almost always reveal something that sounds off, too formal, too long, or missing a nuance only you know. That editing is not extra work. It’s the work.

Asking for tone without showing an example. “Make this more casual” is hard for AI to act on without knowing what casual means to you. If you have a past email you think struck the right tone, paste it in and say: “Write the new email in a similar style to this example.” You’ll get much closer results.

Using AI for high-stakes emails without review. For an email to your skip-level manager, a difficult conversation with a report, or anything that could affect a relationship, don’t just clean up the AI draft. Write the core yourself, then use AI to review and sharpen it. The judgment layer on important communication should stay with you.

Expecting AI to know your relationships. Claude and ChatGPT have no idea who your boss is, what your history with a vendor looks like, or what language your team uses internally. The more relationship context you give, the better. Think of it as briefing a capable temp on your team dynamics.

From the practice: what shifts when email stops being friction

One thing that comes up regularly in our community sessions is how much cognitive energy professionals spend on email they haven’t started yet. The draft sitting in your head while you’re in a meeting, the reply you’re mentally composing while you’re trying to focus on something else.

Using AI for email writing doesn’t just save time on the email itself. It reduces the mental load of having emails pending. When you know you can sit down, give Claude a paragraph of context, and have a solid draft in 90 seconds, you stop carrying those drafts around in the background.

That shift, from email as something you brace for to email as something you process efficiently, is what professionals who’ve built this habit describe most often. It’s not about the words on the screen. It’s about getting the drafts out of your head.

See also the section on drafting work in our How to Use AI at Work guide, which covers this as one of four categories of AI-friendly tasks across your full workday.

Where to start today

Pick one email you need to write in the next hour. Something real, not a test. Give Claude the full context: who you’re writing to, what the situation is, what you want to happen, what tone fits. Read what comes back. Edit until it sounds like you. Send it.

That’s it. One email. The point is not to get it perfect. The point is to build the habit of giving AI enough context to actually help.

AI for Finance Professionals: Analysis, Forecasting, and Reporting
How finance teams are using Claude and ChatGPT to speed up analysis, sharpen forecasts, and get reporting done without adding headcount.

Your variance report is due in two hours, your FP&A model needs three more scenarios, and someone just emailed asking for a board-ready summary of last quarter. Finance professionals don't have a bandwidth problem. They have a time problem. And AI is one of the few tools that actually addresses both.

Finance is one of the roles where AI for finance teams has the clearest, most immediate ROI. The work is structured, the outputs are predictable, and the gap between "raw data" and "decision-ready insight" is exactly where Claude and ChatGPT do their best work.

This guide covers the three highest-impact ways finance professionals are using AI right now, with specific walkthroughs, tool recommendations, and a first-week plan you can start tomorrow.

## Why Finance Is One of the Best Fits for AI

Most roles have a mix of structured and unstructured work. Finance skews heavily structured: data goes in, analysis comes out, the formats are known, the audiences are consistent, and the stakes are clear. That structure is exactly what makes AI unusually effective here.

You're not asking AI to invent something. You're asking it to move faster through work you already know how to do. That's the sweet spot.

The three use cases below are ranked by how quickly you'll see results, not by how impressive they sound.

## The Three Highest-Impact Use Cases

### Use Case 1: Drafting Financial Narratives and Reports

The numbers in your variance report are the easy part. Writing the narrative that explains what happened, why it matters, and what leadership should do about it, that's where most finance professionals spend way too much time.

Claude is particularly good at this task because it handles structured input well. You give it the data, the context, and the audience. It produces a clear first draft. You edit for accuracy and voice. Total time savings: often 40-60% on narrative-heavy deliverables.

### Use Case 2: Scenario Analysis and Forecast Sensitivity

Running multiple scenarios manually is tedious. Rebuilding a model three times for different assumptions, then writing up what each scenario implies, eats hours. AI can't run your Excel model, but it can help you think through scenarios, draft the narrative for each, and surface assumptions you might have missed.

The combination looks like this: you run the numbers in your model, paste the outputs into Claude with context about your business, and ask it to help you articulate what each scenario means for the business and what it implies for decisions. The thinking gets faster.

### Use Case 3: Synthesizing Data for Executive Communication

Getting from a 40-row data table to a three-sentence exec summary is a skill. AI accelerates that skill considerably. You paste in the data and ask Claude to pull out the three most decision-relevant insights for a specific audience (your CFO, your board, your operating committee). You review and edit. The synthesis that used to take 30 minutes takes 5.

## Detailed Walkthrough: Drafting Financial Narratives with Claude

This is the use case with the fastest payoff, so we'll walk through it step by step.

### Step 1: Set up the context prompt

Before you paste any data, tell Claude who you are and what you need. The more context you provide upfront, the better the first draft.

A good setup prompt looks like this:

> "I'm a finance manager at a mid-size SaaS company. I need to write the narrative section of our monthly business review for our CFO and VP of Finance. The audience is financially sophisticated but busy. They want to understand what happened, why, and what we're doing about it. I'll give you the key numbers now."

### Step 2: Paste the key data points

You don't need to paste your entire P&L. Give Claude the numbers that tell the story: actuals vs. budget vs. prior period for the metrics that matter. Add any context you know that the numbers don't show (a one-time event, a timing shift, a new contract that hit late).

> "Revenue: $4.2M actual vs. $4.5M budget (6% unfavorable). The miss was primarily in SMB segment, which came in at $1.1M vs. $1.4M budget. Enterprise was favorable at $3.1M vs. $3.1M budget. The SMB shortfall is partly a timing issue, three deals that were expected to close in Q1 have now been confirmed for Q2. Gross margin was 72% vs. 71% budget, favorable due to reduced hosting costs."

### Step 3: Ask for the narrative draft

> "Write the variance narrative for this monthly review. Two to three paragraphs. Start with the headline, then the driver of the miss, then the one favorable item, then the forward-looking note on the Q2 pipeline. Keep it at an executive summary level, direct and factual."

### Step 4: Edit for accuracy and voice

The draft Claude returns will be close. It won't sound exactly like you, and it won't have the institutional knowledge that you carry in your head. That's expected. Your job at this stage is to review for accuracy, add any nuance the AI couldn't know, and adjust the voice until it reads like something you'd actually send.

This editing step is not optional, and it's not a sign that AI "didn't work." Editing a draft is always faster than creating from scratch.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A finance professional describing the before/after on a specific deliverable, with time saved]

### Common mistakes in this workflow

**Giving too little context.** "Write me a variance report" gives Claude nothing to work with. It needs to know your audience, your format, your business context, and the key numbers. The more specific you are, the less editing you do.

**Accepting the first output without checking the numbers.** Claude doesn't have access to your data systems. If you paste numbers in, it works with those. If your numbers were wrong or incomplete, the narrative will reflect that. Always verify facts before sending anything.

**Using it only for the first draft.** The real leverage comes from the back-and-forth. After the first draft, ask Claude to make the tone more direct, to cut it by 30%, to add a stronger opening line, or to reframe the Q2 outlook more optimistically. The second and third drafts are often where it really earns its place.

## Tool Recommendations

For finance narrative and synthesis work, Claude (at the Pro tier, $20/month) is the stronger choice over ChatGPT. The reason is practical: Claude tends to handle longer documents and more complex prompts without losing track of the context. When you paste a full operating report and ask for targeted analysis, Claude maintains coherence across longer conversations better than ChatGPT in our experience.

For data analysis that involves actual computation, ChatGPT's Code Interpreter (part of ChatGPT Plus, also $20/month) is worth knowing about. You can upload a CSV and ask it to run calculations, generate charts, or flag anomalies. It's not a replacement for your FP&A model, but it's useful for quick exploratory analysis before you build anything formal.

The honest summary: for writing, synthesis, and narrative, use Claude. For exploring raw data files when you need actual math done, ChatGPT's data analysis tools are worth trying.

Neither tool should touch your production financial systems. Both are most valuable as a drafting and thinking layer on top of the work you're already doing.

## What Finance Professionals in Our Community Are Saying

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member in a finance or FP&A role sharing what surprised them about using Claude or ChatGPT for their actual work]

## The Career Angle: Why This Matters Beyond Efficiency

Finance professionals who can move from data to narrative faster and run more scenario analysis in less time have a measurable advantage. Not because AI makes them smarter, but because it removes the friction between having insight and communicating it.

The professionals who are going to stand out in finance over the next few years aren't the ones who mastered a new certification. They're the ones who figured out how to do the analytical work that used to take three people in a fraction of the time, and then put that time back into higher-value thinking.

There's also a leadership angle. If you're working with AI tools before your peers are, you become the person your team asks about it. That's not a small thing. Being the finance person who can answer "what should we actually do with AI?" is a real career asset right now. If you want to read more about that positioning, this piece on [becoming the AI leader on your team](/learn/explain-ai-to-your-team) is worth your time.

The starting point isn't complicated. It's [learning what AI can genuinely do](/learn/what-ai-can-and-cant-do) for your specific work, not AI in general, and then building from there.

## Your First Week: A 5-Day Micro-Practice Plan

You don't need to overhaul your workflow this week. You need five small experiments that build enough familiarity that the tool becomes natural.

**Day 1 (Monday): Narrative from scratch**
Find the last variance commentary you wrote. Open Claude. Paste in the key data points and ask it to write a version with a different tone, more direct, or aimed at a less financially sophisticated audience. Compare your version to what it produces. Notice what it does well and where it misses.

**Day 2 (Tuesday): Exec summary compression**
Take a report you received or wrote this month. Paste it into Claude. Ask: "Summarize this in three sentences for a CFO who has 60 seconds. What are the three most important points?" See how close it gets to what you'd say. Edit it until you'd actually send it.

**Day 3 (Wednesday): Scenario narrative**
Pick a forecast scenario you're already running. Don't paste your model, just the key outputs. Ask Claude to write the narrative implications: what this scenario means for the business, what it implies for decisions, and what risks it surfaces. Use it as a first draft for your next scenario analysis memo.

**Day 4 (Thursday): Draft an email you've been putting off**
There's always an email in finance that requires careful wording: a budget variance explanation to a frustrated department head, a response to a question from the CFO, a memo about a process change. Open Claude, give it context about the relationship and the situation, and ask for a draft. Edit it until it sounds like you.

**Day 5 (Friday): Reflect on what worked**
Open Claude or ChatGPT and tell it what you tried this week. What saved time? What didn't land? What would you want to try next week? This reflection step isn't busywork. It helps you identify the two or three use cases worth building into your regular workflow. The [how to use AI at work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) guide has a good framework for thinking about what types of work AI fits best.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Can I paste actual financial data into Claude or ChatGPT?

Check your company's AI policy before pasting any data. Many organizations have guidelines about what can be shared with external AI tools. When in doubt, anonymize the data or use illustrative numbers that match the real structure without exposing actual figures. You can get substantial value from AI analysis using sanitized or representative data.

### Will AI make mistakes with my numbers?

Yes, sometimes. Claude and ChatGPT can misread, misinterpret, or hallucinate numbers if your input is ambiguous. The rule is: always verify any numerical claims in AI-generated output against your source data before using them. Treat AI output as a starting draft, not a final answer.

### Do I need to learn anything technical to use these tools?

No. Claude and ChatGPT are chat interfaces. If you can write an email, you can use these tools. The skill is in how you describe your task and give context, not in any technical setup. The [AI in 10 minutes guide](/learn/ai-in-10-minutes) covers everything you need to know to get started.

### What if my firm doesn't allow AI tools?

This is increasingly common, and the answer is: respect the policy and ask questions about what's coming. Many firms are actively building out AI policies and approved tooling. Staying informed about what's available and raising your hand as someone who wants to be involved in the rollout is the smart move. The professionals who understand the tools are the ones who get asked to help figure out the policy.

## Where Finance Professionals Go to Figure This Out

The finance professionals who are making the most progress with AI right now aren't the ones who found a perfect tutorial. They're the ones who have somewhere to practice, ask questions, and share what's working.

If you want that community, [MVP Club](/community) is where finance professionals alongside people in operations, HR, marketing, and leadership are putting these tools into practice every week. Come see what others are building. You don't have to have it figured out to show up.
