How Learning Designers Are Actually Using AI Right Now
How learning designers are using Claude and ChatGPT today, how to actually start, and what to watch out for.

Picture this - or maybe you don't have to since you're living it on the regular:

- You have 12 requests for new training in your backlog.
- You have one direct report
- a shared contractor you can book four hours a month
- and a budget that got cut in January.
- The SME you need is available for a thirty-minute call next Thursday.
- The launch date is in three weeks.

Sound familiar?

This is the reality for the learning design world for the better part of a decade. (I have existential thoughts on this that I'll save for another time.) The gap between what organizations need and what learning teams can produce has only gotten wider. I've experienced this firsthand throughout my career, and it has led to burnout, ER visits (not mine!) and total and utter job dissatisfaction. I don't think leveraging AI will close these very real gaps and shortcomings totally, but it can make a meaningful dent in them, while giving you back some of your time and your life.

This guide covers how learning designers are using Claude and ChatGPT today, how to actually start, and what to watch out for.

## Start Here: AI-Assisted Synthesis

If you're new to using AI for learning design work, start with getting to a better starting point for your design and development through AI-assisted synthesis.

Synthesizing SME interview notes, survey responses, and performance data into a clear problem statement and recommendation is time-intensive and doesn't require the creative judgment that content design does. Let AI take some of the load off.

Here's the workflow.

**Step 1: Gather your raw inputs.** Notes from three SME interviews, a summary of a performance management report, a handful of learner feedback comments from a previous program. Doesn't need to be perfect.

**Step 2: Write a context prompt.** Tell Claude what you're trying to produce: "I'm a learning design professional at a 2,000-person healthcare company. I've conducted three SME interviews and reviewed six months of performance data. I'm trying to write a 1-page needs analysis document that my VP can read in five minutes. It should include: the performance gap, the root cause, and a recommended intervention. Here are my raw notes: [paste notes]."

**Step 3: Read the output critically.** Claude will produce a clean, readable synthesis. Your job is to check it. Did it get the root cause right? Did it overemphasize something that isn't actually the central issue? Did it include anything you can't verify? Edit accordingly.

**Step 4: Use the document in your next stakeholder conversation.** Walk in with a one-page that took you ninety minutes instead of half a day.

This approach works for program evaluation summaries, post-training survey analysis, and learning analytics reports. Any task where you're reading a lot of material and producing a shorter, structured synthesis of it. It's low-risk, and it teaches you the most important thing about working with AI: the quality of what you get out is directly proportional to the context you put in.

Once you've got that pattern down, there are ways to supercharge it that I'll talk about in a subsequent post. Things like using projects in Claude, skills (like producing structured reports or design briefs!), and Claude Co-work. But we're learning designers, right? We know how to scaffold learning and make it bite sized, which I'm attempting to do here.

## The Three Highest-Impact Uses of AI for learning design

Not everything in learning design benefits equally from AI. Some tasks are genuinely transformed. Others are incrementally faster but still require the same human judgment. Here's where learning designers in our community are getting the clearest wins.

### 1. First-Draft Content from Structured Input

Content writing is the bottleneck in most learning design workflows. You've gathered all the assets, you've gotten the subject matter expertise, the learning objectives, all of it.  You have the high level design down and the structure of a unit or module. The actual drafting of exercises, facilitator guides, learner workbooks, scenario scripts, knowledge check questions, and job aids eats the time you'd rather spend on design and strategy.

Claude and ChatGPT are genuinely good at producing first drafts from structured input. The key word is "first." You're not asking the tool to replace your judgment. You're asking it to eliminate the blank page and give you something to react to.

The simplest version: you gather your SME notes and learning objectives, paste them into Claude with a prompt specifying your audience and what you need, and get back a working draft in five minutes instead of two hours. Your job is then to edit for accuracy, tone, and alignment to your organization's standards. That editing process goes faster than starting from zero.

But the real leverage comes when you go further. Give the AI a style guide, a source document, your learning objectives, and a clear description of who the reader is and what they should be able to do when they're done. What comes back isn't a generic first draft. It's a structured draft that already reflects your constraints, and your editing pass becomes substantive (fixing logic, tightening voice, catching what doesn't land) rather than generative (staring at a blank page).

I've been doing this in my own work at an AI startup, developing a companion workbook to a published business book. I feed Claude the author's manuscript, a detailed voice style guide, a product requirements document, and specific learning objectives for each chapter. What I get back is first a chapter brief that I use to have a deep design convo, and then a full chapter draft that holds the author's voice, follows the structural patterns I've established, and targets the right audience. I still edit every word. But the difference between editing a draft that's already 70% there and writing one from scratch is the difference between a half-day and a full week.

### 2. Scenario and Case Study Writing

Scenario-based learning is widely understood to be more effective than content dumps. It's also time-consuming to write well. Good scenarios require realistic characters, plausible stakes, branching logic, and enough specificity to feel real without being so specific they alienate half the learner population.

AI is useful here in a specific way: generating scenario variations. Once you've written one strong scenario, you can ask Claude or ChatGPT to generate three variations on the same learning objective, changing the context, the characters, or the stakes. This gives you the range you'd normally spend a full writing sprint producing.

The prompt structure matters. "Write me a scenario about workplace conflict" produces generic results. "Write me a branching scenario where a team lead named Dana discovers that two direct reports have been giving each other negative informal feedback in front of customers. Dana has to decide whether to address it in the moment, wait for a formal 1:1, or escalate to HR. Write three decision points, each with two choices and a consequence. The learner should make mistakes and recover from them." That level of specificity produces something you can actually use.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An learning design professional describing how they use AI to generate scenario variations, particularly how they handle the editing pass to make characters feel more real or culturally specific]

### 3. Teaching AI How You Work (Not Just What You Need)

Here's the leap most learning designers haven't made yet.

Matt, one of my co-founders with a strong education and learning design background puts it this way: "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude how you want it done. The next time, you can just invoke that approach and say, here's the input, go do it. It turns a generically useful tool into a very specifically useful tool." (Note: This is a Skill in Claude)

Start by writing down your process for one recurring task. How you structure a facilitator guide. How you write debrief questions. How you sequence a half-day workshop. Then paste that process into Claude alongside your next real request. What comes back will be closer to how you'd actually do it, not just how "any learning design professional" would do it.

This compounds. The more of your thinking you externalize and feed into the tool, the less time you spend coaching it per request. You're essentially building a co-designer that gets better the more you invest in it.

## The Skill That Actually Matters: The Iterative Loop

There's something missing from most "how to use AI" guides, and it's the thing that actually determines whether AI saves you time or wastes it.

The real workflow isn't: write a prompt, get a draft, done. It's: write a prompt, get a draft, tell the AI what's not working, get a revised draft, push on a specific section, get it tighter, repeat. The back-and-forth is the skill.

If I had a nickel for every time I got a brain dump from an SME that I had to shape into something usable through seemingly endless rounds of "is this what you mean?' ….

If that resonates, then you already know how to do this. You're not starting from zero. You're applying a skill you've been building your entire career, just with a different collaborator.

## Your First Week: A 5-Day Micro-Practice Plan for Learning Design

Start with real work, not experiments. The fastest way to build capability is to apply AI to something you actually need to produce. Here's an idea on a 5 day micro-practice plan I put together to get you spit balling.

**Monday: Reverse-engineer your own work.** Take a piece of content you've already produced, a facilitator guide, an assessment, a job aid. Paste it into Claude and ask: "Here's a facilitator guide I wrote. What learning objectives does it appear to be targeting? What assumptions is it making about the audience?" Read what comes back. This teaches you how the tool sees your work, which teaches you how to direct it better when you're asking it to produce something new.

**Tuesday: Knowledge check draft.** Pick any topic you've been asked to address recently. Write your learning objectives (even rough ones). Then prompt Claude: "Based on these learning objectives, write 5 multiple-choice questions with one correct answer and three plausible distractors each. Avoid trivially easy distractors. Here are my objectives: [paste]." Edit the questions against your content.

**Wednesday: Scenario first draft.** Think of a performance problem you're currently designing for. Describe the situation, the characters, and what decision you want the learner to practice. Give that to Claude and ask for a scenario with two decision points and two choices each. See how close it gets without coaching from you on the first pass.

**Thursday: Facilitator debrief questions.** Take an activity from an existing workshop you've designed or delivered. Paste a description of the activity into Claude and ask: "Write five debrief questions for this activity that move learners from the experience to the principle to application in their own context." Edit for the language and tone of your organization.

**Friday: Reflect.** Which of the four tasks felt most useful? Where did Claude give you something you could work with quickly? Where did you have to heavily edit? That pattern tells you where to focus your practice in week two.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Can AI replace my SME interviews?**

No. AI doesn't know your organization, your learners, or the specific context that makes content accurate and relevant. It can help you prepare for SME interviews (drafting interview questions, synthesizing notes afterward), but it can't replace the source material that comes from talking to real people.

**What if I paste internal documents into Claude or ChatGPT? Is that a security risk?**

This depends on your organization's data policies. Many organizations have guidance on what can and can't be pasted into consumer AI tools. Check with your IT or legal team before pasting anything marked confidential. Claude and ChatGPT both offer enterprise tiers with stronger data privacy commitments if your organization is ready for that conversation.

**Will AI produce content that's accurate for my specific industry?**

Not automatically. And this is the part that matters: the dangerous output isn't the stuff that's obviously wrong. It's the stuff that sounds right but misses a nuance specific to your regulatory environment, your learner population, or your organizational culture. Every piece of AI-generated content needs a human review pass, and the person doing that review needs enough domain knowledge to catch what's plausible but off.

**How do I handle the "AI is going to take my job" anxiety some learning designer colleagues are feeling?**

Acknowledge it rather than dismiss it. The honest answer is that AI is going to change learning design work, not eliminate it. The skills that matter most in this field, understanding how people learn, designing for behavior change, facilitating conversations that shift perspective, are all things AI can't replicate. What AI changes is the production layer: first drafts, variations, synthesis. Your expertise is in the judgment layer above that.

## You Don't Have to Figure This Out Alone

Learning designers are navigating this the same way everyone else is: in real time, with no /little roadmap, while still doing their regular jobs.

The ones figuring it out fastest aren't the ones who took a course on AI or waited until they felt ready. They're the ones practicing regularly and sharing what works with peers who are working through the same questions. That's exactly what happens in MVP Club's community sessions. AI for HR Professionals: Recruiting, Onboarding, and Policy Development
How HR professionals are using AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT for recruiting, onboarding, and policy work. Practical workflows for immediate use.

An HR director in one of our community sessions pulled up Claude on her laptop and, in about four minutes, drafted a job description that would have taken her 90 minutes to write from scratch. She wasn't using a template. She was having a conversation: here's the role, here's the team, here's what good looks like, what questions should I be asking candidates?

That moment is available to you right now. And it's not about replacing your judgment. It's about giving your judgment better material to work with.

This guide covers the three areas where AI for HR professionals delivers the fastest results: recruiting, onboarding, and policy development. For each one, you'll get a concrete workflow and the specific prompts that actually work.

## Why HR Work Is Particularly Well-Suited for AI

HR involves a lot of writing. Job descriptions, offer letters, onboarding documents, policy drafts, performance review templates, communication plans. Most of these follow patterns you've run through dozens of times. You know what good looks like. You can evaluate output quickly.

That's exactly the profile of work where Claude and ChatGPT save the most time. You're not outsourcing the hard thinking. You're offloading the blank-page problem so you can spend your time refining, personalizing, and applying your expertise where it actually matters.

The other factor: HR work requires a lot of context-switching. You're rarely deep in one project for hours at a time. AI gives you a way to move faster across that constant switching without losing quality.

## Three High-Impact Use Cases for AI in HR

Here's where to start, ordered by speed of payoff.

**Recruiting:** Job descriptions, screening questions, interview guides, candidate outreach
**Onboarding:** Welcome packets, role-specific orientation materials, first-30-60-90-day plans
**Policy development:** First drafts, gap analysis, plain-language summaries of complex policies

Start with recruiting. It gives you results you can see in the same sitting.

## Use Case 1: Recruiting (Your Fastest Win)

### Writing Job Descriptions in Minutes, Not Hours

Most job descriptions follow the same arc: role summary, key responsibilities, what you're looking for, why someone should want to work here. You've written this structure many times. AI can draft the skeleton in minutes; your job is to make it real.

Here's the prompt pattern that works:

> "I'm writing a job description for a [job title] on our [team name] team. This person will [primary responsibility]. They'll work closely with [stakeholders]. Our company [brief company description]. What I care most about in this role is [top 2-3 must-haves]. Write a draft job description that leads with what makes this role interesting, not just what the person needs to do. Keep it under 400 words."

The key moves here: give it the team context, name what you actually care about, and ask it to lead with what makes the role compelling. Generic job descriptions read like they were written by committee. Specific ones attract better candidates.

After you get the draft, your editing session with Claude or ChatGPT is where the value compounds. Tell it what's off: "The tone is too corporate. We're a fast-moving team. Make it sound like we're looking for a partner, not an employee." Or: "The requirements list is too long. Cut anything that's a nice-to-have." Each round gets faster as the tool learns what you're going for.

### Building Interview Guides

Once you have a job description, your next move is to ask Claude to generate interview questions from it.

> "Based on this job description [paste it], write 8 interview questions that would help me evaluate whether someone can actually do this work, not just talk about it. For each question, include what a strong answer would include and what a weak answer would look like."

This is one of the more immediately useful things AI can do for recruiting. Structured interview guides with evaluation criteria reduce bias and make your hiring decisions more defensible. They also help managers who are less experienced with interviews. And they're time-consuming to write from scratch every time.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An HR professional sharing how they use Claude to build interview guides or screen candidates, with a specific example of a question that worked well]

### Candidate Outreach That Doesn't Sound Like a Template

Outreach messages for passive candidates are notoriously hard to write. Too generic and they get ignored. Too specific and they sound like you've been stalking someone's LinkedIn profile.

Try this:

> "Write a short outreach message (under 100 words) for a [role] candidate. She [brief description of relevant background]. We're reaching out because [specific reason]. The message should feel personal and specific to her background, not like a mass send. Don't open with 'I hope this message finds you well.' Don't mention 'exciting opportunity.'"

The negative constraints matter. Telling Claude what not to include often produces more useful output than describing what to include.

## Use Case 2: Onboarding Materials That Actually Help New Hires

Onboarding is one of those areas where HR teams know the materials could be better and rarely have time to fix them. AI changes that math.

One member of our community described using Claude to build out a complete onboarding package for a new board member in a single afternoon. She gave it the role description, the organization's structure, and a few notes about what this specific person needed to know. Claude drafted a welcome letter, a role summary, a first-week schedule, and a quick-reference guide. She edited and personalized each piece. The whole process took about three hours instead of three days.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific HR professional describing an onboarding workflow they built with AI, including what materials they generated and roughly how much time it saved]

### The Prompt Pattern for Onboarding Documents

The key to getting useful onboarding materials from Claude or ChatGPT is specificity about the audience. "Write an onboarding guide" produces generic output. "Write a 30-day guide for a new mid-level HR business partner joining a 300-person company in the middle of a system migration" produces something much more useful.

Try this approach:

> "Write a first-week schedule and overview document for a new [job title] joining our team. Context: [2-3 sentences about your organization]. This person is coming from [background]. In their first week, they need to [key goals]. Format as a day-by-day guide with the 'why' behind each item, not just a task list."

Then ask follow-up questions:

> "What questions does a new employee in this role typically have that aren't addressed in a standard onboarding document? Write a FAQ section that anticipates those questions."

That FAQ section alone often becomes one of the most-used documents in the onboarding package.

### 30-60-90 Day Plans

These take a long time to write and are often either too vague to be useful or so detailed they become irrelevant two weeks in.

> "Write a 30-60-90 day plan for a new [role]. Day 1-30 should focus on learning and relationship-building. Day 31-60 should be about early contribution. Day 61-90 should be about independent ownership of [key responsibilities]. Each phase should have 3-5 concrete goals, not vague outcomes like 'understand the business.'"

The editing process is where you make it real. Take what Claude gives you and replace the generic goals with the specific outcomes that matter to your team. Ten minutes of editing makes the difference between a document that looks good and one that actually guides behavior.

## Use Case 3: Policy Development Without the Two-Month Slog

Writing new policies or updating old ones is one of the most time-consuming things HR does. It involves drafting, legal review, stakeholder input, plain-language revision, and distribution. AI can significantly compress the first and third steps.

### First Drafts

Before AI, the first draft of a policy usually took days: research comparable policies, identify the key components, write something, send it to legal, get it back red-lined, revise. Claude can generate that first draft in minutes.

> "Draft an AI use policy for a company of about [size] employees in [industry]. The policy should cover: what tools employees are allowed to use, what data they should never input into AI systems, what review is required before sharing AI-generated work externally, and what the consequences are for misuse. Write in plain English. Avoid legal jargon. This is the starting point for review, not the final document."

The output gives you something concrete to edit and send to legal, rather than starting from scratch. Your legal team's time is better spent reviewing and refining than writing from zero.

### Gap Analysis

If you already have policies, Claude can help you identify what's missing.

> "Here are our current HR policies on [topic]. What important scenarios are these policies not addressing? What common questions would employees have that aren't answered here? What have other organizations included in similar policies that we should consider?"

This is using Claude as a thinking partner, not a document generator. It's one of the more valuable ways AI helps with policy work.

### Plain-Language Summaries

The policy goes through legal. It comes back in legalese. Employees don't read it.

> "Here is our [policy name]. Write a plain-language summary that employees will actually read and understand. Use short sentences. Use examples where helpful. The goal is comprehension, not legal precision. Keep it under 300 words."

This has become standard practice for HR teams that work with Claude regularly. The full policy lives in the policy library. The plain-language version goes in the employee handbook and onboarding documents.

## Tool Recommendations

For HR work, Claude is particularly strong at longer, more structured documents: policy drafts, onboarding guides, interview frameworks. The context window is larger, so you can paste more material into a single conversation.

ChatGPT is comparable for shorter, more transactional tasks like outreach messages or screening questions.

Start with whichever one you have access to. The learning is in the doing, not in finding the right tool first.

One practical note: never paste personally identifiable employee information into AI tools. When you need AI's help with something that involves specific people, describe the situation in general terms. "I'm working with an employee who has been late to meetings consistently" rather than naming the person or including HR file details.

## The Career Angle: What This Does for Your Professional Positioning

HR professionals who get comfortable with AI have something increasingly valuable: they can move faster without cutting corners. In practice, this means you're able to respond to requests for new policies, updated job descriptions, or onboarding materials in days instead of weeks.

That speed changes how your internal stakeholders experience working with you. It also positions you to take on the more strategic work, because the operational work takes less of your time.

The HR professionals who will carry the most influence in the next few years are the ones who know how to direct AI well, which is a judgment skill, not a technical skill. You already have the domain expertise. The tool is just making it faster to apply.

## Your First Week: A 5-Day Practice Plan for AI in HR

The fastest way to get comfortable is to bring real work to Claude or ChatGPT every day for a week.

**Monday:** Take a job description you've written before and ask Claude to rewrite it from scratch using the same role details. Compare the two. What did it miss? What did it catch that you hadn't emphasized?

**Tuesday:** Build an interview guide for a role you're currently hiring for. Start with the job description, ask Claude for 8-10 questions with evaluation criteria, then edit it down to the ones you'd actually use.

**Wednesday:** Pick a policy you think could be clearer. Ask Claude to write a plain-language summary, then share it with one colleague who isn't in HR and see if they understand it.

**Thursday:** Draft a first-30-day onboarding plan for a role you frequently hire. Focus on making the goals specific enough to be actionable.

**Friday:** Reflect on what worked and what felt awkward. The things that felt awkward are almost always cases where you gave the tool too little context. That's the pattern to watch for.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Can I use AI for things like performance reviews or disciplinary documentation?**

Yes, but with care. Claude and ChatGPT can help you draft frameworks, templates, and plain-language summaries. They can help you think through what to include and what language to avoid. They should not be generating the actual performance assessments or discipline letters without significant human review. The judgment about a specific employee's performance belongs to the manager. AI can support the process, not replace it.

**What about bias in recruiting?**

AI can actually help here, not just hurt. Asking Claude to review a job description for language that might discourage qualified candidates is a useful step. Asking it to generate diverse interview questions helps. But AI can also reproduce bias if you give it biased inputs. Check your job descriptions for unnecessarily gendered language. Ask Claude: "Does this job description include any language that might discourage women or underrepresented candidates from applying?" It's a useful gut-check.

**What's the biggest mistake HR teams make when starting with AI?**

Giving it too little context and then concluding it "doesn't work." Every AI tool needs to know who you are, what the situation is, and what good output looks like. The more you tell it about your organization, your team, and your constraints, the more useful the output. Start with one specific task, give it full context, and build from there. That's covered in more depth in [how to use AI at work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work).

**Do I need technical knowledge to use these tools?**

No. Claude and ChatGPT are conversational. You type your request in plain English. If you can describe your work to a smart colleague, you can get useful output from these tools. The skills that matter are the same ones you already use: clear communication, domain knowledge, and the judgment to evaluate what comes back. For a deeper foundation, [your first week with AI](/learn/first-week-with-ai) walks through the basics without any jargon.

## Practicing Alongside People in the Same Boat

The HR professionals making the most progress with AI right now are doing it in conversation with others. What prompts work for job descriptions? How are people handling the data privacy question? What does a good AI-assisted onboarding document actually look like?

Those conversations are happening every week in the MVP Club community. If you want to see how other HR professionals are figuring this out and share what's working for you, that's exactly where to find them: [mvpclub.ai/community](/community).

An HR director in one of our community sessions pulled up Claude on her laptop and, in about four minutes, drafted a job description that would have taken her 90 minutes to write from scratch. She wasn’t using a template. She was having a conversation: here’s the role, here’s the team, here’s what good looks like, what questions should I be asking candidates?

That moment is available to you right now. And it’s not about replacing your judgment. It’s about giving your judgment better material to work with.

This guide covers the three areas where AI for HR professionals delivers the fastest results: recruiting, onboarding, and policy development. For each one, you’ll get a concrete workflow and the specific prompts that actually work.

Why HR Work Is Particularly Well-Suited for AI

HR involves a lot of writing. Job descriptions, offer letters, onboarding documents, policy drafts, performance review templates, communication plans. Most of these follow patterns you’ve run through dozens of times. You know what good looks like. You can evaluate output quickly.

That’s exactly the profile of work where Claude and ChatGPT save the most time. You’re not outsourcing the hard thinking. You’re offloading the blank-page problem so you can spend your time refining, personalizing, and applying your expertise where it actually matters.

The other factor: HR work requires a lot of context-switching. You’re rarely deep in one project for hours at a time. AI gives you a way to move faster across that constant switching without losing quality.

Three High-Impact Use Cases for AI in HR

Here’s where to start, ordered by speed of payoff.

Recruiting: Job descriptions, screening questions, interview guides, candidate outreach Onboarding: Welcome packets, role-specific orientation materials, first-30-60-90-day plans Policy development: First drafts, gap analysis, plain-language summaries of complex policies

Start with recruiting. It gives you results you can see in the same sitting.

Use Case 1: Recruiting (Your Fastest Win)

Writing Job Descriptions in Minutes, Not Hours

Most job descriptions follow the same arc: role summary, key responsibilities, what you’re looking for, why someone should want to work here. You’ve written this structure many times. AI can draft the skeleton in minutes; your job is to make it real.

Here’s the prompt pattern that works:

“I’m writing a job description for a [job title] on our [team name] team. This person will [primary responsibility]. They’ll work closely with [stakeholders]. Our company [brief company description]. What I care most about in this role is [top 2-3 must-haves]. Write a draft job description that leads with what makes this role interesting, not just what the person needs to do. Keep it under 400 words.”

The key moves here: give it the team context, name what you actually care about, and ask it to lead with what makes the role compelling. Generic job descriptions read like they were written by committee. Specific ones attract better candidates.

After you get the draft, your editing session with Claude or ChatGPT is where the value compounds. Tell it what’s off: “The tone is too corporate. We’re a fast-moving team. Make it sound like we’re looking for a partner, not an employee.” Or: “The requirements list is too long. Cut anything that’s a nice-to-have.” Each round gets faster as the tool learns what you’re going for.

Building Interview Guides

Once you have a job description, your next move is to ask Claude to generate interview questions from it.

“Based on this job description [paste it], write 8 interview questions that would help me evaluate whether someone can actually do this work, not just talk about it. For each question, include what a strong answer would include and what a weak answer would look like.”

This is one of the more immediately useful things AI can do for recruiting. Structured interview guides with evaluation criteria reduce bias and make your hiring decisions more defensible. They also help managers who are less experienced with interviews. And they’re time-consuming to write from scratch every time.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An HR professional sharing how they use Claude to build interview guides or screen candidates, with a specific example of a question that worked well]

Candidate Outreach That Doesn’t Sound Like a Template

Outreach messages for passive candidates are notoriously hard to write. Too generic and they get ignored. Too specific and they sound like you’ve been stalking someone’s LinkedIn profile.

Try this:

“Write a short outreach message (under 100 words) for a [role] candidate. She [brief description of relevant background]. We’re reaching out because [specific reason]. The message should feel personal and specific to her background, not like a mass send. Don’t open with ‘I hope this message finds you well.’ Don’t mention ‘exciting opportunity.’”

The negative constraints matter. Telling Claude what not to include often produces more useful output than describing what to include.

Use Case 2: Onboarding Materials That Actually Help New Hires

Onboarding is one of those areas where HR teams know the materials could be better and rarely have time to fix them. AI changes that math.

One member of our community described using Claude to build out a complete onboarding package for a new board member in a single afternoon. She gave it the role description, the organization’s structure, and a few notes about what this specific person needed to know. Claude drafted a welcome letter, a role summary, a first-week schedule, and a quick-reference guide. She edited and personalized each piece. The whole process took about three hours instead of three days.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific HR professional describing an onboarding workflow they built with AI, including what materials they generated and roughly how much time it saved]

The Prompt Pattern for Onboarding Documents

The key to getting useful onboarding materials from Claude or ChatGPT is specificity about the audience. “Write an onboarding guide” produces generic output. “Write a 30-day guide for a new mid-level HR business partner joining a 300-person company in the middle of a system migration” produces something much more useful.

Try this approach:

“Write a first-week schedule and overview document for a new [job title] joining our team. Context: [2-3 sentences about your organization]. This person is coming from [background]. In their first week, they need to [key goals]. Format as a day-by-day guide with the ‘why’ behind each item, not just a task list.”

Then ask follow-up questions:

“What questions does a new employee in this role typically have that aren’t addressed in a standard onboarding document? Write a FAQ section that anticipates those questions.”

That FAQ section alone often becomes one of the most-used documents in the onboarding package.

AI for Marketing Professionals: Campaigns, Copy, and Analytics
How marketing managers are using Claude and ChatGPT for campaign briefs, ad copy, and analytics. Practical use cases, real workflows, and a 5-day starter plan.

A marketing manager at a mid-size company recently described her week like this: she spent Monday writing a campaign brief, Tuesday drafting three versions of ad copy for a campaign that launched Thursday, Wednesday pulling analytics from four different platforms and trying to make sense of conflicting numbers, and Friday writing a recap deck. She did all of this in parallel with twelve Slack threads, two agency calls, and a budget review.

That week is not unusual. It is the job.

What's changing is that every single task she described is one where Claude or ChatGPT can meaningfully cut the time and friction. Not replace her judgment. Not automate her job. But act as a capable collaborator who does the drafting, the synthesizing, and the first pass at analysis so she can spend her time on the parts that actually require her.

This guide walks through the three highest-impact use cases for AI in marketing work, with specific workflows, real prompts, and an honest look at where the tools shine and where they still need a human in the loop.

## The three places AI makes the biggest difference for marketing teams

Marketing work has a particular characteristic that makes it a strong fit for AI: most of it involves generating, editing, and analyzing text. Campaigns start as briefs. Briefs become copy. Copy runs, and then you write a performance report. You pitch new ideas, respond to stakeholder feedback, and then do it all again next quarter.

AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT are built for exactly this kind of work. Where AI currently falls short is anywhere that requires deep institutional knowledge, relationship context, or creative instinct that comes from years of understanding what a specific audience actually responds to. You bring that. The AI handles the volume.

Here are the three use cases where AI for marketing managers delivers the most consistent return.

### 1. Campaign briefs and strategy documents

The brief is both the most important document in marketing and the one most likely to be written in a rush on a deadline. A weak brief produces weak creative. A strong brief creates alignment, saves revision cycles, and gives agencies and internal teams something they can actually act on.

Claude is particularly good at brief development because it can help you think through structure, surface questions you haven't answered yet, and generate a complete draft from a conversation. Most marketers find that talking through a campaign with Claude (describing the product, the goal, the audience, the channel, the timing) produces a usable brief draft in about 20 minutes. Without AI, the same document might take two hours of writing and internal alignment.

The prompt that works well here: "I'm developing a campaign brief for [product/service]. The goal is [specific outcome]. Primary audience is [description]. We're launching on [channels] in [timeframe]. Here's what we know about the audience already: [paste any research or personas]. Help me draft a complete brief including objectives, key messages, audience insights, creative direction, and success metrics."

What you get back will likely be too long and not specific enough to your brand. That's fine. You edit it down. The point is having something to react to instead of a blank page.

### 2. Ad copy and content at scale

This is where the time savings are most obvious. Writing three versions of a Facebook headline, five variations of a subject line, and two email body drafts used to be an afternoon of work. With Claude or ChatGPT, it's 15 minutes.

The key is giving the tool real context, not generic prompts. "Write me a Facebook ad" produces something generic. "Write five Facebook ad headlines for a project management tool aimed at operations managers at 200-person companies. The campaign goal is trial signups. The main pain point we're addressing is that their teams are using three different tools to track work and nothing syncs. Tone: direct, slightly skeptical of hype, not corporate." That produces something you can actually use as a starting point.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A marketing professional describing their copy workflow with Claude, specifically the difference between a generic prompt and a specific, context-rich prompt]

Two things to keep in mind about copy generation. First, the first draft will sound like a competent-but-generic marketing professional wrote it. That's intentional. You then edit it to sound like your brand. The editing phase is where your expertise matters. Second, ask for multiple variations with different tones and angles. Claude can produce "professional," "conversational," "punchy," and "storytelling" versions of the same ad in one response. Pick the direction you like and iterate from there.

For longer content like blog posts, landing pages, or email sequences, the workflow is slightly different. Use Claude to outline first, then draft section by section, then edit. Trying to generate a 1,000-word landing page in a single prompt usually produces something that lacks the specific angle and proof points your audience needs.

### 3. Analytics interpretation and reporting

This is the most underused AI use case in marketing, and also one of the most valuable.

Most marketing teams spend a lot of time pulling numbers and not enough time interpreting them. You export a CSV from Google Analytics, another from your email platform, another from your ad manager, and then spend an hour trying to figure out what the story actually is. Did the campaign work? Why did open rates drop? What should we do differently next time?

Claude can help you interpret data when you give it context. The workflow: copy your data into the conversation (or describe the key numbers), tell it what the campaign goal was, and ask it to help you identify the story and the next actions. You're not asking it to make decisions for you. You're asking it to help you see the patterns faster.

Here's a prompt structure that works well: "Here are the performance numbers from our Q1 email campaign: [paste numbers]. The campaign goal was [specific goal]. Our benchmark from last quarter was [comparison data]. Help me identify the three most important findings and suggest what we should test or change next quarter."

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific marketing professional describing a data interpretation session with Claude, including what the actual insight was that they wouldn't have seen as quickly on their own]

One thing to be clear about: Claude does not have access to your analytics platforms directly unless you've set up an integration. You're copying the data into the conversation. For most marketing reporting use cases, this is fine. For ongoing monitoring and alerts, you'll need platform-specific tools.

## A detailed walkthrough: writing a campaign brief from scratch

Because brief development is the highest-leverage use case and the one that creates the most downstream value, here's a step-by-step workflow you can follow this week.

**Step 1: Open Claude and start a conversation, not a prompt.**

Instead of asking for a brief immediately, describe the campaign like you would to a smart colleague who's new to the company. Tell Claude what you're selling, who you're selling it to, what the campaign is supposed to accomplish, and what you already know about the audience. This context-setting step takes 5 minutes and dramatically improves everything that comes after.

**Step 2: Ask it to identify gaps.**

Before generating a draft, ask: "Based on what I've described, what information am I missing that a strong campaign brief would typically include?" Claude will often surface things like: "You mentioned the target audience but haven't described their current alternatives" or "You've described the goal but haven't specified success metrics." These are exactly the questions a good creative director would ask. Answering them makes your brief better.

**Step 3: Generate the first draft.**

Ask for a complete brief in the format you use. If you have a template, paste it in. If you don't, Claude will generate a standard structure. Expect the first draft to be longer and more generic than you want.

**Step 4: Edit for specificity and brand voice.**

This is your job, not the AI's. Read through the draft and ask yourself: does this sound like us? Does it reflect what we actually know about this audience, not what a generic marketing playbook says about this audience type? Add the specifics only you know. Delete anything that could apply to any company.

**Step 5: Run it back through Claude for a final check.**

Ask: "Read this brief and tell me the three weakest sections. Where is the language too vague to guide creative work effectively?" This is where Claude is genuinely useful as a reviewer. It will often catch things like: "The 'key message' section says 'we make your work easier,' which is too vague to translate into creative direction. What specifically makes their work easier, and for which part of the day?"

The whole process takes 30-45 minutes instead of a half day. More importantly, the brief is usually better because the AI pushed back on vague language and helped you surface the things you hadn't fully thought through.

## Tool recommendation: start with Claude

For marketing work specifically, Claude is our recommendation over ChatGPT for most tasks. The writing quality for long-form content, briefs, and nuanced copy tends to be stronger, and it's better at following specific tonal direction without reverting to corporate default. ChatGPT has an edge for brainstorming volume (it generates more ideas faster) and for any workflows that connect to other tools via custom integrations.

Our practical recommendation: use Claude (claude.ai, $20/month for Claude Pro) as your primary writing collaborator. The paid version gives you longer conversations, which matters for campaign brief work where you're building context over multiple exchanges.

> **Carmella Thompson, MVP Club member:** When she was building workflows for her real estate clients, she found Claude's approach to market reports more adaptable than other tools. "Real estate agents have immediate use cases: automating market reports into blogs and generating branded content," she noted in one of our sessions. The same pattern holds for marketing teams: the real time savings come when you build repeatable workflows, not one-off prompts.

For analytics interpretation, Claude is strong. For direct spreadsheet manipulation, you'll still want to use Excel or Google Sheets. Where AI helps is the interpretation layer, not the data layer.

## The career angle: why this positions you now

Marketing teams are under pressure to produce more content across more channels with the same or smaller headcount. The professionals who figure out how to use AI effectively are not going to lose their jobs to AI. They're going to be the ones doing the jobs of two people, delivering faster, and building a reputation for output that their peers can't match.

The [AI and Your Career pillar](/learn/ai-learning-path) covers this in more depth. The short version: the marketing manager who can produce a campaign brief in 30 minutes instead of three hours, generate 10 copy variations before breakfast, and turn an analytics export into a clear performance narrative in a 20-minute Claude session has a real advantage. That advantage compounds.

This is not about replacing creative judgment. It's about applying it to more things, faster.

## Your first week: a 5-day starter plan for AI marketing managers

You don't need to rebuild your workflow to start getting value from AI. Here's a low-commitment entry point.

**Monday:** Write your next campaign brief or strategy document with Claude's help. Use the step-by-step workflow above. Time yourself. Compare to how long it usually takes.

**Tuesday:** Take a piece of copy you need to write this week (an email, an ad, a social post) and generate five variations using Claude. Include a context paragraph before asking for the copy. Edit the best version into something you'd actually send.

**Wednesday:** Find a recent performance report or analytics export. Paste the key numbers into Claude. Ask for three findings and suggested next steps. See if it surfaces anything you hadn't noticed or framed it in a way you'd use.

**Thursday:** Try Claude as a creative thinking partner on a campaign idea you're developing. Describe the concept and ask: "What's the weakest assumption in this campaign concept?" or "What angle haven't I considered for this audience?"

**Friday:** Spend 15 minutes reflecting. Which of the four tasks saved you meaningful time? Which felt clunky or produced output you couldn't use? Write down what you'd repeat next week and what you'd skip.

## Common mistakes marketing professionals make with AI

After seeing a lot of professionals go through their first weeks with these tools, a few patterns come up repeatedly.

**Prompting without context.** "Write me a campaign brief" produces a template. "Help me write a brief for a Q3 retention campaign targeting customers who haven't purchased in 90 days, with a $50 reactivation offer, aimed at 35-55-year-old female shoppers who originally bought from our wellness category" produces something you can work with.

**Expecting brand-perfect output on the first try.** Claude does not know your brand voice unless you teach it. Build a context paragraph that describes your tone, your audience, and what you want to avoid. Save it somewhere and paste it at the start of any new creative conversation.

**Using it for one task instead of a conversation.** The real value is the back-and-forth. Ask Claude to generate copy. Tell it what's wrong. Ask for revisions. Push back on the revision. The third or fourth version is usually where it gets interesting.

**Skipping the editing step.** AI-generated copy that goes out unedited almost always reads as AI-generated copy. Your job is not to prompt; your job is to edit. The tool speeds up the drafting. Your expertise makes it real.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can I paste confidential campaign data into Claude?**

Check your company's AI policy first. Most companies have guidance on what data can be shared with external AI tools. For analytics interpretation, you can often share aggregated numbers rather than raw customer data. When in doubt, anonymize or talk to your IT or legal team.

**How do I make the copy sound less like AI wrote it?**

Give Claude more specific direction before it drafts. Tell it to avoid certain phrases, match a tone reference, or write like a specific type of person ("write this like a brand director who reads The Atlantic, not like a tech startup"). Then edit for your specific voice. The more specific the prompt, the less generic the output.

**Will using AI get me in trouble with my agency or creative team?**

It depends on your relationship and what you're using it for. Most agencies are already using AI internally. Using it to speed up brief writing, prepare more thorough creative direction, or generate options to react to is generally seen as making their work easier, not replacing them. Be transparent about your process.

**My company doesn't allow ChatGPT or Claude. What should I do?**

Many companies have approved Microsoft Copilot (built on GPT) or Google Gemini as enterprise options. The workflows described in this guide apply to any capable language model. If none are approved, this is worth raising with your IT or leadership team. The [how to pitch AI to your boss](/learn/explain-ai-to-your-team) guide covers how to make that case.

## Practice alongside people figuring this out too

If you want to see what other marketing professionals are doing with these tools and share what's working for you, that's exactly what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions. Members share their actual workflows, get unstuck on the parts that feel clunky, and pick up approaches they wouldn't have thought of on their own.

[Join us at MVP Club](/community) and come to a session.

A marketing manager at a mid-size company recently described her week like this: she spent Monday writing a campaign brief, Tuesday drafting three versions of ad copy for a campaign that launched Thursday, Wednesday pulling analytics from four different platforms and trying to make sense of conflicting numbers, and Friday writing a recap deck. She did all of this in parallel with twelve Slack threads, two agency calls, and a budget review.

That week is not unusual. It is the job.

What’s changing is that every single task she described is one where Claude or ChatGPT can meaningfully cut the time and friction. Not replace her judgment. Not automate her job. But act as a capable collaborator who does the drafting, the synthesizing, and the first pass at analysis so she can spend her time on the parts that actually require her.

This guide walks through the three highest-impact use cases for AI in marketing work, with specific workflows, real prompts, and an honest look at where the tools shine and where they still need a human in the loop.

The three places AI makes the biggest difference for marketing teams

Marketing work has a particular characteristic that makes it a strong fit for AI: most of it involves generating, editing, and analyzing text. Campaigns start as briefs. Briefs become copy. Copy runs, and then you write a performance report. You pitch new ideas, respond to stakeholder feedback, and then do it all again next quarter.

AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT are built for exactly this kind of work. Where AI currently falls short is anywhere that requires deep institutional knowledge, relationship context, or creative instinct that comes from years of understanding what a specific audience actually responds to. You bring that. The AI handles the volume.

Here are the three use cases where AI for marketing managers delivers the most consistent return.

1. Campaign briefs and strategy documents

The brief is both the most important document in marketing and the one most likely to be written in a rush on a deadline. A weak brief produces weak creative. A strong brief creates alignment, saves revision cycles, and gives agencies and internal teams something they can actually act on.

Claude is particularly good at brief development because it can help you think through structure, surface questions you haven’t answered yet, and generate a complete draft from a conversation. Most marketers find that talking through a campaign with Claude (describing the product, the goal, the audience, the channel, the timing) produces a usable brief draft in about 20 minutes. Without AI, the same document might take two hours of writing and internal alignment.

The prompt that works well here: “I’m developing a campaign brief for [product/service]. The goal is [specific outcome]. Primary audience is [description]. We’re launching on [channels] in [timeframe]. Here’s what we know about the audience already: [paste any research or personas]. Help me draft a complete brief including objectives, key messages, audience insights, creative direction, and success metrics.”

What you get back will likely be too long and not specific enough to your brand. That’s fine. You edit it down. The point is having something to react to instead of a blank page.

2. Ad copy and content at scale

This is where the time savings are most obvious. Writing three versions of a Facebook headline, five variations of a subject line, and two email body drafts used to be an afternoon of work. With Claude or ChatGPT, it’s 15 minutes.

The key is giving the tool real context, not generic prompts. “Write me a Facebook ad” produces something generic. “Write five Facebook ad headlines for a project management tool aimed at operations managers at 200-person companies. The campaign goal is trial signups. The main pain point we’re addressing is that their teams are using three different tools to track work and nothing syncs. Tone: direct, slightly skeptical of hype, not corporate.” That produces something you can actually use as a starting point.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A marketing professional describing their copy workflow with Claude, specifically the difference between a generic prompt and a specific, context-rich prompt]

Two things to keep in mind about copy generation. First, the first draft will sound like a competent-but-generic marketing professional wrote it. That’s intentional. You then edit it to sound like your brand. The editing phase is where your expertise matters. Second, ask for multiple variations with different tones and angles. Claude can produce “professional,” “conversational,” “punchy,” and “storytelling” versions of the same ad in one response. Pick the direction you like and iterate from there.

For longer content like blog posts, landing pages, or email sequences, the workflow is slightly different. Use Claude to outline first, then draft section by section, then edit. Trying to generate a 1,000-word landing page in a single prompt usually produces something that lacks the specific angle and proof points your audience needs.

3. Analytics interpretation and reporting

This is the most underused AI use case in marketing, and also one of the most valuable.

Most marketing teams spend a lot of time pulling numbers and not enough time interpreting them. You export a CSV from Google Analytics, another from your email platform, another from your ad manager, and then spend an hour trying to figure out what the story actually is. Did the campaign work? Why did open rates drop? What should we do differently next time?

Claude can help you interpret data when you give it context. The workflow: copy your data into the conversation (or describe the key numbers), tell it what the campaign goal was, and ask it to help you identify the story and the next actions. You’re not asking it to make decisions for you. You’re asking it to help you see the patterns faster.

Here’s a prompt structure that works well: “Here are the performance numbers from our Q1 email campaign: [paste numbers]. The campaign goal was [specific goal]. Our benchmark from last quarter was [comparison data]. Help me identify the three most important findings and suggest what we should test or change next quarter.”

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific marketing professional describing a data interpretation session with Claude, including what the actual insight was that they wouldn’t have seen as quickly on their own]

One thing to be clear about: Claude does not have access to your analytics platforms directly unless you’ve set up an integration. You’re copying the data into the conversation. For most marketing reporting use cases, this is fine. For ongoing monitoring and alerts, you’ll need platform-specific tools.

A detailed walkthrough: writing a campaign brief from scratch

Because brief development is the highest-leverage use case and the one that creates the most downstream value, here’s a step-by-step workflow you can follow this week.

Step 1: Open Claude and start a conversation, not a prompt.

Instead of asking for a brief immediately, describe the campaign like you would to a smart colleague who’s new to the company. Tell Claude what you’re selling, who you’re selling it to, what the campaign is supposed to accomplish, and what you already know about the audience. This context-setting step takes 5 minutes and dramatically improves everything that comes after.

Step 2: Ask it to identify gaps.

Before generating a draft, ask: “Based on what I’ve described, what information am I missing that a strong campaign brief would typically include?” Claude will often surface things like: “You mentioned the target audience but haven’t described their current alternatives” or “You’ve described the goal but haven’t specified success metrics.” These are exactly the questions a good creative director would ask. Answering them makes your brief better.

Step 3: Generate the first draft.

Ask for a complete brief in the format you use. If you have a template, paste it in. If you don’t, Claude will generate a standard structure. Expect the first draft to be longer and more generic than you want.

Step 4: Edit for specificity and brand voice.

This is your job, not the AI’s. Read through the draft and ask yourself: does this sound like us? Does it reflect what we actually know about this audience, not what a generic marketing playbook says about this audience type? Add the specifics only you know. Delete anything that could apply to any company.

Step 5: Run it back through Claude for a final check.

Ask: “Read this brief and tell me the three weakest sections. Where is the language too vague to guide creative work effectively?” This is where Claude is genuinely useful as a reviewer. It will often catch things like: “The ‘key message’ section says ‘we make your work easier,’ which is too vague to translate into creative direction. What specifically makes their work easier, and for which part of the day?”

The whole process takes 30-45 minutes instead of a half day. More importantly, the brief is usually better because the AI pushed back on vague language and helped you surface the things you hadn’t fully thought through.

AI for Data Analysis: Turning Spreadsheets into Insights Without Coding
How to use ChatGPT and Claude for data analysis without writing a single line of code. A practical guide for non-technical professionals.

You have a spreadsheet with 800 rows of survey responses and your manager wants a summary by end of day. Two years ago, you had two options: spend three hours doing it manually or wait for someone in data to help. Now you have a third: paste it into ChatGPT or Claude, ask a question, and get an answer in 30 seconds.

AI for data analysis without coding is one of the most practical things these tools can do for non-technical professionals. You do not need Python, SQL, or a data science background. You need a spreadsheet, a question, and a chat window.

This guide walks you through exactly how to do it.

## What "AI data analysis" actually means for non-coders

When people talk about using AI for data analysis, they usually mean one of two things. Either a developer is building something sophisticated, or a non-technical person is having a conversation with an AI tool about their data. This guide is about the second one.

Both ChatGPT (with its Advanced Data Analysis feature, available on the Plus plan) and Claude (available on the free tier and Pro) can accept spreadsheet uploads and answer questions about them in plain English. You describe what you want to know, and the AI reads your data, runs calculations, and explains what it finds.

ChatGPT's approach is to write and run Python code behind the scenes, then give you the result. You never see the code unless you ask for it. Claude reads the data directly and reasons about it. Both produce useful outputs. The right choice depends on your data size and what you are trying to do. [OpenAI's Advanced Data Analysis documentation](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8437071-data-analysis-with-chatgpt) covers the full feature set if you want the technical details.

## Step 1: Prepare your data for the conversation

Before you upload anything, spend two minutes making sure your data is clean enough to talk about. AI tools do better with structured data: column headers in the first row, one piece of information per column, and no merged cells.

You do not need a perfect dataset. You need one that a thoughtful colleague could read without getting confused. If you would not hand this spreadsheet to a smart intern and expect them to understand it, add a row of column headers and remove any formatting that is there just for aesthetics.

Export from Excel or Google Sheets as a CSV file. Most AI tools accept Excel files directly, but CSV tends to work more reliably across both ChatGPT and Claude. Keep it under 50MB. If your file is larger than that, consider filtering to the rows most relevant to your question before uploading.

## Step 2: Upload and ask your first question

In ChatGPT Plus, click the paperclip icon in the chat window and upload your file. In Claude, you can drag and drop a file directly into the chat (or paste tabular data if the file is small). Once the file is attached, ask your question in plain language.

Good first questions tend to be specific and grounded in what you actually need to know:

- "How many responses mentioned price as a concern? Break it down by customer segment."
- "What is the average deal size in column D, grouped by the sales rep in column B?"
- "Which three products had the highest return rate last quarter? Show me the counts."

Avoid vague openers like "analyze this data" or "what do you see?" Those tend to produce generic summaries that do not answer your actual question. The more specific your question, the more useful the answer.

## Step 3: Work the conversation, not just the upload

The biggest mistake people make with AI data analysis is treating the upload as a one-shot transaction. You upload, you get an answer, you close the tab. That approach leaves most of the value on the table.

Instead, treat it like working with a smart colleague. Ask a follow-up. Push back on something that seems off. Ask for the result in a different format.

A typical useful conversation might look like this:

1. "What is the average response time per team?"
2. "The marketing team number seems high. Can you check if there are any outliers pulling that up?"
3. "Yes, remove those three outliers and recalculate."
4. "Now format this as a table I can copy into a slide."

That four-step conversation produces a clean, usable output in about five minutes. The same thing done manually in Excel might take 45 minutes, and that assumes you know how to write the formulas.

## Step 4: Ask for charts and summaries

Both ChatGPT and Claude can produce written summaries of your data that you can paste directly into reports, emails, or presentations. Claude can now create actual Excel files and other formatted documents, not just text responses, which makes it particularly useful if you need to hand someone a clean output.

For charts, ChatGPT is stronger. It will generate PNG files of bar charts, pie charts, and trend lines that you can download and drop into a presentation. Tell it exactly what you want:

"Create a bar chart showing total sales by region. Use a clean, professional style with no gridlines."

If the first version is not right, describe the change. "Make the bars horizontal. Add the actual dollar amounts as labels on each bar." The back-and-forth takes seconds, not the 15 minutes of clicking through Excel chart options.

## Common mistakes that produce bad results

**Uploading without context.** If your column headers are things like "Q3_Rev_Adj_v2," the AI will do its best but will probably misinterpret something. Add a brief note at the start of the conversation: "The column 'Q3_Rev_Adj_v2' is the revenue figure adjusted for returns in Q3 2025."

**Asking compound questions.** "What are the top five products, who are the top customers, and what is the month-over-month trend?" is three questions pretending to be one. Ask them one at a time. You will get better answers and it will be easier to spot if something is wrong.

**Taking every number at face value.** AI tools do make errors with calculations, especially on complex formulas or data with inconsistencies. Always sanity-check important numbers. If the AI says your average deal size is $847 and you know from experience it should be around $700, ask it to show you the calculation. Usually you will find a data issue rather than an AI error, but sometimes the AI got it wrong.

## From the Practice

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what members say about using ChatGPT or Claude for spreadsheet analysis or reporting at work]

One pattern that comes up consistently in MVP Club sessions: members who do this for the first time almost always pick a dataset they have been avoiding. Not a test file, not something low-stakes. The actual report they have been dreading. That choice matters. It means the result is immediately useful, and it means they remember exactly how much time they saved. The habit forms faster when the first win is a real one.

Carmella Thompson, a real estate agent in our community, has built a daily workflow around automating market reports. She uses AI to turn raw listing data into formatted client summaries, a process that used to take her over an hour each morning. It now takes about ten minutes.

## Which tool is better for data analysis: ChatGPT or Claude?

Both work. The honest answer is that they are good at different things.

ChatGPT is stronger for chart generation and running complex calculations. Its Advanced Data Analysis feature (on ChatGPT Plus) executes actual code, which means it can handle larger datasets and more computational tasks. It also has an interactive table view so you can scroll through your data inside the chat.

Claude handles larger amounts of text alongside data more gracefully. It can hold far more content in a single conversation, which matters when your analysis involves reading through long documents or combining data with written context (like survey comments alongside scores). [Anthropic's guide on using Claude for Excel](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12650343-use-claude-for-excel) covers the specific file types and capabilities in detail.

For most professionals doing everyday reporting and analysis tasks, either one works well. The better question is which one you are already using. Start there.

## The career angle: why this matters beyond saving time

Non-technical professionals who can turn raw data into clear insights are becoming the most useful people in the room. You do not need to become a data analyst. You need to be the person who can take the spreadsheet from the data team and actually answer the question the VP is asking, without waiting three days for a report.

AI for data analysis without coding changes the skill ceiling for what a generalist can do. It does not replace analysts who do complex modeling and data engineering. It gives everyone else access to capabilities that used to require their help for every question.

## Try it yourself

The fastest way to understand what this can do for you is to try it on something real. Open ChatGPT or Claude, upload a spreadsheet you actually use at work, and ask it one specific question. Do not pick a perfect dataset. Pick the messy one. The answer does not have to be perfect either. What you are building in that first session is not a report. It is a sense of what is possible.

Professionals across roles are figuring out the data workflows that fit their actual jobs, not generic tutorials. You can find that community at [/community](/community).

You have a spreadsheet with 800 rows of survey responses and your manager wants a summary by end of day. Two years ago, you had two options: spend three hours doing it manually or wait for someone in data to help. Now you have a third: paste it into ChatGPT or Claude, ask a question, and get an answer in 30 seconds.

AI for data analysis without coding is one of the most practical things these tools can do for non-technical professionals. You do not need Python, SQL, or a data science background. You need a spreadsheet, a question, and a chat window.

This guide walks you through exactly how to do it.

What “AI data analysis” actually means for non-coders

When people talk about using AI for data analysis, they usually mean one of two things. Either a developer is building something sophisticated, or a non-technical person is having a conversation with an AI tool about their data. This guide is about the second one.

Both ChatGPT (with its Advanced Data Analysis feature, available on the Plus plan) and Claude (available on the free tier and Pro) can accept spreadsheet uploads and answer questions about them in plain English. You describe what you want to know, and the AI reads your data, runs calculations, and explains what it finds.

ChatGPT’s approach is to write and run Python code behind the scenes, then give you the result. You never see the code unless you ask for it. Claude reads the data directly and reasons about it. Both produce useful outputs. The right choice depends on your data size and what you are trying to do. OpenAI’s Advanced Data Analysis documentation covers the full feature set if you want the technical details.

Step 1: Prepare your data for the conversation

Before you upload anything, spend two minutes making sure your data is clean enough to talk about. AI tools do better with structured data: column headers in the first row, one piece of information per column, and no merged cells.

You do not need a perfect dataset. You need one that a thoughtful colleague could read without getting confused. If you would not hand this spreadsheet to a smart intern and expect them to understand it, add a row of column headers and remove any formatting that is there just for aesthetics.

Export from Excel or Google Sheets as a CSV file. Most AI tools accept Excel files directly, but CSV tends to work more reliably across both ChatGPT and Claude. Keep it under 50MB. If your file is larger than that, consider filtering to the rows most relevant to your question before uploading.

Step 2: Upload and ask your first question

In ChatGPT Plus, click the paperclip icon in the chat window and upload your file. In Claude, you can drag and drop a file directly into the chat (or paste tabular data if the file is small). Once the file is attached, ask your question in plain language.

Good first questions tend to be specific and grounded in what you actually need to know:

  • “How many responses mentioned price as a concern? Break it down by customer segment.”
  • “What is the average deal size in column D, grouped by the sales rep in column B?”
  • “Which three products had the highest return rate last quarter? Show me the counts.”

Avoid vague openers like “analyze this data” or “what do you see?” Those tend to produce generic summaries that do not answer your actual question. The more specific your question, the more useful the answer.

Step 3: Work the conversation, not just the upload

The biggest mistake people make with AI data analysis is treating the upload as a one-shot transaction. You upload, you get an answer, you close the tab. That approach leaves most of the value on the table.

Instead, treat it like working with a smart colleague. Ask a follow-up. Push back on something that seems off. Ask for the result in a different format.

A typical useful conversation might look like this:

  1. “What is the average response time per team?”
  2. “The marketing team number seems high. Can you check if there are any outliers pulling that up?”
  3. “Yes, remove those three outliers and recalculate.”
  4. “Now format this as a table I can copy into a slide.”

That four-step conversation produces a clean, usable output in about five minutes. The same thing done manually in Excel might take 45 minutes, and that assumes you know how to write the formulas.

Step 4: Ask for charts and summaries

Both ChatGPT and Claude can produce written summaries of your data that you can paste directly into reports, emails, or presentations. Claude can now create actual Excel files and other formatted documents, not just text responses, which makes it particularly useful if you need to hand someone a clean output.

For charts, ChatGPT is stronger. It will generate PNG files of bar charts, pie charts, and trend lines that you can download and drop into a presentation. Tell it exactly what you want:

“Create a bar chart showing total sales by region. Use a clean, professional style with no gridlines.”

If the first version is not right, describe the change. “Make the bars horizontal. Add the actual dollar amounts as labels on each bar.” The back-and-forth takes seconds, not the 15 minutes of clicking through Excel chart options.

Common mistakes that produce bad results

Uploading without context. If your column headers are things like “Q3_Rev_Adj_v2,” the AI will do its best but will probably misinterpret something. Add a brief note at the start of the conversation: “The column ‘Q3_Rev_Adj_v2’ is the revenue figure adjusted for returns in Q3 2025.”

Asking compound questions. “What are the top five products, who are the top customers, and what is the month-over-month trend?” is three questions pretending to be one. Ask them one at a time. You will get better answers and it will be easier to spot if something is wrong.

Taking every number at face value. AI tools do make errors with calculations, especially on complex formulas or data with inconsistencies. Always sanity-check important numbers. If the AI says your average deal size is $847 and you know from experience it should be around $700, ask it to show you the calculation. Usually you will find a data issue rather than an AI error, but sometimes the AI got it wrong.
