AI for Operations Managers: Process Improvement and Workflow Automation
How operations managers are using AI to find process bottlenecks, draft SOPs, and cut hours from weekly reporting. Practical use cases and a 5-day start plan.

Your Monday morning probably looks something like this: a pile of status updates to consolidate, a vendor who missed a deadline, a process that keeps breaking in the same place, and a meeting in 45 minutes. Operations managers carry an enormous amount of institutional knowledge in their heads, and most of the work is just moving that knowledge around: into reports, into procedures, into emails, into decisions.

That's exactly where AI helps most. Not by replacing your judgment, but by handling the moving-and-organizing so you can focus on the thinking and deciding.

This guide covers the three highest-impact use cases for operations managers using AI today, a detailed walkthrough of the one to start with, and a five-day plan for getting from curious to actually using it.

## Why operations is one of the best roles for AI right now

Operations work is full of tasks that are cognitively demanding but not cognitively interesting. Writing the same status report every week. Translating your mental model of a process into a document someone else can follow. Reviewing vendor communications and drafting responses. Synthesizing information from five different sources into one coherent update for leadership.

These tasks require real judgment (you have to know what matters), but the execution is repetitive. That combination is where Claude and ChatGPT earn their keep. If you're still building a baseline habit of daily AI use, [How to Use AI at Work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) covers that foundation first.

AI use cases for operations aren't about removing people from processes. They're about getting the tedious execution off your plate so your judgment can go where it actually counts.

## The three highest-impact use cases

### Use case 1: Process documentation and SOP drafting

Ask most operations managers what they never have time for, and process documentation comes up immediately. Everyone knows the procedures should be written down. And yet they rarely get written, because the time never materializes. The result is institutional knowledge that lives entirely in one person's head, which creates fragility, onboarding problems, and handoff failures.

Claude can close this gap significantly. You describe how a process works, in conversational language, the way you'd explain it to a new hire. Claude turns that into a structured SOP with numbered steps, decision points, and edge cases. The draft won't be perfect (it never is), but it gives you something to react to and edit, which is always faster than starting from scratch.

This is the quickest win in AI use cases for operations because the raw material is already in your head. You just need a way to get it out without spending three hours formatting a document.

### Use case 2: Status report and stakeholder communication drafting

Operations managers spend a lot of time translating what's actually happening into what leadership needs to hear. That's a skill. It's also time-consuming.

Claude can handle the first draft. You give it the raw inputs: what's on track, what's at risk, what decisions you need, what changed since last week. It assembles them into a structured update in whatever format your organization uses. You edit for accuracy and tone, which takes fifteen minutes instead of an hour.

This works particularly well for weekly operational reports, incident post-mortems, vendor performance summaries, and project status updates.

### Use case 3: Identifying bottlenecks and diagnosing process failures

This is the use case people are most surprised by. You can describe a process to Claude, including where it tends to break down, and ask it to help you think through why. What assumptions is the process making? What would need to be true for step four to fail the way it keeps failing? What's the most likely root cause given these symptoms?

Claude doesn't have knowledge of your organization, but it's surprisingly useful as a thinking partner for process analysis. It asks questions you might not have thought to ask. It surfaces patterns. It helps you structure a diagnosis before you bring it to your team. If you want to go deeper on this approach, [AI as a thinking tool](/blog/ai-thinking-tool-build-scaffolds-expand-capability) covers how to use AI to reason through complex problems rather than just produce outputs.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An operations professional sharing a moment where talking through a process with Claude helped them identify a root cause or bottleneck they'd been stuck on]

## Detailed walkthrough: SOP drafting with Claude

This is the use case to start with. It delivers the most visible results with the least setup, and it solves a real problem that has probably been on your to-do list for months.

### Step 1: Pick one process you know well

Don't start with your most complex process. Start with something you do (or oversee) frequently, understand end-to-end, and have been meaning to document. A vendor onboarding checklist, a weekly reporting workflow, an escalation procedure.

### Step 2: Open Claude and set context

Claude Pro ($20/month) is worth it for this work. The conversations are longer, the output is more detailed, and you won't hit limits mid-document. Open a new chat and start like this:

*"I'm an operations manager. I want to document a process so my team can follow it consistently without me. I'll describe how it works, and I'd like you to turn it into a step-by-step SOP with numbered steps, decision points where the path varies, and notes for common edge cases. Ask me any clarifying questions before you start."*

That last sentence matters. Letting Claude ask questions before drafting means the output is better calibrated to what you actually need.

### Step 3: Describe the process in plain language

Talk through the process the way you'd explain it to someone in your first week on the job. Don't worry about format or completeness. Just explain it. If you leave something out, Claude will ask.

You can do this in one long message, or in a back-and-forth conversation. The conversational approach often produces better output because you can catch gaps as they come up.

### Step 4: Review and edit the draft

Claude will produce a structured draft. Read it against your actual process. Where does it get the sequence right? Where does it miss a decision point? Where does it leave out context that matters?

Your edits are where the document becomes real. The goal isn't to accept what Claude produced; it's to spend your editing time on substance (what's accurate, what's missing) instead of structure (how to organize and format it).

### Step 5: Save the prompt, not just the document

Once you have an SOP you're happy with, save the approach. Copy the prompt you used, note what worked, and keep it somewhere you can find it. Next time you need to document a process, you'll start from a working template instead of figuring it out again.

Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club's co-founders, describes this pattern as the core habit: "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude: here's how I want you to do it. Just go." For operations managers, that applies to the documentation work itself, not just the processes you're documenting.

## Tool recommendations

For the use cases above, **Claude** is the better choice over ChatGPT for most operations work, in our experience. Claude handles long documents and complex instructions more reliably, and it's particularly good at following multi-step structured formats (exactly what SOPs require).

Start with **Claude.ai** at the free tier to try a few prompts. Upgrade to Claude Pro ($20/month) when you're ready to use it consistently. The paid version handles longer conversations without cutting off, which matters when you're working through a complicated process.

For anything involving your actual data (spreadsheets, reports, databases), **ChatGPT with the data analysis feature** or **Claude with file upload** both work well. You can paste a data table and ask specific questions about it without any coding.

One note: check your organization's AI policy before pasting sensitive internal documents into any AI tool. Many companies have approved tools or guidance on what's safe to share. When in doubt, anonymize or describe the data rather than pasting it directly.

## The career angle: becoming the operations leader who uses AI well

Operations is one of the roles where AI capability compounds fastest. The reason is that operations work is already about systems: you think in processes, you manage information flows, you design for consistency and repeatability. Those instincts translate directly into effective AI use.

An operations manager who uses AI well can document processes that used to exist only in their head. They can produce stakeholder communications in a fraction of the time. They can think through process failures more systematically and arrive at root causes faster.

That makes you more effective in your current role. It also positions you as the person in your organization who knows how to apply AI to operational problems, which is a genuinely valuable thing to be right now.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An operations professional or similar role describing how their AI capability changed how their team or leadership sees them]

## Your first week: a 5-day micro-practice plan

You don't need to overhaul anything. Five short sessions will show you where AI fits and where it doesn't.

**Day 1 (Monday): Document one small process**
Pick the simplest process you can think of. Even ten steps. Describe it to Claude using the approach above and review the draft. The point is to go through the full loop once, not to produce a perfect document.

**Day 2 (Tuesday): Draft one status update**
Take a real status update you need to write this week. Give Claude the raw inputs: what's done, what's at risk, what you need from leadership. Let it draft. Edit. Notice how long the editing takes compared to writing from scratch.

**Day 3 (Wednesday): Think through a process problem**
Pick something that keeps breaking. Describe the process to Claude, describe where it fails, and ask it to help you think through possible root causes. Don't accept its answers uncritically. Use them as a starting point for your own diagnosis.

**Day 4 (Thursday): Draft a vendor or stakeholder communication**
Find an email or message you need to write today. Give Claude the context: who you're writing to, what the situation is, what you want them to do or understand. Review what it produces and edit it until it sounds like you.

**Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and pick one habit**
Which of the four tasks saved you the most time? Which felt most useful? Pick one to make a regular part of how you work. That's it. One habit, repeated, compounds faster than four habits started and abandoned.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific operations manager or similar professional who went through a first week with AI and the particular task that clicked for them, with concrete details about what they tried and what happened]

## Frequently asked questions

**Can I use AI with my company's proprietary data?**
Check your IT or legal team first. Many organizations have approved tools or specific guidance. A safe approach in the meantime: describe processes without pasting confidential documents, or anonymize data before sharing it. Claude and ChatGPT can still be very helpful when you describe a situation in general terms.

**What if Claude gets the process wrong in the SOP?**
It will. That's expected. The draft is a starting point, not a finished document. Your job is to read it against how the process actually works and correct what's off. The time savings come from editing instead of drafting from scratch, not from getting a perfect first output.

**How much time should I expect to save?**
That depends heavily on what you're doing and how you use it. A reasonable expectation for your first month: you'll find one or two tasks where AI saves you 30 to 60 minutes per week. That's not transformational. It's also real, and it compounds as you get better at directing the tool.

**Do I need technical skills to use these tools?**
No. Everything described here works in a regular chat interface, no different from sending a message. You type, Claude responds, you reply. The only skill is learning to give it good context, and that comes with practice, not technical background.

## Practice alongside the people figuring this out with you

Operations managers are often the ones in their organization who understand how work actually gets done. Right now, figuring out where AI fits in that picture is one of the more interesting problems in the field, and it's better to work through it alongside other professionals than to work through it alone.

If you want to see how other operations professionals and their peers are applying these techniques, MVP Club runs weekly sessions where members share what's working, get unstuck on specific tasks, and practice together. Come see what's happening at [/community](/community).

Claude can handle the first draft. You give it the raw inputs: what’s on track, what’s at risk, what decisions you need, what changed since last week. It assembles them into a structured update in whatever format your organization uses. You edit for accuracy and tone, which takes fifteen minutes instead of an hour.

This works particularly well for weekly operational reports, incident post-mortems, vendor performance summaries, and project status updates.

Use case 3: Identifying bottlenecks and diagnosing process failures

This is the use case people are most surprised by. You can describe a process to Claude, including where it tends to break down, and ask it to help you think through why. What assumptions is the process making? What would need to be true for step four to fail the way it keeps failing? What’s the most likely root cause given these symptoms?

Claude doesn’t have knowledge of your organization, but it’s surprisingly useful as a thinking partner for process analysis. It asks questions you might not have thought to ask. It surfaces patterns. It helps you structure a diagnosis before you bring it to your team. If you want to go deeper on this approach, AI as a thinking tool covers how to use AI to reason through complex problems rather than just produce outputs.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An operations professional sharing a moment where talking through a process with Claude helped them identify a root cause or bottleneck they’d been stuck on]

Detailed walkthrough: SOP drafting with Claude

This is the use case to start with. It delivers the most visible results with the least setup, and it solves a real problem that has probably been on your to-do list for months.

Step 1: Pick one process you know well

Don’t start with your most complex process. Start with something you do (or oversee) frequently, understand end-to-end, and have been meaning to document. A vendor onboarding checklist, a weekly reporting workflow, an escalation procedure.

Step 2: Open Claude and set context

Claude Pro ($20/month) is worth it for this work. The conversations are longer, the output is more detailed, and you won’t hit limits mid-document. Open a new chat and start like this:

“I’m an operations manager. I want to document a process so my team can follow it consistently without me. I’ll describe how it works, and I’d like you to turn it into a step-by-step SOP with numbered steps, decision points where the path varies, and notes for common edge cases. Ask me any clarifying questions before you start.”

That last sentence matters. Letting Claude ask questions before drafting means the output is better calibrated to what you actually need.

Step 3: Describe the process in plain language

Talk through the process the way you’d explain it to someone in your first week on the job. Don’t worry about format or completeness. Just explain it. If you leave something out, Claude will ask.

You can do this in one long message, or in a back-and-forth conversation. The conversational approach often produces better output because you can catch gaps as they come up.

Step 4: Review and edit the draft

Claude will produce a structured draft. Read it against your actual process. Where does it get the sequence right? Where does it miss a decision point? Where does it leave out context that matters?

Your edits are where the document becomes real. The goal isn’t to accept what Claude produced; it’s to spend your editing time on substance (what’s accurate, what’s missing) instead of structure (how to organize and format it).

Step 5: Save the prompt, not just the document

Once you have an SOP you’re happy with, save the approach. Copy the prompt you used, note what worked, and keep it somewhere you can find it. Next time you need to document a process, you’ll start from a working template instead of figuring it out again.

Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club’s co-founders, describes this pattern as the core habit: “Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude: here’s how I want you to do it. Just go.” For operations managers, that applies to the documentation work itself, not just the processes you’re documenting.

Tool recommendations

For the use cases above, Claude is the better choice over ChatGPT for most operations work, in our experience. Claude handles long documents and complex instructions more reliably, and it’s particularly good at following multi-step structured formats (exactly what SOPs require).

Start with Claude.ai at the free tier to try a few prompts. Upgrade to Claude Pro ($20/month) when you’re ready to use it consistently. The paid version handles longer conversations without cutting off, which matters when you’re working through a complicated process.

For anything involving your actual data (spreadsheets, reports, databases), ChatGPT with the data analysis feature or Claude with file upload both work well. You can paste a data table and ask specific questions about it without any coding.

One note: check your organization’s AI policy before pasting sensitive internal documents into any AI tool. Many companies have approved tools or guidance on what’s safe to share. When in doubt, anonymize or describe the data rather than pasting it directly.

The career angle: becoming the operations leader who uses AI well

Operations is one of the roles where AI capability compounds fastest. The reason is that operations work is already about systems: you think in processes, you manage information flows, you design for consistency and repeatability. Those instincts translate directly into effective AI use.

An operations manager who uses AI well can document processes that used to exist only in their head. They can produce stakeholder communications in a fraction of the time. They can think through process failures more systematically and arrive at root causes faster.

That makes you more effective in your current role. It also positions you as the person in your organization who knows how to apply AI to operational problems, which is a genuinely valuable thing to be right now.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An operations professional or similar role describing how their AI capability changed how their team or leadership sees them]

Your first week: a 5-day micro-practice plan

You don’t need to overhaul anything. Five short sessions will show you where AI fits and where it doesn’t.

Day 1 (Monday): Document one small process Pick the simplest process you can think of. Even ten steps. Describe it to Claude using the approach above and review the draft. The point is to go through the full loop once, not to produce a perfect document.

Day 2 (Tuesday): Draft one status update Take a real status update you need to write this week. Give Claude the raw inputs: what’s done, what’s at risk, what you need from leadership. Let it draft. Edit. Notice how long the editing takes compared to writing from scratch.

Day 3 (Wednesday): Think through a process problem Pick something that keeps breaking. Describe the process to Claude, describe where it fails, and ask it to help you think through possible root causes. Don’t accept its answers uncritically. Use them as a starting point for your own diagnosis.

Day 4 (Thursday): Draft a vendor or stakeholder communication Find an email or message you need to write today. Give Claude the context: who you’re writing to, what the situation is, what you want them to do or understand. Review what it produces and edit it until it sounds like you.

Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and pick one habit Which of the four tasks saved you the most time? Which felt most useful? Pick one to make a regular part of how you work. That’s it. One habit, repeated, compounds faster than four habits started and abandoned.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific operations manager or similar professional who went through a first week with AI and the particular task that clicked for them, with concrete details about what they tried and what happened]

Frequently asked questions

Can I use AI with my company’s proprietary data? Check your IT or legal team first. Many organizations have approved tools or specific guidance. A safe approach in the meantime: describe processes without pasting confidential documents, or anonymize data before sharing it. Claude and ChatGPT can still be very helpful when you describe a situation in general terms.

What if Claude gets the process wrong in the SOP? It will. That’s expected. The draft is a starting point, not a finished document. Your job is to read it against how the process actually works and correct what’s off. The time savings come from editing instead of drafting from scratch, not from getting a perfect first output.

How much time should I expect to save? That depends heavily on what you’re doing and how you use it. A reasonable expectation for your first month: you’ll find one or two tasks where AI saves you 30 to 60 minutes per week. That’s not transformational. It’s also real, and it compounds as you get better at directing the tool.

Do I need technical skills to use these tools? No. Everything described here works in a regular chat interface, no different from sending a message. You type, Claude responds, you reply. The only skill is learning to give it good context, and that comes with practice, not technical background.

Practice alongside the people figuring this out with you

Operations managers are often the ones in their organization who understand how work actually gets done. Right now, figuring out where AI fits in that picture is one of the more interesting problems in the field, and it’s better to work through it alongside other professionals than to work through it alone.

If you want to see how other operations professionals and their peers are applying these techniques, MVP Club runs weekly sessions where members share what’s working, get unstuck on specific tasks, and practice together. Come see what’s happening at /community.

AI for Nonprofit Professionals: Grant Writing, Donor Outreach, and Reporting
How nonprofit staff are using Claude and ChatGPT for grant writing, donor outreach, and impact reporting. Practical workflows for lean teams.

Your grant deadline is Friday. You have a program report due Monday. And your executive director just forwarded a major donor request asking for a personalized update by end of week. You have one full-time staff person and two part-timers.

This is the daily reality for most nonprofit professionals. More communication, more documentation, more reporting, less time. And unlike corporate roles, there's usually no budget for consultants, no marketing department to hand things off to, and no one whose job it is to write for you.

That's exactly where tools like Claude and ChatGPT can make a real difference, not by replacing the expertise and mission-knowledge only you have, but by eliminating the blank-page problem on tasks you'd do anyway.

## Where AI actually fits in nonprofit work

Before diving into specific workflows, it helps to get clear on what AI is genuinely useful for in this context versus where your human judgment is irreplaceable.

AI does well with: first drafts, synthesizing information into readable formats, adapting the same message for multiple audiences, pulling structure from your notes, and handling repetitive writing tasks. These are exactly the tasks that eat nonprofit staff time without requiring the deep expertise you've spent years building.

AI does not do well with: knowing your specific community, understanding what your funders actually care about, or replacing the relationship-building that is the core of nonprofit work. A grant written entirely by AI and unreviewed by staff will almost certainly miss the nuance that wins funding.

The useful mental model here is the same one that applies across every professional role: [using AI is a managerial relationship](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work). You direct the work, give it context, evaluate the output, and push back when it misses the mark. You bring the mission expertise. Claude or ChatGPT brings the drafting capacity.

## The three highest-impact use cases for nonprofit professionals

After working with professionals across roles and sectors, these three areas consistently produce the most time savings for people in nonprofit and community-focused work.

### 1. Grant writing (the biggest time sink, and the best fit for AI)

Grant writing is a perfect AI use case because it is largely a drafting problem. You know your program. You know your outcomes. You know your community. The hard part is translating that knowledge into the specific format a funder wants, often under deadline pressure and for multiple funders with different requirements.

AI can dramatically accelerate that translation work.

### 2. Donor outreach and stewardship communications

Writing personalized donor communications at scale is something most small nonprofits simply don't have time for. A major donor portfolio of 50 people should theoretically get individualized touchpoints throughout the year. In practice, most organizations send one or two generic newsletter blasts and hope that counts as stewardship.

AI changes that calculation.

### 3. Impact reporting and program documentation

Board reports, annual reports, grant progress reports: these are necessary, important, and completely demoralizing to write when you're staring at a year's worth of program notes, spreadsheets, and anecdotes. AI can synthesize that raw material into readable narrative quickly.

---

## Detailed walkthrough: Grant writing with Claude

This is where most nonprofit staff get the most immediate value, so let's go through a full workflow.

**What you need before you open Claude:**
- The grant guidelines and application questions (paste the actual text, not a summary of it)
- Your program description (even rough notes are fine)
- Any data you have on outcomes, participants, or impact
- Any previous successful grants for this program (if you have them)

**Step 1: Feed Claude the context**

Start a new Claude conversation and give it everything it needs to understand your organization and program. Something like:

> "I'm the program director at [Organization Name], a nonprofit that [brief description: who you serve, what you do, your geography]. I'm writing a grant application for [funder name]. Here are the grant guidelines: [paste full guidelines]. Here's background on our program: [paste your program description or notes]. Here's our outcome data: [paste whatever you have]. Please read all of this before we start, and let me know what you've understood about our work."

That last sentence matters. Asking Claude to confirm its understanding before drafting helps you catch any misreads before you're deep into a draft.

**Step 2: Answer application questions one at a time**

Resist the urge to ask Claude to "write the entire application." Instead, take each question individually. Ask Claude to draft a response, then read it critically: does it sound like your organization? Does it address what the funder is actually asking? Is the emphasis right?

The first draft is almost never what you'd submit. That's fine. Your job at this stage is to react, correct, and direct. "This paragraph oversells the outcomes data we have. Scale it back and be more specific about what we've actually measured." That conversation is where the value is.

**Step 3: Adapt, don't start over**

Once you have a strong grant response to one funder, you have a foundation. The next funder application is not a blank page. It's an editing project. Ask Claude: "Here's the grant response I wrote for Funder A. Now I need to adapt this for Funder B, whose guidelines are attached. Their focus is on [X] instead of [Y]. Help me revise."

This is where the time savings compound. Your second grant application might take 40% of the time your first one took. Your third, less still.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A nonprofit professional describing how they used Claude to get a grant application done under deadline pressure, with specifics about what they gave the AI and what it helped them accomplish]

---

## Donor outreach: Making stewardship feel personal at scale

Personalized donor communication is the gold standard of nonprofit fundraising. It is also the thing most understaffed development teams sacrifice first when time is short.

Here's a workflow that makes individualized outreach realistic without adding hours to your week.

**The "donor context card" approach**

For each donor you want to steward personally, keep a simple note in a document or spreadsheet: their giving history (years of support, approximate level), any personal connection to your work (why they give, family history with the issue, past conversations they've mentioned), and any recent news about them you'd want to acknowledge (a promotion, a family milestone, anything you'd note if you were keeping up with a friend).

This doesn't have to be elaborate. Even two or three sentences per donor gives Claude enough to work with.

When it's time to write a touchpoint, open Claude and say: "I need to write a brief, personal email to a donor named [Name]. Here's what I know about them: [paste your context card]. The purpose of this email is [thank them for a recent gift / update them on a program they care about / invite them to an event]. I want this to sound warm and genuine, not like a mail merge. Can you draft something I can edit?"

Read what comes back. Edit it until it actually sounds like you. The goal is not to send AI-written email unreviewed. The goal is to spend 10 minutes on a personal outreach instead of 45, and to send more of them.

**Adapting one message for multiple audiences**

Say you have a program update you want to share: a cohort just graduated, or you hit a milestone, or you have a powerful participant story (with permission to share). You need to communicate this to major donors, your email list, and your board. Three different audiences, three different tones, three different levels of detail.

Ask Claude to draft all three versions from a single brief. "I have a program update I need to share with three different audiences. Here's the core story: [paste]. Can you draft: (1) a 2-paragraph personal email to a major donor who has funded this program, (2) a newsletter paragraph for our general email list, (3) a brief board update for our quarterly board report?"

One piece of information. Three outputs. That's where the time savings are real.

---

## Impact reporting: Turning raw data into readable narrative

The board report you've been putting off probably exists somewhere as a pile of: program notes, attendance spreadsheets, a few compelling participant stories, some outcome numbers that feel hard to contextualize, and a general sense of "we did a lot this year."

Claude is genuinely good at helping you shape that raw material into coherent narrative.

**Paste in the raw material, not a summary**

The key mistake people make when using AI for reports is summarizing first, then asking for help. That loses information. Instead, paste your actual notes, your actual data, your actual stories (de-identified if needed for privacy). Let Claude work with the real material.

"Here are my program notes from this quarter: [paste]. Here's our attendance data: [paste]. Here's a participant story I'm allowed to share: [paste]. I need to write a 2-page program update for our board. It should cover: what we did, who we served, what we're seeing in terms of outcomes, and one story that illustrates the impact. Please draft this in a clear, professional tone."

You'll still need to edit. Claude doesn't know your board, doesn't know what the ED has already told them, and doesn't know which metrics they care most about. But getting from raw notes to a readable draft in 20 minutes instead of two hours is a real change.

**Grant progress reports**

These are the least beloved documents in the nonprofit world: required, formulaic, and usually due when you're already slammed. Most funders have a specific format. Feed Claude the format, feed it your program data, and ask for a first draft. Then review it carefully: funders notice if the report doesn't match what you proposed, and they notice if the outcomes you're claiming don't match what your contract said you'd deliver.

Claude can draft. You verify and vouch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A nonprofit professional (or someone who has done volunteer or community work) describing a specific moment where AI helped them get through a reporting or communication pile, with concrete time savings]

---

## Tool recommendations

For most of these workflows, **Claude** (claude.ai) is the better choice. Claude handles long documents well, which matters when you're pasting in grant guidelines, program notes, and outcome data all at once. It also tends to write in a more measured, professional tone by default, which suits grant writing and formal donor communications.

**ChatGPT** is a solid alternative and either works for the core workflows above. The paid tier ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) gives you significantly longer conversations and better reasoning, which matters when you're working with large amounts of program context. If you're doing this work regularly, the paid tier is worth it.

One important note: before pasting any sensitive information into an AI tool, check your organization's data policies. Most nonprofits do not have formal AI policies yet, but you should avoid pasting personally identifiable information about participants, donor financial details, or confidential grant communications. De-identify examples when you can, and treat these tools the way you'd treat any cloud document: useful and generally fine, but not a place for truly sensitive data.

---

## What members in community-focused roles are seeing

In our community sessions, a pattern shows up for people doing mission-driven work: the biggest benefit isn't the individual time savings on any one task. It's getting past what T Gaines described in one of our Wednesday demo sessions as "getting the app actually impacting the real world" — the turning point where using a tool stops feeling like an experiment and starts feeling like a real shift in how you work.

For nonprofit staff, that moment often comes when they realize they can get a complete grant draft done in one focused hour instead of spreading the dread across three days. Not because the AI wrote something perfect, but because having something to react to is so much faster than staring at a blank page.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A nonprofit professional or volunteer-focused professional sharing a specific example of how AI changed their writing process for grant applications, donor communications, or program reports]

---

## Career angle: AI capability positions you as a force multiplier

In a sector where most organizations are chronically understaffed and under-resourced, a professional who can produce grant-quality writing, personalized donor communications, and polished program reports in a fraction of the time is genuinely hard to replace.

This is not about automating your job away. The relationship-building, community knowledge, program expertise, and mission alignment that makes nonprofit work effective cannot be automated. But the documentation and communication work that supports those relationships can be made significantly more efficient.

Being the person on your team who knows how to use these tools well, who can help a colleague get a grant application done under deadline, who can draft the impact narrative for a board presentation in a morning, is a form of professional leadership. It's also [how people become the AI advocate at their organization](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work).

---

## Your first week: a five-day practice plan

You don't need a major project to start. Here's a low-stakes entry point for your first week.

**Monday:** Find one piece of writing you've been putting off. A grant question you're dreading, a donor email you've been meaning to send, a program update nobody's asked for yet but you know you'll need. Open Claude. Give it context. Ask for a draft. Read it and revise.

**Tuesday:** Take that revised draft and improve the prompt you used. What would you tell Claude differently now that you've seen what it does with basic context? Try again with a better setup.

**Wednesday:** Adapt the same content for a different audience. If Monday was a grant narrative, Wednesday is the donor newsletter paragraph from the same material. Same program story, different format and tone.

**Thursday:** Try a reporting task. Take your messiest pile of program notes and ask Claude to help you organize them into a readable summary. You're not trying to produce a finished document. You're practicing giving AI raw material and seeing what it does with it.

**Friday:** Reflect for ten minutes. Where did Claude actually save you time? Where did it produce something that needed heavy editing? Where did it miss the mark entirely? This reflection is part of the practice.

---

## Frequently asked questions

**Is it okay for grant applications to be AI-assisted?**

Most funders don't have explicit policies on this yet, though that's changing. The more important question is whether the application accurately represents your organization and program. AI-assisted writing that is carefully reviewed, fact-checked, and edited by the person who actually runs the program is no different in substance from hiring a grant writer or working with a consultant. The expertise, the outcomes, and the accountability are still yours. Unreviewed AI output submitted without checking for accuracy is a different matter. Review what you submit.

**What if I give Claude our program information and it ends up training on it?**

Anthropic's standard terms for Claude Pro do not use your conversations to train models by default. You can verify Anthropic's current privacy policy at anthropic.com. If your organization has strict data policies, ask your leadership or legal counsel before using any cloud-based tool with organizational information.

**How do I get my team to try this?**

Start with yourself and a real task, not a demo or an experiment. When you show a colleague a grant draft that took you 45 minutes instead of half a day, that's more persuasive than any workshop. Offer to sit with one person and work through a specific document together. That's how this tends to spread.

**What if the AI output doesn't sound like us?**

It won't at first. That's expected, and it's not a failure. Your job is to edit it until it does sound like you. Over time, you'll get better at giving Claude context about your organization's voice, your funder relationships, and your community, and the drafts will need less editing. The practice builds on itself.

Your grant deadline is Friday. You have a program report due Monday. And your executive director just forwarded a major donor request asking for a personalized update by end of week. You have one full-time staff person and two part-timers.

This is the daily reality for most nonprofit professionals. More communication, more documentation, more reporting, less time. And unlike corporate roles, there’s usually no budget for consultants, no marketing department to hand things off to, and no one whose job it is to write for you.

That’s exactly where tools like Claude and ChatGPT can make a real difference, not by replacing the expertise and mission-knowledge only you have, but by eliminating the blank-page problem on tasks you’d do anyway.

Where AI actually fits in nonprofit work

Before diving into specific workflows, it helps to get clear on what AI is genuinely useful for in this context versus where your human judgment is irreplaceable.

AI does well with: first drafts, synthesizing information into readable formats, adapting the same message for multiple audiences, pulling structure from your notes, and handling repetitive writing tasks. These are exactly the tasks that eat nonprofit staff time without requiring the deep expertise you’ve spent years building.

AI does not do well with: knowing your specific community, understanding what your funders actually care about, or replacing the relationship-building that is the core of nonprofit work. A grant written entirely by AI and unreviewed by staff will almost certainly miss the nuance that wins funding.

The useful mental model here is the same one that applies across every professional role: using AI is a managerial relationship. You direct the work, give it context, evaluate the output, and push back when it misses the mark. You bring the mission expertise. Claude or ChatGPT brings the drafting capacity.

The three highest-impact use cases for nonprofit professionals

After working with professionals across roles and sectors, these three areas consistently produce the most time savings for people in nonprofit and community-focused work.

1. Grant writing (the biggest time sink, and the best fit for AI)

Grant writing is a perfect AI use case because it is largely a drafting problem. You know your program. You know your outcomes. You know your community. The hard part is translating that knowledge into the specific format a funder wants, often under deadline pressure and for multiple funders with different requirements.

AI can dramatically accelerate that translation work.

2. Donor outreach and stewardship communications

Writing personalized donor communications at scale is something most small nonprofits simply don’t have time for. A major donor portfolio of 50 people should theoretically get individualized touchpoints throughout the year. In practice, most organizations send one or two generic newsletter blasts and hope that counts as stewardship.

AI changes that calculation.

3. Impact reporting and program documentation

Board reports, annual reports, grant progress reports: these are necessary, important, and completely demoralizing to write when you’re staring at a year’s worth of program notes, spreadsheets, and anecdotes. AI can synthesize that raw material into readable narrative quickly.

Detailed walkthrough: Grant writing with Claude

This is where most nonprofit staff get the most immediate value, so let’s go through a full workflow.

What you need before you open Claude:

  • The grant guidelines and application questions (paste the actual text, not a summary of it)
  • Your program description (even rough notes are fine)
  • Any data you have on outcomes, participants, or impact
  • Any previous successful grants for this program (if you have them)

Step 1: Feed Claude the context

Start a new Claude conversation and give it everything it needs to understand your organization and program. Something like:

“I’m the program director at [Organization Name], a nonprofit that [brief description: who you serve, what you do, your geography]. I’m writing a grant application for [funder name]. Here are the grant guidelines: [paste full guidelines]. Here’s background on our program: [paste your program description or notes]. Here’s our outcome data: [paste whatever you have]. Please read all of this before we start, and let me know what you’ve understood about our work.”

That last sentence matters. Asking Claude to confirm its understanding before drafting helps you catch any misreads before you’re deep into a draft.

Step 2: Answer application questions one at a time

Resist the urge to ask Claude to “write the entire application.” Instead, take each question individually. Ask Claude to draft a response, then read it critically: does it sound like your organization? Does it address what the funder is actually asking? Is the emphasis right?

The first draft is almost never what you’d submit. That’s fine. Your job at this stage is to react, correct, and direct. “This paragraph oversells the outcomes data we have. Scale it back and be more specific about what we’ve actually measured.” That conversation is where the value is.

Step 3: Adapt, don’t start over

Once you have a strong grant response to one funder, you have a foundation. The next funder application is not a blank page. It’s an editing project. Ask Claude: “Here’s the grant response I wrote for Funder A. Now I need to adapt this for Funder B, whose guidelines are attached. Their focus is on [X] instead of [Y]. Help me revise.”

This is where the time savings compound. Your second grant application might take 40% of the time your first one took. Your third, less still.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A nonprofit professional describing how they used Claude to get a grant application done under deadline pressure, with specifics about what they gave the AI and what it helped them accomplish]

Donor outreach: Making stewardship feel personal at scale

Personalized donor communication is the gold standard of nonprofit fundraising. It is also the thing most understaffed development teams sacrifice first when time is short.

Here’s a workflow that makes individualized outreach realistic without adding hours to your week.

The “donor context card” approach

For each donor you want to steward personally, keep a simple note in a document or spreadsheet: their giving history (years of support, approximate level), any personal connection to your work (why they give, family history with the issue, past conversations they’ve mentioned), and any recent news about them you’d want to acknowledge (a promotion, a family milestone, anything you’d note if you were keeping up with a friend).

This doesn’t have to be elaborate. Even two or three sentences per donor gives Claude enough to work with.

When it’s time to write a touchpoint, open Claude and say: “I need to write a brief, personal email to a donor named [Name]. Here’s what I know about them: [paste your context card]. The purpose of this email is [thank them for a recent gift / update them on a program they care about / invite them to an event]. I want this to sound warm and genuine, not like a mail merge. Can you draft something I can edit?”

Read what comes back. Edit it until it actually sounds like you. The goal is not to send AI-written email unreviewed. The goal is to spend 10 minutes on a personal outreach instead of 45, and to send more of them.

Adapting one message for multiple audiences

Say you have a program update you want to share: a cohort just graduated, or you hit a milestone, or you have a powerful participant story (with permission to share). You need to communicate this to major donors, your email list, and your board. Three different audiences, three different tones, three different levels of detail.

Ask Claude to draft all three versions from a single brief. “I have a program update I need to share with three different audiences. Here’s the core story: [paste]. Can you draft: (1) a 2-paragraph personal email to a major donor who has funded this program, (2) a newsletter paragraph for our general email list, (3) a brief board update for our quarterly board report?”

One piece of information. Three outputs. That’s where the time savings are real.

Impact reporting: Turning raw data into readable narrative

The board report you’ve been putting off probably exists somewhere as a pile of: program notes, attendance spreadsheets, a few compelling participant stories, some outcome numbers that feel hard to contextualize, and a general sense of “we did a lot this year.”

Claude is genuinely good at helping you shape that raw material into coherent narrative.

Paste in the raw material, not a summary

The key mistake people make when using AI for reports is summarizing first, then asking for help. That loses information. Instead, paste your actual notes, your actual data, your actual stories (de-identified if needed for privacy). Let Claude work with the real material.

“Here are my program notes from this quarter: [paste]. Here’s our attendance data: [paste]. Here’s a participant story I’m allowed to share: [paste]. I need to write a 2-page program update for our board. It should cover: what we did, who we served, what we’re seeing in terms of outcomes, and one story that illustrates the impact. Please draft this in a clear, professional tone.”

You’ll still need to edit. Claude doesn’t know your board, doesn’t know what the ED has already told them, and doesn’t know which metrics they care most about. But getting from raw notes to a readable draft in 20 minutes instead of two hours is a real change.

Grant progress reports

These are the least beloved documents in the nonprofit world: required, formulaic, and usually due when you’re already slammed. Most funders have a specific format. Feed Claude the format, feed it your program data, and ask for a first draft. Then review it carefully: funders notice if the report doesn’t match what you proposed, and they notice if the outcomes you’re claiming don’t match what your contract said you’d deliver.

Claude can draft. You verify and vouch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A nonprofit professional (or someone who has done volunteer or community work) describing a specific moment where AI helped them get through a reporting or communication pile, with concrete time savings]

Tool recommendations

For most of these workflows, Claude (claude.ai) is the better choice. Claude handles long documents well, which matters when you’re pasting in grant guidelines, program notes, and outcome data all at once. It also tends to write in a more measured, professional tone by default, which suits grant writing and formal donor communications.

ChatGPT is a solid alternative and either works for the core workflows above. The paid tier ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) gives you significantly longer conversations and better reasoning, which matters when you’re working with large amounts of program context. If you’re doing this work regularly, the paid tier is worth it.

One important note: before pasting any sensitive information into an AI tool, check your organization’s data policies. Most nonprofits do not have formal AI policies yet, but you should avoid pasting personally identifiable information about participants, donor financial details, or confidential grant communications. De-identify examples when you can, and treat these tools the way you’d treat any cloud document: useful and generally fine, but not a place for truly sensitive data.

What members in community-focused roles are seeing

In our community sessions, a pattern shows up for people doing mission-driven work: the biggest benefit isn’t the individual time savings on any one task. It’s getting past what T Gaines described in one of our Wednesday demo sessions as “getting the app actually impacting the real world” — the turning point where using a tool stops feeling like an experiment and starts feeling like a real shift in how you work.

For nonprofit staff, that moment often comes when they realize they can get a complete grant draft done in one focused hour instead of spreading the dread across three days. Not because the AI wrote something perfect, but because having something to react to is so much faster than staring at a blank page.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A nonprofit professional or volunteer-focused professional sharing a specific example of how AI changed their writing process for grant applications, donor communications, or program reports]

Career angle: AI capability positions you as a force multiplier

In a sector where most organizations are chronically understaffed and under-resourced, a professional who can produce grant-quality writing, personalized donor communications, and polished program reports in a fraction of the time is genuinely hard to replace.

This is not about automating your job away. The relationship-building, community knowledge, program expertise, and mission alignment that makes nonprofit work effective cannot be automated. But the documentation and communication work that supports those relationships can be made significantly more efficient.

Being the person on your team who knows how to use these tools well, who can help a colleague get a grant application done under deadline, who can draft the impact narrative for a board presentation in a morning, is a form of professional leadership. It’s also how people become the AI advocate at their organization.

Your first week: a five-day practice plan

You don’t need a major project to start. Here’s a low-stakes entry point for your first week.

Monday: Find one piece of writing you’ve been putting off. A grant question you’re dreading, a donor email you’ve been meaning to send, a program update nobody’s asked for yet but you know you’ll need. Open Claude. Give it context. Ask for a draft. Read it and revise.

Tuesday: Take that revised draft and improve the prompt you used. What would you tell Claude differently now that you’ve seen what it does with basic context? Try again with a better setup.

Wednesday: Adapt the same content for a different audience. If Monday was a grant narrative, Wednesday is the donor newsletter paragraph from the same material. Same program story, different format and tone.

Thursday: Try a reporting task. Take your messiest pile of program notes and ask Claude to help you organize them into a readable summary. You’re not trying to produce a finished document. You’re practicing giving AI raw material and seeing what it does with it.

Friday: Reflect for ten minutes. Where did Claude actually save you time? Where did it produce something that needed heavy editing? Where did it miss the mark entirely? This reflection is part of the practice.

Frequently asked questions

Is it okay for grant applications to be AI-assisted?

Most funders don’t have explicit policies on this yet, though that’s changing. The more important question is whether the application accurately represents your organization and program. AI-assisted writing that is carefully reviewed, fact-checked, and edited by the person who actually runs the program is no different in substance from hiring a grant writer or working with a consultant. The expertise, the outcomes, and the accountability are still yours. Unreviewed AI output submitted without checking for accuracy is a different matter. Review what you submit.

What if I give Claude our program information and it ends up training on it?

Anthropic’s standard terms for Claude Pro do not use your conversations to train models by default. You can verify Anthropic’s current privacy policy at anthropic.com. If your organization has strict data policies, ask your leadership or legal counsel before using any cloud-based tool with organizational information.

How do I get my team to try this?

Start with yourself and a real task, not a demo or an experiment. When you show a colleague a grant draft that took you 45 minutes instead of half a day, that’s more persuasive than any workshop. Offer to sit with one person and work through a specific document together. That’s how this tends to spread.

What if the AI output doesn’t sound like us?

It won’t at first. That’s expected, and it’s not a failure. Your job is to edit it until it does sound like you. Over time, you’ll get better at giving Claude context about your organization’s voice, your funder relationships, and your community, and the drafts will need less editing. The practice builds on itself.

Come figure this out with others

Nonprofit professionals often navigate this work without much peer support, especially when it comes to new tools. If you want to see how other mission-driven professionals are applying Claude and ChatGPT in their day-to-day work, and to share what’s working for you, that’s exactly what happens in MVP Club’s community sessions. Come see what we’re doing at mvpclub.ai/community.

How to Use AI for Meeting Notes and Action Items
Step-by-step guide to using Claude or ChatGPT to turn meeting transcripts into clear summaries and action items in minutes.

You finish a 45-minute meeting, close the Zoom window, and immediately wonder: who is doing that thing, and by when? Somewhere in your notes is the answer, if you took notes, if you can find them, if you wrote down the right thing.

AI for meeting notes is one of the fastest-return use cases you will find. The setup is minimal. The output is immediately useful. And once you do it a few times, you will not want to run a meeting any other way.

Here is how to do it with Claude or ChatGPT, step by step.

## What you actually need (it is less than you think)

You do not need a dedicated AI meeting assistant with a bot sitting in your Zoom. Those tools are useful, but they require integrations, IT approvals sometimes, and a different kind of setup. This guide covers the version that works right now with a tool you likely already have.

What you need:

- A Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus subscription ($20/month)
- A transcript of your meeting, or your own rough notes
- About 10 minutes after the meeting ends

If your company uses Zoom, Google Meet, or Microsoft Teams, all three platforms offer built-in transcription. Turn it on before the meeting starts. After the meeting, download the transcript as a text file. That file is your raw material.

If you do not have transcription enabled, even rough notes you typed during the meeting work. The AI does not need perfection. It needs enough to work from.

## Step 1: Get your transcript into Claude or ChatGPT

Open Claude (claude.ai) or ChatGPT (chat.openai.com) and start a new conversation. Paste your transcript directly into the chat window.

If the transcript is long, do not worry. Claude in particular handles long documents well, and the paid tiers of both tools allow for much longer inputs than the free versions.

Once you have pasted the transcript, do not just ask for "a summary." That produces something generic. Instead, tell the AI exactly what you need.

## Step 2: Give it a specific prompt, not a generic one

The difference between a useful AI summary and a useless one comes down to the specificity of your prompt. Generic prompts get generic output. Specific prompts get something you can actually use.

Here are three prompts worth trying, depending on what you need:

**For a clean meeting summary:**
> "This is a transcript from a [meeting type: team standup / client call / project kickoff]. Summarize the key discussion points in 3-5 bullets. Focus on decisions made, not on who said what."

**For action items only:**
> "Read this meeting transcript and pull out every action item. For each one, list: the task, the person responsible, and the deadline mentioned. If no deadline was stated, write 'TBD.' Format this as a table."

**For a follow-up email draft:**
> "Based on this transcript, draft a follow-up email I can send to the meeting participants. Tone should be direct and professional. Include a summary paragraph and a bulleted list of next steps with owners."

Start with one of these. Then iterate. If the summary missed something important, tell Claude: "You left out the conversation about the Q3 budget. Add that in." The back-and-forth is where the real value happens.

## Step 3: Review what it gives you before sending anything

AI for meeting notes is not a send-it-without-reading workflow. The AI will occasionally attribute an action item to the wrong person, misread a name, or summarize a nuanced decision in a way that loses the nuance.

Your job is not to generate the notes. Your job is to review and correct them. That is much faster than writing them from scratch, but it still requires your eyes.

Two things to check in every AI summary:

- **Ownership:** Did the right person get assigned the right task? AI makes mistakes on this when multiple people are speaking or when someone says "we should do X" without naming who.
- **Tone:** Does the summary match what actually happened? If a decision was contested or tentative, a flat summary might make it sound settled. Add a note if needed.

The [research on AI and knowledge worker productivity](https://hbr.org/2026/02/ai-doesnt-reduce-work-it-intensifies-it) keeps pointing to the same insight: AI does not replace human judgment in professional contexts. It accelerates the lower-effort parts so you have more bandwidth for the higher-effort ones. Meeting note review is exactly that.

## Step 4: Build a reusable prompt you come back to every time

After you have done this a few times, you will notice you keep writing similar prompts. That is a signal to save a template.

Here is a starting template you can adapt:

> "This is a transcript from [meeting type] on [date] with [attendee list or roles]. Please do three things:
> 1. Write a 3-sentence summary of what was decided.
> 2. List every action item as a table with columns: Task | Owner | Due Date.
> 3. Draft a follow-up email I can send to all attendees.
>
> Tone: professional but conversational. Do not include any small talk or off-topic discussion. If a deadline was not mentioned, write 'TBD' in the Due Date column."

Save this somewhere easy to reach. Over time, you will customize it for your specific context: your team's communication style, the types of meetings you run most often, the format your manager expects for summaries.

This is what Jill Ozovek, co-founder of MVP Club, calls the "meeting extraction skill": a documented workflow you can invoke the same way every time. In an MVP Club session, Jill walked through exactly this kind of setup for her consulting work, where she processes client call transcripts into structured reports. Her workflow: paste the transcript, run the skill, get a report in a consistent format she can share immediately. "Here's a meeting transcript," she said. "Let's do the report and write the follow-up email." One prompt. Both outputs. Done.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing how AI meeting notes changed a specific post-meeting routine, with concrete time savings]

## Step 5: Handle the "I don't have a transcript" problem

Not every meeting produces a clean transcript. In-person meetings, off-the-record conversations, calls where transcription was not turned on: these all leave you with whatever you wrote down in the moment.

Good news: rough notes work too.

If you have handwritten notes or a messy list of points you jotted down, paste those in instead. Tell Claude: "These are rough notes from a meeting I just had. Please organize them into: (1) key decisions, (2) action items with owners and deadlines, (3) any open questions we need to follow up on."

You will get something back that is more organized and usable than what you put in, even if it is not perfect. From there, you edit.

The same applies if you had a meeting where you were too engaged to take notes at all. Right after the meeting, spend 3 minutes writing down everything you remember, then paste that into Claude. Your recall right after the meeting is much sharper than it will be in two hours.

## Common mistakes to avoid

**Trusting it completely on names and ownership.** AI will sometimes assign an action item to the wrong person, especially in longer transcripts with multiple speakers. Always verify who owns what before sharing the summary.

**Asking for a summary when you need a decision log.** A summary tells people what was discussed. A decision log tells people what was decided and who owns the follow-through. Know which one you need before you prompt.

**Waiting too long to do it.** The ideal time to process your meeting notes with AI is within 30 minutes of the meeting ending. The transcript is right there, the context is fresh, and you can catch any AI errors because you were in the room. Letting transcripts pile up for three days means more cognitive load to review the output.

**Skipping the human check before sharing.** According to [Otter.ai's research on meeting productivity](https://otter.ai/), the biggest gains from AI meeting tools come when professionals treat the AI output as a first draft, not a finished product. The correction step is what makes the final output trustworthy.

## From the practice: what we see in coaching

The people who get the most from AI meeting notes are not the ones with the most sophisticated setup. They are the ones who do it consistently, even imperfectly.

A simple habit that works: at the end of every meeting, give yourself a 10-minute buffer before your next call. Paste the transcript into Claude, run your prompt, and send the summary to attendees right away. The immediacy matters. Summaries shared within an hour of a meeting get read. Summaries shared the next day get skimmed.

For professionals who run multiple meetings a day, this habit compounds quickly. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after from someone who shifted to AI meeting summaries, with concrete details about the time and how their follow-through changed]

The bigger shift is what happens to your attention during the meeting itself. When you know you will have AI help processing the transcript afterward, you stop trying to capture everything in real time. You can actually listen. That changes the quality of the meeting, not just the quality of the notes.

## Where to go from here

If you want to take this further, read [how to get better results from AI at work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work), which covers the broader framework for using Claude and ChatGPT as a daily work tool. If meeting notes are something you handle on behalf of an executive, the guide on [AI for executive assistants](/learn/ai-for-executive-assistants) has a section specifically on meeting prep and post-meeting workflows.

The best version of this skill is the one you actually use. Start with one meeting this week. Paste the transcript. Send the notes. See what happens.

Professionals who are getting better at this faster are not doing it alone. They are watching how other people run their AI workflows, sharing what works, and building on each other's templates. That is what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions. If that sounds useful, you are welcome to [come see for yourself](/community).

After you have done this a few times, you will notice you keep writing similar prompts. That is a signal to save a template.

Here is a starting template you can adapt:

“This is a transcript from [meeting type] on [date] with [attendee list or roles]. Please do three things:

  1. Write a 3-sentence summary of what was decided.
  2. List every action item as a table with columns: Task | Owner | Due Date.
  3. Draft a follow-up email I can send to all attendees.

Tone: professional but conversational. Do not include any small talk or off-topic discussion. If a deadline was not mentioned, write ‘TBD’ in the Due Date column.”

Save this somewhere easy to reach. Over time, you will customize it for your specific context: your team’s communication style, the types of meetings you run most often, the format your manager expects for summaries.

This is what Jill Ozovek, co-founder of MVP Club, calls the “meeting extraction skill”: a documented workflow you can invoke the same way every time. In an MVP Club session, Jill walked through exactly this kind of setup for her consulting work, where she processes client call transcripts into structured reports. Her workflow: paste the transcript, run the skill, get a report in a consistent format she can share immediately. “Here’s a meeting transcript,” she said. “Let’s do the report and write the follow-up email.” One prompt. Both outputs. Done.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing how AI meeting notes changed a specific post-meeting routine, with concrete time savings]

Step 5: Handle the “I don’t have a transcript” problem

Not every meeting produces a clean transcript. In-person meetings, off-the-record conversations, calls where transcription was not turned on: these all leave you with whatever you wrote down in the moment.

Good news: rough notes work too.

If you have handwritten notes or a messy list of points you jotted down, paste those in instead. Tell Claude: “These are rough notes from a meeting I just had. Please organize them into: (1) key decisions, (2) action items with owners and deadlines, (3) any open questions we need to follow up on.”

You will get something back that is more organized and usable than what you put in, even if it is not perfect. From there, you edit.

The same applies if you had a meeting where you were too engaged to take notes at all. Right after the meeting, spend 3 minutes writing down everything you remember, then paste that into Claude. Your recall right after the meeting is much sharper than it will be in two hours.

Common mistakes to avoid

Trusting it completely on names and ownership. AI will sometimes assign an action item to the wrong person, especially in longer transcripts with multiple speakers. Always verify who owns what before sharing the summary.

Asking for a summary when you need a decision log. A summary tells people what was discussed. A decision log tells people what was decided and who owns the follow-through. Know which one you need before you prompt.

Waiting too long to do it. The ideal time to process your meeting notes with AI is within 30 minutes of the meeting ending. The transcript is right there, the context is fresh, and you can catch any AI errors because you were in the room. Letting transcripts pile up for three days means more cognitive load to review the output.

Skipping the human check before sharing. According to Otter.ai’s research on meeting productivity, the biggest gains from AI meeting tools come when professionals treat the AI output as a first draft, not a finished product. The correction step is what makes the final output trustworthy.

From the practice: what we see in coaching

The people who get the most from AI meeting notes are not the ones with the most sophisticated setup. They are the ones who do it consistently, even imperfectly.

A simple habit that works: at the end of every meeting, give yourself a 10-minute buffer before your next call. Paste the transcript into Claude, run your prompt, and send the summary to attendees right away. The immediacy matters. Summaries shared within an hour of a meeting get read. Summaries shared the next day get skimmed.

For professionals who run multiple meetings a day, this habit compounds quickly. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after from someone who shifted to AI meeting summaries, with concrete details about the time and how their follow-through changed]

The bigger shift is what happens to your attention during the meeting itself. When you know you will have AI help processing the transcript afterward, you stop trying to capture everything in real time. You can actually listen. That changes the quality of the meeting, not just the quality of the notes.

Where to go from here

If you want to take this further, read how to get better results from AI at work, which covers the broader framework for using Claude and ChatGPT as a daily work tool. If meeting notes are something you handle on behalf of an executive, the guide on AI for executive assistants has a section specifically on meeting prep and post-meeting workflows.

The best version of this skill is the one you actually use. Start with one meeting this week. Paste the transcript. Send the notes. See what happens.

Professionals who are getting better at this faster are not doing it alone. They are watching how other people run their AI workflows, sharing what works, and building on each other’s templates. That is what happens in MVP Club’s weekly sessions. If that sounds useful, you are welcome to come see for yourself.

Step 1: Get your transcript into Claude or ChatGPT
Step 2: Give it a specific prompt, not a generic one
Step 3: Review what it gives you before sending anything
Step 4: Build a reusable prompt you come back to every time
