AI for People Managers: Performance Reviews, 1:1 Prep, and Team Communication
How people managers are using Claude and ChatGPT for performance reviews, 1:1 prep, and team communication. Real use cases, prompts, and a 5-day plan.

Your performance review cycle is three weeks out. You have 12 direct reports. You haven't looked at your notes from anyone's last check-in since January. And your calendar has 14 hours of back-to-back meetings between now and the deadline.

This is exactly the kind of situation where AI for people managers stops being a productivity experiment and starts being a genuine lifeline.

People managers carry a specific kind of cognitive load that most AI advice completely ignores. You're not just managing your own work. You're tracking the progress, moods, goals, blockers, and development arcs of multiple people at once, while also writing your own deliverables, attending every meeting, and somehow being "present" in your 1:1s instead of distracted. That's an enormous amount to hold.

## Where AI actually helps for people managers

There are three areas where managers in our community have found the most consistent value. Performance review writing. 1:1 preparation. And team communication.

None of these are about AI replacing your judgment. You're the one who knows your team. You're the one who notices that Sarah seemed off in last Thursday's call, or that Marcus has been killing it on a project nobody else has noticed. What AI does is help you turn what you know into something useful, faster.

Before diving into each one, it's worth naming the mental model that makes all of this work. Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club's co-founders, put it this way in a community session: "When I'm managing people, I make a ton of process documentation, because I find that managing the how work gets done is a great way to set people up for success. And that's basically what working with Claude is: you're managing the how you want it to do things."

That framing changes how you prompt. You're not asking AI to write something for you. You're managing it the way you'd manage a capable new team member who doesn't know your context yet.

## Use Case 1: Performance reviews (the highest-impact place to start)

Performance reviews are the most universally painful part of people management. Not because managers don't care about their teams. It's the opposite: most managers care deeply and feel the weight of getting this right. The blank page, the pressure to be fair, the fear of saying the wrong thing, the sheer volume of writing required. That's what makes reviews so hard.

Here's what actually helps.

### Before you open Claude, gather your raw material

This is the most important step, and it's the one most people skip. Claude can only work with what you give it. Before you start writing anything, spend 15-20 minutes pulling together your actual notes:

- Any 1:1 notes or check-in summaries from the review period
- The employee's self-review, if your organization uses them
- Key projects they worked on, with outcomes (specific is better: "launched the redesign in Q3, which reduced support tickets by 15%" not "worked on the redesign")
- One or two concrete examples of how they handled something difficult
- Any feedback you gave them informally throughout the year, if you can remember it

The richer your input, the better the output. If you give Claude a bullet-pointed brain dump, you'll get a usable draft. If you give it a single sentence, you'll get a generic one.

### A prompt that works

Here's a starting structure you can adapt in Claude or ChatGPT:

> I'm writing a performance review for [role title]. Here's what I know about their performance this review period:
>
> [paste your notes, bullet points, key projects, examples]
>
> The tone should be direct and specific. Please draft a performance review that:
> - Opens with an overall assessment
> - Highlights 2-3 specific strengths with concrete examples
> - Names 1-2 areas for development with specific, constructive language
> - Closes with a forward-looking statement about growth
>
> Do not use corporate jargon. Write as if a thoughtful manager is speaking directly to their employee.

Read the draft critically. It will almost always get some things right and some things wrong. The development areas in particular tend to come out too soft or too vague. Push back: "Make the development feedback more specific. What behavior should change and why does it matter?" That conversation is faster than writing from scratch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A manager in the MVP Club community describing how using Claude for performance reviews changed their process, with a specific detail about what was different]

### A common mistake: giving AI too little context

The single most frequent thing we see in coaching sessions is managers pasting a name and a job title into Claude and expecting something usable to come out. It doesn't. And then they conclude "AI doesn't work for performance reviews."

What they've actually discovered is that AI requires the same thing good management requires: knowing your people well enough to say something specific about them. If you can't fill in your notes before writing the prompt, that's useful diagnostic information about where your 1:1 practice needs to improve, not an AI limitation.

## Use Case 2: 1:1 preparation

The best 1:1 conversations happen when both people come prepared. In practice, most managers are joining a recurring invite with a vague memory of what happened last week and a vague hope that something important will surface.

AI can help you build a better 1:1 habit in two ways: preparing before the meeting and capturing after it.

### Before the meeting

At the start of each week, spend 5-10 minutes per direct report building a context brief in Claude. You can do this for all your reports at once if you set it up right.

Here's a simple prompt format:

> I have a 1:1 tomorrow with [name], who works in [role]. Here's context on where things stand:
>
> [Paste any recent notes, project updates, things they mentioned last week, anything you've been meaning to follow up on]
>
> Help me prepare:
> 1. What are the 3 most important things to check in on?
> 2. What questions would help me understand how they're doing beyond the task list?
> 3. Is there anything in this context that suggests I should proactively address something?

The third prompt is the one that pays off most often. It surfaces things you've been unconsciously tracking but haven't named explicitly.

### After the meeting

Right after your 1:1, paste your notes into Claude and ask it to summarize the key takeaways and any commitments made. This takes about two minutes and creates a record that's genuinely useful the next week, rather than a set of cryptic bullet points you can't decode three weeks later.

This also becomes the raw material for performance reviews. If you build this habit now, your next review cycle is dramatically easier.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after showing what a manager's 1:1 notes looked like before and after using Claude to summarize them, with a specific detail about what they caught that they would have missed]

## Use Case 3: Team communication

This is the category where managers are most hesitant, for understandable reasons. Communication is the thing people most associate with the "human side" of management. It feels wrong to use AI to write something to your team.

But there's an important distinction: using AI to help you communicate more clearly and consistently is different from outsourcing your voice.

### Where it genuinely helps

**Difficult feedback.** When you need to give someone feedback that you're worried about landing wrong, write a first draft in Claude. Tell it the situation, your relationship with this person, what the behavior is, and what you want the outcome to be. Ask it to draft language that's direct without being harsh. You'll almost always edit it significantly, but having a starting point gets you past the paralysis of "how do I even say this?"

**Team updates on complex topics.** When there's been an org change, a project pivot, or something ambiguous you need to communicate, Claude can help you think through what your team actually needs to know and in what order. Ask it: "I need to communicate [situation] to my team of [n] people. What are the key questions they're likely to have? Help me draft an update that addresses those."

**Recognition messages.** It sounds counterintuitive, but specific and genuine recognition is hard to write well. You know what someone did was great, but putting it into words in a way that actually lands can be surprisingly difficult. Give Claude the specifics and ask it to help you articulate why it mattered. Then make it sound like you.

### The voice check

Any time you send something to your team that started with AI, read it aloud before you send it. If it doesn't sound like how you actually talk, fix it. The goal is clarity and speed, not a communication that feels like it came from corporate HQ.

## Tool recommendations

For people management work specifically, Claude tends to be the better choice for writing tasks that require nuance and tone. Its outputs are more conversational by default, and it's better at following style instructions than ChatGPT in most direct comparisons our members have done.

For quick 1:1 prep when you have 5 minutes before a meeting, ChatGPT works just as well and is slightly faster. The $20/month cost for either Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus is worth it if you're using this regularly. The free tiers have context limits that will frustrate you with longer performance review drafts.

Don't try to use both at the same time. Pick one, learn it well for two or three weeks, then decide if you want to compare. Jumping between tools slows down the learning.

## The career angle: what this positions you for

Here's something worth naming directly. Managers who develop good AI workflows aren't just more efficient. They become the people their organizations turn to when figuring out how to integrate AI into people processes.

HR teams, learning and development teams, and senior leadership are all trying to figure out how AI fits into performance management, coaching, and team development. A people manager who has actually done this work, tried it, iterated on it, knows what works and what doesn't, is in a different conversation than a manager who's read about it.

This is one of the fastest paths to becoming known as an AI leader in your organization. You already have the use case. You don't have to go looking for it. If you're in a [people management role](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work), the work is right in front of you.

## Your first week: a 5-day plan

You don't need to overhaul everything at once. Here's a realistic starting schedule.

**Day 1: Performance review draft for one person (45 minutes)**
Pick one direct report. Pull together your notes. Run through the prompt format above. Read the draft, edit it, and notice what Claude got right and what it missed. Don't use this draft yet. Use it to understand how the tool thinks.

**Day 2: 1:1 prep for two people (20 minutes)**
Before two of your 1:1s, try the preparation prompt. See if the questions it surfaces are ones you wouldn't have thought of yourself.

**Day 3: 1:1 capture for one meeting (10 minutes)**
After one 1:1, paste your notes and ask for a summary. See if it catches anything you'd already forgotten.

**Day 4: Draft a difficult message (30 minutes)**
Think of a piece of communication you've been putting off. A tricky feedback conversation, a team update on something ambiguous. Draft it in Claude. Edit it heavily. Send it if it's ready, or save it as a template.

**Day 5: Reflect on the week (15 minutes)**
What saved you time? What felt off? What would you change about how you're prompting? Write down three observations. This reflection is what separates people who build a real practice from people who try it once and move on.

## Frequently asked questions

**Is it okay to use AI to write performance reviews?**
That depends on your organization's policies, and it's worth checking. In most companies, there's nothing in the policy that prohibits it, because the policies were written before this was a practical question. The relevant question is whether the output is accurate and yours. If you're using Claude to help articulate what you already know about someone, and you're reviewing and editing the output carefully, the review reflects your judgment. If you're making up context and letting AI fill in the blanks, that's a different situation, and a problem with or without AI involved.

**What if I don't have good notes from the review period?**
Start building them now. Even if you're two weeks from a deadline, a quick brain dump of what you remember, organized by person, is better than nothing. For next cycle, the 1:1 capture habit described above is the most reliable way to have good material when you need it.

**Will my team notice that I'm using AI?**
If you edit the output to sound like you, probably not. If you send Claude's draft unchanged, they might. The goal is to use AI to think more clearly and communicate more effectively, not to outsource the human parts of management. Those parts are still yours.

**What if Claude writes something I disagree with?**
Push back. That's the core skill. Tell it what's wrong: "That development feedback is too vague. This person struggled with following through on commitments and I need to name that specifically." Work the conversation until you have something true. The first draft is a starting point.

## Where to take it from here

The managers who get the most out of these workflows are the ones who share what they're learning. What prompts are working. What didn't work the first three times. What surprised them about the output.

That kind of peer learning is exactly what happens in MVP Club's community sessions each week. Professionals in people management roles share what they're building and where they're stuck, and get input from people who've already worked through the same problems. If you want to see how others are putting this into practice, that's [where to find them](/community).

Day 1: Performance review draft for one person (45 minutes) Pick one direct report. Pull together your notes. Run through the prompt format above. Read the draft, edit it, and notice what Claude got right and what it missed. Don’t use this draft yet. Use it to understand how the tool thinks.

Day 2: 1:1 prep for two people (20 minutes) Before two of your 1:1s, try the preparation prompt. See if the questions it surfaces are ones you wouldn’t have thought of yourself.

Day 3: 1:1 capture for one meeting (10 minutes) After one 1:1, paste your notes and ask for a summary. See if it catches anything you’d already forgotten.

Day 4: Draft a difficult message (30 minutes) Think of a piece of communication you’ve been putting off. A tricky feedback conversation, a team update on something ambiguous. Draft it in Claude. Edit it heavily. Send it if it’s ready, or save it as a template.

Day 5: Reflect on the week (15 minutes) What saved you time? What felt off? What would you change about how you’re prompting? Write down three observations. This reflection is what separates people who build a real practice from people who try it once and move on.

Frequently asked questions

Is it okay to use AI to write performance reviews? That depends on your organization’s policies, and it’s worth checking. In most companies, there’s nothing in the policy that prohibits it, because the policies were written before this was a practical question. The relevant question is whether the output is accurate and yours. If you’re using Claude to help articulate what you already know about someone, and you’re reviewing and editing the output carefully, the review reflects your judgment. If you’re making up context and letting AI fill in the blanks, that’s a different situation, and a problem with or without AI involved.

What if I don’t have good notes from the review period? Start building them now. Even if you’re two weeks from a deadline, a quick brain dump of what you remember, organized by person, is better than nothing. For next cycle, the 1:1 capture habit described above is the most reliable way to have good material when you need it.

Will my team notice that I’m using AI? If you edit the output to sound like you, probably not. If you send Claude’s draft unchanged, they might. The goal is to use AI to think more clearly and communicate more effectively, not to outsource the human parts of management. Those parts are still yours.

What if Claude writes something I disagree with? Push back. That’s the core skill. Tell it what’s wrong: “That development feedback is too vague. This person struggled with following through on commitments and I need to name that specifically.” Work the conversation until you have something true. The first draft is a starting point.

Where to take it from here

The managers who get the most out of these workflows are the ones who share what they’re learning. What prompts are working. What didn’t work the first three times. What surprised them about the output.

That kind of peer learning is exactly what happens in MVP Club’s community sessions each week. Professionals in people management roles share what they’re building and where they’re stuck, and get input from people who’ve already worked through the same problems. If you want to see how others are putting this into practice, that’s where to find them.

AI for Presentations: Creating Decks in Minutes, Not Hours
How to use Claude, ChatGPT, and Gamma to build presentation decks faster. A practical workflow for professionals who hate making slides.

One MVP Club member spent four hours building a twelve-slide deck for a leadership update. She knew what she wanted to say. The slides took four hours anyway. The next week, she tried it with Claude. The whole thing was done in forty minutes, and her director said it was the clearest update she'd given all year.

That gap is real. AI for presentations is one of the highest-return places to start if you haven't already, and the workflow is simpler than most people expect.

## Why presentations take so long (and where AI helps most)

The actual time sink in most presentation builds is not design. It is the structural thinking: figuring out what to say, in what order, to make a specific point land for a specific audience. That is also exactly where AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT are most useful.

Most people open PowerPoint or Google Slides and start building slides before they have a clear story. The result is a deck that looks like notes, not a narrative. AI reverses the order. You build the argument first, in plain language, and then translate it into slides. That shift alone saves an hour on a typical presentation.

## The two-step workflow that actually works

### Step 1: Get the structure right with Claude or ChatGPT

Before you touch a slide deck, open Claude or ChatGPT and describe what you need. Give it context: who the audience is, what decision or reaction you want from them, what you already know, and how long you have to present.

A prompt that works well:

> "I'm presenting to my leadership team on Tuesday. The topic is our Q2 marketing results. The audience already knows the basics. I want to walk out with approval for two new campaigns. I have 15 minutes. Help me outline a deck that makes a clear case for the campaigns without overwhelming them with data. Then give me a talking-points version for each slide."

Claude will draft a slide-by-slide outline with suggested headings and supporting points. It takes about ninety seconds. What you get back is a skeleton you can disagree with, reshape, and make your own. That is the part most people skip: the AI gives you something to react to, which is much faster than starting from nothing.

Paste the outline back and ask Claude to tighten it, or flag the slides where you want to lead with data versus story. Treat it like a fast back-and-forth with a capable colleague who has no ego about being wrong.

### Step 2: Build the deck (with or without a dedicated AI tool)

Once you have the structure, you have two paths.

**Option A: Use Claude or ChatGPT to draft slide-level content, then paste into your preferred tool.** This works in any slide software. You write the deck content in the AI conversation, slide by slide, and then manually assemble in PowerPoint, Google Slides, or Keynote. It takes longer than Option B but gives you full control over templates and formatting.

**Option B: Use a dedicated AI presentation tool.** Gamma (gamma.app) is the most widely used option right now, with over 70 million users as of 2026. You give Gamma a prompt or paste in your Claude-drafted outline, and it generates a complete deck with layout choices, design consistency, and images in under a minute. The result is not always final, but it is a strong starting point that would have taken a designer an hour to mock up.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member experience using Gamma for first time, what surprised them about the output quality]

Gamma lets you export to PowerPoint or Google Slides if you need to work in your company's template afterward. Beautiful.ai works similarly and is often preferred by teams that need consistent brand styling across decks. Neither tool replaces your judgment about what to say. They just handle the layout and visual structure so you can stay focused on the argument.

## A practical example: the status update deck

Say you have a standing monthly update to deliver. You give stakeholders project status, flag blockers, and share what is coming next. This type of deck takes most people forty-five minutes to an hour to put together, mostly because you have to pull from notes, emails, and your own memory to reconstruct what happened.

Here is a faster version using Claude:

1. Open Claude and paste in your raw notes from the month (meeting notes, Slack messages, status emails, anything you have).
2. Ask: "Based on these notes, create a status update deck outline. Audience is senior leaders who care about progress, blockers, and what help I need from them. Six slides maximum."
3. Review what comes back, add anything missing, and adjust the framing.
4. Paste the final outline into Gamma (or back to Claude for slide text) and build the deck.

The whole process runs twenty to thirty minutes instead of an hour. You spend your time reviewing and adjusting, not assembling from scratch.

This connects to the broader idea behind [how to use AI at work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work): the highest-return habit is feeding AI your raw material and letting it do the assembly. Your judgment stays essential. The mechanical parts get handled.

## Common mistakes people make the first time

**Prompting without context.** "Write me a presentation about our Q2 results" gets you a generic deck. "Write me a ten-minute presentation for a CFO audience who needs to understand why we missed our lead target and what we're changing in Q3" gets you something actually usable. The more specific you are, the less editing you do.

**Accepting the first output without questioning it.** Claude's first draft is a starting point, not a final answer. Read the structure critically. If the slide order does not match the story you need to tell, push back. Ask Claude to reorder, reframe, or cut slides. The conversation is the work.

**Skipping the outline step and going straight to design.** AI design tools are fast but they are not magic. If you paste a vague prompt into Gamma without a solid outline, you get a fast mediocre deck instead of a slow mediocre deck. Spend five minutes on structure first. It changes everything about the output.

**Using it only for big decks.** AI for presentations is just as useful for a three-slide summary you drop in Slack or a quick talking-point list before a hallway meeting. The habit builds fastest when you use it on small things, not just major deliverables.

## From the Practice

In MVP Club's weekly sessions, presentations come up constantly as a use case where people see immediate results. The pattern that works best: bring the AI into the narrative stage, before any slide software opens. Members who skip directly to Gamma or PowerPoint with only a vague topic tend to get a deck that looks polished but needs a full restructure. Members who spend ten minutes with Claude on the story first, then generate visuals, consistently get decks that require less revision.

One pattern that has shown up repeatedly: using Claude to write the "So what?" sentence for each slide (the one-sentence takeaway you would say out loud for each section), and then building the slide content backward from that. It forces clarity that improves both the deck and the live delivery.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: specific deck topic and the "so what" sentences Claude generated, with before/after comparison]

Anthropic has also published [best practices for using Claude with PowerPoint](https://www.anthropic.com/webinars/best-practices-for-claude-in-excel-and-powerpoint), which covers setting up templates and giving Claude brand guidelines upfront so its output fits your company's formats rather than requiring cleanup.

## What this practice actually builds

Getting good at AI for presentations is not just about saving forty minutes. It changes how you think about presentations. When building a deck takes four hours, you avoid making them unless you have to. When it takes forty minutes, you make them more often. You pitch ideas you would have left in email. You share updates that would have stayed in your head.

[Project managers](/learn/ai-for-project-managers) in particular report that faster deck-building changes how often they communicate upward. The threshold for "worth presenting" drops. That turns out to matter a lot for career visibility.

The skill that compounds fastest is learning to write a clear, specific prompt. If you can describe your audience, their existing knowledge, the decision you want them to make, and your time constraint in two sentences, Claude or ChatGPT will give you something genuinely useful in about ninety seconds. That prompt discipline transfers to every other AI task you pick up.

## Practicing alongside others makes it click faster

The professionals who get fluent with AI presentations quickly tend to be the ones comparing notes with peers. What prompts are other people using? What does a thirty-minute deck build actually look like in someone else's workflow? What did they try that did not work?

That is a lot of what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions. If you want to see how other professionals are working through this and share what is working for you, [the community is a good place to do that](/community).

AI for Project Managers: Planning, Risk Tracking, and Status Reporting
How AI for project managers actually works in practice: planning, risk tracking, and status reports that don't take your whole Friday afternoon.

The status report that used to take a project manager ninety minutes on Friday afternoon now takes fifteen. Not because the work is different, but because the assembly of it is.

If you manage projects, you already have the hardest part: the judgment, the context, the relationships, the institutional knowledge no tool can replicate. What Claude and ChatGPT can do is take the raw material of your week and help you turn it into something your stakeholders can actually read. That's a real return on a small investment of time.

This guide covers the three highest-impact places AI for project managers shows up: planning, risk tracking, and status reporting. You don't need to automate your whole workflow. Start with one of these and see what happens.

## Where AI for project managers actually makes a difference

Before we get into specifics, let's be clear about what AI does well here and what it doesn't.

AI is excellent at synthesis and drafting. It's a strong thinking partner when you describe a problem clearly. It's genuinely useful for turning your rough notes into polished communication. It's not good at knowing your project's politics, your stakeholders' real priorities, or why that one dependency is riskier than it looks on paper. Your judgment stays essential.

Think of it the way you think about a capable new team member who can write fast and read documents thoroughly, but needs you to tell them what matters. That's the relationship. You bring the context. Claude or ChatGPT does the assembly.

## The three highest-impact use cases

### 1. Status reporting: from raw notes to polished update in minutes

This is the quickest win for most project managers, and it's where the time savings are most obvious.

Here's what the workflow looks like. Instead of staring at a blank email on Friday afternoon and trying to reconstruct your week from memory, you keep rough notes during the week. Daily bullet points. Meeting notes you jot in a doc. Slack messages you paste in. A running list of blockers and wins. Nothing polished.

Then you paste it all into Claude with a prompt like this:

*"Here are my rough notes from this week's project updates. Please draft a status report for our executive stakeholders. Keep it to three paragraphs: what we accomplished, where we stand against plan, and what decisions we need from leadership next week. Use plain language. We're behind on the vendor integration, so flag that clearly but not alarmingly."*

The output you get back is a starting point, not a finished product. Read it, adjust the tone, add the one specific thing that only you know matters, and send it. The whole process takes fifteen minutes instead of ninety.

The critical skill here is giving good context. AI can draft status updates in the abstract, but the good ones come from real information: your actual milestones, your actual blockers, your actual stakeholders' concerns. The more specific you are in your prompt, the better the draft. [This is the same principle that applies across all professional AI use](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work): AI needs direction, not just a task.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A project manager in MVP Club sharing what their status reporting workflow looks like now compared to before using AI]

### 2. Risk tracking: thinking through what you might be missing

Risk identification is where AI works as a thinking partner rather than a drafter.

Here's a prompt worth trying. Take your current project plan (or a plain-text description of the project scope, timeline, and team), paste it into Claude, and ask:

*"I'm a project manager overseeing this project. Based on what I've described, what are the five most common types of risk that projects like this typically face? For each one, give me a quick note on what early warning signs might look like and what I should be tracking."*

What you get back is a checklist of categories you can validate against your own knowledge. Some will be obvious things you're already tracking. A few will be things you hadn't articulated. Occasionally one will surface something you genuinely hadn't considered.

You're not outsourcing your risk judgment. You're running a second pass with a well-informed thinking partner who has no stake in the outcome and no political reason to downplay a concern.

A related use case: if you have a specific risk you're worried about but can't quite articulate, describe it to Claude in plain language and ask it to help you frame it for stakeholders. Project managers often have a gut sense that something is off before they can name it precisely. AI is good at helping you find the words.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific project scenario where a PM used Claude to surface a risk category they'd overlooked, with the prompt and what came back]

### 3. Project planning: first drafts and gap analysis

When you're building out a project plan, AI is most useful at two moments: the initial structure and the gap check.

For initial structure, describe the project to Claude in plain English. Include the scope, the rough timeline, the team size, and any known constraints. Ask it to suggest a high-level work breakdown structure, or a list of the key milestones and dependencies you should be thinking about. Don't expect this output to be your plan. Expect it to be a starting point that surfaces the things you need to think about before you build the real plan.

For the gap check, take a plan you've already drafted (even if it's just a list of milestones in a doc) and ask Claude to review it for common planning gaps. A prompt like this works well:

*"Here's the current draft of my project plan. What phases, handoffs, or dependencies does it look like I might be underestimating or missing entirely? Be specific."*

This is a quick sanity check that takes five minutes and occasionally catches a dependency you'd mentally skipped. It's not a replacement for your experience or your team's input. It's a second pair of eyes with no agenda.

One thing project managers consistently find: Claude is better at this when you give it the constraints, not just the tasks. Telling it "we have a team of four and a 10-week timeline with no flexibility" produces more useful output than just listing what you're building.

## A detailed walkthrough: the status report use case

Status reporting is the fastest win, so let's walk through it in detail.

**What you need:** A running doc or notes from your week. This doesn't have to be organized. Bullet points, meeting notes, even a few sentences about each day are enough.

**Step 1: Gather your raw material.** Before you open Claude, take three to five minutes to brain-dump your week. What was accomplished? What slipped? What decisions got made? What's still unclear? What does leadership need to decide before next week? Jot this down without worrying about format.

**Step 2: Open Claude and paste it in.** Go to [Claude](https://claude.ai). If you have a Pro subscription, you're set. If not, the free tier will work for this.

**Step 3: Give it a clear prompt with context.** Don't just paste your notes and say "write a status report." Tell Claude:
- Who the audience is (executive stakeholders, your direct manager, the delivery team)
- The format or length you need
- The tone (formal, conversational, whatever your organization expects)
- Any specific things you want highlighted or softened

A good prompt looks like: *"Here are my rough weekly notes. Please draft a status report for my project's steering committee. Three sections: accomplishments this week, current status against plan (we're running one week behind on the testing phase), and decisions needed from leadership. Keep it under 300 words. Professional but not stiff."*

**Step 4: Read it and edit it.** The draft you get back will be close but not there yet. Read it once for factual accuracy, once for tone. Add the one thing only you know (the political context, the specific stakeholder concern, the nuance that changes the read). The editing pass takes five minutes, not forty-five.

**Step 5: Build the habit.** The real value comes when you do this consistently. After a few weeks, you'll notice the quality of your updates improves because you're giving Claude better raw material. Keeping a running doc during the week becomes second nature.

Within a month, your status reports stop being a Friday afternoon chore and start being a fifteen-minute routine.

## Tool recommendations

**Claude** (claude.ai) is worth trying first for project management writing tasks. Its ability to hold long, structured context makes it good for synthesizing detailed project notes into coherent updates. The $20/month Pro plan removes the session limits that can interrupt longer workflows.

**ChatGPT** is also solid for status drafting, particularly if your organization already uses it and you want to keep your AI interactions in one place. The reasoning is comparable; use whichever you're already logging into.

For both tools: you're pasting real project information into an AI chat. Check your organization's data policies first, particularly if you're managing confidential client projects or sensitive internal data. Many organizations are developing guidance on this. When in doubt, remove specifics that shouldn't leave your organization (client names, financial figures) and work with the structure instead.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: What project managers in MVP Club are using and how they handle the data sensitivity question]

## The career angle: becoming the PM who actually communicates well

Project managers who communicate clearly under pressure stand out. This is not new. What's changed is how achievable that standard has become.

The project managers getting noticed right now are the ones whose status updates are consistently readable, whose risk logs are thoughtfully maintained, and whose planning documents actually get used. AI doesn't make you a better PM. But it removes the friction that causes even skilled project managers to let communication slide when things get busy.

When you stop dreading the status report, you start sending better ones. When risk tracking doesn't require building a spreadsheet from scratch, you actually maintain it. The work is still yours. The output just matches what you're capable of.

There's also a second-order effect worth noting. As you develop fluency with AI for your own project management work, you become someone your team and leadership look to when AI questions come up. You're the one who's actually been using the tools on real work, not just reading about them. [That positioning matters](/learn/ai-learning-path) in any organization right now.

## Your first week plan

Five days, one new thing per day. Low stakes, real work.

**Monday:** Take your notes from a recent week and run them through Claude with a status report prompt. Even if the week is over, the exercise teaches you what context Claude needs from you.

**Tuesday:** Copy your current project's scope or a project brief into Claude and ask it: "What risks am I most likely to underestimate on a project like this?" Don't take the output as gospel. Use it as a checklist to compare against what you're already tracking.

**Wednesday:** Draft your next status report with Claude's help instead of on your own. Time yourself. Note how long the editing pass takes versus the drafting time you saved.

**Thursday:** Try the gap-check prompt on your current project plan. Ask Claude to look for dependencies, handoffs, or phases that seem underspecified. Flag one thing it surfaced that you want to think more about.

**Friday:** Write three bullet points about what worked and what didn't. This isn't busywork. Knowing which use cases actually saved you time versus which felt like overhead is how you build an AI practice that sticks.

## Frequently asked questions

**Do I need a paid AI subscription for this to work?**

The free tiers of Claude and ChatGPT handle all of this. The paid tiers ($20/month) are worth it if you're doing this consistently: faster responses, longer context, and no session limits. For most project managers doing this daily, the paid tier pays for itself in the first week.

**What if my organization restricts AI tool use?**

Check your internal policies before pasting project content into any external AI tool. Many organizations are still figuring this out. In the meantime, you can practice with sanitized versions of your work: replace client names with placeholders, remove financial specifics, work with structure instead of sensitive details. The skills transfer when your organization's policies catch up.

**How much time does this realistically save?**

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: Specific time savings from a PM in the community with before/after data on status report time, risk tracking time, or planning time]

For the status reporting use case specifically, most project managers report the drafting time dropping from forty-plus minutes to ten-to-fifteen. The editing still takes time. The blank-page problem goes away.

**What should I not use AI for in project management?**

Don't rely on AI for decisions that require political judgment or relationship context it doesn't have. Don't use it to generate risk assessments you haven't reviewed and validated yourself. Don't send an AI-drafted status report without reading it carefully; it can mischaracterize tone or emphasis in ways that matter in your specific stakeholder environment.

AI is a drafting and thinking tool. The accountability is still yours.

## Practice alongside people doing the same work

Project managers in the MVP Club community share real examples of their AI workflows in weekly sessions: what's working, what they've tried that didn't, how they handle the data sensitivity question at their organizations. If you want to see what this looks like in practice, not in theory, that's where people are figuring it out.

[Come see what we're doing.](/community)

The status report that used to take a project manager ninety minutes on Friday afternoon now takes fifteen. Not because the work is different, but because the assembly of it is.

If you manage projects, you already have the hardest part: the judgment, the context, the relationships, the institutional knowledge no tool can replicate. What Claude and ChatGPT can do is take the raw material of your week and help you turn it into something your stakeholders can actually read. That’s a real return on a small investment of time.

This guide covers the three highest-impact places AI for project managers shows up: planning, risk tracking, and status reporting. You don’t need to automate your whole workflow. Start with one of these and see what happens.

Where AI for project managers actually makes a difference

Before we get into specifics, let’s be clear about what AI does well here and what it doesn’t.

AI is excellent at synthesis and drafting. It’s a strong thinking partner when you describe a problem clearly. It’s genuinely useful for turning your rough notes into polished communication. It’s not good at knowing your project’s politics, your stakeholders’ real priorities, or why that one dependency is riskier than it looks on paper. Your judgment stays essential.

Think of it the way you think about a capable new team member who can write fast and read documents thoroughly, but needs you to tell them what matters. That’s the relationship. You bring the context. Claude or ChatGPT does the assembly.

The three highest-impact use cases

1. Status reporting: from raw notes to polished update in minutes

This is the quickest win for most project managers, and it’s where the time savings are most obvious.

Here’s what the workflow looks like. Instead of staring at a blank email on Friday afternoon and trying to reconstruct your week from memory, you keep rough notes during the week. Daily bullet points. Meeting notes you jot in a doc. Slack messages you paste in. A running list of blockers and wins. Nothing polished.

Then you paste it all into Claude with a prompt like this:

“Here are my rough notes from this week’s project updates. Please draft a status report for our executive stakeholders. Keep it to three paragraphs: what we accomplished, where we stand against plan, and what decisions we need from leadership next week. Use plain language. We’re behind on the vendor integration, so flag that clearly but not alarmingly.”

The output you get back is a starting point, not a finished product. Read it, adjust the tone, add the one specific thing that only you know matters, and send it. The whole process takes fifteen minutes instead of ninety.

The critical skill here is giving good context. AI can draft status updates in the abstract, but the good ones come from real information: your actual milestones, your actual blockers, your actual stakeholders’ concerns. The more specific you are in your prompt, the better the draft. This is the same principle that applies across all professional AI use: AI needs direction, not just a task.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A project manager in MVP Club sharing what their status reporting workflow looks like now compared to before using AI]

2. Risk tracking: thinking through what you might be missing

Risk identification is where AI works as a thinking partner rather than a drafter.

Here’s a prompt worth trying. Take your current project plan (or a plain-text description of the project scope, timeline, and team), paste it into Claude, and ask:

“I’m a project manager overseeing this project. Based on what I’ve described, what are the five most common types of risk that projects like this typically face? For each one, give me a quick note on what early warning signs might look like and what I should be tracking.”

What you get back is a checklist of categories you can validate against your own knowledge. Some will be obvious things you’re already tracking. A few will be things you hadn’t articulated. Occasionally one will surface something you genuinely hadn’t considered.

You’re not outsourcing your risk judgment. You’re running a second pass with a well-informed thinking partner who has no stake in the outcome and no political reason to downplay a concern.

A related use case: if you have a specific risk you’re worried about but can’t quite articulate, describe it to Claude in plain language and ask it to help you frame it for stakeholders. Project managers often have a gut sense that something is off before they can name it precisely. AI is good at helping you find the words.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific project scenario where a PM used Claude to surface a risk category they’d overlooked, with the prompt and what came back]

3. Project planning: first drafts and gap analysis

When you’re building out a project plan, AI is most useful at two moments: the initial structure and the gap check.

For initial structure, describe the project to Claude in plain English. Include the scope, the rough timeline, the team size, and any known constraints. Ask it to suggest a high-level work breakdown structure, or a list of the key milestones and dependencies you should be thinking about. Don’t expect this output to be your plan. Expect it to be a starting point that surfaces the things you need to think about before you build the real plan.

For the gap check, take a plan you’ve already drafted (even if it’s just a list of milestones in a doc) and ask Claude to review it for common planning gaps. A prompt like this works well:

“Here’s the current draft of my project plan. What phases, handoffs, or dependencies does it look like I might be underestimating or missing entirely? Be specific.”

This is a quick sanity check that takes five minutes and occasionally catches a dependency you’d mentally skipped. It’s not a replacement for your experience or your team’s input. It’s a second pair of eyes with no agenda.

One thing project managers consistently find: Claude is better at this when you give it the constraints, not just the tasks. Telling it “we have a team of four and a 10-week timeline with no flexibility” produces more useful output than just listing what you’re building.

A detailed walkthrough: the status report use case

Status reporting is the fastest win, so let’s walk through it in detail.

What you need: A running doc or notes from your week. This doesn’t have to be organized. Bullet points, meeting notes, even a few sentences about each day are enough.

Step 1: Gather your raw material. Before you open Claude, take three to five minutes to brain-dump your week. What was accomplished? What slipped? What decisions got made? What’s still unclear? What does leadership need to decide before next week? Jot this down without worrying about format.

Step 2: Open Claude and paste it in. Go to Claude. If you have a Pro subscription, you’re set. If not, the free tier will work for this.

Step 3: Give it a clear prompt with context. Don’t just paste your notes and say “write a status report.” Tell Claude:

  • Who the audience is (executive stakeholders, your direct manager, the delivery team)
  • The format or length you need
  • The tone (formal, conversational, whatever your organization expects)
  • Any specific things you want highlighted or softened

A good prompt looks like: “Here are my rough weekly notes. Please draft a status report for my project’s steering committee. Three sections: accomplishments this week, current status against plan (we’re running one week behind on the testing phase), and decisions needed from leadership. Keep it under 300 words. Professional but not stiff.”

Step 4: Read it and edit it. The draft you get back will be close but not there yet. Read it once for factual accuracy, once for tone. Add the one thing only you know (the political context, the specific stakeholder concern, the nuance that changes the read). The editing pass takes five minutes, not forty-five.

Step 5: Build the habit. The real value comes when you do this consistently. After a few weeks, you’ll notice the quality of your updates improves because you’re giving Claude better raw material. Keeping a running doc during the week becomes second nature.

Within a month, your status reports stop being a Friday afternoon chore and start being a fifteen-minute routine.

Tool recommendations

Claude (claude.ai) is worth trying first for project management writing tasks. Its ability to hold long, structured context makes it good for synthesizing detailed project notes into coherent updates. The $20/month Pro plan removes the session limits that can interrupt longer workflows.

ChatGPT is also solid for status drafting, particularly if your organization already uses it and you want to keep your AI interactions in one place. The reasoning is comparable; use whichever you’re already logging into.

For both tools: you’re pasting real project information into an AI chat. Check your organization’s data policies first, particularly if you’re managing confidential client projects or sensitive internal data. Many organizations are developing guidance on this. When in doubt, remove specifics that shouldn’t leave your organization (client names, financial figures) and work with the structure instead.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: What project managers in MVP Club are using and how they handle the data sensitivity question]

The career angle: becoming the PM who actually communicates well

Project managers who communicate clearly under pressure stand out. This is not new. What’s changed is how achievable that standard has become.

The project managers getting noticed right now are the ones whose status updates are consistently readable, whose risk logs are thoughtfully maintained, and whose planning documents actually get used. AI doesn’t make you a better PM. But it removes the friction that causes even skilled project managers to let communication slide when things get busy.

When you stop dreading the status report, you start sending better ones. When risk tracking doesn’t require building a spreadsheet from scratch, you actually maintain it. The work is still yours. The output just matches what you’re capable of.

There’s also a second-order effect worth noting. As you develop fluency with AI for your own project management work, you become someone your team and leadership look to when AI questions come up. You’re the one who’s actually been using the tools on real work, not just reading about them. That positioning matters in any organization right now.

Your first week plan

Five days, one new thing per day. Low stakes, real work.

Monday: Take your notes from a recent week and run them through Claude with a status report prompt. Even if the week is over, the exercise teaches you what context Claude needs from you.

Tuesday: Copy your current project’s scope or a project brief into Claude and ask it: “What risks am I most likely to underestimate on a project like this?” Don’t take the output as gospel. Use it as a checklist to compare against what you’re already tracking.

Wednesday: Draft your next status report with Claude’s help instead of on your own. Time yourself. Note how long the editing pass takes versus the drafting time you saved.

Thursday: Try the gap-check prompt on your current project plan. Ask Claude to look for dependencies, handoffs, or phases that seem underspecified. Flag one thing it surfaced that you want to think more about.

Friday: Write three bullet points about what worked and what didn’t. This isn’t busywork. Knowing which use cases actually saved you time versus which felt like overhead is how you build an AI practice that sticks.

Frequently asked questions

Do I need a paid AI subscription for this to work?

The free tiers of Claude and ChatGPT handle all of this. The paid tiers ($20/month) are worth it if you’re doing this consistently: faster responses, longer context, and no session limits. For most project managers doing this daily, the paid tier pays for itself in the first week.

What if my organization restricts AI tool use?

Check your internal policies before pasting project content into any external AI tool. Many organizations are still figuring this out. In the meantime, you can practice with sanitized versions of your work: replace client names with placeholders, remove financial specifics, work with structure instead of sensitive details. The skills transfer when your organization’s policies catch up.

How much time does this realistically save?

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: Specific time savings from a PM in the community with before/after data on status report time, risk tracking time, or planning time]

For the status reporting use case specifically, most project managers report the drafting time dropping from forty-plus minutes to ten-to-fifteen. The editing still takes time. The blank-page problem goes away.

What should I not use AI for in project management?

Don’t rely on AI for decisions that require political judgment or relationship context it doesn’t have. Don’t use it to generate risk assessments you haven’t reviewed and validated yourself. Don’t send an AI-drafted status report without reading it carefully; it can mischaracterize tone or emphasis in ways that matter in your specific stakeholder environment.

AI is a drafting and thinking tool. The accountability is still yours.

Practice alongside people doing the same work

Project managers in the MVP Club community share real examples of their AI workflows in weekly sessions: what’s working, what they’ve tried that didn’t, how they handle the data sensitivity question at their organizations. If you want to see what this looks like in practice, not in theory, that’s where people are figuring it out.

AI for Sales Professionals: Prospecting, Proposals, and Follow-Up
How sales professionals are using Claude and ChatGPT to research prospects faster, write better proposals, and follow up without burning hours.

Your CRM is full of leads you haven't touched in three weeks, your pipeline review is tomorrow, and you just got a request for a proposal that needs to go out by Friday. This is Tuesday.

If that scenario feels familiar, you're in good company. Sales is one of the highest-volume, highest-repetition white-collar jobs out there: constant outreach, constant personalization, constant follow-up. And it's exactly the kind of work where AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT can buy back real hours, if you know where to apply them.

This guide walks through the three highest-impact places AI fits into a modern sales workflow: prospecting and research, proposals and presentations, and follow-up sequences. Plus a tool recommendation, a five-day starter plan, and answers to the questions we hear most from sales professionals in our community.

## Where AI Actually Fits in Sales

Before getting into specifics, it's worth naming what AI does well in a sales context and what it doesn't. This sets realistic expectations and helps you spend time where the return is real.

**Where AI earns its keep:**

- Researching a prospect and their company before an outreach call
- Drafting the first version of any written deliverable (emails, proposals, decks, follow-up notes)
- Synthesizing information from multiple sources (earnings reports, LinkedIn, news) into a coherent profile
- Generating variations on outreach copy so you're not sending the same message to 50 people
- Turning meeting notes into action items and next-step emails while the conversation is still fresh

**Where AI can't replace you:**

- Reading the room during a live call
- Building real trust and rapport with a buyer
- Knowing which objections are real versus smoke screens
- Making the judgment call on whether to push or give space
- Anything that depends on your institutional knowledge of the account

The pattern: AI handles the setup work. You do the selling. That division of labor is where the time savings actually happen.

## The Three Highest-Impact Use Cases

### 1. Prospect Research (The Quickest Win)

This is the use case that gets the fastest "oh" moment from sales professionals who try it. Pre-call research used to mean 20-30 minutes per prospect: their LinkedIn, their company website, recent news, Glassdoor for culture context, maybe an earnings call transcript if they're public. Now that work takes closer to five minutes.

The key is giving Claude enough context to do real research synthesis, not just a Google summary. A prompt that works:

> "I'm preparing for a discovery call with [Name], VP of Operations at [Company]. Before the call, I want to understand: what does the company do and who is their buyer, what recent news or announcements might affect their priorities right now, what operational challenges are common in their industry, and what kind of language do operations leaders in this space typically use when talking about their problems? I'll be selling [your product/service type]. Help me build a call prep profile."

What you get back is a starting point. You'll edit it, add what you know from the account history, and cross-reference against what's actually in your CRM. But instead of starting from scratch, you're reacting and refining. That's consistently faster.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A sales professional describing their pre-call research workflow before and after adding Claude, with specific time comparison]

### 2. Proposals and Presentations

Proposals are where sales professionals lose the most time to the blank page. Most proposal templates have a structure everyone knows, and a lot of the language is similar from deal to deal. The specific parts, the ones that actually win business, are the sections that speak directly to what this particular buyer cares about.

Claude and ChatGPT are genuinely useful here, but only if you give them the right inputs. The common mistake is pasting in your generic proposal template and asking AI to fill it in. That produces generic output. What works better:

1. Start with your discovery call notes (or a rough dump of what you know about the buyer's situation)
2. Add context about their stated priorities and any objections they raised
3. Specify the sections you need
4. Ask Claude to draft each section with the buyer's language and priorities woven in

The output will still need your editing. Proposal language often needs to match your company's voice, hit specific terms from the RFP if there is one, and reflect the nuances of your relationship with the buyer. But a first draft that reflects the buyer's actual situation beats a blank page by an order of magnitude.

For slide-based proposals, the same principle applies. Give Claude a structure and the key points you want to make per slide. Ask it to write slide headlines as complete sentences (not fragments), with one-paragraph speaker notes. Then import the structure into PowerPoint or Google Slides and build from there.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A sales professional describing a specific proposal they built faster with AI, with details on the time saved and what the editing process looked like]

### 3. Follow-Up Sequences

The research on this is consistent: most deals require five to eight touches before a buyer moves forward, and most sales reps give up after two. The gap isn't motivation. It's time and creative energy. Writing the fourth follow-up email after someone has gone quiet is genuinely hard, and most salespeople either send something generic or don't send anything at all.

This is where AI makes a structural difference. You can give Claude the context of a stalled deal and ask it to generate a sequence of three to five follow-up emails, each with a different angle. It might come back with: a value reminder, a relevant case study hook, a check-in that leads with their specific situation, a low-friction "just closing the loop" note, and a soft break-up email.

You review, pick the ones that fit, adjust the tone so it sounds like you, and schedule them. What used to take 45 minutes of dreaded blank-page time now takes about 15 minutes. And critically, you're more likely to actually send them.

A prompt that works for generating follow-up sequences:

> "I'm following up with a prospect who went quiet after our second call. Here's what I know about their situation: [context]. Here's where we left things: [last touchpoint]. Help me write a sequence of four follow-up emails, each taking a different angle. Keep them short (under 100 words), direct, and low-pressure. They should sound like a peer, not a salesperson."

## Tool Recommendations: Start Here

For sales work, **Claude** is the better starting point for most use cases. A few reasons:

Claude's output tends to read more naturally, with less of the corporate-formal register that makes email outreach feel generic. It handles longer documents well, which matters when you're giving it a prospect's annual report or a 20-page RFP to synthesize. And its instruction-following is strong enough that if you tell it to keep an email under 80 words and avoid the word "excited," it actually does.

**ChatGPT** is also solid, particularly if you're already using it for other work and don't want to switch tools mid-day. The quality difference for most sales tasks is not significant enough to change workflows.

The paid tier ($20/month for either) is worth it for sales use. The free versions hit rate limits at the worst times, and the reasoning quality is noticeably better on the paid plans. Think of it as the same cost as one lunch with a prospect.

One practical note: do not paste confidential prospect information or internal deal data into any AI tool without checking your company's data policy first. Many companies have guidance on what can and can't go into external AI tools.

## What Sales Professionals in Our Community Are Building

The members of MVP Club in sales roles have found that the learning curve is short. Most people have a workflow that's saving them real time within the first week. A few patterns we're seeing:

**The "meeting brain dump" workflow:** Right after a call, dictate or type a rough dump of what happened into Claude. Ask it to extract: key pain points mentioned, objections raised, next steps agreed to, and a draft follow-up email to send within the hour. The whole thing takes five minutes, and the email lands while the conversation is still fresh.

**The personalized outreach template library:** Instead of sending the same email to different prospects, build five to six outreach frames in Claude that vary by buyer persona or industry. Then for each new prospect, give Claude the frame and the specific company context and ask it to personalize it. You get consistent structure with genuine personalization, faster than doing it from scratch each time.

**The RFP first-pass:** When a long RFP lands, paste relevant sections into Claude and ask it to flag: what they're actually asking for, what language from the RFP you should use in your response, and what they seem to value most based on how they've weighted sections. Use that analysis to prioritize where to spend your proposal-writing energy.

> "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here's how I want you to do it. Just go." — Matt Hastings, MVP Club

That principle applies directly to sales workflows. Your best outreach email ever, your most effective follow-up sequence, the way you structure a discovery debrief: document those patterns and give them to Claude as context. You turn a generic tool into one that knows how you work.

## The Career Angle: Why This Matters Beyond Productivity

Getting good at AI-assisted sales work positions you differently inside a team or an organization. Not because you're "doing AI," but because you're doing more, doing it faster, and doing it with less visible strain.

The sales professionals who figure this out early tend to show up to pipeline reviews with better-researched accounts, send more proposals, follow up more consistently, and still have energy at the end of the week. Those patterns are visible to managers and to clients.

If you're in a quota-carrying role, AI capability can translate directly to more pipeline activity and, over time, more closed business. If you're in a relationship-selling role where the pressure is on deepening a few high-value accounts, it buys back the hours you'd spend on administrative work and lets you put more time into the calls and conversations that actually move things forward.

The [AI learning path](/learn/ai-learning-path) we recommend starts with your actual work, not with AI concepts. For sales professionals, that means picking one of the three use cases above, trying it on a real deal, and seeing what happens. You're not transforming your process overnight. You're adding one tool to one step and finding out if it saves you time.

## Your First Week With AI in Sales (5-Day Micro-Practice)

If you want a structured on-ramp, here's a five-day plan that builds the habit without overhauling your workflow.

**Day 1 (Monday): Research a prospect you already know**

Pick a prospect you're already working. Before your next touchpoint, run them through Claude using the prospect research prompt above. Compare what Claude gives you to what you already knew. Where was it useful? Where did it miss things only you would know? This calibration exercise helps you understand the tool's limits from the start.

**Day 2 (Tuesday): Turn your last meeting notes into a follow-up**

Find your notes from a recent call. Paste the rough version into Claude and ask it to extract: three key points the buyer made, any implied objections or concerns, and a draft follow-up email to send within 24 hours. Edit until it sounds like you, then send it. Note the time it took compared to writing from scratch.

**Day 3 (Wednesday): Write a cold outreach variation**

Take one of your current outreach emails and give it to Claude with this prompt: "This is my current outreach email. I want you to write three variations, each with a different angle: one leading with the business problem, one leading with a result we've helped similar companies get, and one that's shorter and more direct. Keep all three under 100 words." Pick the one you'd actually send and test it.

**Day 4 (Thursday): Draft a follow-up for a stalled deal**

Go into your pipeline and find a deal that's been stuck for two weeks or more. Give Claude the context and ask for a four-email follow-up sequence with different angles. You won't use all four, but having options removes the blank-page paralysis that usually kills follow-up consistency.

**Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and pick one workflow to keep**

Look at the week. Which of the four use cases saved you the most time or produced the most useful output? That's the one to build into your standard process next week. Don't try to adopt all four at once. One well-executed workflow change compounds over time. Four half-adopted ones don't.

## Common Questions from Sales Professionals

**Will my prospects be able to tell that AI wrote my emails?**

If you send what Claude gives you without editing, possibly. AI output has patterns that some people notice. The fix is simple: read everything out loud before you send it. If it doesn't sound like how you talk, edit it until it does. AI gives you a draft. You make it yours.

**I'm in enterprise sales with long cycles. Is this still relevant?**

Yes, maybe more so. Long-cycle sales involve more written communication (proposal documents, executive summaries, business cases), longer periods of follow-up, and more complex prospect research. All of those benefit from AI assistance. The personalization and human judgment still come from you. AI just handles the first draft faster.

**My company has restrictions on using external AI tools. What should I do?**

Check your policy before you start, not after. Many companies are developing guidance on acceptable AI use, and some have enterprise agreements with specific tools. If your company has Microsoft Copilot through an enterprise license, that may be the appropriate tool for work that touches client data. For research and writing that doesn't involve confidential information, external tools are usually fine, but verify first.

**What if I try this and the output is bad?**

It will be, sometimes. That's normal. The output quality is directly related to the quality of the context you give it. [Understanding what AI can and can't do](/learn/what-ai-can-and-cant-do) helps set the right expectations. When the output is bad, don't write off the tool. Write a better prompt, add more context, and try again. The iteration skill is more important than the prompting skill.

## Practice Alongside Other Professionals

Sales can be isolating in a specific way: you're competing internally for pipeline and externally for buyers, which doesn't leave a lot of room for sharing what's actually working. That's part of why figuring out AI workflows on your own takes so much longer than it needs to.

The sales professionals in [MVP Club](/community) share what's working in weekly sessions: the prompts that landed, the workflows that saved real time, the approaches that flopped. If you want to build these habits faster and alongside peers who are navigating the same questions, that's the environment we've built.
