Your CRM is full of leads you haven’t touched in three weeks, your pipeline review is tomorrow, and you just got a request for a proposal that needs to go out by Friday. This is Tuesday.

If that scenario feels familiar, you’re in good company. Sales is one of the highest-volume, highest-repetition white-collar jobs out there: constant outreach, constant personalization, constant follow-up. And it’s exactly the kind of work where AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT can buy back real hours, if you know where to apply them.

This guide walks through the three highest-impact places AI fits into a modern sales workflow: prospecting and research, proposals and presentations, and follow-up sequences. Plus a tool recommendation, a five-day starter plan, and answers to the questions we hear most from sales professionals in our community.

Where AI Actually Fits in Sales

Before getting into specifics, it’s worth naming what AI does well in a sales context and what it doesn’t. This sets realistic expectations and helps you spend time where the return is real.

Where AI earns its keep:

  • Researching a prospect and their company before an outreach call
  • Drafting the first version of any written deliverable (emails, proposals, decks, follow-up notes)
  • Synthesizing information from multiple sources (earnings reports, LinkedIn, news) into a coherent profile
  • Generating variations on outreach copy so you’re not sending the same message to 50 people
  • Turning meeting notes into action items and next-step emails while the conversation is still fresh

Where AI can’t replace you:

  • Reading the room during a live call
  • Building real trust and rapport with a buyer
  • Knowing which objections are real versus smoke screens
  • Making the judgment call on whether to push or give space
  • Anything that depends on your institutional knowledge of the account

The pattern: AI handles the setup work. You do the selling. That division of labor is where the time savings actually happen.

The Three Highest-Impact Use Cases

1. Prospect Research (The Quickest Win)

This is the use case that gets the fastest “oh” moment from sales professionals who try it. Pre-call research used to mean 20-30 minutes per prospect: their LinkedIn, their company website, recent news, Glassdoor for culture context, maybe an earnings call transcript if they’re public. Now that work takes closer to five minutes.

The key is giving Claude enough context to do real research synthesis, not just a Google summary. A prompt that works:

“I’m preparing for a discovery call with [Name], VP of Operations at [Company]. Before the call, I want to understand: what does the company do and who is their buyer, what recent news or announcements might affect their priorities right now, what operational challenges are common in their industry, and what kind of language do operations leaders in this space typically use when talking about their problems? I’ll be selling [your product/service type]. Help me build a call prep profile.”

What you get back is a starting point. You’ll edit it, add what you know from the account history, and cross-reference against what’s actually in your CRM. But instead of starting from scratch, you’re reacting and refining. That’s consistently faster.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A sales professional describing their pre-call research workflow before and after adding Claude, with specific time comparison]

2. Proposals and Presentations

Proposals are where sales professionals lose the most time to the blank page. Most proposal templates have a structure everyone knows, and a lot of the language is similar from deal to deal. The specific parts, the ones that actually win business, are the sections that speak directly to what this particular buyer cares about.

Claude and ChatGPT are genuinely useful here, but only if you give them the right inputs. The common mistake is pasting in your generic proposal template and asking AI to fill it in. That produces generic output. What works better:

  1. Start with your discovery call notes (or a rough dump of what you know about the buyer’s situation)
  2. Add context about their stated priorities and any objections they raised
  3. Specify the sections you need
  4. Ask Claude to draft each section with the buyer’s language and priorities woven in

The output will still need your editing. Proposal language often needs to match your company’s voice, hit specific terms from the RFP if there is one, and reflect the nuances of your relationship with the buyer. But a first draft that reflects the buyer’s actual situation beats a blank page by an order of magnitude.

For slide-based proposals, the same principle applies. Give Claude a structure and the key points you want to make per slide. Ask it to write slide headlines as complete sentences (not fragments), with one-paragraph speaker notes. Then import the structure into PowerPoint or Google Slides and build from there.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A sales professional describing a specific proposal they built faster with AI, with details on the time saved and what the editing process looked like]

3. Follow-Up Sequences

The research on this is consistent: most deals require five to eight touches before a buyer moves forward, and most sales reps give up after two. The gap isn’t motivation. It’s time and creative energy. Writing the fourth follow-up email after someone has gone quiet is genuinely hard, and most salespeople either send something generic or don’t send anything at all.

This is where AI makes a structural difference. You can give Claude the context of a stalled deal and ask it to generate a sequence of three to five follow-up emails, each with a different angle. It might come back with: a value reminder, a relevant case study hook, a check-in that leads with their specific situation, a low-friction “just closing the loop” note, and a soft break-up email.

You review, pick the ones that fit, adjust the tone so it sounds like you, and schedule them. What used to take 45 minutes of dreaded blank-page time now takes about 15 minutes. And critically, you’re more likely to actually send them.

A prompt that works for generating follow-up sequences:

“I’m following up with a prospect who went quiet after our second call. Here’s what I know about their situation: [context]. Here’s where we left things: [last touchpoint]. Help me write a sequence of four follow-up emails, each taking a different angle. Keep them short (under 100 words), direct, and low-pressure. They should sound like a peer, not a salesperson.”

Tool Recommendations: Start Here

For sales work, Claude is the better starting point for most use cases. A few reasons:

Claude’s output tends to read more naturally, with less of the corporate-formal register that makes email outreach feel generic. It handles longer documents well, which matters when you’re giving it a prospect’s annual report or a 20-page RFP to synthesize. And its instruction-following is strong enough that if you tell it to keep an email under 80 words and avoid the word “excited,” it actually does.

ChatGPT is also solid, particularly if you’re already using it for other work and don’t want to switch tools mid-day. The quality difference for most sales tasks is not significant enough to change workflows.

The paid tier ($20/month for either) is worth it for sales use. The free versions hit rate limits at the worst times, and the reasoning quality is noticeably better on the paid plans. Think of it as the same cost as one lunch with a prospect.

One practical note: do not paste confidential prospect information or internal deal data into any AI tool without checking your company’s data policy first. Many companies have guidance on what can and can’t go into external AI tools.

What Sales Professionals in Our Community Are Building

The members of MVP Club in sales roles have found that the learning curve is short. Most people have a workflow that’s saving them real time within the first week. A few patterns we’re seeing:

The “meeting brain dump” workflow: Right after a call, dictate or type a rough dump of what happened into Claude. Ask it to extract: key pain points mentioned, objections raised, next steps agreed to, and a draft follow-up email to send within the hour. The whole thing takes five minutes, and the email lands while the conversation is still fresh.

The personalized outreach template library: Instead of sending the same email to different prospects, build five to six outreach frames in Claude that vary by buyer persona or industry. Then for each new prospect, give Claude the frame and the specific company context and ask it to personalize it. You get consistent structure with genuine personalization, faster than doing it from scratch each time.

The RFP first-pass: When a long RFP lands, paste relevant sections into Claude and ask it to flag: what they’re actually asking for, what language from the RFP you should use in your response, and what they seem to value most based on how they’ve weighted sections. Use that analysis to prioritize where to spend your proposal-writing energy.

“Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here’s how I want you to do it. Just go.” — Matt Hastings, MVP Club

That principle applies directly to sales workflows. Your best outreach email ever, your most effective follow-up sequence, the way you structure a discovery debrief: document those patterns and give them to Claude as context. You turn a generic tool into one that knows how you work.

The Career Angle: Why This Matters Beyond Productivity

Getting good at AI-assisted sales work positions you differently inside a team or an organization. Not because you’re “doing AI,” but because you’re doing more, doing it faster, and doing it with less visible strain.

The sales professionals who figure this out early tend to show up to pipeline reviews with better-researched accounts, send more proposals, follow up more consistently, and still have energy at the end of the week. Those patterns are visible to managers and to clients.

If you’re in a quota-carrying role, AI capability can translate directly to more pipeline activity and, over time, more closed business. If you’re in a relationship-selling role where the pressure is on deepening a few high-value accounts, it buys back the hours you’d spend on administrative work and lets you put more time into the calls and conversations that actually move things forward.

The AI learning path we recommend starts with your actual work, not with AI concepts. For sales professionals, that means picking one of the three use cases above, trying it on a real deal, and seeing what happens. You’re not transforming your process overnight. You’re adding one tool to one step and finding out if it saves you time.

Your First Week With AI in Sales (5-Day Micro-Practice)

If you want a structured on-ramp, here’s a five-day plan that builds the habit without overhauling your workflow.

Day 1 (Monday): Research a prospect you already know

Pick a prospect you’re already working. Before your next touchpoint, run them through Claude using the prospect research prompt above. Compare what Claude gives you to what you already knew. Where was it useful? Where did it miss things only you would know? This calibration exercise helps you understand the tool’s limits from the start.

Day 2 (Tuesday): Turn your last meeting notes into a follow-up

Find your notes from a recent call. Paste the rough version into Claude and ask it to extract: three key points the buyer made, any implied objections or concerns, and a draft follow-up email to send within 24 hours. Edit until it sounds like you, then send it. Note the time it took compared to writing from scratch.

Day 3 (Wednesday): Write a cold outreach variation

Take one of your current outreach emails and give it to Claude with this prompt: “This is my current outreach email. I want you to write three variations, each with a different angle: one leading with the business problem, one leading with a result we’ve helped similar companies get, and one that’s shorter and more direct. Keep all three under 100 words.” Pick the one you’d actually send and test it.

Day 4 (Thursday): Draft a follow-up for a stalled deal

Go into your pipeline and find a deal that’s been stuck for two weeks or more. Give Claude the context and ask for a four-email follow-up sequence with different angles. You won’t use all four, but having options removes the blank-page paralysis that usually kills follow-up consistency.

Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and pick one workflow to keep

Look at the week. Which of the four use cases saved you the most time or produced the most useful output? That’s the one to build into your standard process next week. Don’t try to adopt all four at once. One well-executed workflow change compounds over time. Four half-adopted ones don’t.

Common Questions from Sales Professionals

Will my prospects be able to tell that AI wrote my emails?

If you send what Claude gives you without editing, possibly. AI output has patterns that some people notice. The fix is simple: read everything out loud before you send it. If it doesn’t sound like how you talk, edit it until it does. AI gives you a draft. You make it yours.

I’m in enterprise sales with long cycles. Is this still relevant?

Yes, maybe more so. Long-cycle sales involve more written communication (proposal documents, executive summaries, business cases), longer periods of follow-up, and more complex prospect research. All of those benefit from AI assistance. The personalization and human judgment still come from you. AI just handles the first draft faster.

My company has restrictions on using external AI tools. What should I do?

Check your policy before you start, not after. Many companies are developing guidance on acceptable AI use, and some have enterprise agreements with specific tools. If your company has Microsoft Copilot through an enterprise license, that may be the appropriate tool for work that touches client data. For research and writing that doesn’t involve confidential information, external tools are usually fine, but verify first.

What if I try this and the output is bad?

It will be, sometimes. That’s normal. The output quality is directly related to the quality of the context you give it. Understanding what AI can and can’t do helps set the right expectations. When the output is bad, don’t write off the tool. Write a better prompt, add more context, and try again. The iteration skill is more important than the prompting skill.

Practice Alongside Other Professionals

Sales can be isolating in a specific way: you’re competing internally for pipeline and externally for buyers, which doesn’t leave a lot of room for sharing what’s actually working. That’s part of why figuring out AI workflows on your own takes so much longer than it needs to.

The sales professionals in MVP Club share what’s working in weekly sessions: the prompts that landed, the workflows that saved real time, the approaches that flopped. If you want to build these habits faster and alongside peers who are navigating the same questions, that’s the environment we’ve built.

AI for Small Business Owners: Doing More with a Lean Team
How small business owners are using Claude and ChatGPT to handle emails, proposals, content, and customer work—without adding headcount.

Running a small business means you are the marketing team, the sales rep, the customer service manager, and the operations person, often in the same afternoon. The question "should I be using AI?" stopped being interesting a while ago. The question that matters now is: which parts of my week can AI actually take off my plate?

The answer, for most small business owners, is more than you think, and less setup than you're imagining.

This guide covers three high-impact use cases, a first-week plan you can actually follow, and honest answers to the questions most small business owners have before they start.

## Why AI fits the small business context well

Big companies have IT departments, change management processes, and legal review cycles that slow down AI adoption. You don't have any of that. That's an advantage.

You can open Claude or ChatGPT tomorrow morning and start using it on real work. No approval needed. No rollout plan. Just you, a browser tab, and whatever's already in your inbox.

The other thing that works in your favor: you know your business, your customers, and your voice better than anyone. AI needs that context to be useful, and you have it. When you tell Claude "I run a small landscape design firm, my clients are homeowners in the Denver suburbs, and my tone is warm but professional," the output immediately gets more relevant. The more specific you are about who you are and who you're talking to, the better the results.

## The three highest-impact use cases for small business owners

### Use Case 1: Customer communication (your quickest win)

Email is the first place most small business owners see real time savings, and it makes sense. You're probably writing variations of the same five emails over and over: follow-ups after meetings, responses to questions about pricing, project updates, proposals, and "thanks for your business" notes after a job wraps up.

AI is excellent at first drafts. Not because it writes better than you, but because it gives you something to react to instead of starting from a blank screen. A blank email takes 20 minutes. Editing a draft takes 5.

Here's how Carmella Thompson, a real estate professional in our community, described her workflow after building an AI-assisted email process: she automated her market report summaries into client-facing communications so she could send them the same day instead of the next morning. The time savings weren't huge per email, but across a week of outreach, she was getting back several hours.

**Try this today:** Find an email you're dreading writing. Open Claude (claude.ai) and paste in this prompt, filling in the brackets:

> "I run [type of business]. I need to write an email to [who you're writing to] about [situation]. The context is [what happened or what they need to know]. My tone is [warm/direct/professional/casual]. Draft a response under 200 words."

Read what it gives you. It probably won't be perfect. Edit it until it sounds like you. That editing process is the skill you're building.

### Use Case 2: Proposals and client-facing documents

Writing proposals is one of the highest-leverage tasks in any small business, and one of the most time-consuming. Most proposals follow the same basic arc: here's what we understood about your situation, here's what we'd do, here's why we're the right fit, here's what it costs, here's what happens next.

AI can draft that structure in minutes. Your job is to inject the specifics: the client's actual situation, the details of your approach, your pricing.

The pattern that works well is to paste in your notes from the client conversation, your pricing and service structure, and a few examples of language you like from your previous proposals. Then ask Claude to assemble a draft. You'll spend much less time on the blank-page problem and more time on the parts that actually require your judgment, which is the part where your experience differentiates you anyway.

**The key insight:** AI handles the structure so you can focus on the substance. You're not replacing your thinking. You're removing the part of the process that doesn't need your thinking.

### Use Case 3: Content and marketing materials

If you're running social media, sending newsletters, updating your website, or producing any kind of marketing content on top of your actual client work, this is where AI can feel almost unfair.

Consider the math: a monthly newsletter that takes you three hours to write can take 45 minutes when you use Claude to draft sections, suggest headlines, and help you organize your ideas. A LinkedIn post that would sit in your drafts for a week gets done in 20 minutes when you describe the idea to Claude and ask it to draft three versions you can edit.

The important thing is giving AI context each time. "My audience is small restaurant owners, I'm a restaurant consultant, and my newsletter focuses on practical operations tips, not trends or hype" produces completely different output than "write me a newsletter."

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A small business owner in our community describing how they use AI for a specific content task, with a concrete detail about time saved or quality improvement]

## A closer look at Use Case 1: your first AI email workflow

Because email is the fastest win, here's a more detailed walkthrough of how to actually build this into your day.

**Step 1: Build your context block (10 minutes, one time)**

Open a document and write three things about your business in plain language: what you do, who your clients are, and how you like to communicate with them. This becomes your "context block" that you paste into Claude at the start of each session. Something like:

> "I run a home organizing business serving families in suburban Chicago. My clients are homeowners, mostly parents with young kids, who feel overwhelmed by clutter. My tone is warm, practical, and encouraging. I don't use jargon."

**Step 2: Pick your 5 most common email types**

Write a short description of each one. "Follow-up after a consultation." "Estimate delivery." "Project completed, asking for a review." "Responding to someone who's ghosted after getting a quote."

These become templates you reuse. You're not writing them from scratch each time. You're giving Claude the context and asking it to draft the version for this specific client.

**Step 3: Edit for voice, not from scratch**

This is the biggest mindset shift. You're not using AI to write your emails. You're using it to draft your emails, which you then edit. The editing step is where your voice, your judgment, and your relationship with that client go in. AI gives you the structure. You give it the soul.

Most business owners who build this workflow say the same thing after a few weeks: they don't want to go back to the blank screen. Not because the AI output is better, but because they spend their time on the parts that matter.

## Tool recommendations

For small business owners, the two tools worth your time are **Claude** (claude.ai) and **ChatGPT** (chat.openai.com). Both have free tiers that are fine for experimenting. If you're going to commit to daily use, the paid plans ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) give you better output and longer conversations.

Claude tends to handle nuanced writing tasks and longer documents better, which makes it a good fit for proposals, complex customer communications, and content work. ChatGPT has strong general capabilities and a large library of community-shared prompts if you want ideas for how others are using it.

Start with one. Don't try to evaluate both at the same time. Use it for real work on real tasks for two weeks before deciding if you want to try the other.

For small business owners specifically, Claude's ability to hold a lot of context in a single conversation (your business background, a long email thread, a draft proposal) without losing the thread is particularly useful when you're working on something that requires sustained back-and-forth.

## What AI doesn't do well (the honest version)

A few things that will frustrate you if you go in expecting them to work:

**Replacing your judgment on pricing and strategy.** AI doesn't know your market, your margins, or what your clients will actually pay. Don't ask it to set your prices or make strategic decisions. Use it to draft the email that communicates your pricing once you've made the decision yourself.

**Getting it right the first time.** The first draft is a starting point, not a finished product. Every experienced AI user has learned this. The value comes from the conversation: "That's close, but the tone is too formal. Can you make it more direct and cut the third paragraph?" Iteration is the skill.

**Knowing your clients.** Claude doesn't know that your biggest client is going through a rough patch and needs a softer touch right now. That's your job. AI gives you the bones. You supply the relationship.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific small business owner who used AI to handle a customer situation and what they had to add or change to make the AI's output actually appropriate for that client relationship]

## The career angle: what this does for your positioning

This one's easy to miss when you're focused on the day-to-day.

Small business owners who get comfortable with AI now are building a real capability gap between themselves and competitors who haven't started. Not because AI is magic, but because the time compounding works in your favor. If you're getting back even five hours a week from AI-assisted communication and content work, that's five hours you can spend on client relationships, business development, or work that genuinely requires your expertise.

The professionals who are furthest ahead with AI right now share one thing: they started using it on real work before they felt ready. As Matt Hastings at MVP Club puts it: "The only way to understand what AI means for you and your career is to pick up the tool and use it every day." That's as true for a solopreneur as it is for someone at a Fortune 500.

For more on why practice beats preparation with AI, see [How to Find Time for AI When You're Already Busy](/learn/finding-time-for-ai) and [The Biggest Mistakes People Make When They Start Using AI](/learn/ai-beginner-mistakes).

## Your first week: a 5-day plan for small business owners

**Monday:** Write down your five most common email types. For each one, write one sentence describing the situation. This takes 15 minutes and you only do it once.

**Tuesday:** Build your context block. Write three to four sentences about your business, your clients, and your communication style. Paste it into Claude and send one real email using a Claude draft you edit.

**Wednesday:** Find a proposal or project brief you need to write this week. Paste your notes from the client conversation into Claude and ask for a first draft. Edit until it sounds like you.

**Thursday:** Write one piece of content you've been putting off: a newsletter section, a social post, an update for your website. Describe the idea to Claude, ask for three versions, pick the one closest to what you want, and edit it.

**Friday:** Reflect. What worked? What felt clunky? What would you want to try next? Write it down. The reflection helps you identify which use cases are worth repeating and which ones aren't worth the setup.

By the end of the week, you'll have a clearer sense of where AI fits your specific business than any amount of reading about AI could give you.

## FAQ

**Is it safe to put client information into Claude or ChatGPT?**

For general client communications and proposals, most small business owners use the consumer versions (claude.ai, chat.openai.com) without concern. Don't paste in anything you wouldn't put in a shared Google Doc: no social security numbers, financial account numbers, or sensitive personal health information. For the day-to-day work of running a small business, this isn't usually an issue. If you have specific contractual obligations around client data, check with your attorney, not an article on the internet.

**Do I need to use AI every day?**

You don't need to, but the people who get the most out of it do. Daily practice is what builds the skill. Using it once a week keeps it feeling like a novelty. The goal is for it to feel like how you work, not like a thing you occasionally try.

**What if the output doesn't sound like me?**

That's expected, especially at first. Editing the output is part of the workflow, not a sign something went wrong. As you use it more, you'll get better at giving Claude context about your voice, and the starting point will get closer to where you want it. Many business owners find it helpful to paste in two or three examples of their own writing and ask Claude to match that style.

**What if I try it and nothing saves me time?**

Give it two weeks on real tasks before deciding. Most people's first attempts at using AI are either too low-stakes (testing it with fake prompts) or too ambitious (trying to automate a complex process on day one). The sweet spot is real tasks you do regularly, starting with email and simple documents. That's where the time savings are most predictable.

## Where small business owners figure this out together

The professionals who get the most from AI aren't the ones who read the most about it. They're the ones who practice regularly and compare notes with peers who are doing the same thing.

In MVP Club's community sessions, small business owners, consultants, and independent professionals share what's actually working in their workflows: the prompts that save them time, the tasks where AI falls short, and the techniques they've found through trial and error. If you want to practice alongside people figuring this out in real time, [come see what's happening in the community](/community).

Running a small business means you are the marketing team, the sales rep, the customer service manager, and the operations person, often in the same afternoon. The question “should I be using AI?” stopped being interesting a while ago. The question that matters now is: which parts of my week can AI actually take off my plate?

The answer, for most small business owners, is more than you think, and less setup than you’re imagining.

This guide covers three high-impact use cases, a first-week plan you can actually follow, and honest answers to the questions most small business owners have before they start.

Why AI fits the small business context well

Big companies have IT departments, change management processes, and legal review cycles that slow down AI adoption. You don’t have any of that. That’s an advantage.

You can open Claude or ChatGPT tomorrow morning and start using it on real work. No approval needed. No rollout plan. Just you, a browser tab, and whatever’s already in your inbox.

The other thing that works in your favor: you know your business, your customers, and your voice better than anyone. AI needs that context to be useful, and you have it. When you tell Claude “I run a small landscape design firm, my clients are homeowners in the Denver suburbs, and my tone is warm but professional,” the output immediately gets more relevant. The more specific you are about who you are and who you’re talking to, the better the results.

The three highest-impact use cases for small business owners

Use Case 1: Customer communication (your quickest win)

Email is the first place most small business owners see real time savings, and it makes sense. You’re probably writing variations of the same five emails over and over: follow-ups after meetings, responses to questions about pricing, project updates, proposals, and “thanks for your business” notes after a job wraps up.

AI is excellent at first drafts. Not because it writes better than you, but because it gives you something to react to instead of starting from a blank screen. A blank email takes 20 minutes. Editing a draft takes 5.

Here’s how Carmella Thompson, a real estate professional in our community, described her workflow after building an AI-assisted email process: she automated her market report summaries into client-facing communications so she could send them the same day instead of the next morning. The time savings weren’t huge per email, but across a week of outreach, she was getting back several hours.

Try this today: Find an email you’re dreading writing. Open Claude (claude.ai) and paste in this prompt, filling in the brackets:

“I run [type of business]. I need to write an email to [who you’re writing to] about [situation]. The context is [what happened or what they need to know]. My tone is [warm/direct/professional/casual]. Draft a response under 200 words.”

Read what it gives you. It probably won’t be perfect. Edit it until it sounds like you. That editing process is the skill you’re building.

Use Case 2: Proposals and client-facing documents

Writing proposals is one of the highest-leverage tasks in any small business, and one of the most time-consuming. Most proposals follow the same basic arc: here’s what we understood about your situation, here’s what we’d do, here’s why we’re the right fit, here’s what it costs, here’s what happens next.

AI can draft that structure in minutes. Your job is to inject the specifics: the client’s actual situation, the details of your approach, your pricing.

The pattern that works well is to paste in your notes from the client conversation, your pricing and service structure, and a few examples of language you like from your previous proposals. Then ask Claude to assemble a draft. You’ll spend much less time on the blank-page problem and more time on the parts that actually require your judgment, which is the part where your experience differentiates you anyway.

The key insight: AI handles the structure so you can focus on the substance. You’re not replacing your thinking. You’re removing the part of the process that doesn’t need your thinking.

Use Case 3: Content and marketing materials

If you’re running social media, sending newsletters, updating your website, or producing any kind of marketing content on top of your actual client work, this is where AI can feel almost unfair.

Consider the math: a monthly newsletter that takes you three hours to write can take 45 minutes when you use Claude to draft sections, suggest headlines, and help you organize your ideas. A LinkedIn post that would sit in your drafts for a week gets done in 20 minutes when you describe the idea to Claude and ask it to draft three versions you can edit.

The important thing is giving AI context each time. “My audience is small restaurant owners, I’m a restaurant consultant, and my newsletter focuses on practical operations tips, not trends or hype” produces completely different output than “write me a newsletter.”

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A small business owner in our community describing how they use AI for a specific content task, with a concrete detail about time saved or quality improvement]

A closer look at Use Case 1: your first AI email workflow

Because email is the fastest win, here’s a more detailed walkthrough of how to actually build this into your day.

Step 1: Build your context block (10 minutes, one time)

Open a document and write three things about your business in plain language: what you do, who your clients are, and how you like to communicate with them. This becomes your “context block” that you paste into Claude at the start of each session. Something like:

“I run a home organizing business serving families in suburban Chicago. My clients are homeowners, mostly parents with young kids, who feel overwhelmed by clutter. My tone is warm, practical, and encouraging. I don’t use jargon.”

Step 2: Pick your 5 most common email types

Write a short description of each one. “Follow-up after a consultation.” “Estimate delivery.” “Project completed, asking for a review.” “Responding to someone who’s ghosted after getting a quote.”

These become templates you reuse. You’re not writing them from scratch each time. You’re giving Claude the context and asking it to draft the version for this specific client.

Step 3: Edit for voice, not from scratch

This is the biggest mindset shift. You’re not using AI to write your emails. You’re using it to draft your emails, which you then edit. The editing step is where your voice, your judgment, and your relationship with that client go in. AI gives you the structure. You give it the soul.

Most business owners who build this workflow say the same thing after a few weeks: they don’t want to go back to the blank screen. Not because the AI output is better, but because they spend their time on the parts that matter.

Tool recommendations

For small business owners, the two tools worth your time are Claude (claude.ai) and ChatGPT (chat.openai.com). Both have free tiers that are fine for experimenting. If you’re going to commit to daily use, the paid plans ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) give you better output and longer conversations.

Claude tends to handle nuanced writing tasks and longer documents better, which makes it a good fit for proposals, complex customer communications, and content work. ChatGPT has strong general capabilities and a large library of community-shared prompts if you want ideas for how others are using it.

Start with one. Don’t try to evaluate both at the same time. Use it for real work on real tasks for two weeks before deciding if you want to try the other.

For small business owners specifically, Claude’s ability to hold a lot of context in a single conversation (your business background, a long email thread, a draft proposal) without losing the thread is particularly useful when you’re working on something that requires sustained back-and-forth.

What AI doesn’t do well (the honest version)

A few things that will frustrate you if you go in expecting them to work:

Replacing your judgment on pricing and strategy. AI doesn’t know your market, your margins, or what your clients will actually pay. Don’t ask it to set your prices or make strategic decisions. Use it to draft the email that communicates your pricing once you’ve made the decision yourself.

Getting it right the first time. The first draft is a starting point, not a finished product. Every experienced AI user has learned this. The value comes from the conversation: “That’s close, but the tone is too formal. Can you make it more direct and cut the third paragraph?” Iteration is the skill.

Knowing your clients. Claude doesn’t know that your biggest client is going through a rough patch and needs a softer touch right now. That’s your job. AI gives you the bones. You supply the relationship.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific small business owner who used AI to handle a customer situation and what they had to add or change to make the AI’s output actually appropriate for that client relationship]

The career angle: what this does for your positioning

This one’s easy to miss when you’re focused on the day-to-day.

Small business owners who get comfortable with AI now are building a real capability gap between themselves and competitors who haven’t started. Not because AI is magic, but because the time compounding works in your favor. If you’re getting back even five hours a week from AI-assisted communication and content work, that’s five hours you can spend on client relationships, business development, or work that genuinely requires your expertise.

The professionals who are furthest ahead with AI right now share one thing: they started using it on real work before they felt ready. As Matt Hastings at MVP Club puts it: “The only way to understand what AI means for you and your career is to pick up the tool and use it every day.” That’s as true for a solopreneur as it is for someone at a Fortune 500.

For more on why practice beats preparation with AI, see How to Find Time for AI When You’re Already Busy and The Biggest Mistakes People Make When They Start Using AI.

Your first week: a 5-day plan for small business owners

Monday: Write down your five most common email types. For each one, write one sentence describing the situation. This takes 15 minutes and you only do it once.

Tuesday: Build your context block. Write three to four sentences about your business, your clients, and your communication style. Paste it into Claude and send one real email using a Claude draft you edit.

Wednesday: Find a proposal or project brief you need to write this week. Paste your notes from the client conversation into Claude and ask for a first draft. Edit until it sounds like you.

Thursday: Write one piece of content you’ve been putting off: a newsletter section, a social post, an update for your website. Describe the idea to Claude, ask for three versions, pick the one closest to what you want, and edit it.

Friday: Reflect. What worked? What felt clunky? What would you want to try next? Write it down. The reflection helps you identify which use cases are worth repeating and which ones aren’t worth the setup.

By the end of the week, you’ll have a clearer sense of where AI fits your specific business than any amount of reading about AI could give you.

FAQ

Is it safe to put client information into Claude or ChatGPT?

For general client communications and proposals, most small business owners use the consumer versions (claude.ai, chat.openai.com) without concern. Don’t paste in anything you wouldn’t put in a shared Google Doc: no social security numbers, financial account numbers, or sensitive personal health information. For the day-to-day work of running a small business, this isn’t usually an issue. If you have specific contractual obligations around client data, check with your attorney, not an article on the internet.

Do I need to use AI every day?

You don’t need to, but the people who get the most out of it do. Daily practice is what builds the skill. Using it once a week keeps it feeling like a novelty. The goal is for it to feel like how you work, not like a thing you occasionally try.

What if the output doesn’t sound like me?

That’s expected, especially at first. Editing the output is part of the workflow, not a sign something went wrong. As you use it more, you’ll get better at giving Claude context about your voice, and the starting point will get closer to where you want it. Many business owners find it helpful to paste in two or three examples of their own writing and ask Claude to match that style.

What if I try it and nothing saves me time?

Give it two weeks on real tasks before deciding. Most people’s first attempts at using AI are either too low-stakes (testing it with fake prompts) or too ambitious (trying to automate a complex process on day one). The sweet spot is real tasks you do regularly, starting with email and simple documents. That’s where the time savings are most predictable.

A practical AI governance guide for non-technical leaders: what policies to write, what to skip, and how to keep your team moving without endless red tape.

Most AI governance conversations start in the wrong place. They start with risk, restriction, and a list of tools employees are not allowed to use. That's understandable. But the organizations getting the most out of AI adoption right now started somewhere different: they asked what their people needed to feel confident using these tools, and then built the minimal structure to make that possible.

AI governance isn't primarily a legal exercise. It's a clarity exercise. Your team is already using ChatGPT, Claude, and a half-dozen other tools whether you have a policy or not. [NEEDS VERIFIED STAT: organizations lacking formal AI governance policies] That gap isn't going to close by hoping people stop. It closes when you give them clear guidance on how to proceed.

This is a guide for the leader who isn't a technologist but is responsible for how AI gets used on their team.

## What AI Governance Actually Needs to Cover

Before writing a single policy, it helps to understand what you're actually governing. Most of what gets called "AI governance" at the team or department level comes down to four questions:

1. Which tools are approved for which types of work?
2. What data can and can't go into those tools?
3. Who reviews and owns the outputs?
4. Who decides when a use case is new enough to need a conversation?

That's it. Everything else (model selection, vendor contracts, enterprise licensing, compliance audits) is real but lives above the team level. If you're a VP, a director, or a department lead trying to get your team moving safely, these four questions are the right scope.

I keep coming back to this in coaching sessions: "All these companies are hyper competing right now. They all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?" That's the right frame for governance, too. Not "what do the vendors promise" but "what does my team actually need to do the work safely and well?"

## The One Document You Need Before Anything Else

Call it an Acceptable Use Policy, a Team AI Charter, or whatever fits your organization's culture. The name doesn't matter. What matters is that it answers the four governance questions above in plain language that any team member can understand in five minutes.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: Matt's governance coaching experience — the moment a simple one-page charter changed how a team engaged with AI tools]

Here's what a simple version looks like. On approved tools: Claude (Pro or Team accounts), ChatGPT (Team or Enterprise), Microsoft Copilot if your organization has it. Personal free-tier accounts are fine for experimentation but not for work involving real project data.

On what stays out of AI tools: client names and contact information, employee compensation or HR records, unreleased financial information, anything under NDA, login credentials. When in doubt, anonymize before you paste. Replace "Acme Corp" with "Client A," swap real budget numbers for ballpark figures. You keep 90 percent of the usefulness and eliminate nearly all the risk.

On who owns the output: the person who used the tool owns the output. They're responsible for reviewing it, editing it, and standing behind it. AI produces drafts. Humans produce deliverables.

On flagging something new: if you're not sure whether a use case is covered, bring it to your manager before you start, not after. This isn't a gotcha process. It's a conversation.

That document, even if it's two pages, gives your team something to stand on. It replaces the anxiety of guessing with the clarity of knowing.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An MVP Club member in a leadership role describing what happened when they gave their team a simple AI charter — ideally someone who was surprised by how quickly their team adopted AI once the guidance existed]

## The Data Question Is the Only Hard Part

Most AI governance for non-technical leaders comes down to one practical skill: knowing what data belongs in an AI tool and what doesn't. The rest is process.

The risk isn't that your team will use AI wrong. The risk is that someone will paste something into a personal-tier account that shouldn't be there, and that information will potentially be used to train the model. That's the actual exposure. Everything else is noise.

One member from our Getting Unstuck sessions described her company's situation clearly: "Legal won't approve that for us. There's a lot of things they're still holding off on." That friction is real. But it usually signals that legal and IT haven't had the conversation yet, not that the use case is actually risky. Your job as the non-technical leader is to bring those stakeholders the right question: not "can we use AI" but "here's the specific use case and the data involved. What do you need to see to feel comfortable?"

The framing shift matters. A blanket question gets a blanket cautious answer. A specific question about a specific workflow gets a specific useful answer.

For a deeper look at what the individual employee needs to know about data handling and account tiers, [AI workplace policies: what you need to know before using AI at your job](/learn/ai-workplace-policies) covers the personal and professional dimensions in detail.

## How to Choose What to Allow (and What to Wait On)

Not every use case needs to wait for enterprise licensing and legal review. Some things are safe to green-light now. Others need more runway. Here's a practical way to think about it.

Some things are safe to green-light now: using Claude or ChatGPT to draft internal communications, researching market context, improving writing clarity on documents the employee already authored, generating templates the team then customizes. Low stakes, no sensitive data, plenty of human review before anything ships.

Talk to IT or legal first for anything that involves client data (even anonymized), AI features embedded in tools your organization hasn't formally approved, output that goes directly to a client without human review, or use cases involving regulated data like HIPAA or FERPA.

Some things need more runway: fully automated workflows with no human review, AI tools that connect to internal systems, or anything that influences personnel decisions. That's not a permanent no. It's a "not yet."

The goal isn't to lock down the hard category forever. It's to move the first two forward fast while you build the trust and processes needed for the rest.

## Building a One-Page Team AI Log

As your team's AI use grows, you'll want one simple document that tracks what's in use and who owns it. Not a compliance database. A living one-page list.

For each AI use case or tool your team has approved, track four things: what it does (one sentence), who owns it (the person responsible for reviewing outputs), what data goes into it, and when you last reviewed it (a date, updated quarterly).

This document serves a practical purpose: it gives you something to show when leadership, legal, or IT asks "what exactly are you doing with AI?" It also helps you spot when a team member has started using something that isn't on the list, which opens a conversation rather than a confrontation.

The teams doing this well aren't running it as compliance theater. They update it when something changes, bring it to quarterly planning, and use it to identify where they want to expand.

## The Skeptics on Your Team

Any team of more than five people probably has at least one person who is genuinely skeptical of AI, not as a performance but as a considered position. That person has a job to do, and your governance structure should make space for them.

The right governance framework isn't one that mandates AI use. It's one that clears the path for people who want to use it and doesn't penalize people who are still figuring it out. "You don't have to have it all figured out," is the right framing. The policy creates an environment where trying is safe. Individual pace is still individual.

The professionals who find it hardest to get started are often the ones who are most skilled at their current work. They've built deep expertise over years, and AI tools can feel like they're questioning that expertise rather than extending it. [How to explain AI to your boss, team, or skeptical colleague](/learn/explain-ai-to-your-team) goes into this dynamic with specific conversation approaches that actually work.

## What "Leading AI at Your Organization" Actually Means

Most governance conversations miss the most important part. The people who become the AI leaders at their organizations don't start by drafting perfect policies. They start by using the tools themselves.

I say this constantly in coaching sessions: the only way to understand what AI means for you and your career is to pick up the tool and use it every day. That's not just advice for the individual contributor. It applies directly to the leader who wants to govern AI well. You can't write useful policy for something you haven't used. You'll write restrictions that don't address the real risk and miss the protections that actually matter.

The most effective non-technical AI leaders in our community are the ones who spent a month using Claude and ChatGPT for their own work before they started thinking about what to put in a policy. They came to the governance conversation with specific use cases, a real sense of where the output quality was good and where it needed review, and the credibility of someone who had actually done the thing they were governing.

Start with your own work, not a committee. A thirty-day habit of trying something new each week will teach you more than any policy document you could draft from scratch.

For a practical way to begin using these tools in your own work before expanding to your team, [how to use AI at work: a practical guide for busy professionals](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) is the right starting point.

## The Governance Document Your Team Needs (And When to Update It)

A few practical notes on keeping this alive after you write it.

Review it quarterly. The AI tool landscape moves fast enough that a policy written six months ago may reference tools or account tiers that no longer exist in the same form. Put a date on the document. Set a calendar reminder.

Update it when something surprising happens. If a team member uses AI in a way that wasn't covered and it went well, add it to the green-light list. If something went wrong (or almost went wrong), update the policy to reflect the lesson. Governance that learns from real use is worth ten times the governance written from first principles and never touched again.

Make it easy to find. The policy that lives in a SharePoint folder nobody knows about is not a policy. A two-page doc in the team Notion or Slack pinned resources is.

Don't mistake the document for the culture. The document is a starting point. The culture is what happens when a team member asks a question and gets a useful answer, or tries something new and gets credit for being curious. The leader sets that tone. No policy does it for you.

---

The leaders who navigate this well aren't doing it alone. In MVP Club's weekly sessions, non-technical leaders across industries share what their governance frameworks look like in practice, where they've gotten stuck, and what's actually working. If you're building this out at your organization and want a room full of people who are doing the same thing, [come see what we're building together](/community).

Most AI governance conversations start in the wrong place. They start with risk, restriction, and a list of tools employees are not allowed to use. That’s understandable. But the organizations getting the most out of AI adoption right now started somewhere different: they asked what their people needed to feel confident using these tools, and then built the minimal structure to make that possible.

AI governance isn’t primarily a legal exercise. It’s a clarity exercise. Your team is already using ChatGPT, Claude, and a half-dozen other tools whether you have a policy or not. [NEEDS VERIFIED STAT: organizations lacking formal AI governance policies] That gap isn’t going to close by hoping people stop. It closes when you give them clear guidance on how to proceed.

This is a guide for the leader who isn’t a technologist but is responsible for how AI gets used on their team.

What AI Governance Actually Needs to Cover

Before writing a single policy, it helps to understand what you’re actually governing. Most of what gets called “AI governance” at the team or department level comes down to four questions:

  1. Which tools are approved for which types of work?
  2. What data can and can’t go into those tools?
  3. Who reviews and owns the outputs?
  4. Who decides when a use case is new enough to need a conversation?

That’s it. Everything else (model selection, vendor contracts, enterprise licensing, compliance audits) is real but lives above the team level. If you’re a VP, a director, or a department lead trying to get your team moving safely, these four questions are the right scope.

I keep coming back to this in coaching sessions: “All these companies are hyper competing right now. They all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?” That’s the right frame for governance, too. Not “what do the vendors promise” but “what does my team actually need to do the work safely and well?”

The One Document You Need Before Anything Else

Call it an Acceptable Use Policy, a Team AI Charter, or whatever fits your organization’s culture. The name doesn’t matter. What matters is that it answers the four governance questions above in plain language that any team member can understand in five minutes.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: Matt’s governance coaching experience — the moment a simple one-page charter changed how a team engaged with AI tools]

Here’s what a simple version looks like. On approved tools: Claude (Pro or Team accounts), ChatGPT (Team or Enterprise), Microsoft Copilot if your organization has it. Personal free-tier accounts are fine for experimentation but not for work involving real project data.

On what stays out of AI tools: client names and contact information, employee compensation or HR records, unreleased financial information, anything under NDA, login credentials. When in doubt, anonymize before you paste. Replace “Acme Corp” with “Client A,” swap real budget numbers for ballpark figures. You keep 90 percent of the usefulness and eliminate nearly all the risk.

On who owns the output: the person who used the tool owns the output. They’re responsible for reviewing it, editing it, and standing behind it. AI produces drafts. Humans produce deliverables.

On flagging something new: if you’re not sure whether a use case is covered, bring it to your manager before you start, not after. This isn’t a gotcha process. It’s a conversation.

That document, even if it’s two pages, gives your team something to stand on. It replaces the anxiety of guessing with the clarity of knowing.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An MVP Club member in a leadership role describing what happened when they gave their team a simple AI charter — ideally someone who was surprised by how quickly their team adopted AI once the guidance existed]

The Data Question Is the Only Hard Part

Most AI governance for non-technical leaders comes down to one practical skill: knowing what data belongs in an AI tool and what doesn’t. The rest is process.

The risk isn’t that your team will use AI wrong. The risk is that someone will paste something into a personal-tier account that shouldn’t be there, and that information will potentially be used to train the model. That’s the actual exposure. Everything else is noise.

One member from our Getting Unstuck sessions described her company’s situation clearly: “Legal won’t approve that for us. There’s a lot of things they’re still holding off on.” That friction is real. But it usually signals that legal and IT haven’t had the conversation yet, not that the use case is actually risky. Your job as the non-technical leader is to bring those stakeholders the right question: not “can we use AI” but “here’s the specific use case and the data involved. What do you need to see to feel comfortable?”

The framing shift matters. A blanket question gets a blanket cautious answer. A specific question about a specific workflow gets a specific useful answer.

For a deeper look at what the individual employee needs to know about data handling and account tiers, AI workplace policies: what you need to know before using AI at your job covers the personal and professional dimensions in detail.

How to Choose What to Allow (and What to Wait On)

Not every use case needs to wait for enterprise licensing and legal review. Some things are safe to green-light now. Others need more runway. Here’s a practical way to think about it.

Some things are safe to green-light now: using Claude or ChatGPT to draft internal communications, researching market context, improving writing clarity on documents the employee already authored, generating templates the team then customizes. Low stakes, no sensitive data, plenty of human review before anything ships.

Talk to IT or legal first for anything that involves client data (even anonymized), AI features embedded in tools your organization hasn’t formally approved, output that goes directly to a client without human review, or use cases involving regulated data like HIPAA or FERPA.

Some things need more runway: fully automated workflows with no human review, AI tools that connect to internal systems, or anything that influences personnel decisions. That’s not a permanent no. It’s a “not yet.”

The goal isn’t to lock down the hard category forever. It’s to move the first two forward fast while you build the trust and processes needed for the rest.

Building a One-Page Team AI Log

As your team’s AI use grows, you’ll want one simple document that tracks what’s in use and who owns it. Not a compliance database. A living one-page list.

For each AI use case or tool your team has approved, track four things: what it does (one sentence), who owns it (the person responsible for reviewing outputs), what data goes into it, and when you last reviewed it (a date, updated quarterly).

This document serves a practical purpose: it gives you something to show when leadership, legal, or IT asks “what exactly are you doing with AI?” It also helps you spot when a team member has started using something that isn’t on the list, which opens a conversation rather than a confrontation.

The teams doing this well aren’t running it as compliance theater. They update it when something changes, bring it to quarterly planning, and use it to identify where they want to expand.

The Skeptics on Your Team

Any team of more than five people probably has at least one person who is genuinely skeptical of AI, not as a performance but as a considered position. That person has a job to do, and your governance structure should make space for them.

The right governance framework isn’t one that mandates AI use. It’s one that clears the path for people who want to use it and doesn’t penalize people who are still figuring it out. “You don’t have to have it all figured out,” is the right framing. The policy creates an environment where trying is safe. Individual pace is still individual.

The professionals who find it hardest to get started are often the ones who are most skilled at their current work. They’ve built deep expertise over years, and AI tools can feel like they’re questioning that expertise rather than extending it. How to explain AI to your boss, team, or skeptical colleague goes into this dynamic with specific conversation approaches that actually work.

What “Leading AI at Your Organization” Actually Means

Most governance conversations miss the most important part. The people who become the AI leaders at their organizations don’t start by drafting perfect policies. They start by using the tools themselves.

I say this constantly in coaching sessions: the only way to understand what AI means for you and your career is to pick up the tool and use it every day. That’s not just advice for the individual contributor. It applies directly to the leader who wants to govern AI well. You can’t write useful policy for something you haven’t used. You’ll write restrictions that don’t address the real risk and miss the protections that actually matter.

The most effective non-technical AI leaders in our community are the ones who spent a month using Claude and ChatGPT for their own work before they started thinking about what to put in a policy. They came to the governance conversation with specific use cases, a real sense of where the output quality was good and where it needed review, and the credibility of someone who had actually done the thing they were governing.

Start with your own work, not a committee. A thirty-day habit of trying something new each week will teach you more than any policy document you could draft from scratch.

For a practical way to begin using these tools in your own work before expanding to your team, how to use AI at work: a practical guide for busy professionals is the right starting point.

The Governance Document Your Team Needs (And When to Update It)

A few practical notes on keeping this alive after you write it.

Review it quarterly. The AI tool landscape moves fast enough that a policy written six months ago may reference tools or account tiers that no longer exist in the same form. Put a date on the document. Set a calendar reminder.

Update it when something surprising happens. If a team member uses AI in a way that wasn’t covered and it went well, add it to the green-light list. If something went wrong (or almost went wrong), update the policy to reflect the lesson. Governance that learns from real use is worth ten times the governance written from first principles and never touched again.

Make it easy to find. The policy that lives in a SharePoint folder nobody knows about is not a policy. A two-page doc in the team Notion or Slack pinned resources is.

Don’t mistake the document for the culture. The document is a starting point. The culture is what happens when a team member asks a question and gets a useful answer, or tries something new and gets credit for being curious. The leader sets that tone. No policy does it for you.

The leaders who navigate this well aren’t doing it alone. In MVP Club’s weekly sessions, non-technical leaders across industries share what their governance frameworks look like in practice, where they’ve gotten stuck, and what’s actually working. If you’re building this out at your organization and want a room full of people who are doing the same thing, come see what we’re building together.

AI for Writing: Reports, Proposals, and Professional Documents
How to use AI tools for writing reports, proposals, and professional documents. Step-by-step guidance on the best AI tools for writing at work.

The proposal that used to take you half a day now takes a morning. Not because AI writes it for you, but because the blank page problem is gone.

Professional writing is one of the highest-leverage places to bring Claude or ChatGPT into your work. Reports, proposals, executive summaries, project briefs, recommendation memos: these are tasks that eat hours every week for most professionals. You were in the meeting. You have the email chain. You know the situation. But assembling it into something readable, structured, and persuasive takes time you often don't have.

This guide covers the practical workflow for using AI on professional documents that eat hours every week across most roles.

## Why professional writing is a strong fit for AI

The blank page is the enemy of professional writing. Most of the delay in writing a report or proposal has nothing to do with not knowing the content. You know the content. You were in the meeting, you have the data, you understand the situation. The delay is about getting started and then shaping what you know into the right structure.

AI is excellent at first drafts. Not because it writes better than you (it often doesn't), but because it gives you something to react to. A mediocre draft you can edit is faster to work with than a blank screen.

A Harvard Business Review study (December 2024) found that professionals using AI writing tools completed writing tasks more than 50% faster, with quality ratings that met or exceeded the unassisted versions. ([Source](https://hbr.org/2024/12/should-you-write-with-gen-ai)) The speed gain is real. But so is the catch: AI-generated text can introduce factual errors and a flat, generic tone that experienced readers notice immediately. Your job isn't to delegate the writing. It's to direct the draft and then make it yours.

## The mental model: think of it as a first-draft machine

Before you open Claude or ChatGPT, the most useful shift is to stop thinking of AI as a writer and start thinking of it as a capable assistant who can produce a rough draft from your notes. The key phrase is "from your notes."

The quality of what you get out is directly tied to the quality of what you put in. If you paste in your raw meeting notes, key decisions, audience context, and the outcome you need the document to achieve, you'll get something useful. If you type "write me a quarterly report," you'll get a generic outline that doesn't know your organization, your stakeholders, or what actually matters this quarter.

The biggest leverage comes from building a repeatable prompt for each document type you write regularly. As Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club's founders, puts it: "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here's how I want you to do it. Just go." That's the shift from using AI occasionally to using it efficiently.

[Using AI is a managerial relationship](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work): you provide the inputs, goals, and context, and you evaluate and shape the outputs. Professional writing is one of the clearest examples of that dynamic in action.

## Step 1: Gather your raw materials before you open Claude

The single most common mistake people make with AI writing is starting from nothing. They open Claude, type a vague request, and then wonder why the output is generic.

Before you prompt anything, collect:

- Your notes, meeting summaries, or source data
- The audience for the document (their role, their priorities, their objections)
- The outcome you want (decision, buy-in, information, action)
- The format expected (memo, deck, report, proposal, email)
- Any constraints (length, tone, confidentiality)

This takes five minutes and changes everything. When you paste that context into Claude, the output shifts from a generic template to something that reflects your actual situation.

## Step 2: Write a directive prompt, not a request

Most people ask AI to write things. What works better is directing a draft.

A request sounds like: "Write me a proposal for a new vendor."

A directive sounds like: "I need to write a proposal recommending we switch our project management software from Asana to Monday.com. The audience is our VP of Operations, who is skeptical about switching costs. My main arguments are: (1) we've had persistent sync issues with our current stack for six months, (2) Monday.com integrates directly with Salesforce, which would save my team about 3 hours a week, and (3) the price difference is $200/month. Please draft a 1-page recommendation memo in a direct, professional tone."

That second prompt gives Claude a decision to support, a skeptical audience to address, specific evidence to work with, and format constraints to follow. The draft it produces will be genuinely useful as a starting point.

## Step 3: Edit for your voice, add your judgment

The draft Claude produces will be structurally sound but will probably sound a little flat. That's expected. AI writing tends toward the smooth and the generic.

This is where your expertise comes in. Read through the draft and:

- Add specifics only you know (names, dates, dollar amounts, the history of the issue)
- Cut anything that sounds like it was written for anyone rather than for your reader
- Rewrite sentences that don't sound like you
- Add the judgment calls AI can't make ("what I didn't include here is the political context around our CFO's concerns")

The need for this editing work shows up clearly in how people describe using these tools. Brian Lightfoot, an MVP Club member who coaches executives, puts it this way: "When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I'm conscious: does this sound like AI?" That consciousness is the editing instinct you want to develop. The goal is for the final document to sound like you wrote it with a lot of help, not like a machine wrote it and you approved it.

## Step 4: Iterate in the same conversation

One of the most common mistakes is treating the first output as the final draft. AI writing tools are designed for back-and-forth. After you get the initial draft:

- "The second paragraph is too long. Tighten it to two sentences."
- "The tone is too formal for this audience. Make it more direct."
- "Add a section on implementation timeline between the cost section and the conclusion."
- "The recommendation at the end is too hedged. Make it clearer that I'm recommending we move forward."

Each of these follow-ups takes 10 seconds. The cumulative effect is a document that's shaped around your actual needs, not a generic template.

## The three document types where AI adds the most value

### Status reports and project updates

Status reports are ideal for AI assistance because they follow a predictable structure (what was accomplished, what's in progress, what's blocked, what's next) and require synthesis of information you already have. The bottleneck is assembly, not thinking. (If you manage projects specifically, the [AI for project managers](/learn/ai-for-project-managers) guide covers this in more depth.)

**The workflow:** At the end of the week, paste your task list, meeting notes, and any relevant updates into Claude. Ask it to structure a status report for your manager or stakeholders, and specify how long the update should be and what format they prefer (bullets vs. paragraphs, formal vs. casual).

Carmella Thompson, a real estate agent in our community (from an MVP Club session), built a version of this for her market reports. She automated the process of pulling weekly market data into a structured narrative for her clients, cutting the time she spent on it from about an hour to fifteen minutes. The insights were still hers. The assembly was Claude's.

### Recommendation memos and proposals

These documents require you to make a case. The structure is usually: here's the situation, here's what I'm recommending, here's the evidence, here's what it will cost, here's the next step. AI is very good at generating this structure once you give it the arguments.

The critical piece is the evidence. Claude can't know your organization's specific data or political context. You provide that. Claude helps you arrange it into an argument that holds together.

### Executive summaries

The hardest thing about executive summaries is knowing what to cut. You've spent weeks on a project or report, and condensing it to one page feels like losing something.

Ask Claude to read your full document and produce an executive summary focused on the three things a senior decision-maker needs to know. Then read what it gives you and ask yourself: "Did it pick the right three things?" Usually it gets two of them right. Your job is to correct the third and make sure the framing matches what your audience actually cares about.

## Common mistakes with AI writing tools

**Giving no context and expecting good output.** "Write me a quarterly business review" gives Claude nothing to work with. Paste your actual data, notes, and audience context first.

**Accepting AI structure without questioning it.** AI tends toward the same formats: intro, three points, conclusion. That structure might be wrong for your situation. Question whether the format serves your reader before you commit to it.

**Not editing for your voice.** A document that reads as AI-generated is a document that signals you didn't care enough to make it yours. The editing step is not optional. It's where the work becomes yours.

**Using it for high-stakes first drafts without understanding the limitations.** AI can introduce confident-sounding errors, especially when it speculates about data or specifics. Anything that matters should be verified. The tool speeds up drafting; it does not replace your judgment.

## Which AI tool is best for professional writing?

For most professional writing tasks, Claude is the better choice right now. Its outputs tend to be more structured and readable, it handles long documents well, and it follows complex instructions more reliably. For writing tasks specifically, Claude Pro ($20/month) is worth the cost if you're writing more than a few substantial documents per week.

ChatGPT is a strong alternative and many professionals prefer it for interactive back-and-forth on complex proposals. The honest answer is that the best AI tool for writing depends on your specific workflow, and the differences between them matter less than the quality of context and direction you provide.

Both tools have free tiers that are good enough to start with. Try your first report or proposal on the free version and see if it changes how you work. If it does, the paid tier is worth considering.

## From the practice: what we see in coaching

Every week in our community sessions, professionals share what they're building and where they're getting stuck. On writing tasks specifically, a few patterns show up regularly.

The "aha" moment usually isn't about prompting technique. It's the moment someone pastes their actual meeting notes or project data into Claude and realizes it can produce a coherent first draft from material they already have. The tool stops feeling abstract and starts feeling useful.

The second pattern is that people underestimate how much editing the draft needs. The AI draft is not the finished document. Professionals who get the best results treat it as scaffolding: useful for structure, requiring real editorial work to become something worth sending.

The writing workflows that save the most time are the ones built around repeatable prompts: context, audience, and format expectations captured once, then reused every time. Paste the inputs, review the draft, make it yours.

## Where to go from here

For your next step: pick one document you have to write this week. It could be a status update, a project brief, or a recommendation email. Gather your notes, write a directive prompt (not a request), paste in your context, and see what comes back. Edit it until it sounds like you sent it. That's the whole workflow.

Once you've done it once with a low-stakes document, you'll know exactly where AI helps and where your judgment is irreplaceable. That's more useful than any guide.

If you want to practice this alongside other professionals who are working through the same questions, that's what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions. We'd be glad to have you. Come find us at [/community](/community).

For further reading, the MIT Sloan Management Review has a useful overview of [AI writing and content creation tools](https://mitsloanedtech.mit.edu/ai/tools/) that covers both the capability landscape and the organizational considerations.
