The blank page is the enemy of professional writing. Most of the delay in writing a report or proposal has nothing to do with not knowing the content. You know the content. You were in the meeting, you have the data, you understand the situation. The delay is about getting started and then shaping what you know into the right structure.

AI is excellent at first drafts. Not because it writes better than you (it often doesn’t), but because it gives you something to react to. A mediocre draft you can edit is faster to work with than a blank screen.

A Harvard Business Review study (December 2024) found that professionals using AI writing tools completed writing tasks more than 50% faster, with quality ratings that met or exceeded the unassisted versions. (Source) The speed gain is real. But so is the catch: AI-generated text can introduce factual errors and a flat, generic tone that experienced readers notice immediately. Your job isn’t to delegate the writing. It’s to direct the draft and then make it yours.

The mental model: think of it as a first-draft machine

Before you open Claude or ChatGPT, the most useful shift is to stop thinking of AI as a writer and start thinking of it as a capable assistant who can produce a rough draft from your notes. The key phrase is “from your notes.”

The quality of what you get out is directly tied to the quality of what you put in. If you paste in your raw meeting notes, key decisions, audience context, and the outcome you need the document to achieve, you’ll get something useful. If you type “write me a quarterly report,” you’ll get a generic outline that doesn’t know your organization, your stakeholders, or what actually matters this quarter.

The biggest leverage comes from building a repeatable prompt for each document type you write regularly. As Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club’s founders, puts it: “Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here’s how I want you to do it. Just go.” That’s the shift from using AI occasionally to using it efficiently.

Using AI is a managerial relationship: you provide the inputs, goals, and context, and you evaluate and shape the outputs. Professional writing is one of the clearest examples of that dynamic in action.

Step 1: Gather your raw materials before you open Claude

The single most common mistake people make with AI writing is starting from nothing. They open Claude, type a vague request, and then wonder why the output is generic.

Before you prompt anything, collect:

  • Your notes, meeting summaries, or source data
  • The audience for the document (their role, their priorities, their objections)
  • The outcome you want (decision, buy-in, information, action)
  • The format expected (memo, deck, report, proposal, email)
  • Any constraints (length, tone, confidentiality)

This takes five minutes and changes everything. When you paste that context into Claude, the output shifts from a generic template to something that reflects your actual situation.

Step 2: Write a directive prompt, not a request

Most people ask AI to write things. What works better is directing a draft.

A request sounds like: “Write me a proposal for a new vendor.”

A directive sounds like: “I need to write a proposal recommending we switch our project management software from Asana to Monday.com. The audience is our VP of Operations, who is skeptical about switching costs. My main arguments are: (1) we’ve had persistent sync issues with our current stack for six months, (2) Monday.com integrates directly with Salesforce, which would save my team about 3 hours a week, and (3) the price difference is $200/month. Please draft a 1-page recommendation memo in a direct, professional tone.”

That second prompt gives Claude a decision to support, a skeptical audience to address, specific evidence to work with, and format constraints to follow. The draft it produces will be genuinely useful as a starting point.

Step 3: Edit for your voice, add your judgment

The draft Claude produces will be structurally sound but will probably sound a little flat. That’s expected. AI writing tends toward the smooth and the generic.

This is where your expertise comes in. Read through the draft and:

  • Add specifics only you know (names, dates, dollar amounts, the history of the issue)
  • Cut anything that sounds like it was written for anyone rather than for your reader
  • Rewrite sentences that don’t sound like you
  • Add the judgment calls AI can’t make (“what I didn’t include here is the political context around our CFO’s concerns”)

The need for this editing work shows up clearly in how people describe using these tools. Brian Lightfoot, an MVP Club member who coaches executives, puts it this way: “When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I’m conscious: does this sound like AI?” That consciousness is the editing instinct you want to develop. The goal is for the final document to sound like you wrote it with a lot of help, not like a machine wrote it and you approved it.

Step 4: Iterate in the same conversation

One of the most common mistakes is treating the first output as the final draft. AI writing tools are designed for back-and-forth. After you get the initial draft:

  • “The second paragraph is too long. Tighten it to two sentences.”
  • “The tone is too formal for this audience. Make it more direct.”
  • “Add a section on implementation timeline between the cost section and the conclusion.”
  • “The recommendation at the end is too hedged. Make it clearer that I’m recommending we move forward.”

Each of these follow-ups takes 10 seconds. The cumulative effect is a document that’s shaped around your actual needs, not a generic template.

The three document types where AI adds the most value

Status reports and project updates

Status reports are ideal for AI assistance because they follow a predictable structure (what was accomplished, what’s in progress, what’s blocked, what’s next) and require synthesis of information you already have. The bottleneck is assembly, not thinking. (If you manage projects specifically, the AI for project managers guide covers this in more depth.)

The workflow: At the end of the week, paste your task list, meeting notes, and any relevant updates into Claude. Ask it to structure a status report for your manager or stakeholders, and specify how long the update should be and what format they prefer (bullets vs. paragraphs, formal vs. casual).

Carmella Thompson, a real estate agent in our community (from an MVP Club session), built a version of this for her market reports. She automated the process of pulling weekly market data into a structured narrative for her clients, cutting the time she spent on it from about an hour to fifteen minutes. The insights were still hers. The assembly was Claude’s.

Recommendation memos and proposals

These documents require you to make a case. The structure is usually: here’s the situation, here’s what I’m recommending, here’s the evidence, here’s what it will cost, here’s the next step. AI is very good at generating this structure once you give it the arguments.

The critical piece is the evidence. Claude can’t know your organization’s specific data or political context. You provide that. Claude helps you arrange it into an argument that holds together.

Executive summaries

The hardest thing about executive summaries is knowing what to cut. You’ve spent weeks on a project or report, and condensing it to one page feels like losing something.

Ask Claude to read your full document and produce an executive summary focused on the three things a senior decision-maker needs to know. Then read what it gives you and ask yourself: “Did it pick the right three things?” Usually it gets two of them right. Your job is to correct the third and make sure the framing matches what your audience actually cares about.

Common mistakes with AI writing tools

Giving no context and expecting good output. “Write me a quarterly business review” gives Claude nothing to work with. Paste your actual data, notes, and audience context first.

Accepting AI structure without questioning it. AI tends toward the same formats: intro, three points, conclusion. That structure might be wrong for your situation. Question whether the format serves your reader before you commit to it.

Not editing for your voice. A document that reads as AI-generated is a document that signals you didn’t care enough to make it yours. The editing step is not optional. It’s where the work becomes yours.

Using it for high-stakes first drafts without understanding the limitations. AI can introduce confident-sounding errors, especially when it speculates about data or specifics. Anything that matters should be verified. The tool speeds up drafting; it does not replace your judgment.

Which AI tool is best for professional writing?

For most professional writing tasks, Claude is the better choice right now. Its outputs tend to be more structured and readable, it handles long documents well, and it follows complex instructions more reliably. For writing tasks specifically, Claude Pro ($20/month) is worth the cost if you’re writing more than a few substantial documents per week.

ChatGPT is a strong alternative and many professionals prefer it for interactive back-and-forth on complex proposals. The honest answer is that the best AI tool for writing depends on your specific workflow, and the differences between them matter less than the quality of context and direction you provide.

Both tools have free tiers that are good enough to start with. Try your first report or proposal on the free version and see if it changes how you work. If it does, the paid tier is worth considering.

From the practice: what we see in coaching

Every week in our community sessions, professionals share what they’re building and where they’re getting stuck. On writing tasks specifically, a few patterns show up regularly.

The “aha” moment usually isn’t about prompting technique. It’s the moment someone pastes their actual meeting notes or project data into Claude and realizes it can produce a coherent first draft from material they already have. The tool stops feeling abstract and starts feeling useful.

The second pattern is that people underestimate how much editing the draft needs. The AI draft is not the finished document. Professionals who get the best results treat it as scaffolding: useful for structure, requiring real editorial work to become something worth sending.

The writing workflows that save the most time are the ones built around repeatable prompts: context, audience, and format expectations captured once, then reused every time. Paste the inputs, review the draft, make it yours.

Where to go from here

For your next step: pick one document you have to write this week. It could be a status update, a project brief, or a recommendation email. Gather your notes, write a directive prompt (not a request), paste in your context, and see what comes back. Edit it until it sounds like you sent it. That’s the whole workflow.

Once you’ve done it once with a low-stakes document, you’ll know exactly where AI helps and where your judgment is irreplaceable. That’s more useful than any guide.

If you want to practice this alongside other professionals who are working through the same questions, that’s what happens in MVP Club’s weekly sessions. We’d be glad to have you. Come find us at /community.

For further reading, the MIT Sloan Management Review has a useful overview of AI writing and content creation tools that covers both the capability landscape and the organizational considerations.

AI in 10 Minutes: What You Actually Need to Know (and What You Can Skip)
The no-jargon orientation for getting started with AI at work. What it actually does, which tools matter, and where to spend your first 10 minutes.

You have 10 minutes, a vague sense that you should be doing something with AI, and no idea where to start. That is a completely normal place to be. Here is the shortest version of what actually matters.

This is not a full guide. You will find those elsewhere (including [a detailed day-by-day practice plan for your first week with AI](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work)). This is just the orientation: what AI actually does, what to ignore for now, and where to put your first 10 minutes.

## What AI actually is (the only explanation you need right now)

Claude and ChatGPT are text tools. You type something, they respond. What makes them different from search is that they can read, write, summarize, draft, think through, and respond in natural language, including your language, about your specific situation.

You don't need to know how they work. You need to know what to ask them to do.

Think of it like this: you have a new colleague who is extremely well-read, has no ego, never gets tired, and will attempt any task you give them on the first try. They need direction, though. They do not know your job, your boss, your company's tone, or what you are trying to accomplish. Your job is to give them that context. Their job is to do the work.

That mental model, of managing a capable but context-free collaborator, will serve you better than any prompt hack or tutorial you can find.

## What you can skip (for now)

There is a lot of AI noise. Here is what you do not need to worry about this week:

**Prompt engineering.** You do not need to learn any formal prompting frameworks. Write to Claude or ChatGPT the same way you would write to a smart colleague in a message. Be specific, give context, say what you want. That is it.

**Which model is best.** Claude and ChatGPT are both excellent for professional work. Pick one and start. You can compare them later. [MATT REVIEW: should we name Claude as the slight preference here based on coaching experience?]

**Automation and agents.** You will eventually get to AI workflows that run on their own or handle repetitive tasks end-to-end. Not this week. This week, you are just having conversations.

**The existential stuff.** Whether AI will change your career in five years is a real question worth thinking about, but it has no bearing on whether you should open Claude today.

## The only three things to try in your first 10 minutes

### 1. Paste something real into Claude

Open Claude (claude.ai) or ChatGPT (chatgpt.com). Do not try to think of something interesting to ask. Instead, paste in something from your actual work today. A meeting agenda. A document you need to read. An email you are not sure how to respond to.

Then ask a specific question about it: "What are the two or three things I need to act on here?" or "What is the main point of this?" or "Draft a response that agrees with the core ask but pushes back on the timeline."

The first time you paste real work context into one of these tools and get a useful response, something clicks. The tool becomes real instead of abstract. This is the step most people skip, because they try to come up with a test prompt instead of just using their actual work.

### 2. Ask it to write something you would normally write yourself

Pick one thing on your to-do list that involves writing: an email, a summary, a bullet-point update, a short document. Give Claude the context (who you are writing for, what the situation is, what you want to accomplish, what tone fits) and ask it to write a first draft.

Read the draft. It will probably not be exactly right. Edit it. The key insight is this: editing is much faster than writing from scratch. Most people find they can get to a finished draft in half the time by starting from something imperfect rather than starting from nothing.

> "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, hey, here's how I want you to do it. Just go." — Matt Hastings, from a recent MVP Club community session on building repeatable workflows with Claude

### 3. Ask it to help you think through something

This is the one that surprises people. AI tools are genuinely useful as thinking partners, not just drafting machines.

If you are stuck on a decision, a conversation you are dreading, or a project that is not moving, describe the situation to Claude and ask it to help you think through the angles you might be missing. Not "make the decision for me." More like: "Here is what I am weighing, here are my constraints, here is what I am worried about. What am I not seeing?"

You will not agree with everything it surfaces. That is fine. The value is in the friction, the moment where you read something and think "no, that is wrong, because..." because that is you clarifying your own thinking.

## What happens after 10 minutes

Those three exercises take about 10 minutes combined. Here is what you are likely to notice:

One of them worked better than the others for your specific situation. That is your starting point. The next day, do that one thing again. Build the habit before you expand the use cases.

The thing that will not happen: you will not feel ready. You will not feel like you have figured it out. The learning with these tools does not come from reading about them or watching videos. It comes from using them with your actual work every day, seeing what works, and getting a little better each time.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing the first task where Claude or ChatGPT actually saved them time, with specific detail about what they were working on]

## What you can safely ignore for months

Just so you have permission: you do not need to understand large language models. You do not need to take a course. You do not need a system for organizing your prompts. You do not need to read every AI newsletter.

The gap between people who are getting real value from these tools and people who are not is almost never a knowledge gap. It is a practice gap. People who use Claude or ChatGPT with their real work every day get better fast. People who read about using it do not.

This is what we mean when we say [AI is a do-first, understand-later tool](/blog/what-to-actually-do-about-something-big-is-happening). The understanding arrives through doing, not through preparation.

## One last thing: the $20 question

The free versions of Claude and ChatGPT are good enough to start. If you find yourself using one of them daily and hitting limits, the paid tier ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) is meaningfully better: faster, higher quality responses, longer conversations, and access to the best available models.

It is worth it once you have established the habit. Do not spend the $20 before you have opened the tool at least five times. Earn it by building the habit first.

## Your actual next step

Open claude.ai right now. Paste in something from your work today. Ask one question about it.

That is the whole orientation. If you want to go deeper after that, the [pillar page for Getting Started with AI](/learn) has a week-by-week practice plan, a guide on [what AI can and cannot do honestly](/blog/what-80-non-technical-people-proved-about-ai), and more resources for building AI into your daily work.

Most professionals who get good at this did not start with a course or a certification. They started with one real task, on one real day, and kept going from there. You can too.

If you want to practice alongside people who are figuring this out at the same time, that is what MVP Club exists for. It is a community of professionals who believe the best way to learn something new is by doing it together. Come see what we are building at [mvpclub.ai/community](/community).

AI Jargon Decoded: The Only Terms That Matter for Non-Technical People
Plain-English definitions for 22 AI terms non-technical professionals actually encounter, with a 'why it matters' note for each one.

Every time I run a session with new MVP Club members, someone apologizes before asking a question: "Sorry, this is probably basic, but what does X even mean?" Spoiler: it's never basic. The AI world has developed its own vocabulary fast, and half the time even the people using the words can't agree on what they mean.

This is the list I wish existed when I started. Twenty-two terms you'll actually encounter as a non-technical professional, in plain English, with a note on why each one matters for your real work.

Nothing here requires a CS degree. A few of these I used wrong for months before someone corrected me. That's fine. You learn by doing.

## The Foundational Terms

### Large Language Model (LLM)

**What it means:** The underlying technology behind tools like Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini. An LLM is trained on enormous amounts of text and learns patterns in language well enough to generate coherent, contextually appropriate responses.

**Why it matters for your work:** You don't need to understand how LLMs work to use them well. But knowing the term helps you parse news articles and vendor pitches. When someone says "we're building on top of an LLM," they mean they're using an existing model (like Claude or GPT-4) as the engine under their product.

### Model

**What it means:** The specific version of an AI you're talking to. Claude Sonnet, Claude Opus, GPT-4o, and Gemini Pro are all different models, each with different strengths, speeds, and price points.

**Why it matters for your work:** Different models are better at different things. Opus and GPT-4o tend to reason more carefully but take longer and cost more. Sonnet is faster and cheaper, which is fine for most everyday tasks. When Claude asks you to choose a model, this is what it's asking. I tell people to start with whatever is selected by default and only change it if something feels off.

### Prompt

**What it means:** The message or instruction you send to an AI. It's your side of the conversation.

**Why it matters for your work:** Better prompts get better results. A prompt is not a search query. You're not Google-ing for a keyword. You're giving instructions to a capable assistant who needs context to do good work. "Write an email" is a prompt. "Write a follow-up email to a client who missed our third check-in call. Tone should be professional but warm. The goal is to reschedule without making them feel bad about the no-shows" is a much better prompt.

### Context

**What it means:** All the information available to an AI in your current conversation: your messages, documents you've shared, prior conversation history, and any background instructions. Context is what the AI knows as it generates its response.

**Why it matters for your work:** This is the concept that unlocks most of the AI frustration I see. People give AI very little context ("write me a report") and then wonder why they get generic output. The more relevant context you provide (your audience, your goals, the situation, existing materials), the more useful the response. Think of it as briefing a contractor before they start a job.

### Context Window

**What it means:** The maximum amount of text an AI can process in a single conversation. Think of it as the AI's working memory. If your conversation gets very long or you paste in a huge document, you might bump up against this limit.

**Why it matters for your work:** Most of the time you'll never hit this limit in normal use. But if you're pasting in long documents or having very long back-and-forth sessions, you might notice the AI start to forget things from earlier in the conversation. That's the context window running out. The fix is usually to start a fresh conversation and include a brief summary of what you were working on.

---

## How AI Actually Works

### Token

**What it means:** The unit AI models use to measure text. Roughly speaking, one token equals about three-quarters of a word. "Hello there" is about three tokens. AI pricing is usually calculated per thousand tokens (both input and output).

**Why it matters for your work:** If you have a paid API subscription or you're building AI workflows that process a lot of text, token counts affect your bill. For most people using Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus at $20/month, tokens are mostly invisible. Worth knowing if you're ever asked "how many tokens does this take?"

### Training Data

**What it means:** The text an AI model was trained on before you ever talked to it. LLMs learn from reading an enormous amount of human-written text: websites, books, articles, code, and more.

**Why it matters for your work:** This is why AI knows things without you telling it. It's also why AI has a knowledge cutoff date (it stopped learning at some point, usually six to twelve months before you're using it). When you ask Claude what happened last week, it genuinely might not know. That's not a bug. It's a feature of how training works.

### Hallucination

**What it means:** When an AI generates something that sounds confident and plausible but is factually wrong. The model produces the most statistically likely next words, and sometimes that leads it somewhere that sounds right but isn't.

**Why it matters for your work:** This is the one that trips people up most. AI will cite a book that doesn't exist, invent a statistic, or confidently describe a policy that was changed two years ago. The output will read exactly like correct information. Your job is to verify anything important before acting on it, especially specific facts, numbers, names, and dates. I check anything that matters. Treat AI like a very smart intern who sometimes gets confident about things they're making up.

### Temperature

**What it means:** A setting that controls how "creative" or "random" an AI's responses are. High temperature means more varied, creative, sometimes unpredictable output. Low temperature means more focused, predictable, conservative output.

**Why it matters for your work:** Most consumer tools like Claude and ChatGPT handle this for you. But if you're using an API directly or configuring a custom AI tool, someone might ask you about temperature. For professional writing and research tasks, lower is usually better. For brainstorming or creative work, higher can be useful.

### Parameters (or Weights)

**What it means:** The internal numerical values that make up a trained AI model. When someone says a model has "70 billion parameters," they're describing how complex the model's internal structure is.

**Why it matters for your work:** Mostly you don't need this one. But when tech articles compare models by saying "it has more parameters," they're roughly equating more parameters with more capability (with many asterisks). Knowing the term keeps you from feeling lost in AI news coverage.

---

## The Tools You'll Actually Use

### Generative AI

**What it means:** AI that creates new content rather than just analyzing or classifying existing content. Text, images, audio, video, code. Claude generating an email draft is generative AI. A spam filter is not.

**Why it matters for your work:** Most of the AI you'll use at work is generative AI. When people say "I used AI to do this," they almost always mean generative AI. The distinction matters because this category of AI has specific limitations (it can hallucinate, it requires iteration) that classification AI doesn't have in the same way.

### Foundation Model

**What it means:** A large, general-purpose AI model trained on broad data that can be adapted or "built on top of" for specific applications. GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini are all foundation models.

**Why it matters for your work:** When a company says "our product is powered by GPT-4" or "we use Claude under the hood," they mean they've built a specific application on top of a foundation model. The foundation model does the heavy lifting; their product wraps it in a specific interface or workflow. This is useful context when evaluating AI tools at work.

### API (Application Programming Interface)

**What it means:** A way for software systems to talk to each other. When a company builds an app that uses Claude, they're calling Anthropic's API. You send text in, you get text out, programmatically.

**Why it matters for your work:** If you're a non-technical user, you probably don't call APIs directly. But you use products built on APIs every day. Knowing the term helps you understand vendor pricing structures ("we charge $X per API call") and why different AI tools have usage limits. If your team wants to embed AI into an internal tool, someone will need to work with APIs.

### Fine-Tuning

**What it means:** Taking an existing foundation model and training it further on a specific, smaller dataset to make it better at a particular task or domain. A legal firm might fine-tune a model on thousands of contracts to make it better at legal reasoning.

**Why it matters for your work:** If your organization is evaluating enterprise AI tools, fine-tuning is how vendors customize models for your industry. It's also expensive and usually only makes sense for very specific, repetitive tasks at scale. For most professionals, fine-tuning is not something you'll do yourself, but you'll hear the word in vendor pitches.

### RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

**What it means:** A technique where an AI can search through a specific set of documents (your company files, a knowledge base, a database) before generating a response. Instead of relying only on what it was trained on, it retrieves relevant documents first, then answers based on them.

**Why it matters for your work:** This is how enterprise AI tools like "chat with your documents" products work. When you ask an AI "what does our company policy say about remote work?" and it finds the right answer in your actual HR documents instead of guessing, that's RAG. Understanding this helps you evaluate whether an AI tool is "grounded" in your real company data or just making things up.

---

## The Output and Quality Terms

### Inference

**What it means:** The process of running a trained model to generate a response. When you send a message to Claude and it responds, that's inference happening.

**Why it matters for your work:** You'll see this term in AI pricing and performance discussions. "Inference cost" is what you're actually paying for when you use a model. "Inference speed" is how fast the model responds. Not something you need to think about daily, but useful when comparing tools or reviewing vendor contracts.

### Output / Completion

**What it means:** The AI's response to your prompt. Also called a "completion" because technically the AI is completing a sequence of text. Both words mean the same thing in practice.

**Why it matters for your work:** Just vocabulary. When someone says "evaluate the output" or "review the completion," they mean: read what the AI gave you and decide if it's good. The key insight about AI outputs is that the first one is almost never the final one. Iteration is normal, not failure. [That's the difference between working with AI and working with traditional software](/blog/probabilistic-vs-deterministic-ai).

### Iteration

**What it means:** Going back and forth with an AI to refine an output. You respond to what it gave you, point out what's off, ask for changes, and repeat until you get something useful.

**Why it matters for your work:** This is probably the most important concept on this list that gets zero coverage in the mainstream press. Most people think AI should get it right on the first try. It rarely does. And that's not a failure. It's how the tool is designed to work. Asking "make it shorter and less formal" is a completely normal next step, not a sign that AI didn't work.

### System Prompt

**What it means:** Background instructions given to an AI before the conversation starts, usually by whoever built the product you're using. The system prompt shapes how the AI behaves: its persona, its constraints, what it will and won't do.

**Why it matters for your work:** When you use a company-built AI tool and it has a specific personality or set of behaviors, that's the system prompt at work. If you're building AI workflows for your team (or working with someone who is), system prompts are how you give the AI standing instructions it uses every time, without you having to repeat yourself. I use system prompts in Claude projects to tell Claude who I am, what I'm working on, and how I like responses formatted.

---

## The Organizational Terms

### Agentic AI / AI Agent

**What it means:** An AI that can take multi-step actions on its own, rather than just responding to a single message. An AI agent might receive a goal ("research this topic and summarize three articles"), break it into steps, execute them (search, read, write), and return a finished result.

**Why it matters for your work:** This is where AI is heading fast. Tools like Claude's computer use feature, or AI that can browse the web, draft a document, and send an email without you managing every step, are agentic. You'll see this term increasingly in product announcements. The honest version: most agentic tools are still early and require supervision. Worth knowing about but not something to bet your workflow on yet.

### Multimodal

**What it means:** An AI that can work with multiple types of input, not just text. A multimodal model can look at an image, listen to audio, or read a chart, in addition to processing text.

**Why it matters for your work:** Claude and GPT-4o are both multimodal. You can take a photo of a whiteboard after a meeting and ask Claude to transcribe and organize the notes. You can upload a chart from a report and ask it to explain what it shows. These use cases are genuinely useful and most people don't realize they exist.

### MCP (Model Context Protocol)

**What it means:** A standard for connecting AI models to external tools and data sources. When an AI can check your calendar, query a database, or interact with another app, MCP is often part of how that connection works.

**Why it matters for your work:** If your company is building internal AI tools, or if you're using advanced Claude features in Claude Code, you'll see MCP come up. For most everyday users, MCP is invisible. But it's the reason AI can feel increasingly integrated into your actual work systems rather than being a separate chat window you copy-paste between.

---

## A Note on the Terminology Explosion

The list above is longer than I wanted it to be. And honestly, it'll be outdated within six months. New terms appear every time a major model is released. Old terms get redefined when vendors find a buzzier word for the same concept.

The pattern I've seen in coaching sessions with dozens of non-technical professionals: the people who get the most from these tools aren't the ones who mastered the vocabulary first. They're the ones who started using the tools and picked up the vocabulary as they went.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing how they felt about AI jargon early on, and what changed once they just started using the tools]

Matt says it well: "All these companies are hyper competing right now. They all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?" The jargon serves them more than it serves you. Your job is to figure out what's useful for your actual work.

If you want to see where these terms show up in practice, the [how-to guide for using AI at work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) walks through real use cases where you'll encounter most of them. And if you want to understand the underlying concept that makes iteration (not prompting) the real skill, the post on [probabilistic vs. deterministic thinking](/blog/probabilistic-vs-deterministic-ai) is the one I point people to most.

Start with the terms on this list you're already hearing. Look up the rest when you need them. The vocabulary gets less intimidating the more you actually use the tools.

## You Don't Have to Know All of This

Professionals who've worked with AI every day for the past year still ask me what half these terms mean. That's normal. The field moves faster than anyone can track.

What matters is that you know enough to not feel lost when a vendor or a colleague drops these words into a meeting. You know enough to ask a follow-up question instead of nodding along. And you know enough to start using [Claude](https://claude.ai) or [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com) on a real task today, without waiting until you've studied up.

The vocabulary will follow from the doing. It always does.

If you want to work through these concepts with people who are in the same place you are, that's what our community sessions are for. Plenty of folks in [MVP Club](/community) came in not knowing what an LLM was and are now explaining RAG to their colleagues. The understanding comes through practice, not preparation.

System Prompt

What it means: Background instructions given to an AI before the conversation starts, usually by whoever built the product you’re using. The system prompt shapes how the AI behaves: its persona, its constraints, what it will and won’t do.

Why it matters for your work: When you use a company-built AI tool and it has a specific personality or set of behaviors, that’s the system prompt at work. If you’re building AI workflows for your team (or working with someone who is), system prompts are how you give the AI standing instructions it uses every time, without you having to repeat yourself. I use system prompts in Claude projects to tell Claude who I am, what I’m working on, and how I like responses formatted.

The Organizational Terms

Agentic AI / AI Agent

What it means: An AI that can take multi-step actions on its own, rather than just responding to a single message. An AI agent might receive a goal (“research this topic and summarize three articles”), break it into steps, execute them (search, read, write), and return a finished result.

Why it matters for your work: This is where AI is heading fast. Tools like Claude’s computer use feature, or AI that can browse the web, draft a document, and send an email without you managing every step, are agentic. You’ll see this term increasingly in product announcements. The honest version: most agentic tools are still early and require supervision. Worth knowing about but not something to bet your workflow on yet.

Multimodal

What it means: An AI that can work with multiple types of input, not just text. A multimodal model can look at an image, listen to audio, or read a chart, in addition to processing text.

Why it matters for your work: Claude and GPT-4o are both multimodal. You can take a photo of a whiteboard after a meeting and ask Claude to transcribe and organize the notes. You can upload a chart from a report and ask it to explain what it shows. These use cases are genuinely useful and most people don’t realize they exist.

MCP (Model Context Protocol)

What it means: A standard for connecting AI models to external tools and data sources. When an AI can check your calendar, query a database, or interact with another app, MCP is often part of how that connection works.

Why it matters for your work: If your company is building internal AI tools, or if you’re using advanced Claude features in Claude Code, you’ll see MCP come up. For most everyday users, MCP is invisible. But it’s the reason AI can feel increasingly integrated into your actual work systems rather than being a separate chat window you copy-paste between.

A Note on the Terminology Explosion

The list above is longer than I wanted it to be. And honestly, it’ll be outdated within six months. New terms appear every time a major model is released. Old terms get redefined when vendors find a buzzier word for the same concept.

The pattern I’ve seen in coaching sessions with dozens of non-technical professionals: the people who get the most from these tools aren’t the ones who mastered the vocabulary first. They’re the ones who started using the tools and picked up the vocabulary as they went.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing how they felt about AI jargon early on, and what changed once they just started using the tools]

Matt says it well: “All these companies are hyper competing right now. They all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?” The jargon serves them more than it serves you. Your job is to figure out what’s useful for your actual work.

If you want to see where these terms show up in practice, the how-to guide for using AI at work walks through real use cases where you’ll encounter most of them. And if you want to understand the underlying concept that makes iteration (not prompting) the real skill, the post on probabilistic vs. deterministic thinking is the one I point people to most.

Start with the terms on this list you’re already hearing. Look up the rest when you need them. The vocabulary gets less intimidating the more you actually use the tools.

You Don’t Have to Know All of This

Professionals who’ve worked with AI every day for the past year still ask me what half these terms mean. That’s normal. The field moves faster than anyone can track.

What matters is that you know enough to not feel lost when a vendor or a colleague drops these words into a meeting. You know enough to ask a follow-up question instead of nodding along. And you know enough to start using Claude or ChatGPT on a real task today, without waiting until you’ve studied up.

The vocabulary will follow from the doing. It always does.

If you want to work through these concepts with people who are in the same place you are, that’s what our community sessions are for. Plenty of folks in MVP Club came in not knowing what an LLM was and are now explaining RAG to their colleagues. The understanding comes through practice, not preparation.

You Don’t Have to Know All of This
The AI Learning Path: What to Focus on First, Second, and Third
A clear progression from absolute beginner to confident AI practitioner. No jargon, no overwhelm. Here's the order that actually works.

Most people try to start learning AI the same way they'd approach a new app: find the feature list, click through the menus, figure out what everything does. That approach works great for Excel. It doesn't work for AI. There's so much it can do that lies beneath the surface, and it's activated through the completely free-form "menu" of a conversation. This is the path we walk people through in our Getting Started series. Not the only path, but the one that consistently gets mid-career professionals from "I've barely touched this thing" to "I use this every day and it's changed how I work." Three phases, built on each other.

The learning path

Three phases from "I've barely touched this" to "I use this every day."

1 WEEKS 1–2

Get a Repeatable Win

One real win on real work. Evidence beats theory.

2 WEEKS 3–6

Build the Habit

20 minutes a day, five days a week, on actual tasks.

3 MONTH 2+

Expand Your Range

AI becomes part of your toolkit, not a separate project.

Each phase builds on the one before. Consistency is the lever.

## Phase 1: Get a Repeatable Win The single goal of phase one is not to "understand AI." It is to have one experience where AI saves you real time on real work. One. That's it. This matters because motivation runs on evidence, not theory. Reading about AI is not the same as using AI. The best predictor of whether someone builds a lasting habit with these tools is whether they got a concrete win in the first two weeks. ### What to do in phase one Pick one task you do regularly, that has a clear output, and that you've done enough times that you'd know immediately if the result was good or bad. Good candidates: drafting a routine email, summarizing a meeting agenda before a call, generating a first outline for a report you're already writing. Open Claude or ChatGPT (either works here; most pro tiers start at $20/month, but the free version is fine to start). Give it real context about your actual situation, not a test scenario. Then evaluate what comes back. The point is not to submit whatever the tool produces. The point is to notice how long it would have taken you to produce that same draft starting from scratch, versus how long it took you to edit what the AI gave you. That gap is your first win. > "All these companies are hyper competing right now... they all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?" — Matt Hastings, in a Getting Started session with community members That question is the right frame for phase one. Not "what can AI do?" but "what do I want it to do for me?" ### What not to do in phase one Don't start with your most complex, high-stakes work. Don't try to automate an entire workflow. Don't spend time reading about AI instead of using it. AI is a practice skill, not a knowledge skill: the understanding comes through doing, not through preparation. Don't expect it to match your own output quality right away either. It needs training, just like you did! It needs to gain from your experience. You teach it to approach your skill level through repetition and refinement. It can help push your own skill level higher, but at the end of the day, ## Phase 2: Build the Habit Once you have one win, the work of phase two is to stop treating AI as something you "try" and start treating it as something you reach for. Regardless of whether AI can do the task from start to finish, bring it to the conversation anyway. Treat it like your new intern, don't freeze it out of the interesting stuff. This is harder than it sounds. New behaviors require repetition before they feel natural. Most people who abandon AI tools do it between weeks two and four, right after the novelty wears off and before the habit has set. Phase two is about surviving that gap. ### The 20-minute daily practice What works is small and consistent: 20 minutes a day, on real tasks, five days a week. Not a course. Not a weekend deep dive. Daily contact with the tool, using your actual work. A few work styles to mix and match for AI experimentation: 1. **Drafting:** Take one thing you need to write this week and use Claude or ChatGPT to get a first draft. Edit it until it sounds like you. 2. **Synthesizing:** Paste a document you need to process (a report, a proposal, meeting notes) and ask a specific question about it. Not "summarize this." Something like: "What are the three biggest risks in this proposal?" 3. **Thought Partner:** Bring a problem you're stuck on to Claude. Describe it in detail. Ask for angles you might be missing. Push back on what it gives you, and make sure you're not just looking for confirmation bias. I like to lie to it and frame my position as "a teammate says this, but I'm not sure. What do you think?" That way it resists the temptation to flatter me. The variety matters. If you only ever use AI for drafting, you develop a narrow sense of what it can do. Rotating through different task types in phase two is how you start to see the fuller picture. ### What you're building in phase two You're building two things simultaneously: a habit (reaching for the tool without thinking about it) and a mental model (a feel for what kinds of tasks AI handles well versus where your own judgment is irreplaceable). By the end of phase two, most people have 3-4 use cases that reliably save them time. They also have a growing sense of where AI falls short, which is just as valuable. Knowing when NOT to use the tool is part of getting good at the tool. ## Phase 3: Expand Your Range, Build Something Phase three is where the interesting stuff happens. By now you have the habit, you have the mental model, and you start to notice something: AI belongs in places you hadn't considered. This phase is less about following a plan and more about paying attention. When you hit a task that used to feel tedious, your brain now automatically asks: "Could Claude help with this?" That's the shift you're looking for. You can also start to try building something from scratch, also known as [Vibe Coding](/learn/what-is-vibe-coding). An internal tool, a helpful new excel spreadsheet, a repeatable bank of prompts leading to a full workflow...try building something as a tool others can use without having to copy-paste your prompts. ### What expands in phase three **Prompting gets better.** You stop writing prompts that say "write me a summary" and start writing prompts that say "here is a 12-page proposal, here is my role on this project, and here are the three questions my director will ask in tomorrow's review. What should I focus on?" The difference in output quality is significant, and you won't understand it until you've lived it. You start using the "Prompt for Prompt" technique, realizing you can just ask the LLM for a better prompt for your main task before proceeding to the main task session itself! **Context management becomes a skill.** You start giving AI background about who you are, your work context, your audience, and your constraints before asking for anything. This is the managerial relationship in practice: you're managing inputs, goals, and output quality the way you'd manage a capable new hire who doesn't know your world yet. **You start seeing building as possible.** Some people in phase three start wondering if they could build something with AI, not just use it. A small internal tool. A custom workflow. A personal site. This is the point where vibe coding starts to feel like something worth trying, and that's its own whole path. ### The honest timeline I want to say something real about timing, because a lot of AI content either overpromises ("transform your workflow in a week!") or vaguely reassures ("just stick with it"). Neither is useful. Here's what the progression actually looks like for most people we coach: **Weeks 1-2:** One or two wins. Still feels like effort. You're choosing to use AI; it's not automatic yet. **Weeks 3-6:** A handful of reliable use cases. Reaching for the tool more naturally. Still getting surprised by what works and what doesn't. **Month 2-3:** AI feels like part of your toolkit rather than a separate project. You're in conversations at work about how you're using it. You have opinions about which tool to use for which tasks. **Month 4 and beyond:** You stop thinking about "using AI." You just work. Sometimes with AI, sometimes without it. The question is no longer "should I use AI for this?" It's "what's the fastest way to get a good result?" That timeline is for people who practice consistently. Consistency is the lever. Someone who spends 20 minutes a day for six weeks will outpace someone who spends a full weekend every month. ## A note on which tools to use Phase one: Claude or ChatGPT. Either works. If you want one recommendation, Claude handles professional writing well and tends to follow complex instructions more carefully. Start there. Phase two: Same tools, but consider the paid tier. Claude Pro and ChatGPT Plus are each $20/month. For the amount of time you'll be saving, it's worth it. You get better reasoning, longer conversations, and access to the best current models. Phase three: Still Claude and ChatGPT as your core, but you'll start developing opinions about when each is better for specific tasks. You may also start exploring tools that specialize in particular workflows, like Perplexity for research or Claude Code for building things. Don't try to evaluate every AI tool that launches. There will always be a new one, and most people who try to keep up with every announcement spend more time reading about AI than using it. Pick your core tools, get good at them, then expand. ## The honest truth about the path There is no shortcut from "I've barely tried this" to "I use AI every day and it's changed how I work." The path goes through actually doing it. This is genuinely good news, by the way, not a warning. It means the advantage isn't available to the person who reads the most or knows the most. It's available to whoever shows up and practices. AI is for all of us, and the people getting the most from it right now are not the most technical. They're the most persistent.

What actually predicts who gets good at AI

It's not what most people think.

DOESN'T PREDICT SUCCESS

Knowing the most

  • Tech background
  • Reading every new announcement
  • Prior programming experience
  • Technical vocabulary
→
DOES PREDICT SUCCESS

Showing up

  • 20 minutes a day
  • Real tasks, real context
  • Weeks of repetition
  • Noticing what works, what doesn't

AI belongs to whoever shows up and practices, not whoever knows the most.

If you want to practice alongside other professionals working through the same phases, that's exactly what happens in [MVP Club's weekly sessions](/community). People share what they're building, get unstuck, and pick up techniques from watching how others work. The progression goes faster when you're not figuring it out alone.

How to Talk About AI on Your Resume (and LinkedIn)
Practical guidance for mid-career professionals on how to present AI skills on a resume and LinkedIn profile without overpromising or underselling.

Most people either leave AI off their resume entirely or list "ChatGPT" under skills and hope for the best. Both approaches are leaving something on the table.

If you've been using AI tools regularly at work (even informally, even imperfectly), you have more to say than you think. The challenge isn't whether to include it. The challenge is figuring out how to describe it in a way that's honest, specific, and actually lands with the person reading your resume.

This isn't a guide to gaming the system. It's a guide to getting credit for the work you're already doing.

## Why "Familiar with AI Tools" Doesn't Cut It Anymore

The bar has moved. According to a 2026 [hiring insights report from ResumeGenius](https://resumegenius.com/blog/job-hunting/hiring-insights-report), more than 60% of hiring managers say they want to test, discuss, or see evidence of AI capabilities rather than take resume claims at face value. [NEEDS CITATION: verify this is independent research, not marketing] Simply listing vague AI claims doesn't tell a reader anything useful.

What hiring managers actually want to know: Can you apply AI to the work? Can you describe what you've done with it? Are you someone who uses AI as part of how you work, or someone who took a webinar about it once?

The good news is that if you've been using Claude, ChatGPT, or any other tool to draft documents, research, prep for meetings, or speed up your workflows, you already have specifics to work with.

## The Framework: What, How, and So What

Before you touch your resume, do this exercise. For each AI use in your work, fill in three things:

**What** tool did you use? (Claude, ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Perplexity)

**How** did you use it? (Drafting first passes on reports, generating meeting summaries, building outreach sequences, analyzing feedback data, creating first-draft training content)

**So what?** What was the outcome? (Shortened a process from three days to one, cut document review time in half, expanded output volume without adding headcount)

This framework keeps you grounded. You're not claiming to be an AI expert. You're describing what you actually did and what happened because of it. That combination (specific tool, specific task, specific outcome) is what reads as credible.

## Where to Put AI on Your Resume

### In Your Work Experience Bullets

This is the highest-value placement. Instead of a skills section checkbox, weave AI into the work itself. The goal is to show AI as part of your workflow, not as a credential you're collecting.

Compare these two:

**Before:** "Managed content production for the L&D team"

**After:** "Managed content production for the L&D team; used Claude to generate first-draft facilitator guides and scenario variations, reducing average content development time by 40%"

The second version doesn't overclaim. You're not saying you automated the job. You're saying you changed how you did the job, and you can say specifically how.

If you don't have a percentage or concrete metric, a qualitative description still works. "Piloted AI-assisted research workflows across a three-person team" is more specific than "familiar with AI."

### In a Skills Section

A skills section is a reasonable place to list the tools you use regularly, but do it with at least one qualifier. Instead of just listing "Claude, ChatGPT," try something like:

*AI Tools: Claude (daily use for writing, research, and document drafting), ChatGPT (prompt iteration and content ideation), Perplexity (research synthesis)*

This tells the reader more than a bare tool list. It shows you actually use these tools in context.

### In Your Summary or Profile Statement

If AI capability is genuinely central to how you work (and increasingly it is), it's fair to mention it briefly in your summary. Something like:

*"...brings a working knowledge of Claude and ChatGPT to [role function], integrating AI-assisted workflows into [type of work]."*

Keep it short. The summary should pull them to the experience section, not try to contain everything.

## What About LinkedIn?

LinkedIn rewards specificity in the same way resumes do, but you have more room to tell a story. A few places to focus:

**Your About section.** If you're actively integrating AI into your work, mention it in plain language. "I've spent the past year building AI into how I [core function], using Claude and ChatGPT to [specific application]." One or two sentences is enough. This gets picked up in recruiter searches and signals that you're current.

**The Skills section.** LinkedIn lets you add skills that connections can endorse. Adding specific tools (Claude, ChatGPT, generative AI) here puts you in relevant search results. It's low-effort and worth doing.

**Posts and shares.** This is where LinkedIn differentiates from a resume. If you share what you're actually building or figuring out with AI, even informally, you create evidence. A short post about a workflow you tried, a tool comparison, or a problem you solved with Claude's help is worth more than a skill endorsement from someone who's never seen you work.

One of our community members, T Gaines, built a GitHub portfolio in a two-hour session on Getting Unstuck Friday, after realizing that employers were asking to see portfolios, not just resumes. She described it this way: "They're asking for a portfolio, not just my repos. Here's what I'm sharing with people: a profile README, a portfolio repo, and then my resume links to it."

The portfolio is the evidence that the resume points to. That's the move.

## The Honest Part: What Not to Claim

There's a version of AI self-promotion that backfires. Here are the claims that tend to raise flags:

**Claiming expertise in things you haven't done.** "Expert in AI implementation" or "AI strategist" suggests you've led AI initiatives at organizational scale. If that's not accurate, a single pointed question in an interview will make it obvious. Stick to what you've actually done.

**Being vague to sound bigger.** "Used AI to improve productivity" tells a reader nothing. It sounds like AI-generated resume copy (which it might be). Specifics are always more credible than abstractions.

**Omitting the human judgment layer.** The most sophisticated thing you can say about AI work is that you know where it falls short. "I use Claude for first drafts and spend roughly the same amount of time editing as I used to spend writing" is a more compelling sentence than "AI reduces my writing time by 80%." Hiring managers who know anything about these tools will trust you more for the nuance.

## A Note on Non-Technical Backgrounds

If you came to AI without a technical background (and most mid-career professionals did), that's not a liability. It's actually a strength worth naming, because the majority of AI work in most organizations isn't engineering. It's judgment, communication, and workflow design.

Coming from a non-technical background actually helps you coach and work with others on AI adoption because you remember what the learning curve felt like. "I figured this out without a technical background and have since helped colleagues do the same" is a real thing to say on a resume or in an interview. It positions you as someone who can bridge.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific MVP Club community member who landed a role or advancement opportunity partly by demonstrating AI capability, with their specific framing (e.g., how they described their AI use in an interview)]

## A Simple Starting Prompt

If you're staring at your resume and don't know where to start, try this in Claude or ChatGPT:

*"Here is my current resume. I've been using [tools] regularly in my work to [describe how]. Help me rewrite 3-4 bullet points in my most recent role to accurately reflect this AI integration. Don't overstate or invent anything. Work only with the tasks I describe. Keep the tone professional but specific."*

Then paste your resume and your actual AI use cases. What comes back will need editing, but it gives you something real to react to rather than a blank page.

> **Matt Hastings** on why the specifics matter: "There's something really interesting about telling a story that when somebody sees it, it's just compelling on its own. It's like, oh, I need this person. I don't know their title or what specifically they're going to do, but I need them to do this for me."

That's the goal. Not a skills section that checks a box. A picture of how you work.

## Where to Go From Here

If you want to get clear on the AI skills that actually matter for career advancement (vs. the ones that just sound good), [The AI Skills That Matter for Career Advancement](/learn/ai-project-portfolio) is worth reading alongside this one. And if you're building toward becoming the person on your team who leads AI adoption, [How to Become the AI Person on Your Team](/learn/becoming-ai-leader-case-study) covers what that actually looks like in practice.

The professionals who are figuring this out fastest aren't doing it alone. In MVP Club's weekly sessions, members share how they're positioning their AI work, get feedback on how it reads, and see what's actually resonating with hiring managers right now. If you want to work through your own resume framing alongside people navigating the same questions, [that's exactly what the community is for](/community).

Most people either leave AI off their resume entirely or list “ChatGPT” under skills and hope for the best. Both approaches are leaving something on the table.

If you’ve been using AI tools regularly at work (even informally, even imperfectly), you have more to say than you think. The challenge isn’t whether to include it. The challenge is figuring out how to describe it in a way that’s honest, specific, and actually lands with the person reading your resume.

This isn’t a guide to gaming the system. It’s a guide to getting credit for the work you’re already doing.

Why “Familiar with AI Tools” Doesn’t Cut It Anymore

The bar has moved. According to a 2026 hiring insights report from ResumeGenius, more than 60% of hiring managers say they want to test, discuss, or see evidence of AI capabilities rather than take resume claims at face value. [NEEDS CITATION: verify this is independent research, not marketing] Simply listing vague AI claims doesn’t tell a reader anything useful.

What hiring managers actually want to know: Can you apply AI to the work? Can you describe what you’ve done with it? Are you someone who uses AI as part of how you work, or someone who took a webinar about it once?

The good news is that if you’ve been using Claude, ChatGPT, or any other tool to draft documents, research, prep for meetings, or speed up your workflows, you already have specifics to work with.

The Framework: What, How, and So What

Before you touch your resume, do this exercise. For each AI use in your work, fill in three things:

What tool did you use? (Claude, ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Perplexity)

How did you use it? (Drafting first passes on reports, generating meeting summaries, building outreach sequences, analyzing feedback data, creating first-draft training content)

So what? What was the outcome? (Shortened a process from three days to one, cut document review time in half, expanded output volume without adding headcount)

This framework keeps you grounded. You’re not claiming to be an AI expert. You’re describing what you actually did and what happened because of it. That combination (specific tool, specific task, specific outcome) is what reads as credible.

Where to Put AI on Your Resume

In Your Work Experience Bullets

This is the highest-value placement. Instead of a skills section checkbox, weave AI into the work itself. The goal is to show AI as part of your workflow, not as a credential you’re collecting.

Compare these two:

Before: “Managed content production for the L&D team”

After: “Managed content production for the L&D team; used Claude to generate first-draft facilitator guides and scenario variations, reducing average content development time by 40%”

The second version doesn’t overclaim. You’re not saying you automated the job. You’re saying you changed how you did the job, and you can say specifically how.

If you don’t have a percentage or concrete metric, a qualitative description still works. “Piloted AI-assisted research workflows across a three-person team” is more specific than “familiar with AI.”

In a Skills Section

A skills section is a reasonable place to list the tools you use regularly, but do it with at least one qualifier. Instead of just listing “Claude, ChatGPT,” try something like:

AI Tools: Claude (daily use for writing, research, and document drafting), ChatGPT (prompt iteration and content ideation), Perplexity (research synthesis)

This tells the reader more than a bare tool list. It shows you actually use these tools in context.

In Your Summary or Profile Statement

If AI capability is genuinely central to how you work (and increasingly it is), it’s fair to mention it briefly in your summary. Something like:

“…brings a working knowledge of Claude and ChatGPT to [role function], integrating AI-assisted workflows into [type of work].”

Keep it short. The summary should pull them to the experience section, not try to contain everything.

What About LinkedIn?

LinkedIn rewards specificity in the same way resumes do, but you have more room to tell a story. A few places to focus:

Your About section. If you’re actively integrating AI into your work, mention it in plain language. “I’ve spent the past year building AI into how I [core function], using Claude and ChatGPT to [specific application].” One or two sentences is enough. This gets picked up in recruiter searches and signals that you’re current.

The Skills section. LinkedIn lets you add skills that connections can endorse. Adding specific tools (Claude, ChatGPT, generative AI) here puts you in relevant search results. It’s low-effort and worth doing.

Posts and shares. This is where LinkedIn differentiates from a resume. If you share what you’re actually building or figuring out with AI, even informally, you create evidence. A short post about a workflow you tried, a tool comparison, or a problem you solved with Claude’s help is worth more than a skill endorsement from someone who’s never seen you work.

One of our community members, T Gaines, built a GitHub portfolio in a two-hour session on Getting Unstuck Friday, after realizing that employers were asking to see portfolios, not just resumes. She described it this way: “They’re asking for a portfolio, not just my repos. Here’s what I’m sharing with people: a profile README, a portfolio repo, and then my resume links to it.”

The portfolio is the evidence that the resume points to. That’s the move.

The Honest Part: What Not to Claim

There’s a version of AI self-promotion that backfires. Here are the claims that tend to raise flags:

Claiming expertise in things you haven’t done. “Expert in AI implementation” or “AI strategist” suggests you’ve led AI initiatives at organizational scale. If that’s not accurate, a single pointed question in an interview will make it obvious. Stick to what you’ve actually done.

Being vague to sound bigger. “Used AI to improve productivity” tells a reader nothing. It sounds like AI-generated resume copy (which it might be). Specifics are always more credible than abstractions.

Omitting the human judgment layer. The most sophisticated thing you can say about AI work is that you know where it falls short. “I use Claude for first drafts and spend roughly the same amount of time editing as I used to spend writing” is a more compelling sentence than “AI reduces my writing time by 80%.” Hiring managers who know anything about these tools will trust you more for the nuance.

It positions you as someone who can bridge.
