The AI Skills That Actually Matter for Career Advancement
AI skills for career advancement aren't about certifications. Here's what employers actually notice and how to make your work visible.

Most people using AI at work are doing it quietly. They've figured out a Claude workflow that saves them an hour a week. They're using ChatGPT to cut their meeting prep in half. And then they say nothing about it. Not in their one-on-ones. Not in their performance reviews. Not on LinkedIn.

That silence is the actual career problem.

According to a [PwC Global AI Jobs Barometer](https://www.pwc.com/gx/en/news-room/press-releases/2025/ai-linked-to-a-fourfold-increase-in-productivity-growth.html), workers in AI-exposed roles who actively develop and demonstrate AI skills earn 56% more than peers in the same roles who don't. [NEEDS CITATION: verify exact figure in linked document] The wage premium for AI capability has more than doubled in the past two years. The work isn't the issue. The packaging is.

## You're probably further along than you think

When I ran a workshop for a women's AI meetup group in March 2026, I asked participants to self-audit where they fell on a four-zone spectrum: Hidden (using AI but telling no one), Aware (knows it's an advantage, no language for it yet), Emerging (starting to name and share it), and Positioned (actively using AI capability in visibility moments).

Almost everyone landed in Zone 1 or Zone 2. Not because they weren't doing real things with AI. Because they hadn't made a habit of naming what they were doing.

The talk track I gave that day: "You're not behind. You're just not packaging it yet."

That distinction matters, because the skills conversation keeps getting framed as a technical one. Do you know prompt engineering? Have you taken any AI courses? Can you code? And for a lot of professionals (myself included, with zero technical background when I started), that framing makes the whole thing feel like a test you haven't studied for.

The AI skills that actually advance careers are behavioral, not technical.

## What employers are actually measuring

"AI skills" as a job requirement has grown 70% year over year in US job postings. [NEEDS CITATION] But the skills hiring managers are citing most often aren't about model architecture or API integration. They're about judgment, workflow application, and the ability to manage AI as a collaborative tool.

When I coach professionals through this, I tell them to stop thinking about AI as something to "learn" and start thinking about it as something to demonstrate. The three things that show up again and again in career conversations:

**1. Daily use, not occasional use.** The difference between someone who "uses AI" and someone who has built real capability is repetition. Using Claude once a week is exploration. Using it every day is a skill. Employers can feel the difference in how you talk about it. You either have actual examples or you don't.

**2. Applied to a specific workflow, not just writing.** Anyone can use ChatGPT to make an email sound better. What creates career signal is using AI to redesign a workflow: turning a 45-minute weekly report into a 10-minute process, speeding up data prep before a presentation, building a client intake system that used to live in your head. Those specific use cases are the stories that stick in a hiring conversation.

**3. Willingness to share it.** The professionals who move fastest in the AI era are the ones who show their work before they feel ready. A screen share with a colleague. A Slack message with a screenshot. A mention in a team standup. Visibility compounds in ways that staying quiet never does.

## The packaging gap

In that same Women in AI workshop, I watched one participant realize she had been using Claude to synthesize competitive research every week for three months. She'd never mentioned it to her manager. Never framed it as a skill on her LinkedIn. In her mind, it was just "how she worked now."

That's Zone 1: doing real work, telling no one.

The exercise that shifts this is simple: write down three things you've done with AI in the last month that made your work genuinely better. Not three things you tried. Three things that worked. Then ask yourself: does my manager know about any of these? Does my network?

For most people, the answer is no. Not because the wins aren't real, but because they haven't built a habit of naming them.

The habit sounds like this, in a one-on-one: "I've been using Claude to help with status reporting. I want to show you what I built." In a performance review: "One thing I've done this quarter to increase efficiency is..." In a LinkedIn update: a simple before-and-after of a deliverable.

None of that requires a course. It requires practice and the willingness to make your work visible.

## The three skills worth building (in order)

If you're thinking about where to actually focus, here's how I frame it with the professionals I coach.

### Daily use habit (start here)

Before any of the career-positioning work matters, you need a consistent practice. Not a weekly check-in. A daily one. Pick one task you do every day that involves writing, analysis, or research, and bring Claude or ChatGPT into it. Do that for 30 days.

This sounds too simple to be the advice. It's the whole advice. Understanding comes through doing, and nothing you read about AI (including this article) substitutes for the hour you spend iterating with a real tool on a real problem.

If you're not sure where to start, [How to Build AI into Your Daily Workflow](/learn/ai-daily-workflow) has a specific 30-minute structure you can follow starting tomorrow.

### Workflow redesign (where the career signal lives)

Once you have a daily habit, the next move is to take one recurring task from "I use AI to help with this" to "I've redesigned this with AI." The difference is specificity and repeatability.

Using Claude to help write something is fine. Building a repeatable system where you paste in raw notes and get a formatted, on-brand deliverable in five minutes is a workflow. Workflows are what you put in performance reviews. Workflows are what you demo for colleagues. Workflows are what interviewers ask you to describe.

T Gaines, who works in tech education and was part of the MVP Club portfolio workshop in early March 2026, built a complete GitHub portfolio page in two hours during a Getting Unstuck Friday session. Before the session, she didn't know portfolios existed as a concept for non-technical professionals. After: "They're asking for a portfolio, not just my repos. Here's what I'm sharing with people: a profile README, a portfolio repo, and then my resume links to it." [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission]

That two-hour session changed how she described herself to employers. Not because she learned something abstract. Because she built something specific and made it visible.

### Packaging and communication (the last mile)

The third skill is translating what you're doing into language that lands in a professional context. This is where a lot of people get stuck (not because they don't have wins, but because "I've been using AI" doesn't communicate anything useful).

Concrete packaging sounds like:

- "I built a Claude workflow that takes client intake notes and generates a first-draft proposal brief. It's cut our turnaround time from 48 hours to same-day."
- "I've been using ChatGPT for competitive research synthesis. What used to take me three hours I can now do in 45 minutes, with better source organization."
- "I automated our weekly status report format with Claude. I paste in my notes, it structures the output to match the template our director prefers."

Notice what those have in common: a specific task, a specific tool, a specific before-and-after. That's the format. Vague claims ("I'm good with AI") land differently than grounded ones.

> "There's something really interesting about telling a story that when somebody sees it, it's just compelling on its own. It's like, oh, I need this person. I don't know their title or what specifically they're going to do, but I need them to do this for me." — Matt Hastings, MVP Club co-founder

That quote was about portfolio building, but it applies here too. The goal isn't to have the AI skill. It's to tell the story of what you built with it in a way that makes someone say: I need that person.

## What doesn't work (and why)

A few patterns I see in coaching that feel productive but don't actually move careers forward.

**Collecting AI knowledge without applying it.** There are a lot of people who have read extensively about large language models, attended webinars, and could describe the difference between Claude and ChatGPT in detail. But they haven't built a single workflow that changed how they work. Employers don't hire AI readers. They hire AI users.

**Waiting until you're an expert to say anything.** The identity question ("Am I really qualified to call myself AI-capable?") is the biggest invisible barrier I see. Yes. You are. If you've built even one workflow that consistently saves you time, you are ahead of most of the workforce. The hesitation is real, but it isn't informative.

**Focusing on the wrong skills.** Prompt engineering as a standalone skill has largely commoditized. What employers want is demonstrated judgment: knowing when AI output needs revision, how to structure a task so the output is useful, and how to build AI into a workflow someone else could follow. That's not technical. That's managerial. (And the managerial frame is actually the more accurate one: using AI is about managing inputs, goals, and outputs, not about mastering a tool.)

## A practical four-week arc

Week 1: Identify the one daily task where you're already using AI (or could start). Do it every day this week. Don't skip.

Week 2: Write down your three best AI wins from the past month. Be specific. Then share one of them with a colleague or your manager. Informally. "Hey, I've been doing something with Claude you might find interesting."

Week 3: Build your packaging language. Draft two to three sentences that describe what you're doing with AI, in concrete before-and-after terms. This is your answer to "what's your AI experience?" when someone asks.

Week 4: Make it visible somewhere. Update your LinkedIn summary. Mention it in a review conversation. Write a brief LinkedIn post about a workflow win. You don't have to have all the answers. You just have to be the person who's paying attention.

For the mechanics of making AI work visible on your resume and profile, [How to Talk About AI on Your Resume](/learn/ai-on-your-resume) has specific frameworks. [Becoming the AI Leader at Your Organization](/learn/becoming-ai-leader-case-study) covers what this looks like when you extend the practice to your team.

## The professionals figuring this out fastest aren't doing it alone

The people who make the most progress on this aren't doing it in isolation. They're in rooms (virtual or otherwise) where other professionals are building things, sharing wins, and talking honestly about where they're stuck.

That's what MVP Club's weekly sessions are built for. If you want to practice alongside people navigating the same questions and share what's working, that's what [our community](/community) is.

AI Skills vs. AI Understanding: What Employers Actually Want
Most employers aren't hunting for AI tool experts. They want people who can think with AI. Here's what that distinction means for your career.

Job postings requiring AI fluency grew more than 70% year over year in 2026, according to LinkedIn's latest workforce data [NEEDS CITATION: verify exact figure and source]. That number gets shared a lot. What doesn't get shared is that most of those roles are not asking for AI engineers. They're asking for people who can use Claude to draft a proposal, catch when ChatGPT gets a fact wrong, and explain to their team why a particular output needs another pass before it goes to the client.

That's a different skill set than what "AI skills" sounds like in most conversations.

## The Two Things Employers Are Actually Measuring

Here's the split I see when I work with professionals navigating this moment. On one side: people who have accumulated AI tool experience. They've used ChatGPT, they know their way around a prompt, they can produce a first draft faster than they could six months ago. That's real. It counts.

On the other side: people who have developed AI judgment. They know when to trust the output and when to push back. They can break a complex task into steps a model can actually follow. They understand the difference between a prompt that gets a generic answer and one that gets a useful one. They catch the subtle hallucination buried in paragraph three before it reaches a client.

Employers want both, but they are shorter on the second. Anyone can tell a candidate "I've used ChatGPT." Fewer can demonstrate that they know how to work with AI on a problem that has real stakes, real constraints, and real consequences if it goes sideways.

## Why "AI Skills" Became the Wrong Frame

The term "AI skills" implies something learnable in a workshop, certifiable with a badge, demonstrable in a bulleted list. That framing was borrowed from how we talk about technical skills (Excel, Salesforce, Photoshop) and it does not quite fit what working with AI actually requires.

McKinsey's research on the future of work puts it plainly [NEEDS CITATION]: as AI absorbs routine tasks like drafting content, sifting information, and organizing data, what employers need from humans is judgment, critical thinking, and the ability to validate outputs. These are not new skills. They are the skills you have already been developing throughout your career, now needed in a new context.

This is actually good news. It means the people who feel behind because they haven't taken an AI course are not as behind as they think. What you know about your industry, your clients, your organization's priorities: all of that is what makes AI outputs useful or useless in your specific situation. A generalist AI tool produces generalist outputs. Your expertise is what turns those outputs into something decision-ready.

## What This Looks Like in Practice

The split between tool familiarity and genuine AI judgment shows up in a few specific ways.

**Prompt quality.** Two people can use the same tool for the same task and get wildly different results. The difference is rarely the tool. It's how clearly the person can articulate what they need, what constraints matter, and what "good" looks like for their specific situation. That clarity comes from domain knowledge plus practice, not from knowing a list of prompting techniques.

**Output evaluation.** AI tools produce confident-sounding text. The person who can recognize when that confidence is misplaced, when a fact needs checking, when an analysis is missing a key variable, that person is worth more to an employer than someone who accepts outputs at face value. This is judgment, not a skill you learn from a tutorial.

**Knowing what to delegate.** There's a real art to figuring out which parts of a task AI genuinely accelerates and which parts still require your full attention. Over-delegating leads to sloppy work. Under-delegating leaves productivity on the table. Finding that calibration takes experimentation.

> "Building a personal brand narrative is crucial for career transition in the AI era; people want to hire 'a person,' not just skills, and storytelling matters more than specific job titles." (From an MVP Club Demo Share session, on how employers evaluate candidates in the AI era)

## The Resume and LinkedIn Trap

Here's something I see often: professionals who are doing genuinely sophisticated AI work, but describing it in their professional profiles as if they're checking an "AI tools" box.

"Proficient in ChatGPT and Claude" reads like a line on a technical skills list. It says nothing about how you actually work with these tools or what you've produced with them.

What gets attention is specificity about outcomes. Not "used AI for content creation" but "reduced a weekly report from 4 hours to 45 minutes by building a Claude workflow that pulls from four source documents and outputs a formatted brief my manager uses directly." Not "familiar with AI tools" but "built a client onboarding process that uses Claude to draft customized intake summaries, reviewed and edited by me before client delivery."

The second versions say something about judgment. They show that a real human with expertise is in the loop, making decisions, and taking responsibility for the output. That's what employers want to see.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member who revised their LinkedIn or resume to show AI judgment vs. AI tool familiarity, and noticed a difference in how they were received]

## What to Build vs. What to List

The professionals I coach who are getting the most traction aren't the ones who have the longest AI tool list on their resume. They're the ones who can point to something they made.

A workflow that saves their team three hours a week. A template that eliminated a recurring bottleneck. A process that used to require four rounds of revision and now requires two. These are the things that signal AI judgment to an interviewer or a client, because they demonstrate that you understood a real problem clearly enough to apply AI to it effectively.

This connects to something T Gaines discovered during one of our community sessions. She went from learning that portfolios even existed, to building a GitHub profile README, to troubleshooting the setup live with the community, all in about two hours. At the end of that session, she had something concrete to show. A page that demonstrated that she wasn't just talking about doing technical work. She was doing it.

That compression of timeline (weeks of career development work into an afternoon) is one of the genuinely interesting things AI makes possible right now. And it's more visible to employers than any tool list.

## The Three Questions to Ask Yourself

If you're trying to assess where you actually are on the skills versus understanding spectrum, three questions are more useful than any checklist.

First: when AI gives you a bad output, can you diagnose why? Not just "this is wrong" but what would need to change about the input, the context, or the framing to get a better result? If yes, you have judgment. If you mostly just keep regenerating and hope, that's a skill you can build. (Read more about this in [What to Do When AI Gives You Bad Output](/learn/when-ai-gives-bad-output).)

Second: can you explain your AI workflow to someone else? Not the mechanics of the tool, but the thinking behind it. Why you prompt it the way you do. What you trust it with and what you don't. What your editing process looks like and why. The ability to articulate this is a signal of understanding, and it's also exactly what hiring managers and clients want to hear.

Third: is your AI use improving your domain work, or replacing it? The professionals who stand out are the ones using AI to do better work in their field, not the ones who have mostly offloaded thinking to the tool. Your expertise plus AI is a durable career advantage. AI without your expertise is something any generalist could produce.

## How to Develop the Judgment Part

Practice is the only real answer here, and I know that's not what people want to hear. But there's no shortcut to the moment when you look at an AI output and just know it's wrong in a specific, articulable way. That knowing comes from repeated exposure, combined with enough domain expertise to notice the gaps.

What you can do to speed this up:

Use AI on real work, not practice problems. Toy prompts produce toy intuitions. The judgment develops fastest when there are real stakes.

Make a habit of editing AI output carefully, even when you're in a hurry. Especially when you're in a hurry. The editing process is where the judgment builds.

Talk through your AI workflow with someone else. Explaining what you're doing and why surfaces assumptions you didn't know you were making. This is part of why community matters, which I'll say more about in a moment.

Get feedback on your outputs. Not just "does this look good" but "does this actually solve the problem." The gap between AI-generated and genuinely useful is the space where your judgment lives.

For the practical side of building daily AI habits, [How to Build AI Into Your Daily Workflow](/learn/ai-daily-workflow) has a structure that works for busy professionals. And if you want to go further with demonstrating your AI work, [Building a Portfolio of AI Projects for Career Advancement](/learn/ai-project-portfolio) covers how to turn your AI practice into something employers can actually see.

## A Note on the People Who Feel Behind

One more thing, because I talk to a lot of professionals who are carrying the weight of feeling like they started too late.

Most people who feel behind are just a few months away from not feeling that way anymore. What changes is starting.

The people who look ahead of you in AI capability are not smarter or more gifted. They are mostly just a few months further into the messy, imperfect, sometimes frustrating work of using these tools on real problems every day. That's all. The gap is bridgeable, and it closes faster than you think once you're in it.

The employers and clients who matter are not looking for people who have mastered AI. They are looking for people who are genuinely capable, self-directed, and honest about what they know and don't know. That's always been true. AI just added a new dimension to demonstrate it.

Building a Portfolio of AI Projects for Career Advancement
How to build a portfolio of AI projects that demonstrates real capability to employers and clients, even without a technical background.

I shipped five products in roughly two months. Geddit Together is live in the App Store. Rendition Video converts any document into a narrated video. TasteMaker helps you build a design style guide in about ten minutes. I built all of them using Claude, Cline, and VS Code, with no engineering team and no outside budget.

I didn't do that to have something to brag about. I did it because the work itself taught me more about AI collaboration than any article I'd read. And somewhere along the way, the collection of projects started to tell a story about what I can do now that I couldn't do two years ago.

That's what a portfolio does. It shows the before and after. Not through a claim on a resume, but through actual things that exist.

## Why Your Projects Are Evidence, Not Just Practice

There's a real gap between professionals who are "learning about AI" and professionals who are building with it. The gap shows up in hiring conversations, client pitches, and team decisions. Managers and hiring teams have been burned enough times by people who could talk about AI but couldn't apply it to an actual problem.

A portfolio closes that gap. Not with credentials or course completions, but with artifacts: a working tool, a documented process, a deployed page that anyone can visit. When someone asks what you've been doing with AI, you stop explaining and start showing.

The professionals in our community who have landed new roles or expanded their freelance work are not the ones with the best LinkedIn descriptions of AI. They are the ones who have something concrete to point to. [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm this observation with community data]

## What Counts as an AI Project

This is where most people overthink it. A portfolio project does not need to be a SaaS product or a funded startup idea. It needs to show that you can take a real problem, use AI to work toward a solution, and produce something that functions in the world.

Some examples from the MVP Club community:

- A GitHub portfolio site built entirely in Claude Code, without touching the terminal or the folder structure
- A custom reporting template generator for weekly status updates, built in an afternoon
- An automation that takes a raw transcript and outputs a formatted meeting summary with action items
- A client intake form workflow that feeds into a branded deliverable automatically

None of these took weeks. Most took a few hours spread across a couple of sessions. The point is that they work, and the person who built them now has something to show.

> "In the span of two hours, T developed a GitHub page and now is troubleshooting it with you on Getting Unstuck Friday. I love it. Just in time support." — Jill Ozovek, MVP Club co-founder, watching a community member go from zero to portfolio in an afternoon [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission for T Gaines reference]

## The Project Selection Framework

When you are picking what to build, three questions are worth asking before you scope anything.

Does it solve a real problem you actually have? The best portfolio projects come from friction you experience yourself. A template you use every week. A manual process that eats two hours you would rather spend on something else. A report your team always asks for. Real problems make better projects because they force you to get specific, and specificity is what separates a portfolio that impresses from one that fills a page.

Can you explain the before and after? Every project should have a two-sentence story: here is what the process looked like before, and here is what it looks like now. Before: I spent 45 minutes formatting meeting notes manually every Friday. After: I paste the raw transcript into a Claude workflow and get a formatted summary in under five minutes. Concrete. Measurable. Believable.

Can someone else see it? A tool that runs on your machine and nobody else can access is worth less than one that lives somewhere. A GitHub repo. A deployed page. A Loom walkthrough. Even a screen recording of it working. Showing beats explaining every time.

## Building Your First Three Projects

I started with five projects and that was too many. By project four I was context-switching constantly and not finishing anything well. Three is what I'd tell someone starting now: enough to show range, not so many that you're maintaining a backlog instead of building.

**Project 1: Something you use every week**

Your first project should be immediately useful to you. Pick the most repetitive text-based task in your current role and handle the most time-consuming part of it with Claude. This is your confidence builder. It gives you a result you care about, which means you will iterate on it, which means you will learn more than any walkthrough video would teach you. My first real project was a reporting template I used at DualEnroll. Unglamorous, but it ran every week and I kept making it better.

**Project 2: Something that showcases a specific skill**

Your second project should target the work you want to do more of. If you are in marketing, build something that touches copy or audience research. If you are in operations, build something that touches documentation or process mapping. Match the project to the audience you are trying to impress, not the audience you already have.

**Project 3: Something slightly outside your comfort zone**

Your third project should push into territory that feels a little uncomfortable. If the first two were Claude chat workflows, try deploying something. Try Cline with VS Code. Claude Pro runs about $20 a month, and Cline charges by token usage, so you're realistically looking at $30 to $50 a month to run a serious build practice. That's it. The goal is not to become a developer. The goal is to show that you learn by doing, which is the actual skill employers and clients want evidence of.

> "There's something really interesting about telling a story that when somebody sees it, it's just compelling on its own. It's like, oh, I need this person. I don't know their title or what specifically they're going to do, but I need them to do this for me." — Matt Hastings

## How to Document What You Build

Building the project is half the work. Documenting it is how it becomes career evidence.

For each project, write a short README or project description that covers:

1. The problem you were solving (one sentence)
2. The approach (what tools, what workflow, what you tried)
3. The result (what it does now, how long it takes, what changed)
4. What you would do differently (shows self-awareness and iteration instinct)

The fourth one is the one most people skip. It is also the one that makes your portfolio feel real instead of curated. Perfect projects do not exist. Showing that you know where your work has edges is a more credible signal than presenting everything as a polished success.

Addy Spiller, a consultant in our community building her portfolio, put it well: "There's something of value to show, like, how I think, how I work." [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission] The documented thinking is the portfolio. The finished product is just proof that the thinking was real.

## Where to Put Your Portfolio

GitHub is the standard for a reason: it is free, it is where technical hiring managers look, and it forces a level of organization that makes your work easier to explain. You do not need to know git to start. Claude can walk you through setting up a portfolio page in an afternoon.

If GitHub feels too technical right now, a simple Notion page, a personal website built with Claude Code, or even a clean PDF with screenshots and descriptions works. The format matters less than the habit of making your work visible.

> "I built a GitHub portfolio site for myself, and I did it all in Claude Code. I didn't need to look at the folder structure." — Carmella Thompson, MVP Club community member [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission]

The bigger shift is psychological. Lesley Richardson, who joined MVP Club to get started with AI, spent her first session assuming she did not belong on GitHub. By the end of the Getting Started session, after building her first page, she had updated that view entirely. "I don't even know if I belong there," she said at the start. By the end she did. [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission] That shift happens when you build something that works and make it visible.

## The Compounding Effect

The tenth project feels categorically different from the first. Not just better. Different in kind.

By the time you have built ten things, you have an intuition for what makes a good problem scope. You know which tools to reach for. You have a sense for when to push and when to start over. You have made enough mistakes in front of your own screen that failure does not slow you down the way it did at the start. A portfolio of real work is how you demonstrate that intuition to someone who has never seen you build anything.

Most people assume AI helps you start faster. That is true. The bigger shift, the one that changed how I work, is that it helps you finish. The gap between "working prototype" and "real thing that exists in the world" used to feel enormous, and for most of my career it was. I shipped more in two months than in the previous twenty years. Not incrementally more. Categorically more. AI collapses that gap, which means professionals who build with it regularly end up with portfolios that, even six months ago, would have required a team.

## A Six-Week Build Plan

If you are starting from zero: spend the first two weeks on the project you use every week. Build it, iterate on it at least twice, document it, and get it into a shareable format. Week three and four go to the second project, the one targeting a specific audience. The last two weeks are for the stretch project. If it is not done by week six, document it anyway, including where you got stuck and what you learned. Incomplete projects with honest documentation are still portfolio material.

Six weeks from now you will have three concrete things to point to.

For the tools: Claude is where I spend most of my building time. For more complex workflows, Cline with VS Code gives you more control over file structure and iterative changes. If you are new to the terminal setup, the [Getting Started with Claude Code guide](/blog/getting-started-with-claude-code-non-developer-guide) walks through the technical setup from scratch.

Once you have projects in motion, the [career advantage of building with AI daily](/learn/ai-daily-workflow) article covers how to integrate building into a sustainable practice rather than letting it be a weekend-only activity.

## Where People Get Stuck

A few patterns come up repeatedly in our community sessions. I have hit most of them personally.

**Scope creep on the first project.** You start trying to automate your meeting notes and end up wanting to build a full knowledge management system. I did this with TasteMaker. The original idea was a ten-minute tool; I kept adding features until it had a preferences engine I never used. Cut it back. The goal of the first project is to ship something, not to build the ideal version of something.

**Perfectionism before sharing.** Waiting until something is polished enough to show is how portfolios never get built. Share early. Ugly works. Functional matters more than beautiful.

**Solving a problem that does not exist.** The most common portfolio mistake is building something technically interesting that very few people would actually use. Start with your own friction. Build for a one-person audience first: yourself.

**Not iterating after the first result.** Claude's first output is almost never the best version. The professionals who get the most out of AI are the ones who treat the first draft as a starting point and keep pushing. That iteration instinct is also the most important thing a portfolio can demonstrate.

## The Portfolio as Career Infrastructure

T Gaines, who works in tech education, built a complete GitHub portfolio in two hours during a Getting Unstuck Friday session. A profile README, a portfolio repo with real project links, formatted and ready to share. Before that session, she had assumed a portfolio was something other people had. After: "They're asking for a portfolio, not just my repos. Here's what I'm sharing with people: a profile README, a portfolio repo, and then my resume links to it." [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission]

That shift, from talking about what you are learning to showing what you have built, is not just psychological. It changes what opportunities you get offered. People who can show a working tool get different conversations than people who can describe one. The next conversation you want to be having starts with what you have already made visible.

The professionals I have watched move fastest in this community are the ones who build in public, share early, and ask for feedback before they feel ready. If you want to do that alongside people doing the same work, that is what MVP Club is for. Find us at [/community](/community).

  • A client intake form workflow that feeds into a branded deliverable automatically

None of these took weeks. Most took a few hours spread across a couple of sessions. The point is that they work, and the person who built them now has something to show.

“In the span of two hours, T developed a GitHub page and now is troubleshooting it with you on Getting Unstuck Friday. I love it. Just in time support.” — Jill Ozovek, MVP Club co-founder, watching a community member go from zero to portfolio in an afternoon [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission for T Gaines reference]

The Project Selection Framework

When you are picking what to build, three questions are worth asking before you scope anything.

Does it solve a real problem you actually have? The best portfolio projects come from friction you experience yourself. A template you use every week. A manual process that eats two hours you would rather spend on something else. A report your team always asks for. Real problems make better projects because they force you to get specific, and specificity is what separates a portfolio that impresses from one that fills a page.

Can you explain the before and after? Every project should have a two-sentence story: here is what the process looked like before, and here is what it looks like now. Before: I spent 45 minutes formatting meeting notes manually every Friday. After: I paste the raw transcript into a Claude workflow and get a formatted summary in under five minutes. Concrete. Measurable. Believable.

Can someone else see it? A tool that runs on your machine and nobody else can access is worth less than one that lives somewhere. A GitHub repo. A deployed page. A Loom walkthrough. Even a screen recording of it working. Showing beats explaining every time.

Building Your First Three Projects

I started with five projects and that was too many. By project four I was context-switching constantly and not finishing anything well. Three is what I’d tell someone starting now: enough to show range, not so many that you’re maintaining a backlog instead of building.

Project 1: Something you use every week

Your first project should be immediately useful to you. Pick the most repetitive text-based task in your current role and handle the most time-consuming part of it with Claude. This is your confidence builder. It gives you a result you care about, which means you will iterate on it, which means you will learn more than any walkthrough video would teach you. My first real project was a reporting template I used at DualEnroll. Unglamorous, but it ran every week and I kept making it better.

Project 2: Something that showcases a specific skill

Your second project should target the work you want to do more of. If you are in marketing, build something that touches copy or audience research. If you are in operations, build something that touches documentation or process mapping. Match the project to the audience you are trying to impress, not the audience you already have.

Project 3: Something slightly outside your comfort zone

Your third project should push into territory that feels a little uncomfortable. If the first two were Claude chat workflows, try deploying something. Try Cline with VS Code. Claude Pro runs about $20 a month, and Cline charges by token usage, so you’re realistically looking at $30 to $50 a month to run a serious build practice. That’s it. The goal is not to become a developer. The goal is to show that you learn by doing, which is the actual skill employers and clients want evidence of.

“There’s something really interesting about telling a story that when somebody sees it, it’s just compelling on its own. It’s like, oh, I need this person. I don’t know their title or what specifically they’re going to do, but I need them to do this for me.” — Matt Hastings

How to Document What You Build

Building the project is half the work. Documenting it is how it becomes career evidence.

For each project, write a short README or project description that covers:

  1. The problem you were solving (one sentence)
  2. The approach (what tools, what workflow, what you tried)
  3. The result (what it does now, how long it takes, what changed)
  4. What you would do differently (shows self-awareness and iteration instinct)

The fourth one is the one most people skip. It is also the one that makes your portfolio feel real instead of curated. Perfect projects do not exist. Showing that you know where your work has edges is a more credible signal than presenting everything as a polished success.

Addy Spiller, a consultant in our community building her portfolio, put it well: “There’s something of value to show, like, how I think, how I work.” [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission] The documented thinking is the portfolio. The finished product is just proof that the thinking was real.

Where to Put Your Portfolio

GitHub is the standard for a reason: it is free, it is where technical hiring managers look, and it forces a level of organization that makes your work easier to explain. You do not need to know git to start. Claude can walk you through setting up a portfolio page in an afternoon.

If GitHub feels too technical right now, a simple Notion page, a personal website built with Claude Code, or even a clean PDF with screenshots and descriptions works. The format matters less than the habit of making your work visible.

“I built a GitHub portfolio site for myself, and I did it all in Claude Code. I didn’t need to look at the folder structure.” — Carmella Thompson, MVP Club community member [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission]

The bigger shift is psychological. Lesley Richardson, who joined MVP Club to get started with AI, spent her first session assuming she did not belong on GitHub. By the end of the Getting Started session, after building her first page, she had updated that view entirely. “I don’t even know if I belong there,” she said at the start. By the end she did. [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission] That shift happens when you build something that works and make it visible.

The Compounding Effect

The tenth project feels categorically different from the first. Not just better. Different in kind.

By the time you have built ten things, you have an intuition for what makes a good problem scope. You know which tools to reach for. You have a sense for when to push and when to start over. You have made enough mistakes in front of your own screen that failure does not slow you down the way it did at the start. A portfolio of real work is how you demonstrate that intuition to someone who has never seen you build anything.

Most people assume AI helps you start faster. That is true. The bigger shift, the one that changed how I work, is that it helps you finish. The gap between “working prototype” and “real thing that exists in the world” used to feel enormous, and for most of my career it was. I shipped more in two months than in the previous twenty years. Not incrementally more. Categorically more. AI collapses that gap, which means professionals who build with it regularly end up with portfolios that, even six months ago, would have required a team.

A Six-Week Build Plan

If you are starting from zero: spend the first two weeks on the project you use every week. Build it, iterate on it at least twice, document it, and get it into a shareable format. Week three and four go to the second project, the one targeting a specific audience. The last two weeks are for the stretch project. If it is not done by week six, document it anyway, including where you got stuck and what you learned. Incomplete projects with honest documentation are still portfolio material.

Six weeks from now you will have three concrete things to point to.

For the tools: Claude is where I spend most of my building time. For more complex workflows, Cline with VS Code gives you more control over file structure and iterative changes. If you are new to the terminal setup, the Getting Started with Claude Code guide walks through the technical setup from scratch.

Once you have projects in motion, the career advantage of building with AI daily article covers how to integrate building into a sustainable practice rather than letting it be a weekend-only activity.

Where People Get Stuck

A few patterns come up repeatedly in our community sessions. I have hit most of them personally.

Scope creep on the first project. You start trying to automate your meeting notes and end up wanting to build a full knowledge management system. I did this with TasteMaker. The original idea was a ten-minute tool; I kept adding features until it had a preferences engine I never used. Cut it back. The goal of the first project is to ship something, not to build the ideal version of something.

Perfectionism before sharing. Waiting until something is polished enough to show is how portfolios never get built. Share early. Ugly works. Functional matters more than beautiful.

Solving a problem that does not exist. The most common portfolio mistake is building something technically interesting that very few people would actually use. Start with your own friction. Build for a one-person audience first: yourself.

Not iterating after the first result. Claude’s first output is almost never the best version. The professionals who get the most out of AI are the ones who treat the first draft as a starting point and keep pushing. That iteration instinct is also the most important thing a portfolio can demonstrate.

The Portfolio as Career Infrastructure

T Gaines, who works in tech education, built a complete GitHub portfolio in two hours during a Getting Unstuck Friday session. A profile README, a portfolio repo with real project links, formatted and ready to share. Before that session, she had assumed a portfolio was something other people had. After: “They’re asking for a portfolio, not just my repos. Here’s what I’m sharing with people: a profile README, a portfolio repo, and then my resume links to it.” [NEEDS VERIFICATION: confirm community member permission]

That shift, from talking about what you are learning to showing what you have built, is not just psychological. It changes what opportunities you get offered. People who can show a working tool get different conversations than people who can describe one. The next conversation you want to be having starts with what you have already made visible.

The professionals I have watched move fastest in this community are the ones who build in public, share early, and ask for feedback before they feel ready. If you want to do that alongside people doing the same work, that is what MVP Club is for. Find us at /community.

AI-Built Tools That Saved Hours: Real Examples
Real examples of non-technical professionals using Claude to build internal tools that replaced hours of manual work each week.

Carmella Thompson built a portfolio site entirely inside Claude Code. No terminal. No folder structure. No developer. She described what she wanted and Claude built it.

That's one example from MVP Club members who have stopped waiting for IT to build things for them. The pattern keeps repeating: someone has a manual task that eats two or three hours a week, they describe it to Claude, and an hour later they have something working. Not perfect. Working.

This is what that actually looks like.

## What "Building Internal Tools" Means for Non-Technical Professionals

Building a tool used to require a developer, a backlog, a few sprints, and a launch meeting. That's still true for complex enterprise software. But for the kind of task-specific tools professionals need every day (a market report formatter, a document intake processor, a quick-look dashboard), the calculus has shifted.

You can now describe what you want in plain English and Claude will write the code, deploy it locally, and let you test it in the same session. The barrier is no longer technical ability. It's knowing what problem you're trying to solve.

That second part is where most professionals already have the edge. You know your workflows. You know where the friction is. You know what takes too long every week. That knowledge, combined with tools like Claude Code or Claude.ai with Projects, is what makes building feel accessible in a way it genuinely wasn't 18 months ago.

## Real Examples from the MVP Club Community

These aren't hypotheticals. These come directly from member sessions.

### Carmella Thompson: Portfolio Site, No Terminal Required

Carmella has been building in Claude Code and recently put together a GitHub portfolio page (a public page that shows your projects to employers or collaborators) entirely within the desktop app. The part she found most surprising: she never had to open a folder, navigate a file tree, or touch the terminal.

"I built a GitHub portfolio site for myself, and I did it all in Claude Code here," she said in a session. "I didn't need to look at the folder structure."

She's also been working toward automating her real estate market report workflow, applying the same approach: describe the input, describe the desired output, let Claude handle the transformation in between. The same skill that built the portfolio is the one that will save a chunk of her morning, every morning.

### Robert Martin: Game Asset Automation

Robert builds games as a side project. One of the most repetitive tasks in game development is resizing sprite images to match different screen resolutions. Every character, every asset, every frame. It's the kind of job that takes an afternoon if you do it manually.

Robert automated it. Using Claude, he built a workflow that batches sprite resizing across the whole asset library. What used to take hours now runs while he works on something else.

His situation illustrates the simplest rule I give people in coaching: "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude: here's how I want you to do it. Just go."

### Angela Frances Peat: Institutional Knowledge as a Tool

Angela, a consultant, has been working through how to describe her clients' businesses as sets of discrete, clearly defined capabilities that an AI can actually act on. Rather than just maintaining a task backlog, she's building documentation that lets AI agents understand entire workflows, not just individual tasks.

"Capability models are increasingly important," she noted in a session. "Not just skills or context windows, but can you give them a full picture of how your business operates?"

The tool she's building is less about automation and more about institutional knowledge becoming actionable. That's a different kind of tool, but it's still something that required weeks of consulting work before you could describe it to an AI and have it do anything useful.

## The Most Common Starting Point: Something You Do Every Week

The best first tool to build is the one you're most annoyed by. Not the biggest problem. Not the most impressive use case. The one that makes you think "I wish I didn't have to do this manually" on a regular Tuesday.

From community sessions, a few categories come up again and again.

**Report reformatting.** You get data in one format, you need it in another. This is almost always automatable. Give Claude a sample input file, describe the output format, and ask it to write a script that handles the conversion.

**Template population.** You have a document template with twenty fields that you fill from a spreadsheet, a database, or an email. Describe the sources, describe the destination, let Claude wire them together.

**Research compilation.** You regularly read from multiple sources and synthesize into a summary. Claude can be set up to pull from designated sources and format the output the way you want it.

None of these require you to know how to code. They require you to know what problem you're solving.

## Why "Vibe Coding" Is the Right Frame for This

The phrase gets used in developer circles, but it applies here just as well. You're not writing code. You're managing a collaboration. You describe what you want, Claude builds a version of it, you evaluate what you got, and you iterate.

"This is the really important step," I tell people in sessions. "You gave it the requirements, it built it, and now you're managing the output through evaluation."

That's a managerial skill, not a technical one. You're the one who knows whether the output is right. Claude can generate a thousand correct lines of code that still don't solve your actual problem if you didn't describe the problem clearly. Your judgment is the critical layer.

This is also why community practice matters here. The people who get good at building tools fastest are the ones who build them alongside others, get feedback on what they made, and watch what other people are making in return.

## What Happens When You Build Your First Tool

There's a specific moment that happens to almost everyone who builds their first working tool. The novelty of "I made this" gives way to something more useful: an intuition for where else this applies.

Once you've built a report formatter, you start noticing every other place in your work where a similar conversion happens. Once you've automated an intake form, you see the three other intake processes on your team that have the same shape.

"Getting the app actually impacting the real world is the turning point that makes vibe coding feel meaningful beyond hobby projects," said T Gaines in one of our community sessions.

That shift is real, and it's contagious. The professionals who are building within MVP Club tend to come back each week with a thing they finished and a new thing they want to try.

## Starting Without Any Technical Background

A few clarifying points for people who want to start but aren't sure how.

You don't need VS Code or a terminal to start. Claude.ai with Projects lets you build workflows in a browser window without touching a command line. Angela Peat put it plainly: "The desktop is probably what I prefer, versus the terminal looks a little bit scary."

Claude Code in the desktop app is more capable, especially for longer sessions, but starting in the browser is a legitimate first step. Build something small there, see what's possible, and move to a more powerful setup when you're ready.

Plan mode is worth knowing about. When you're worried about Claude making changes you didn't intend, you can tell it to discuss with you rather than execute. I describe it this way in sessions: "It won't touch the code. It's only going to chat with you, so basically just turns it back into chat." That's a useful way to think through the design before anything gets built.

Start with something you know. The professionals who struggle are usually trying to build something impressive. The ones who succeed first build something boring that solves a real problem they have.

You can go deeper on the technical setup in [Getting Started with Claude Code: The Non-Developer's Guide](/blog/getting-started-with-claude-code-non-developer-guide) and read more about the broader shift in how work gets done in [Vibe Coding: The New Skill of Work](/blog/you-only-use-10-of-your-computer-vibe-coding-and-the-new-skill-of-work).

## What to Expect (and What Not to Expect)

The tool you build on your first session won't be polished. It will probably have rough edges, edge cases it doesn't handle, and at least one thing that doesn't quite work the way you pictured.

That's fine. That's the version you show your team member or use yourself for two weeks before you figure out what it actually needs to do. The iteration loop is where the real learning happens, and Claude is in the loop with you.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific first-tool story from a non-technical member, ideally with time-before vs. time-after comparison]

## The Career Angle

Building tools, even small ones, changes how you're perceived on your team. You become the person who didn't wait for IT. You become the person who noticed a problem and did something about it.

That reputation compounds. And in interviews, the question is shifting. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE] It's less often "do you use AI?" and more often "what have you made?" If you can answer that question with something specific and real, you're ahead of most candidates.

If career positioning is on your mind, building with AI is one of the fastest ways to answer that question concretely. See [AI Skills for Career Advancement](/learn/ai-learning-path) for more on how this connects to where professional hiring is heading.

The AI Tool Stack: What to Combine for Maximum Results
The best AI tool stack for professionals is two or three tools used intentionally, each with a clear job. Here's what MVP Club members actually use and why.

Most professionals who get real results from AI are using two or three tools, each with a specific job, without much overlap.

Most people build their stack backwards. They pick a tool they've heard of, use it for everything, get inconsistent results, and assume AI isn't as useful as advertised.

The fix: assign each tool a clear job. Stop expecting any single tool to do all of them well. This guide is opinionated about what works, based on what MVP Club members actually run in their workflows.

## Why one tool is almost never enough

Claude is better than ChatGPT for nuanced writing and document work. ChatGPT is more versatile for quick tasks and has broader integrations. Perplexity is the only major tool that reliably cites current sources. Microsoft Copilot is the only one that lives inside your email and spreadsheets.

None of those sentences is false. Which is exactly why you can't pick just one and call it done.

The professionals who get the most out of AI aren't the ones who found the "best" tool. They're the ones who stopped looking for a single winner and built a short list of two or three, each with a clear job to do.

Different tools have genuinely different strengths, and using a hammer for every job means some tasks are going to get mangled. The stack matters more than the subscription.

## How a working stack fits together

Most stacks that actually hold up settle into roughly three slots:

**Your core thinking and writing tool.** This is the one you open most mornings, where you do your primary work: drafting, analyzing, planning, thinking. Most professionals in our community use Claude here. It handles long documents well, gives careful output on tasks with a lot of moving parts, and is particularly strong for anything that requires reasoning across a lot of context (like analyzing a long report or giving feedback on a multi-page proposal). Claude Pro runs $20/month.

**Your research and current-events tool.** This is where you go when you need up-to-date information with sources. Perplexity is the clear choice. Unlike Claude or ChatGPT, which can only draw on their training data, Perplexity queries the web in real time and returns cited sources you can actually check. For anyone doing competitive analysis, summarizing recent news, or preparing for a meeting on a fast-moving topic, this is essential. Perplexity Pro is $20/month.

**Your embedded work tool (if your company uses Microsoft 365).** Microsoft Copilot lives inside Outlook, Teams, Word, and Excel. If your organization already pays for it, it's the fastest way to do AI-assisted summarization of your existing email threads, meeting transcripts, and documents without copying and pasting anything. The reason to use it is not that it's better than Claude. It's that it has access to your actual files and conversations, which neither Claude nor Perplexity does by default.

A minimal two-tool stack (Claude Pro + Perplexity Pro) runs about $40/month. Add Copilot if your company covers it. That's a reasonable starting point for most mid-career professionals.

## Stack recommendations by work style

Not everyone needs the same combination. The right stack depends on how you actually spend your day.

### For writers, communicators, and consultants

**Primary: Claude Pro. Secondary: Perplexity.**

If your work is mostly writing, Claude is where you want to spend your time. It produces careful, readable prose. It holds nuance across long documents. When you feed it a brief and ask for a client memo, it doesn't flatten your thinking the way some tools do.

Use Perplexity when you need to ground your work in recent data. Before you write that industry update or client briefing, spend five minutes in Perplexity pulling in recent context. Then bring what you find into Claude to draft against it.

> **T Gaines, MVP Club community member:** "Claude is just better. He's a better friend." (On switching from ChatGPT to Claude for technical troubleshooting and writing work.)

### For managers and team leads

**Primary: Microsoft Copilot (if available) + Claude Pro. Secondary: Perplexity.**

If you live in Microsoft 365, Copilot is genuinely useful for the volume of communication you're managing. Summarizing long email threads, drafting meeting agendas from Teams notes, pulling action items from recorded calls: these are things Copilot does without requiring you to copy anything out of your inbox.

For anything more substantive (writing a performance review, preparing talking points for a difficult conversation, analyzing team feedback), bring it into Claude. The quality of thinking Claude applies to complex, relationship-heavy writing is meaningfully better than what Copilot produces on the same tasks.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what a manager in MVP Club has said about using Copilot for volume tasks and Claude for high-stakes writing]

### For builders and project-oriented professionals

**Primary: Claude Pro (with Claude Code if you're vibe coding). Secondary: Perplexity or ChatGPT.**

If you're building internal tools, writing project specs, or doing any vibe coding with Claude Code, your stack is straightforward: Claude is your main collaborator. The key is learning to give it a well-structured context file at the start of each session.

[NEEDS QUOTE: Matt on tool stack approach]

For research, Perplexity is still your best source-citation tool. But if you need to bounce an idea off something fast, ChatGPT's interface is quick and capable for shorter tasks where citation doesn't matter.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: community member tool stack story]

### For people who want to start simply

**Primary: Claude Pro. That's it for now.**

If you're reading this before you've built a consistent AI habit, don't start with multiple tools. Start with one. Claude Pro at $20/month is the right choice for most professional writing and thinking work.

Build a habit first. Get a sense of what you're using it for, where it falls short, and what you wish it could do. Then add a second tool to fill that gap. Adding tools before you have a clear job for them creates noise, not results.

## What to use each tool for: a quick reference

**Claude:** Long documents, nuanced writing, analysis that requires reasoning across multiple paragraphs, feedback on professional drafts, strategic planning prompts, anything where tone and care matter.

**ChatGPT:** Quick tasks, creative brainstorming, custom GPTs for recurring workflows, tasks where you want image generation alongside text, integrations through Zapier or similar connectors.

**Perplexity:** Research with current sources, competitive intelligence, summarizing recent news, fact-checking claims before you include them in professional documents.

**Microsoft Copilot:** Email summarization, Teams meeting transcription and action items, first-pass drafts directly inside Word or Excel, anything that lives in your Microsoft ecosystem.

**Claude Code / Claude Cowork:** Vibe coding, building internal tools, automating repeatable multi-step workflows, anything that involves working with files and folders directly.

## Common mistakes with tool stacks

**Using all of them for the same tasks.** This sounds like thoroughness but it's actually confusion. If you're running the same prompt through three tools and comparing outputs, you're not using a stack. You're doing research. Pick the right tool for the job and use it.

**Switching tools every time you're disappointed.** If Claude gives you a mediocre output, the answer is usually a better prompt, not a different tool. Most tool-switching is actually prompt-quality avoidance. Learn to iterate before you switch.

**Treating free tiers as representative.** The free versions of Claude and ChatGPT are good enough for casual use. They're not representative of what the paid versions can do. If you've tried a tool on the free tier and decided it wasn't worth it, you may have tried the wrong version.

**Adding tools without adding workflows.** A tool without a workflow is an app you open twice and forget. Before you subscribe to something new, decide what specific task it will do and when in your week you'll do it.

## From the Practice

One pattern that comes up consistently in our New AI Tools Monday sessions: members who build their stack around one primary tool and add secondary tools as they hit clear limitations tend to get better results than those who spread across five tools from day one.

[NEEDS QUOTE: Ryan on technical tools]

The stack is in service of a routine, not the other way around.

The professionals who get the most from these tools are the ones who've decided what their tools are for. They didn't arrive there from reading comparison guides. They tried things, noticed what was missing, and added accordingly. That process takes a few weeks, not a few hours, and it's worth the patience.

## Your next step

The most useful thing you can do right now is open your primary tool and give it one task you've been avoiding. Notice where it falls short. That gap is your signal for what to add next.

If you're using ChatGPT for everything and finding it weak on long-document analysis, try Claude for that specific task before swapping your whole setup. One swap at a time is how a stack gets built.

Most professionals who get real results from AI are using two or three tools, each with a specific job, without much overlap.

Most people build their stack backwards. They pick a tool they’ve heard of, use it for everything, get inconsistent results, and assume AI isn’t as useful as advertised.

The fix: assign each tool a clear job. Stop expecting any single tool to do all of them well. This guide is opinionated about what works, based on what MVP Club members actually run in their workflows.

Why one tool is almost never enough

Claude is better than ChatGPT for nuanced writing and document work. ChatGPT is more versatile for quick tasks and has broader integrations. Perplexity is the only major tool that reliably cites current sources. Microsoft Copilot is the only one that lives inside your email and spreadsheets.

None of those sentences is false. Which is exactly why you can’t pick just one and call it done.

The professionals who get the most out of AI aren’t the ones who found the “best” tool. They’re the ones who stopped looking for a single winner and built a short list of two or three, each with a clear job to do.

Different tools have genuinely different strengths, and using a hammer for every job means some tasks are going to get mangled. The stack matters more than the subscription.

How a working stack fits together

Most stacks that actually hold up settle into roughly three slots:

Your core thinking and writing tool. This is the one you open most mornings, where you do your primary work: drafting, analyzing, planning, thinking. Most professionals in our community use Claude here. It handles long documents well, gives careful output on tasks with a lot of moving parts, and is particularly strong for anything that requires reasoning across a lot of context (like analyzing a long report or giving feedback on a multi-page proposal). Claude Pro runs $20/month.

Your research and current-events tool. This is where you go when you need up-to-date information with sources. Perplexity is the clear choice. Unlike Claude or ChatGPT, which can only draw on their training data, Perplexity queries the web in real time and returns cited sources you can actually check. For anyone doing competitive analysis, summarizing recent news, or preparing for a meeting on a fast-moving topic, this is essential. Perplexity Pro is $20/month.

Your embedded work tool (if your company uses Microsoft 365). Microsoft Copilot lives inside Outlook, Teams, Word, and Excel. If your organization already pays for it, it’s the fastest way to do AI-assisted summarization of your existing email threads, meeting transcripts, and documents without copying and pasting anything. The reason to use it is not that it’s better than Claude. It’s that it has access to your actual files and conversations, which neither Claude nor Perplexity does by default.

A minimal two-tool stack (Claude Pro + Perplexity Pro) runs about $40/month. Add Copilot if your company covers it. That’s a reasonable starting point for most mid-career professionals.

Use Perplexity when you need to ground your work in recent data. Before you write that industry update or client briefing, spend five minutes in Perplexity pulling in recent context. Then bring what you find into Claude to draft against it.
