Is the $20/Month AI Subscription Worth It? Free vs Paid Compared
ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, and Gemini Advanced all cost $20/month. Here's which is worth paying for, which free tier holds up, and Ryan's honest take.

Three of the biggest AI tools all charge the same price: $20 a month. ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, and Gemini Advanced have landed at nearly identical price points, which makes the comparison feel easy. Until you realize they're actually quite different in what you get and who each one is really for.

If you're a mid-career professional trying to figure out whether to spend $240 a year on an AI subscription, this is the guide that will actually answer that question. Not with a spec sheet, but with a real take on what each paid tier unlocks and whether it matters for the kind of work you actually do.

## The short answer (if you want to skip to the verdict)

Pay for Claude Pro. It's the best tool for professional writing, long documents, and the kind of iterative thinking work that takes up most of your day. The free tier hits its limits too fast for serious daily use.

If you're already deep in the Google ecosystem (Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Meet), Gemini Advanced at $19.99/month is the strongest case for a second subscription. But it's a second subscription, not a first one.

ChatGPT Plus is worth it if you need image generation (DALL-E is included), voice mode, or if your organization has standardized on OpenAI. As a standalone purchase for professional writing and analysis, it comes in third.

---

## What you get on each free tier (and where it runs out)

Before we talk about paid plans, you need to know what the free tiers actually do. Because for lighter users, free is genuinely fine.

**ChatGPT Free** gives you access to GPT-4o with usage limits that kick in when traffic is high. You get the conversational interface, basic image generation (limited), and web search. The limits are fuzzy (OpenAI doesn't publish them precisely), but most people notice throttling after 10-15 heavy prompts in a day.

**Claude Free** gives you access to Claude Sonnet with daily message limits. The limits are meaningful: if you're using Claude for substantive work tasks (drafting, analysis, reviewing documents), you'll hit the cap on a busy day. The free tier is excellent for trying Claude and occasional use. It's not built for daily professional work.

**Gemini Free** gives you access to Gemini 1.5 Flash, which is faster but less capable than the Advanced models. It integrates with Google Search, which is useful. The quality gap between the free and paid Gemini tiers is the largest of the three: Gemini Advanced uses the Pro models, which are a genuine step up.

One MVP Club member [NEEDS ATTRIBUTION: name/role] described the paid upgrade in a recent session: "It's so luxurious just to know you're never gonna run out, just to keep going." That's exactly it. The biggest practical difference between free and paid is not one specific feature. You stop managing the tool and start using it.

## Claude Pro ($20/month): Ryan's pick for professional work

[Pricing and plan details at claude.com/pricing](https://claude.com/pricing)

Claude is the best AI writing tool available right now for the kind of work mid-career professionals actually do: long emails, memos, policy documents, performance reviews, proposals, analysis. The writing sounds like a person wrote it. That's harder to achieve than it sounds, and it's Claude's clearest advantage.

The Pro plan removes the daily message limits and gives you access to Claude's most capable models. For someone using Claude every day, this matters. The free tier will stop you mid-project.

What else you get with Claude Pro:
- Extended context window: Claude can hold very long documents in memory during a conversation, which means you can paste in a 30-page report and actually ask questions about it
- Priority access when demand is high
- Access to Projects (organized workspaces where Claude maintains context across multiple conversations)

**What Claude is specifically better at than its competitors:** Writing that needs to sound human. Analysis that requires holding a lot of nuance at once. Documents where you're going back and forth with revisions. Anything where the output quality matters more than the speed of getting it.

**What Claude is weaker on:** Image generation (Claude can analyze images but generates them in limited ways). Real-time web browsing isn't as seamlessly integrated as in Gemini. If you need an AI tool that searches the web as part of every answer, Claude isn't the strongest choice.

**Ryan's take:** If you're only going to subscribe to one AI tool for professional work, this is the one.

## ChatGPT Plus ($20/month): the best-known tool with the longest feature list

[Pricing at openai.com/chatgpt/pricing](https://openai.com/chatgpt/pricing)

ChatGPT invented the consumer AI subscription market. It has the highest name recognition, the widest user base, and the longest feature list of the three. For professional writing and analysis, Claude has closed the gap and then some. But ChatGPT Plus has capabilities the others don't match, and for the right use cases it's still the right call.

The features you get with ChatGPT Plus are genuinely broad. GPT-4o without usage limits, DALL-E image generation, voice mode, data analysis and code execution, web browsing, and Custom GPTs (pre-built assistants for specific tasks). If you use several of those regularly, the $20 covers a lot of ground.

Where it's the clear pick: image generation (DALL-E is the best consumer option), voice mode for hands-free brainstorming on a commute, and code or data file analysis. If your company has standardized on OpenAI's Team or Enterprise plans, note that the paid plans handle data privacy differently from the free tier ([OpenAI's enterprise privacy page](https://openai.com/enterprise-privacy/) has the specifics).

The tradeoff is writing quality. For longer professional documents, ChatGPT tends toward a recognizable style: confident, well-structured, slightly formal in a way that can read as "AI wrote this." That's fine for a lot of tasks. It's a problem when the output needs to sound like you specifically. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific task where ChatGPT's output needed significant editing vs. Claude's]

For a deeper look at getting real value from ChatGPT in day-to-day work, [the ChatGPT at work guide](/learn/chatgpt-at-work) covers what actually transfers to professional tasks.

## Gemini Advanced ($19.99/month via Google One AI Premium): the ecosystem play

[Pricing at one.google.com](https://one.google.com)

Gemini Advanced is sold as part of Google One AI Premium, which means your $19.99 also buys you 2TB of Google storage. If you're currently paying for Google One storage separately, that math shifts the comparison significantly: the AI access is essentially bundled in.

What you get with Gemini Advanced:
- Access to Google's most capable Gemini Pro models
- 1 million token context window (the longest of the three; this matters if you work with very large documents)
- Deep Research (Gemini can conduct multi-step web research on a topic and synthesize findings into a report)
- Gemini integrated directly into Gmail, Google Docs, Google Sheets, and Google Meet
- Custom Gems (saved prompts and personas)

The Google Workspace integration is the real differentiator. If you live in Google Docs and Gmail, Gemini Advanced puts AI directly into those tools without copy-pasting. You can ask Gemini to summarize your Gmail threads, draft a reply in Docs, or pull insights from a Google Sheet, all without leaving the app you're already in.

The 1M context window is also worth noting (that's roughly enough to hold an entire book-length document in a single conversation). For anyone working with very long contracts, research reports, or large collections of documents, Gemini Advanced handles scale that the other plans don't.

**Where Gemini Advanced makes sense:**
- You're already paying for Google One storage (the AI is effectively included)
- Your work is centered in Google Workspace
- You frequently need AI to pull from real-time web sources
- You work with very long documents

**The real limitation:** Gemini's writing quality for standalone professional documents is behind Claude. The Google integration is excellent. The AI writing itself, especially for anything that needs nuance or a human tone, tends to be more generic. [NEEDS EXAMPLE: a specific prompt where Gemini's output fell noticeably short]

**Who should pay for Gemini Advanced:** Google Workspace power users who would pay for 2TB of storage anyway. As a first AI subscription, it's a harder case to make.

## Side-by-side comparison

| | ChatGPT Plus | Claude Pro | Gemini Advanced |
|---|---|---|---|
| Price | $20/month | $20/month | $19.99/month |
| Best for | Images, voice, code | Professional writing, long docs | Google Workspace users |
| Writing quality | Good | Best | Moderate |
| Image generation | Yes (DALL-E) | Limited | Yes (Imagen) |
| Web search | Yes | Yes | Yes (best integrated) |
| Context window | 128K tokens | 200K tokens | 1M tokens |
| App integrations | OpenAI apps | Limited | Deep Google Workspace |
| Data privacy (paid) | Good | Good | Good |

*Token counts measure how much text the AI can hold in memory at once. 128K tokens is roughly a 300-page book; 200K is closer to 500 pages; 1M tokens can handle entire large reports or multiple long documents at once.*

## What we use at MVP Club

Ryan Brodsky, one of MVP Club's co-founders, has run Claude Pro as his primary AI subscription for over a year. He's noted the specific cost: "The $100/month Claude Pro plan [he uses the Max tier], used daily." For the volume of building, writing, and iteration his work requires, the higher tier pays for itself.

For professional writing and document work specifically, the community consensus in MVP Club's weekly sessions runs toward Claude. Members in marketing, HR, operations, and consulting roles consistently describe Claude's writing as the one that requires the least editing before it's ready to send.

<!-- NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: members describing which paid tool they use for their professional writing and why -->

## The real question: do you need paid at all?

Before you pull out a credit card, try this: use the free tier of the tool you're most interested in for two weeks, doing your actual work with it.

If you hit the limit on a day when you needed more, that's your signal. If you consistently get what you need from the free tier, there's no urgency. If you're not sure which tasks are good fits for AI tools in the first place, [what AI can and can't do right now](/learn/what-ai-can-and-cant-do) is worth a read before you commit. The free tiers of all three tools are genuinely functional for lighter use.

The case for paid gets stronger when AI becomes part of your daily professional workflow, not just occasional use. One session or two a week? Free is probably fine. Five to ten substantive interactions a day? The limits will find you.

A few practical checks before subscribing:
- Does your company have an enterprise plan? (Check with IT before paying out of pocket)
- Does your work already live in Google Workspace? (That shifts the Gemini math)
- Do you need image generation? (ChatGPT Plus is the best option there)

If none of those apply and you want a single paid AI subscription for professional writing and thinking work, the answer is Claude Pro.

## The bottom line

The $20/month AI subscription is worth it when AI becomes part of your daily workflow, not occasional use. The free tiers are real and usable. The paid tiers remove the friction of limits and unlock the capabilities that make these tools work across a full workday.

The fastest way to know which one is right for you: spend two weeks using the free tier of the tool you're most curious about for actual work tasks. Not demos, not test prompts. Real tasks. The answer will surface on its own.

Once you know which tool fits how you work, [the AI tool stack guide](/learn/ai-tool-stack) covers how to think about combining tools rather than picking just one.

**Update log:** Pricing verified April 2, 2026. Review quarterly, this market moves fast.

---

Where people figure out which tools actually fit their work is in practice alongside other professionals. If you want to see what people in roles like yours are using and why, that's a regular conversation in [MVP Club's weekly sessions](/community).

Three of the biggest AI tools all charge the same price: $20 a month. ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, and Gemini Advanced have landed at nearly identical price points, which makes the comparison feel easy. Until you realize they’re actually quite different in what you get and who each one is really for.

If you’re a mid-career professional trying to figure out whether to spend $240 a year on an AI subscription, this is the guide that will actually answer that question. Not with a spec sheet, but with a real take on what each paid tier unlocks and whether it matters for the kind of work you actually do.

The short answer (if you want to skip to the verdict)

Pay for Claude Pro. It’s the best tool for professional writing, long documents, and the kind of iterative thinking work that takes up most of your day. The free tier hits its limits too fast for serious daily use.

If you’re already deep in the Google ecosystem (Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Meet), Gemini Advanced at $19.99/month is the strongest case for a second subscription. But it’s a second subscription, not a first one.

ChatGPT Plus is worth it if you need image generation (DALL-E is included), voice mode, or if your organization has standardized on OpenAI. As a standalone purchase for professional writing and analysis, it comes in third.

What you get on each free tier (and where it runs out)

Before we talk about paid plans, you need to know what the free tiers actually do. Because for lighter users, free is genuinely fine.

ChatGPT Free gives you access to GPT-4o with usage limits that kick in when traffic is high. You get the conversational interface, basic image generation (limited), and web search. The limits are fuzzy (OpenAI doesn’t publish them precisely), but most people notice throttling after 10-15 heavy prompts in a day.

Claude Free gives you access to Claude Sonnet with daily message limits. The limits are meaningful: if you’re using Claude for substantive work tasks (drafting, analysis, reviewing documents), you’ll hit the cap on a busy day. The free tier is excellent for trying Claude and occasional use. It’s not built for daily professional work.

Gemini Free gives you access to Gemini 1.5 Flash, which is faster but less capable than the Advanced models. It integrates with Google Search, which is useful. The quality gap between the free and paid Gemini tiers is the largest of the three: Gemini Advanced uses the Pro models, which are a genuine step up.

One MVP Club member [NEEDS ATTRIBUTION: name/role] described the paid upgrade in a recent session: “It’s so luxurious just to know you’re never gonna run out, just to keep going.” That’s exactly it. The biggest practical difference between free and paid is not one specific feature. You stop managing the tool and start using it.

Claude Pro ($20/month): Ryan’s pick for professional work

Pricing and plan details at claude.com/pricing

Claude is the best AI writing tool available right now for the kind of work mid-career professionals actually do: long emails, memos, policy documents, performance reviews, proposals, analysis. The writing sounds like a person wrote it. That’s harder to achieve than it sounds, and it’s Claude’s clearest advantage.

The Pro plan removes the daily message limits and gives you access to Claude’s most capable models. For someone using Claude every day, this matters. The free tier will stop you mid-project.

What else you get with Claude Pro:

  • Extended context window: Claude can hold very long documents in memory during a conversation, which means you can paste in a 30-page report and actually ask questions about it
  • Priority access when demand is high
  • Access to Projects (organized workspaces where Claude maintains context across multiple conversations)

What Claude is specifically better at than its competitors: Writing that needs to sound human. Analysis that requires holding a lot of nuance at once. Documents where you’re going back and forth with revisions. Anything where the output quality matters more than the speed of getting it.

What Claude is weaker on: Image generation (Claude can analyze images but generates them in limited ways). Real-time web browsing isn’t as seamlessly integrated as in Gemini. If you need an AI tool that searches the web as part of every answer, Claude isn’t the strongest choice.

Ryan’s take: If you’re only going to subscribe to one AI tool for professional work, this is the one.

ChatGPT Plus ($20/month): the best-known tool with the longest feature list

Pricing at openai.com/chatgpt/pricing

ChatGPT invented the consumer AI subscription market. It has the highest name recognition, the widest user base, and the longest feature list of the three. For professional writing and analysis, Claude has closed the gap and then some. But ChatGPT Plus has capabilities the others don’t match, and for the right use cases it’s still the right call.

The features you get with ChatGPT Plus are genuinely broad. GPT-4o without usage limits, DALL-E image generation, voice mode, data analysis and code execution, web browsing, and Custom GPTs (pre-built assistants for specific tasks). If you use several of those regularly, the $20 covers a lot of ground.

Where it’s the clear pick: image generation (DALL-E is the best consumer option), voice mode for hands-free brainstorming on a commute, and code or data file analysis. If your company has standardized on OpenAI’s Team or Enterprise plans, note that the paid plans handle data privacy differently from the free tier (OpenAI’s enterprise privacy page has the specifics).

The tradeoff is writing quality. For longer professional documents, ChatGPT tends toward a recognizable style: confident, well-structured, slightly formal in a way that can read as “AI wrote this.” That’s fine for a lot of tasks. It’s a problem when the output needs to sound like you specifically. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific task where ChatGPT’s output needed significant editing vs. Claude’s]

For a deeper look at getting real value from ChatGPT in day-to-day work, the ChatGPT at work guide covers what actually transfers to professional tasks.

Gemini Advanced ($19.99/month via Google One AI Premium): the ecosystem play

Pricing at one.google.com

Gemini Advanced is sold as part of Google One AI Premium, which means your $19.99 also buys you 2TB of Google storage. If you’re currently paying for Google One storage separately, that math shifts the comparison significantly: the AI access is essentially bundled in.

What you get with Gemini Advanced:

  • Access to Google’s most capable Gemini Pro models
  • 1 million token context window (the longest of the three; this matters if you work with very large documents)
  • Deep Research (Gemini can conduct multi-step web research on a topic and synthesize findings into a report)
  • Gemini integrated directly into Gmail, Google Docs, Google Sheets, and Google Meet
  • Custom Gems (saved prompts and personas)

The Google Workspace integration is the real differentiator. If you live in Google Docs and Gmail, Gemini Advanced puts AI directly into those tools without copy-pasting. You can ask Gemini to summarize your Gmail threads, draft a reply in Docs, or pull insights from a Google Sheet, all without leaving the app you’re already in.

The 1M context window is also worth noting (that’s roughly enough to hold an entire book-length document in a single conversation). For anyone working with very long contracts, research reports, or large collections of documents, Gemini Advanced handles scale that the other plans don’t.

Where Gemini Advanced makes sense:

  • You’re already paying for Google One storage (the AI is effectively included)
  • Your work is centered in Google Workspace
  • You frequently need AI to pull from real-time web sources
  • You work with very long documents

The real limitation: Gemini’s writing quality for standalone professional documents is behind Claude. The Google integration is excellent. The AI writing itself, especially for anything that needs nuance or a human tone, tends to be more generic. [NEEDS EXAMPLE: a specific prompt where Gemini’s output fell noticeably short]

Who should pay for Gemini Advanced: Google Workspace power users who would pay for 2TB of storage anyway. As a first AI subscription, it’s a harder case to make.

Side-by-side comparison

ChatGPT PlusClaude ProGemini Advanced
Price$20/month$20/month$19.99/month
Best forImages, voice, codeProfessional writing, long docsGoogle Workspace users
Writing qualityGoodBestModerate
Image generationYes (DALL-E)LimitedYes (Imagen)
Web searchYesYesYes (best integrated)
Context window128K tokens200K tokens1M tokens
App integrationsOpenAI appsLimitedDeep Google Workspace
Data privacy (paid)GoodGoodGood

Token counts measure how much text the AI can hold in memory at once. 128K tokens is roughly a 300-page book; 200K is closer to 500 pages; 1M tokens can handle entire large reports or multiple long documents at once.

What we use at MVP Club

Ryan Brodsky, one of MVP Club’s co-founders, has run Claude Pro as his primary AI subscription for over a year. He’s noted the specific cost: “The $100/month Claude Pro plan [he uses the Max tier], used daily.” For the volume of building, writing, and iteration his work requires, the higher tier pays for itself.

For professional writing and document work specifically, the community consensus in MVP Club’s weekly sessions runs toward Claude. Members in marketing, HR, operations, and consulting roles consistently describe Claude’s writing as the one that requires the least editing before it’s ready to send.

The real question: do you need paid at all?

Before you pull out a credit card, try this: use the free tier of the tool you’re most interested in for two weeks, doing your actual work with it.

If you hit the limit on a day when you needed more, that’s your signal. If you consistently get what you need from the free tier, there’s no urgency. If you’re not sure which tasks are good fits for AI tools in the first place, what AI can and can’t do right now is worth a read before you commit. The free tiers of all three tools are genuinely functional for lighter use.

The case for paid gets stronger when AI becomes part of your daily professional workflow, not just occasional use. One session or two a week? Free is probably fine. Five to ten substantive interactions a day? The limits will find you.

A few practical checks before subscribing:

  • Does your company have an enterprise plan? (Check with IT before paying out of pocket)
  • Does your work already live in Google Workspace? (That shifts the Gemini math)
  • Do you need image generation? (ChatGPT Plus is the best option there)

If none of those apply and you want a single paid AI subscription for professional writing and thinking work, the answer is Claude Pro.

The bottom line

The $20/month AI subscription is worth it when AI becomes part of your daily workflow, not occasional use. The free tiers are real and usable. The paid tiers remove the friction of limits and unlock the capabilities that make these tools work across a full workday.

The fastest way to know which one is right for you: spend two weeks using the free tier of the tool you’re most curious about for actual work tasks. Not demos, not test prompts. Real tasks. The answer will surface on its own.

Once you know which tool fits how you work, the AI tool stack guide covers how to think about combining tools rather than picking just one.

AI Tools for Research and Analysis: A Professional's Guide
Claude, ChatGPT, and Perplexity compared for professional research. Which AI tool fits your work research needs and when to use each one.

## The quick verdict upfront

If you want citations and live web results, use Perplexity. If you want to reason through a large document or synthesize a pile of information, use Claude. If you want a general research starting point that connects to other tools in your workflow, ChatGPT works well. Most professionals who do regular research work end up using all three, but for different jobs.

## What we use at MVP Club and why

For day-to-day professional research, the pattern that holds up best is: Perplexity first to find and verify current information, Claude to do the analysis and synthesis once you have the pieces in hand.

Both Claude Pro and Perplexity Pro run $20/month each, so $40/month combined if you want both. For anyone doing regular research as part of their job, that tends to pay for itself quickly. Research tasks that used to eat a full afternoon can be done alongside other work, because you're spending your time on judgment calls rather than on pulling information together.

One practical workflow: paste the Perplexity summary (with citations) into a new Claude conversation, then ask Claude to identify what is most relevant to your specific question, what assumptions you should challenge, and what you would still want to verify. The two tools work well together because they are doing different jobs.

If budget is a constraint, start with Perplexity's free tier for research and Claude's free tier for analysis. Both are meaningfully useful before you pay anything.

## What we're comparing and why

This comparison focuses on four things that matter for professional work research:

1. Whether the tool searches the live web or works from training data
2. How well it handles documents you bring to the conversation
3. Whether it cites sources (and whether those citations are real)
4. What it actually costs

Academic research has its own set of tools (Elicit, Consensus, Research Rabbit). This guide is for the rest of it: market research, competitor analysis, background briefings, policy review, vendor evaluation, trend synthesis, and anything else a mid-career professional might need before a meeting or a deliverable.

## Perplexity: best for live research with citations

Perplexity connects to the web in real time. Ask it about a company, a market trend, or a recent event, and it searches current sources and surfaces numbered citations alongside the answer. That citation layer matters more than it sounds: you can click through to the source, verify the claim, and share the reference with a colleague. You are not flying blind on whether the information is current.

The Pro plan ($20/month) includes a "Deep Research" feature that reads dozens of sources before composing a summary. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific use case where a community member used Perplexity Deep Research for a professional research task]

The limitation is depth of analysis. Perplexity is excellent at finding and synthesizing what is publicly available. It is less useful when you need it to reason through a complex scenario, write at length, or work with a document you have uploaded.

One MVP Club member described the experience this way:

> "I really like the browser experience that Perplexity has. It feels like I don't know if I want to go back to another one with how well that setup works." — Community member (Getting Unstuck Friday session)

The free tier gives you a limited number of Deep Research queries per day. The $20/month Pro plan removes most of those limits and also gives access to additional underlying models including Claude and GPT-4o through the same interface.

**Best for:** Competitive intelligence, market research, vendor background checks, staying current on an industry or topic, anything where you need sources you can point to.

## Claude: best for analysis, synthesis, and long documents

Claude (from Anthropic) handles large amounts of text better than any other tool in this category. You can paste in a long report, a policy document, a contract, or a set of meeting notes, and Claude will read all of it and answer questions about it with good precision. The context window on Claude Pro (and even the free tier) is large enough to hold a complete 30-50 page research document in a single conversation, without losing track of earlier sections.

Where Claude earns its place in a research workflow is in the analysis step that comes after you have gathered information. Feed it a vendor proposal and ask it to surface the risks. Upload a competitor's annual report and ask what changed year over year. Paste in three articles on the same topic and ask it to identify where they agree and where they diverge.

McKinsey's [2025 workplace AI report](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/superagency-in-the-workplace-empowering-people-to-unlock-ais-full-potential-at-work) [NEEDS VERIFIED CITATION] found that knowledge management is one of the functions with the highest reported AI use, with research and synthesis among the most common applications. Claude's ability to hold long context makes it well-suited for exactly that work.

Claude does not search the live web by default on the standard plan, which means you need to bring the documents to it rather than asking it to find them. That is a meaningful limitation for current-events research. For analysis of information you already have, it is the strongest tool in this comparison.

[NEEDS VERIFIED MATT QUOTE ON RESEARCH]

Where Claude earns its keep: you have a document or a set of documents and you need to think through what they actually mean. Not finding information. Using it.

## ChatGPT: best for flexible research starting points

ChatGPT (the Plus plan, $20/month) includes web search through its built-in browsing feature, a code interpreter for working with data files, and a large library of custom GPTs that extend its capabilities into specialized research tasks. It is the most flexible of the three tools in terms of what it can connect to.

The Advanced Data Analysis feature lets you upload a spreadsheet and ask questions about it in plain English. For research that involves data alongside text, this is genuinely useful. You can upload a CSV of survey results, ask what the main themes are, and get a real answer rather than a guess. The guide to [AI for data analysis](/learn/ai-for-data-analysis) covers this in detail.

ChatGPT's web search works but is less citation-focused than Perplexity. The results come with links, but the experience of verifying and tracing sources is slightly more effort. For research where you need to hand off a source list to a colleague or cite something in a document, Perplexity makes that step easier.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what MVP Club members actually use ChatGPT for in their research workflows]

If your research involves a spreadsheet, or you need a tool that plays nicely with integrations you already have, ChatGPT is worth having in the rotation.

## Comparison table by use case

| Research task | Best tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Competitor background check | Perplexity | Live web, citations |
| Trend analysis with sources | Perplexity | Real-time, citable |
| Analyzing a long document | Claude | Large context window |
| Synthesizing multiple sources you provide | Claude | Reasoning depth |
| Working with a spreadsheet | ChatGPT | Advanced Data Analysis |
| General research starting point | Any | All three work |
| Current events, news | Perplexity | Live web access |
| Deep reasoning on a complex question | Claude | Strongest analysis |

## What to know about AI hallucination in research contexts

All three tools can produce plausible-sounding information that is wrong. Perplexity's citation system reduces this risk for factual claims about current events because you can verify the source. Claude and ChatGPT, when working from training data rather than live search, can confidently state things that are outdated or incorrect.

The practical rule for professional research: treat AI-generated research the way you would treat a colleague's verbal summary. Useful, often accurate, worth acting on, but not something to paste directly into a client document without reviewing the underlying sources. Use Perplexity's citations to trace claims. Verify any specific statistic or date before it goes into a deliverable.

## Where to go from here

Take a real research question from your job and run it through two of these tools back to back. The difference in outputs will teach you more than any comparison guide, because it will be grounded in something you actually care about.

Once you've done that a few times, the next step is building a repeatable workflow around the pattern that works. That's where things get interesting: not just using these tools once, but knowing which one to reach for without having to think about it.

For more on using these tools in specific workflows, see [how to use AI for better results](/learn/better-ai-results) and [AI for data analysis](/learn/ai-for-data-analysis).

AI Tools That Flopped: Honest Reviews of What Didn't Work
Honest reviews of AI tools and features that got hyped but didn't deliver for real professional work. What we tried, why it failed, and what to do instead.

Microsoft Copilot costs $30 per user per month, requires an existing Microsoft 365 license, and in early enterprise rollouts was chosen by only 8% of workers when they had access to multiple tools simultaneously. That's not a success story. That's a cautionary one.

The AI tool market is full of those right now. Vendors promise transformation. Professionals open a new tab, try something for two weeks, close it, and go back to ChatGPT or Claude. What follows are honest breakdowns of the tools that got real attention, real money, and real professional time, and what actually happened when people used them.

## Why AI tools fail in real work (and it's not about the AI)

Most AI tool failures have nothing to do with the underlying model quality. The common thread is a mismatch between what the tool was designed to do and what a busy professional actually needs from it.

The AI industry spent 2023 and 2024 shipping features. Speed to market mattered more than fit. The result: a generation of tools that are technically impressive in demos and genuinely frustrating in daily use. If industry estimates hold, something like 30% of generative AI projects were abandoned after the proof-of-concept stage by end of 2025, and the real number may be higher. [NEEDS CITATION: Gartner or similar primary source on AI project abandonment rates]

## Microsoft Copilot: The most hyped, least-used AI tool in enterprise

**The promise:** AI built directly into Word, Excel, Outlook, and Teams. No switching tabs. No new learning curve. Productivity gains baked into software you already use every day.

**What actually happened:** Copilot's paid subscriber market share contracted sharply between mid-2025 and early 2026 [NEEDS CITATION]. Users reported basic prompt misunderstandings, inaccurate outputs, and integration bugs. The $30/month-per-user price tag on top of an existing Microsoft 365 subscription made it hard to justify for teams that weren't seeing immediate, measurable results.

The deeper problem: Copilot was designed to sit inside Microsoft products, which means it's only as good as the documents, emails, and meetings you feed it. If your team doesn't have tight, organized processes, Copilot amplifies that messiness rather than fixing it. It's a reasoning engine sitting on top of whatever chaos already lives in your SharePoint.

**What to do instead:** If you're in a Microsoft shop and want to use AI for writing and summarization, try [using Claude or ChatGPT alongside your existing Office tools](/learn/chatgpt-at-work) rather than paying for a native integration that isn't delivering yet. The tab-switching adds two seconds. The quality difference can be significant.

## Dedicated AI writing tools: The "magic button" problem

**The promise:** Tools like Jasper, Writesonic, and Copy.ai promised to generate polished, publish-ready content for marketing, email, and proposals. Type a brief, get a finished product.

**What actually happened:** The content comes out generically good and specifically wrong. These tools are trained to produce text that matches broad patterns, which means the output sounds professional but lacks anything distinctive. You still have to rewrite most of it. For professionals who write to represent their own voice, brand, or judgment, the editing time often exceeds the time it would have taken to draft from scratch.

Ryan Brodsky put it bluntly in a community session when describing the challenge with AI-generated copy: "Does this sound like AI? Because I'm conscious of that." That self-awareness is exactly what dedicated writing tools skip past. They optimize for fluency, not authenticity.

The real issue is that these tools solve for volume, not quality. If you need 500 product descriptions with minor variations, they're genuinely useful. If you need one good proposal that actually sounds like you, they're a detour.

**What to do instead:** Use Claude or ChatGPT as a thinking partner, not a writing machine. Give it your rough draft and ask it to tighten, question, or push back. That workflow gets you a better final product than any "generate from brief" approach. See [how to get better results from AI](/learn/better-ai-results) for the actual iteration pattern.

## AI meeting summary tools: When "summary" means "transcript with bullet points"

Otter, Fireflies, and similar tools make a reasonable pitch: drop in your meeting recording, get a clean summary of decisions, action items, and key points. The problem shows up the first time you run a real meeting through one.

Most AI meeting tools produce summaries that are accurate in that they include what was said, and useless in that they don't understand what mattered. A 45-minute strategy session gets compressed into a list of every topic mentioned, with no sense of relative importance or actual resolution. The "action items" section often captures anything phrased as a future activity, including offhand comments like "we should probably think about that at some point."

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: members who tried Otter, Fireflies, or similar tools and reverted to manual notes]

The tool doesn't know your organizational context. It doesn't know that the budget discussion was the whole point of the meeting and the product update was background. It treats everything as equally weighted because it has no way to do otherwise.

The better workflow: use a meeting tool for raw transcription, then bring that transcript into Claude or ChatGPT with a specific prompt: "Here's the transcript from our strategy meeting. Our key open questions going in were X, Y, and Z. What did we decide on each one, and what are the clear next steps?" That workflow uses AI for what it's actually good at: reading a long document and extracting specific things you asked for.

## AI image generators with style bias: The Studio Ghibli problem

**The promise:** Generate professional images for presentations, social content, and internal docs without needing a designer.

**What actually happened:** OpenAI's DALL-E became so heavily trained on user-loved outputs from a particular aesthetic trend that it started applying that look to almost everything. In a community session on image generation tools, Ryan Brodsky described it: "OpenAI's chat image generator has been cursed forever with the extremely positive reinforcement data it got from users loving the Studio Ghibli trend. Everything coming out of OpenAI is just this pissed tint yellow because everyone loved the warm orange amber."

If you want a clean, modern visual for a client presentation, and DALL-E keeps returning something that looks like an animated film still, you have a tool that can't actually do the job you need. Style consistency across multiple images for the same project is even harder, with each generation drifting slightly.

Matt Hastings noted that the workaround is building your own custom skill with specific negative prompts and reference parameters, which solves the bias problem but adds a meaningful setup cost. Robert Martin, who does game asset generation in the community, shared that negative prompts are essential: "I find negative prompts are really helpful with image gen. When I send things off to Pixel Lab, there's a bunch of elements in the prompt like, 'don't do this, don't do that, never do this.' It helps direct the output."

**What to do instead:** If consistent visual style matters for your use case, Ideogram and Gemini's image generator tend to have different aesthetic defaults than DALL-E. Test across tools rather than assuming one will work for all cases. For professional presentations, be honest that AI image generation is still a skill that requires iteration, not a one-click solution.

## "Learn AI in a weekend" courses: The knowledge-without-practice gap

You can finish the course, pass the quiz, and still open Claude on Monday morning with no idea what to type. That's the gap these courses don't close.

Point-in-time learning disconnected from actual daily use doesn't stick. The gap is practice, not knowledge. Jill Ozovek has made this a core coaching position: "Training doesn't work for AI adoption. Coaching does." The difference is not about the quality of the content. It's about the application loop. Knowing what a good prompt looks like and being able to write one under time pressure for a real deliverable are completely different skills.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member who completed a course and still felt stuck, then made real progress through practice]

If McKinsey's 2025 State of AI findings hold [NEEDS CITATION: McKinsey State of AI 2025, mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai], companies that invested in ongoing coaching and change support during AI rollouts were far more likely to see actual productivity gains than those that ran one-time workshops. That tracks with what we see in the community: the people making the most progress aren't the ones who studied the hardest. They're the ones who started using Claude for real work, even badly, and kept going.

Skip the comprehensive course. Pick one task you do every week and try doing it with Claude or ChatGPT for the next two weeks. Read [your first week with AI](/learn/first-week-with-ai) for a day-by-day structure that gets you into real practice rather than preparation.

## The verdict: What this pattern means for your tool choices

Every tool in this list got attention because the underlying capability was real. AI can genuinely help with writing, meeting summarization, images, and professional learning. The problem is that "AI can do X" and "this specific product will do X well for your specific work" are different claims.

A few filters that help before adopting any new AI tool:

- **Who is this tool actually built for?** Dedicated writing tools built for volume content creation will feel wrong for a professional who writes to represent their own judgment. Know the tool's design intent.
- **What does failure look like here?** If the tool produces bad output, how long does it take you to know? Tools that fail slowly and subtly (like Copilot getting facts wrong without flagging it) are more expensive than tools that fail obviously.
- **Is there a free tier that matches real use?** Many tools perform well in a demo and degrade when you throw your actual messy, complicated work at them. Get 30 days of real use before paying.

The AI tools that have held up for professionals who use them daily, the ones people keep coming back to, are Claude and ChatGPT. Not because they're magic, but because they're flexible enough to handle whatever you throw at them. See [ChatGPT vs. Claude: which is better for your work](/learn/chatgpt-at-work) for a direct comparison.

## Keep comparing notes with people who use this stuff every day

The most reliable source of tool recommendations is people actively using AI in roles similar to yours, comparing notes on what worked and what they gave up on. That's exactly what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions: real professionals sharing what's in their current stack and what they dropped.

If you want to hear what's actually working for people in your field right now, [the community is at /community](/community).

The AI industry spent 2023 and 2024 shipping features. Speed to market mattered more than fit. What actually happened: Copilot’s paid subscriber market share contracted sharply between mid-2025 and early 2026 [NEEDS CITATION]. Users reported basic prompt misunderstandings, inaccurate outputs, and integration bugs. The $30/month-per-user price tag on top of an existing Microsoft 365 subscription made it hard to justify for teams that weren’t seeing immediate, measurable results.

The deeper problem: Copilot was designed to sit inside Microsoft products, which means it’s only as good as the documents, emails, and meetings you feed it. If your team doesn’t have tight, organized processes, Copilot amplifies that messiness rather than fixing it. For professionals who write to represent their own voice, brand, or judgment, the editing time often exceeds the time it would have taken to draft from scratch.

AI Tools Your Company Already Has (And Isn't Using)
Your company is likely paying for Microsoft Copilot, Gemini, or Adobe Firefly. Here's what's actually included and how to start using it today.

Your company is probably paying for AI tools you've never opened. Not because anyone hid them from you, but because nobody told you they were there.

Microsoft 365 now has Copilot built into Word, Outlook, Teams, and Excel. Google Workspace Business and Enterprise plans include Gemini across Gmail, Docs, Slides, and Meet. Adobe Creative Cloud has Firefly baked into Photoshop and Illustrator. Salesforce ships Einstein features with most enterprise licenses. These are not future upgrades. They are already in the software you open every day.

The catch: most employees have no idea. A [BCG survey](https://www.bcg.com/publications/2025/ai-at-work-momentum-builds-but-gaps-remain) found that despite nearly 90% of knowledge workers using AI at work in some form, only 5% say they're using it to meaningfully change how they work. The tools exist. The gap is in knowing where to look.

## How to find out what you actually have

Before you buy a separate Claude or ChatGPT subscription, spend 20 minutes figuring out what's already activated in your current stack.

The simplest move: open Word or Google Docs and look for an AI icon in the toolbar. In Microsoft 365, it will often be a small Copilot icon near the top right. In Google Docs, look for a "Help me write" option when you click in a blank document. If either appears, the feature is turned on.

For Outlook, look for a Copilot button when composing an email. In Gmail, look for a pencil icon in the compose window labeled "Help me write."

Not sure whether your company has paid for these add-ons? Ask your IT department or your manager. "Are we paying for Microsoft Copilot or Google Gemini?" is a completely reasonable question. Asking it tends to surface information that nobody thought to communicate proactively. In some organizations, licenses have been active for months and no announcement ever went out.

## What Microsoft Copilot can do for you right now

Microsoft 365 Copilot is embedded across the apps most professionals use all day. If your organization has purchased Copilot licenses (separate from a base M365 subscription), here's what you can do without installing anything new.

**In Outlook:** Ask Copilot to summarize a long email thread before you respond. Ask it to draft a reply based on the conversation context. Ask it to suggest a meeting time based on your availability. These are small tasks but they compound quickly when email is eating two hours of your day.

**In Word:** Paste in rough notes and ask Copilot to turn them into a structured document draft. Ask it to rewrite a paragraph in a different tone. Ask it to summarize a long report into three bullet points for an executive audience.

**In Teams meetings:** Copilot can take notes during a live meeting, generate a summary of the key decisions, and list the action items. You don't need to stop and take notes while also trying to follow the conversation. That alone is worth a significant amount of time per week for anyone in back-to-back meetings.

**In Excel:** Describe what you want to see in plain language and Copilot will suggest a formula or chart. "Show me which region had the highest growth last quarter" is the kind of prompt that actually works.

One honest caveat on Copilot: [NEEDS VERIFIED STAT: enterprise AI tool adoption rates from Gartner/Forrester] when you have access to both Copilot and a separate ChatGPT subscription, many people default to ChatGPT. Copilot's strength is its integration with Microsoft 365, not its model quality. Use it for tasks where being inside Word or Outlook is an advantage. For more complex writing and reasoning, Claude or ChatGPT may give you better results.

## What Gemini does inside Google Workspace

As of early 2025, Google included Gemini AI features in Workspace Business and Enterprise plans without requiring a separate add-on. If your company is on one of these plans, you have access to a meaningful set of features across Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Slides, Meet, and Chat.

**In Gmail:** The "Help me write" button drafts an email from your bullet points. Give it the recipient, the context, and the tone you want, and it produces a starting draft in seconds. You can also ask Gemini to summarize a long email thread before you respond, which is useful when someone drops a five-paragraph email in your inbox at 4pm on a Friday.

**In Google Docs:** The Gemini side panel lets you work alongside the document without leaving it. Ask it to rewrite a section, generate an outline from a brief, or summarize the document for a specific audience.

**In Google Meet:** Gemini can generate meeting notes and action items automatically. For teams running on Google Workspace, this replaces the person who always gets stuck writing the recap.

**In Google Sheets:** Describe what you want to analyze and Gemini will suggest formulas and structure. Similar to Copilot in Excel, the value is in not having to remember syntax when you need a quick calculation.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what members using Google Workspace say about discovering Gemini was already included in their plan]

## Adobe Firefly: the one marketers keep missing

If your organization uses Adobe Creative Cloud, you likely have Firefly access built in. Firefly is Adobe's generative AI for images, and it's integrated directly into Photoshop, Illustrator, and Adobe Express.

What makes Firefly different from other image AI tools is that it's built specifically for commercial use. Adobe trained Firefly on licensed stock images and Adobe Stock, which means the outputs are cleared for commercial use in ways that some other generative image tools are not. For marketing teams producing visual assets for clients or campaigns, that distinction matters.

**In Photoshop:** Firefly powers the Generative Fill feature. Select an area of an image, describe what you want to add or replace, and Photoshop generates it in context. Removing a distracting background element, extending an image to fit a wider format, or adding an object that wasn't in the original shot are all practical tasks, not demo tricks.

**In Adobe Express:** For people who don't have Photoshop skills, Express gives access to Firefly's image generation and editing through a simpler interface. Marketing coordinators and social media managers can generate on-brand visual variations without waiting on a designer.

If you're on a Creative Cloud plan and you haven't explored Firefly inside Photoshop yet, that's worth fifteen minutes this week. The Generative Fill tool in particular tends to surprise people when they use it for the first time.

## Salesforce Einstein: what's actually included

Salesforce has attached "Einstein" to a range of AI features across its platform, which makes it genuinely confusing to know what you have access to. The short version: basic Einstein features come with Enterprise and Unlimited edition licenses, but the more advanced generative AI capabilities typically require add-ons.

If your company is on Salesforce Enterprise or Unlimited, you likely have access to Einstein Lead Scoring (which predicts which leads are most likely to convert), Case Classification (which routes support cases automatically), and Opportunity Scoring. These aren't flashy features, but for sales and service teams, they're useful and already running in the background.

The more advanced Einstein Copilot and Agentforce features require separate licensing at around $125 per user per month. These are worth asking your Salesforce admin about if your team uses Salesforce heavily, but they're not automatically included.

The practical ask: check with your Salesforce admin what Einstein features are turned on in your org. Basic predictive features may already be active and surfacing recommendations you haven't noticed yet.

## A step-by-step approach for getting started

### Step 1: Audit what you have

This week, open each of the tools your company pays for and look for AI features. Word, Outlook, Teams, Gmail, Docs, Photoshop, Salesforce. Spend five minutes in each one looking for AI buttons, side panels, or "Help me write" prompts. Document what you find.

### Step 2: Pick one task and run it through the built-in tool

What's the most repetitive thing you do each week? Pick that one thing and try it through whatever AI feature is closest to that task. If you write a lot of emails, try Copilot or Gemini for one. If you clean up images regularly, try Firefly's Generative Fill on one image. One task, one tool, one try.

The goal here is a single before-and-after comparison you can evaluate honestly. Did it save time? Was the output usable? That one comparison tells you more than any amount of reading about the feature.

### Step 3: Decide whether the built-in tool is enough

Built-in tools have a real advantage: they're already connected to your workflow and your company's data security policies. If Copilot or Gemini handles 80% of your use case well enough, that's the right call. You don't need a separate subscription for every task.

But built-in tools also have limits. Microsoft Copilot's underlying model is not as strong as Claude or ChatGPT on complex reasoning and nuanced writing. Google Gemini is solid for Workspace-connected tasks but is not yet the default choice for professional writing that needs to sound genuinely thoughtful. If you're hitting walls with the built-in tools, that's when a separate Claude or ChatGPT subscription is worth the $20/month.

### Step 4: Tell one colleague what you found

The fastest way for AI adoption to spread inside a company is peer-to-peer. When you find something that works, tell one colleague. "I found out we have Gemini in Docs and it saved me 20 minutes on the project summary today" is the kind of specific, low-stakes information that actually changes behavior. Not a training presentation. A conversation over Slack.

## What to do when your company hasn't turned these features on

Your company may be paying for Microsoft 365 Copilot licenses but have the feature disabled for most users. This is more common than you might expect: IT and security teams often roll out AI features cautiously while they work through data governance questions.

If you find that AI features aren't available in your tools, the most productive move is to ask directly whether they're being evaluated and when access might expand. Framing the question as "I'd like to help test this and give feedback" tends to land better than "why don't we have this yet." It positions you as a resource rather than a complaint.

[NEEDS QUOTE: Matt on enterprise AI access patterns]

## From the Practice

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: specific member who discovered Copilot/Gemini was already active in their organization]

The AI tools that are easiest to adopt are the ones already inside software you open every day. The learning curve is close to zero because the interface is familiar. The data privacy questions are already handled by your IT department's vendor agreements. And the cost is already baked into your company's software budget.

When you find something that works, you tend to become someone your team asks about AI. That's a useful place to be.

If you want to compare what others in your field are finding inside their enterprise tools, and share what's actually working week to week, that's a regular conversation in MVP Club's community sessions. You can [join us at /community](/community).

## A note on data and privacy inside enterprise tools

One practical advantage of using Copilot, Gemini, or Firefly through your company's enterprise agreements: the data handling is already covered. Microsoft and Google both state clearly in their enterprise terms that they do not use your prompts or outputs to train their models without your organization's permission. Your IT team has already vetted these agreements as part of your software contracts.

That's meaningfully different from using a personal ChatGPT free account on your work computer, where the default settings allow OpenAI to use your conversations for model improvement. If your company has enterprise AI tools available, using them is often the lower-risk path from a data security standpoint, not the higher-risk one.

For a broader look at how to use AI at work without creating data exposure, [How to Use ChatGPT at Work (Without Getting in Trouble with IT)](/learn/chatgpt-at-work) covers the data handling questions in detail. And if you're trying to figure out which AI tools are worth the $20/month subscription for the tasks the built-in tools don't cover well, [the AI tools comparison](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) is a useful next read.

Your company is probably paying for AI tools you’ve never opened. Not because anyone hid them from you, but because nobody told you they were there.

Microsoft 365 now has Copilot built into Word, Outlook, Teams, and Excel. Google Workspace Business and Enterprise plans include Gemini across Gmail, Docs, Slides, and Meet. Adobe Creative Cloud has Firefly baked into Photoshop and Illustrator. Salesforce ships Einstein features with most enterprise licenses. These are not future upgrades. They are already in the software you open every day.

The catch: most employees have no idea. A BCG survey found that despite nearly 90% of knowledge workers using AI at work in some form, only 5% say they’re using it to meaningfully change how they work. The tools exist. The gap is in knowing where to look.

How to find out what you actually have

Before you buy a separate Claude or ChatGPT subscription, spend 20 minutes figuring out what’s already activated in your current stack.

The simplest move: open Word or Google Docs and look for an AI icon in the toolbar. In Microsoft 365, it will often be a small Copilot icon near the top right. In Google Docs, look for a “Help me write” option when you click in a blank document. If either appears, the feature is turned on.

For Outlook, look for a Copilot button when composing an email. In Gmail, look for a pencil icon in the compose window labeled “Help me write.”

Not sure whether your company has paid for these add-ons? Ask your IT department or your manager. “Are we paying for Microsoft Copilot or Google Gemini?” is a completely reasonable question. Asking it tends to surface information that nobody thought to communicate proactively. In some organizations, licenses have been active for months and no announcement ever went out.

What Microsoft Copilot can do for you right now

Microsoft 365 Copilot is embedded across the apps most professionals use all day. If your organization has purchased Copilot licenses (separate from a base M365 subscription), here’s what you can do without installing anything new.

In Outlook: Ask Copilot to summarize a long email thread before you respond. Ask it to draft a reply based on the conversation context. Ask it to suggest a meeting time based on your availability. These are small tasks but they compound quickly when email is eating two hours of your day.

In Word: Paste in rough notes and ask Copilot to turn them into a structured document draft. Ask it to rewrite a paragraph in a different tone. Ask it to summarize a long report into three bullet points for an executive audience.

In Teams meetings: Copilot can take notes during a live meeting, generate a summary of the key decisions, and list the action items. You don’t need to stop and take notes while also trying to follow the conversation. That alone is worth a significant amount of time per week for anyone in back-to-back meetings.

In Excel: Describe what you want to see in plain language and Copilot will suggest a formula or chart. “Show me which region had the highest growth last quarter” is the kind of prompt that actually works.

One honest caveat on Copilot: [NEEDS VERIFIED STAT: enterprise AI tool adoption rates from Gartner/Forrester] when you have access to both Copilot and a separate ChatGPT subscription, many people default to ChatGPT. Copilot’s strength is its integration with Microsoft 365, not its model quality. Use it for tasks where being inside Word or Outlook is an advantage. For more complex writing and reasoning, Claude or ChatGPT may give you better results.

What Gemini does inside Google Workspace

As of early 2025, Google included Gemini AI features in Workspace Business and Enterprise plans without requiring a separate add-on. If your company is on one of these plans, you have access to a meaningful set of features across Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Slides, Meet, and Chat.

In Gmail: The “Help me write” button drafts an email from your bullet points. Give it the recipient, the context, and the tone you want, and it produces a starting draft in seconds. You can also ask Gemini to summarize a long email thread before you respond, which is useful when someone drops a five-paragraph email in your inbox at 4pm on a Friday.

In Google Docs: The Gemini side panel lets you work alongside the document without leaving it. Ask it to rewrite a section, generate an outline from a brief, or summarize the document for a specific audience.

In Google Meet: Gemini can generate meeting notes and action items automatically. For teams running on Google Workspace, this replaces the person who always gets stuck writing the recap.

In Google Sheets: Describe what you want to analyze and Gemini will suggest formulas and structure. Similar to Copilot in Excel, the value is in not having to remember syntax when you need a quick calculation.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: what members using Google Workspace say about discovering Gemini was already included in their plan]

Adobe Firefly: the one marketers keep missing

If your organization uses Adobe Creative Cloud, you likely have Firefly access built in. Firefly is Adobe’s generative AI for images, and it’s integrated directly into Photoshop, Illustrator, and Adobe Express.

What makes Firefly different from other image AI tools is that it’s built specifically for commercial use. Adobe trained Firefly on licensed stock images and Adobe Stock, which means the outputs are cleared for commercial use in ways that some other generative image tools are not. For marketing teams producing visual assets for clients or campaigns, that distinction matters.

In Photoshop: Firefly powers the Generative Fill feature. Select an area of an image, describe what you want to add or replace, and Photoshop generates it in context. Removing a distracting background element, extending an image to fit a wider format, or adding an object that wasn’t in the original shot are all practical tasks, not demo tricks.

In Adobe Express: For people who don’t have Photoshop skills, Express gives access to Firefly’s image generation and editing through a simpler interface. Marketing coordinators and social media managers can generate on-brand visual variations without waiting on a designer.

If you’re on a Creative Cloud plan and you haven’t explored Firefly inside Photoshop yet, that’s worth fifteen minutes this week. The Generative Fill tool in particular tends to surprise people when they use it for the first time.

Salesforce Einstein: what’s actually included

Salesforce has attached “Einstein” to a range of AI features across its platform, which makes it genuinely confusing to know what you have access to. The short version: basic Einstein features come with Enterprise and Unlimited edition licenses, but the more advanced generative AI capabilities typically require add-ons.

If your company is on Salesforce Enterprise or Unlimited, you likely have access to Einstein Lead Scoring (which predicts which leads are most likely to convert), Case Classification (which routes support cases automatically), and Opportunity Scoring. These aren’t flashy features, but for sales and service teams, they’re useful and already running in the background.

The more advanced Einstein Copilot and Agentforce features require separate licensing at around $125 per user per month. These are worth asking your Salesforce admin about if your team uses Salesforce heavily, but they’re not automatically included.

The practical ask: check with your Salesforce admin what Einstein features are turned on in your org. Basic predictive features may already be active and surfacing recommendations you haven’t noticed yet.

A step-by-step approach for getting started

Step 1: Audit what you have

This week, open each of the tools your company pays for and look for AI features. Word, Outlook, Teams, Gmail, Docs, Photoshop, Salesforce. Spend five minutes in each one looking for AI buttons, side panels, or “Help me write” prompts. Document what you find.

Step 2: Pick one task and run it through the built-in tool

What’s the most repetitive thing you do each week? Pick that one thing and try it through whatever AI feature is closest to that task. If you write a lot of emails, try Copilot or Gemini for one. If you clean up images regularly, try Firefly’s Generative Fill on one image. One task, one tool, one try.

The goal here is a single before-and-after comparison you can evaluate honestly. Did it save time? Was the output usable? That one comparison tells you more than any amount of reading about the feature.

Step 3: Decide whether the built-in tool is enough

Built-in tools have a real advantage: they’re already connected to your workflow and your company’s data security policies. If Copilot or Gemini handles 80% of your use case well enough, that’s the right call. You don’t need a separate subscription for every task.

But built-in tools also have limits. Microsoft Copilot’s underlying model is not as strong as Claude or ChatGPT on complex reasoning and nuanced writing. Google Gemini is solid for Workspace-connected tasks but is not yet the default choice for professional writing that needs to sound genuinely thoughtful. If you’re hitting walls with the built-in tools, that’s when a separate Claude or ChatGPT subscription is worth the $20/month.

Step 4: Tell one colleague what you found

The fastest way for AI adoption to spread inside a company is peer-to-peer. When you find something that works, tell one colleague. “I found out we have Gemini in Docs and it saved me 20 minutes on the project summary today” is the kind of specific, low-stakes information that actually changes behavior. Not a training presentation. A conversation over Slack.

What to do when your company hasn’t turned these features on

Your company may be paying for Microsoft 365 Copilot licenses but have the feature disabled for most users. This is more common than you might expect: IT and security teams often roll out AI features cautiously while they work through data governance questions.

If you find that AI features aren’t available in your tools, the most productive move is to ask directly whether they’re being evaluated and when access might expand. Framing the question as “I’d like to help test this and give feedback” tends to land better than “why don’t we have this yet.” It positions you as a resource rather than a complaint.

From the Practice

The AI tools that are easiest to adopt are the ones already inside software you open every day. The learning curve is close to zero because the interface is familiar. The data privacy questions are already handled by your IT department’s vendor agreements. And the cost is already baked into your company’s software budget.

When you find something that works, you tend to become someone your team asks about AI. That’s a useful place to be.

If you want to compare what others in your field are finding inside their enterprise tools, and share what’s actually working week to week, that’s a regular conversation in MVP Club’s community sessions. You can join us at /community.

A note on data and privacy inside enterprise tools

One practical advantage of using Copilot, Gemini, or Firefly through your company’s enterprise agreements: the data handling is already covered. Microsoft and Google both state clearly in their enterprise terms that they do not use your prompts or outputs to train their models without your organization’s permission. Your IT team has already vetted these agreements as part of your software contracts.

That’s meaningfully different from using a personal ChatGPT free account on your work computer, where the default settings allow OpenAI to use your conversations for model improvement. If your company has enterprise AI tools available, using them is often the lower-risk path from a data security standpoint, not the higher-risk one.

For a broader look at how to use AI at work without creating data exposure, How to Use ChatGPT at Work (Without Getting in Trouble with IT) covers the data handling questions in detail. And if you’re trying to figure out which AI tools are worth the $20/month subscription for the tasks the built-in tools don’t cover well, the AI tools comparison is a useful next read.
