AI Tools for Writing: When to Use What
Compare Claude, ChatGPT, Grammarly, Notion AI, and Microsoft Copilot for professional writing. Opinionated guidance on which AI writing tools work best.

Pick a task. Grammarly, Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude: you probably have access to at least three of them right now. The real question is which one to open.

This guide tells you what each tool is actually good at, where each one falls short, and what we use at MVP Club when we're writing anything from a client email to a full proposal. We have opinions. We'll share them.

## The quick verdict (if you're in a hurry)

Before the breakdown, here's the table version:

| Tool | Best for | Skip it when |
|------|----------|--------------|
| **Claude** | Long documents, complex instructions, maintaining your voice | You need web search or real-time data |
| **ChatGPT** | Brainstorming, back-and-forth drafting, broader integrations | The writing quality needs to be excellent |
| **Grammarly** | Editing and proofreading, tightening existing text | You need to generate something from scratch |
| **Microsoft Copilot** | Writing inside Office 365 apps you already use | You want high-quality output without editing it |
| **Notion AI** | Summarizing and editing within your Notion workspace | Anything outside Notion |

Now the details.

## Claude: our pick for professional writing

For most professional writing tasks, Claude is the better choice. Its outputs are more structured and more readable than ChatGPT's, it handles long documents better, and it follows complex instructions more reliably. When you give Claude a detailed brief with tone requirements, audience constraints, and format expectations, it tends to follow all of them. ChatGPT sometimes simplifies.

The clearest example is when you're writing something where voice matters: a proposal to a skeptical executive, a recommendation memo with a political subtext, or an email that has to thread a needle between directness and diplomacy. Claude's outputs require less editing to sound like you actually wrote them.

Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club's co-founders, describes how this shows up in practice: "There's folks like me who have now become comfortable just doing everything through Claude Code. Whether I'm making a PowerPoint, rewriting an email, running an app, I'm just in Claude all day."

The practical limitation: Claude doesn't browse the web in real-time (meaning it can't pull in live search results or check current news while you're working). If you need to write something that requires current information (a blog post about last week's product announcement, a competitive summary that includes recent news), you'll need to paste that information in yourself or switch to a tool with live search.

**Best Claude uses for writing:**
- Executive summaries from your own documents and notes
- Recommendation memos and proposals where you supply the arguments
- Editing drafts for tone and clarity (paste it in, describe what's wrong, iterate)
- Emails that need to be precise without being cold
- Any document over 1,000 words where consistency across sections matters

Claude Pro is $20/month. If you write more than a few substantial documents per week, it's worth the cost.

## ChatGPT: the versatile workhorse

ChatGPT is where most people start, and for good reason. It's been around longer, has a larger ecosystem of integrations, and the free tier is genuinely capable. For professional writing, it's a strong tool, just not always the best one.

Where ChatGPT pulls ahead: brainstorming sessions where you want to generate a lot of ideas quickly, situations where you need to search the web as part of your research, and workflows that connect to other tools (ChatGPT has broader third-party integrations than Claude currently does). ChatGPT's Canvas feature is also useful for back-and-forth document editing, where you can see your document alongside the chat and make revisions iteratively.

Where it falls behind for writing: the output style tends toward the formulaic. The structure is usually solid (intro, three points, conclusion), but the prose can read as smooth and generic. Experienced readers notice. The editing step to make it sound like you rather than like a machine is often a bit more work with ChatGPT than with Claude.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member preference between Claude and ChatGPT for writing tasks]

ChatGPT Plus is also $20/month. Many professionals who use AI heavily subscribe to both and switch depending on the task.

## Grammarly: the editor, not the writer

Most professionals reach for Grammarly before they understand what it's actually built for. Grammarly is an editor, not a writer. It's designed to improve what you've already written, not generate from scratch. Once you know that, you know exactly when to open it.

Grammarly catches the things you stop noticing after you've read your own draft twice: run-on sentences, passive voice that's slowing down your point, punctuation errors, words used slightly wrong. The grammar detection is genuinely good for that specific job.

The Grammarly AI writing features (the ones that generate and rewrite text, not just flag errors) are less impressive. They work, but they produce the kind of smoothed-out, slightly bland output that comes from a tool optimized for correctness over voice. For generating new text, Claude or ChatGPT will give you better results.

Use Grammarly as a final pass before anything important goes out. Use it embedded in your browser for emails, LinkedIn posts, and anything else where you're typing directly into a web form. Don't use it to write your report from scratch.

Grammarly's free tier is good enough for most professional use. The Pro plan ($12/month or so) adds more style suggestions and tone adjustments. It's a reasonable add-on if you're doing a lot of writing, but it's not a replacement for a generation tool.

## Microsoft Copilot: the one you might already have

If your organization uses Microsoft 365, you may already have access to Copilot. It's embedded directly into Word, Outlook, Teams, and PowerPoint, and that integration is its main advantage over everything else on this list.

What Copilot does well: generating a first draft inside a Word document without switching tabs, summarizing a long email thread in Outlook before you respond, suggesting meeting notes from a Teams transcript. The convenience of having it inside the tools you already use is real. You don't have to copy-paste context between apps.

What it does less well: the writing quality. Copilot outputs in Word tend toward the generic. They're structured and complete, but they require significant editing to sound like a human wrote them. In our experience, Claude produces professional reports that need less cleanup before they're ready to send, while Copilot trades quality for convenience.

The cost varies. Copilot is bundled into some Microsoft 365 plans; the premium Copilot features (the ones embedded in Office apps) often require an additional per-seat license. Check with your IT team what you actually have access to before paying for a separate subscription.

If you're already in Office apps all day and just need faster first drafts, Copilot is the path of least resistance. If writing quality is important, use Copilot to get something started and then bring it into Claude for a proper pass.

## Notion AI: useful, but only if you live in Notion

Notion AI is a $10/month add-on to your Notion subscription. It does two things well: summarizing long Notion pages and databases, and generating content within the Notion structure (turning a bulleted list into a paragraph, expanding a sparse outline into a draft section).

If your team runs on Notion and you store meeting notes, project briefs, or research in it, Notion AI is worth considering. The ability to say "summarize this 20-page project spec" and get a one-paragraph answer, without leaving the tool you're already in, is genuinely useful.

Outside of Notion, it's not the right tool. For standalone writing tasks, Claude or ChatGPT will outperform it.

## What we use at MVP Club and why

For professional writing, Claude handles almost everything that matters. When we need live search results or want to generate a lot of ideas fast, we switch to ChatGPT. Before anything goes to a client or gets published, Grammarly gets a pass. That's it.

The editing instinct matters more than tool selection. Brian Lightfoot, an MVP Club member who coaches executives, put it this way: "When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I'm conscious: does this sound like AI?" That consciousness is what separates professionals who get good outputs from those who send the machine's draft and hope nobody notices.

That awareness, knowing when the output sounds right and when it still sounds like a machine, is the actual skill. The tool is almost secondary. Claude produces less of that correction work for professional writing in our experience, which is why it's our default. But we've met plenty of professionals who get excellent results from ChatGPT because they've learned to edit its outputs well.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific member workflow combining two of these tools for a regular writing task]

## The writing workflow that actually works

Here's how to combine these tools:

1. **Gather your notes and context** before opening anything. The quality of AI writing output is almost entirely determined by what you give it.
2. **Draft with Claude or ChatGPT** using a specific directive: audience, format, key arguments, tone. Not "write me an email" but "write a 200-word email to my VP declining this meeting request, in a direct tone, and offer two alternative ways I can support this project."
3. **Edit for your voice.** Read every sentence. Add specifics only you know. Cut what sounds generic.
4. **Run Grammarly (or read it aloud)** before it goes out. Both methods catch different things.

That's the stack. It's not complicated. Do it a few times and it becomes muscle memory: brief, draft, edit, check.

For more on the editing mindset specifically, the [AI for Writing](/learn/ai-for-writing) guide covers how to direct a draft and then make it yours. And if you're wondering which AI subscriptions are actually worth paying for, the [free vs. paid AI tools](/learn/better-ai-results) breakdown has a full cost-benefit breakdown.

## Update log

*Last reviewed: April 2026. Tool capabilities change frequently. Claude, ChatGPT, Grammarly, Copilot, and Notion AI are all actively updated. We review this comparison quarterly.*

Grammarly: the editor, not the writer

Most professionals reach for Grammarly before they understand what it’s actually built for. Grammarly is an editor, not a writer. It’s designed to improve what you’ve already written, not generate from scratch. Once you know that, you know exactly when to open it.

Grammarly catches the things you stop noticing after you’ve read your own draft twice: run-on sentences, passive voice that’s slowing down your point, punctuation errors, words used slightly wrong. The grammar detection is genuinely good for that specific job.

The Grammarly AI writing features (the ones that generate and rewrite text, not just flag errors) are less impressive. They work, but they produce the kind of smoothed-out, slightly bland output that comes from a tool optimized for correctness over voice. For generating new text, Claude or ChatGPT will give you better results.

Use Grammarly as a final pass before anything important goes out. Use it embedded in your browser for emails, LinkedIn posts, and anything else where you’re typing directly into a web form. Don’t use it to write your report from scratch.

Grammarly’s free tier is good enough for most professional use. The Pro plan ($12/month or so) adds more style suggestions and tone adjustments. It’s a reasonable add-on if you’re doing a lot of writing, but it’s not a replacement for a generation tool.

Microsoft Copilot: the one you might already have

If your organization uses Microsoft 365, you may already have access to Copilot. It’s embedded directly into Word, Outlook, Teams, and PowerPoint, and that integration is its main advantage over everything else on this list.

What Copilot does well: generating a first draft inside a Word document without switching tabs, summarizing a long email thread in Outlook before you respond, suggesting meeting notes from a Teams transcript. The convenience of having it inside the tools you already use is real. You don’t have to copy-paste context between apps.

What it does less well: the writing quality. Copilot outputs in Word tend toward the generic. They’re structured and complete, but they require significant editing to sound like a human wrote them. In our experience, Claude produces professional reports that need less cleanup before they’re ready to send, while Copilot trades quality for convenience.

The cost varies. Copilot is bundled into some Microsoft 365 plans; the premium Copilot features (the ones embedded in Office apps) often require an additional per-seat license. Check with your IT team what you actually have access to before paying for a separate subscription.

If you’re already in Office apps all day and just need faster first drafts, Copilot is the path of least resistance. If writing quality is important, use Copilot to get something started and then bring it into Claude for a proper pass.

Notion AI: useful, but only if you live in Notion

Notion AI is a $10/month add-on to your Notion subscription. It does two things well: summarizing long Notion pages and databases, and generating content within the Notion structure (turning a bulleted list into a paragraph, expanding a sparse outline into a draft section).

If your team runs on Notion and you store meeting notes, project briefs, or research in it, Notion AI is worth considering. The ability to say “summarize this 20-page project spec” and get a one-paragraph answer, without leaving the tool you’re already in, is genuinely useful.

Outside of Notion, it’s not the right tool. For standalone writing tasks, Claude or ChatGPT will outperform it.

What we use at MVP Club and why

For professional writing, Claude handles almost everything that matters. When we need live search results or want to generate a lot of ideas fast, we switch to ChatGPT. Before anything goes to a client or gets published, Grammarly gets a pass. That’s it.

The editing instinct matters more than tool selection. Brian Lightfoot, an MVP Club member who coaches executives, put it this way: “When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I’m conscious: does this sound like AI?” That consciousness is what separates professionals who get good outputs from those who send the machine’s draft and hope nobody notices.

That awareness, knowing when the output sounds right and when it still sounds like a machine, is the actual skill. The tool is almost secondary. Claude produces less of that correction work for professional writing in our experience, which is why it’s our default. But we’ve met plenty of professionals who get excellent results from ChatGPT because they’ve learned to edit its outputs well.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific member workflow combining two of these tools for a regular writing task]

The writing workflow that actually works

Here’s how to combine these tools:

  1. Gather your notes and context before opening anything. The quality of AI writing output is almost entirely determined by what you give it.
  2. Draft with Claude or ChatGPT using a specific directive: audience, format, key arguments, tone. Not “write me an email” but “write a 200-word email to my VP declining this meeting request, in a direct tone, and offer two alternative ways I can support this project.”
  3. Edit for your voice. Read every sentence. Add specifics only you know. Cut what sounds generic.
  4. Run Grammarly (or read it aloud) before it goes out. Both methods catch different things.

That’s the stack. It’s not complicated. Do it a few times and it becomes muscle memory: brief, draft, edit, check.

For more on the editing mindset specifically, the AI for Writing guide covers how to direct a draft and then make it yours. And if you’re wondering which AI subscriptions are actually worth paying for, the free vs. paid AI tools breakdown has a full cost-benefit breakdown.

Update log

Last reviewed: April 2026. Tool capabilities change frequently. Claude, ChatGPT, Grammarly, Copilot, and Notion AI are all actively updated. We review this comparison quarterly.

The professionals who settle on a writing stack fastest are usually the ones doing it alongside people experimenting with the same tools. In MVP Club’s weekly sessions, members share what’s clicking and what’s not (in real time, not in a Slack thread). If you want in on those conversations, come join us at /community.
The quick verdict (if you’re in a hurry)
Claude: our pick for professional writing
Grammarly: the editor, not the writer
Microsoft Copilot: the one you might already have
Notion AI: useful, but only if you live in Notion

Case Study: How to Become the AI Leader at Your Organization
A practical look at how mid-career professionals become the go-to AI person at work, without a technical background or a formal mandate.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: community member case study — confirm permission before using name and story]

This is what becoming the AI leader at your organization actually looks like. It's less about authority and more about being the person who figured something out and is willing to show others.

## What "AI leader" actually means in practice

The phrase sounds like something from a job description at a startup. But inside most teams and organizations right now, the AI leader isn't a C-suite hire. It's the person who:

- Has a working habit with Claude or ChatGPT (not just an account)
- Has automated or accelerated something specific and visible
- Is willing to share what they're doing when someone asks

That's it. The bar is lower than you think. Most professionals are still in the "I should try this" phase. Anyone who has moved into the "I use this daily and here's what I've built" phase is already ahead.

The distinction matters because it changes what you do next. You're not trying to become an AI expert. You're trying to be the person on your team who practiced while everyone else waited to feel ready.

## The pattern I see in every case study

I've coached professionals through this transition dozens of times, and the path almost always follows the same arc.

They start with one workflow. Not a vision. Not a strategy. One specific task they do repeatedly that costs them time they resent spending. A weekly status report. Thirty minutes of inbox triage every morning. Sourcing prep for a meeting. They bring Claude into that one task and work with it until the result is genuinely better or faster.

Then they show someone. Not in a formal presentation. Over coffee, on a screen share, in a Slack message with a screenshot. "Hey, I've been doing this with Claude, it's saved me about two hours a week, want to see?" Someone says yes. Someone asks how. The leader didn't announce themselves. The curiosity of others did it for them.

Then they get asked to help. Once one person sees it, another person has a question. Then a third. At some point, someone says: "You should show the whole team." Or: "Can you document this so I can replicate it?" This is the moment where informal influence becomes recognized contribution.

Then, sometimes, it formalizes. Not always. Not in every organization. But for many of the people I've coached, what started as a personal productivity experiment eventually becomes a team workflow, a lunch-and-learn session, or input into which tools the team actually buys.

The key is that none of these steps require permission. They require practice and willingness to share.

## What this looks like in a real professional context

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member who became informal AI lead on their team without a formal role, ideally from a non-technical background]

What I can tell you from what I've observed in coaching sessions and community sessions is that the professionals who successfully step into this role share one thing: they started before they felt ready.

In MVP Club's Getting Unstuck Friday sessions, I've watched people go from "I don't even know how to use this thing" to running screenshares for their peers in the same week. Not because they mastered the tool. Because they tried something, it worked, and they were willing to show their work.

"The average person is not going to push through friction," I've said in more than one session. "They just won't." Which means anyone who pushes through (even a little) is immediately valuable to everyone who hasn't.

## The three things that actually accelerate the role

### 1. Build something specific, not general knowledge

Reading about AI is not the same as building with it. General knowledge of what AI can do doesn't make you the go-to person. A specific workflow that solves a real problem at your actual job does.

Pick the task you do most often that's most repetitive. Now spend one hour working with Claude to build a better version of it. If it works, document it. That document is the beginning of a repeatable system. That repeatable system is what makes you credible to share.

If you're not sure where to start, see [How to Use AI at Work](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) for a framework to identify your first high-impact use case.

### 2. Talk about what you're doing before you have the full answer

This one takes some rewiring. Most people wait until they've fully figured something out before mentioning it. But the AI leader role isn't built on finished expertise. It's built on visible learning.

"I've been experimenting with using Claude for X" is enough to position you. You don't have to have the answer. You have to be the person who's looking for it. That positioning compounds over time. When the team eventually has a question about AI, they'll think of you. Not because you were the expert, but because you were the one paying attention.

### 3. Lower the barrier for others, actively

The fastest way to be recognized as the AI leader is to make it easier for someone else to start. Forward a prompt that worked. Share a before-and-after of a deliverable. Walk someone through a workflow on a screen share. Host an informal "show me what you're doing" session over lunch.

The dynamic works at every level. Visibility and modeling matter more than title. [NEEDS CITATION: verified source on leader modeling and team AI adoption outcomes]

If you're thinking about how to introduce this more broadly, [AI for People Managers](/learn/ai-for-people-managers) covers how to bring AI into your team's day-to-day in ways people will actually adopt.

## The psychological piece that's easy to miss

The thing that stops most people from becoming the AI leader on their team is not capability. I've sat in enough coaching sessions to know: the barrier is almost always the fear of being wrong in public.

AI sometimes gets things wrong (and often in ways that are embarrassingly obvious in hindsight). Showing someone a Claude output that turns out to be off-target can feel mortifying. So people wait until they're sure. But with AI, "until you're sure" never comes. The tools are probabilistic. The outputs require judgment. The only way to build that judgment is to use the tools, publicly, and be willing to say: "Here's what happened, here's what I had to fix."

That willingness to be imperfect in public is actually the core competency. It's what makes someone worth following. (I say this as an ICF-certified coach who has spent 15 years working with people on behavior change. It's not a soft insight. It's the whole thing.)

I've worked with professionals who came to AI with zero technical background, myself included. Non-technical people often make better AI leaders precisely because they understand what it feels like to not know. They're better at meeting skeptics where they're at. They're better at explaining what they're doing in plain language. They're better at making the whole thing feel accessible to people who are intimidated.

If you're not technical, that's a feature of your leadership, not a limitation.

## What doesn't work

A few patterns I've watched fail repeatedly, from [my earlier piece on well-intentioned AI approaches](/blog/4-well-intentioned-ai-approaches-that-miss-the-mark):

If you haven't built your own consistent habit yet, you can't lead others into one. The credibility comes from the practice, not the intention. People can feel the difference between someone who's actually using this daily and someone who read a few articles about it.

Telling people AI will save them time is not as compelling as showing them a workflow that already saved you time. Show before you tell. Every time.

Start with people who are curious, not people who are skeptical. Build visible wins. Skeptics often come around once they see something real, not because someone argued them into it. (And if they don't, that's okay too.)

Finally: start with your immediate team, or even just one or two peers. Proof at small scale opens doors that theory never does. Aiming for the whole org too early is how good momentum dies.

## A practical starting arc (6-8 weeks)

You don't need a six-month roadmap. Here's what the first two months look like in practice:

Weeks 1-2: Pick one recurring task. Open Claude.ai, paste in your last three weekly reports (or whatever the deliverable is), and ask it to help you build a faster version. Spend a total of three to five hours working with it. Document what you built, even if the documentation is just a note to yourself.

Weeks 3-4: Share the workflow with one or two colleagues. Informally. No presentation, no agenda. Just "hey, want to see what I've been doing?"

Weeks 5-6: Based on their response, refine. If they had questions you couldn't answer, go find the answers. If they wanted to try it themselves, help them get started.

Weeks 7-8: If you have a manager who's open to it, bring it up. Not as a pitch. As a report: "I've been using Claude for X, here's what it's saved me, here's what the team could do with it." Let the proof lead.

That's a real trajectory. It's what I've seen work. It doesn't require a title or a budget or anyone's permission to start.

## The career angle

Becoming the AI leader at your organization isn't just good for your team. It's good for you.

When the next round of decisions gets made about AI tools, workflows, or strategy, who do you want to be in that conversation? The person who's been experimenting and can speak from experience. Not from what they read, but from what they built and what happened.

AI adoption skills that are visible, documented, and tied to real results are increasingly what employers are noticing. [NEEDS CITATION: verified source on compensation premium for workers with demonstrated AI skills vs. peers in same role] The advantage isn't the tool. It's the practice and the willingness to show it.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific case where a community member's AI work led to a promotion, new role, or expanded responsibility]

## The professionals figuring this out aren't doing it alone

What makes MVP Club's Getting Unstuck Friday sessions different from reading about this stuff is that you're doing it live, with people at the same stage, watching each other try things and fix things in real time. That peer visibility is hard to replicate on your own. It's also (in my experience) the thing that actually unsticks people who've been "meaning to get started" for months.

How to Become the AI Person on Your Team
How to position yourself as the go-to AI person at work, without a technical background or a formal title. A career strategy guide for mid-career professionals.

The role doesn't get posted on an internal job board. It goes to whoever showed up and started figuring it out.

Every organization has one right now (or is desperately looking for one). In most cases, no one planned it. You just started doing it, and now people ask you questions.

If you're reading this, you're probably already further along than you think.

## What "the AI person" actually means

It's worth separating this from some of the loaded language that floats around this topic. You're not trying to become an "AI thought leader." You're not angling for a Chief AI Officer title (at least not yet). The role I'm talking about is simpler and more valuable in the short term: you're the person on your team who has actually used these tools on real work, who others feel comfortable asking questions, and whose judgment people trust when AI comes up.

That's it. The bar is lower than most people assume. Most teams are still in the "everyone has an account, nobody has a habit" phase. Anyone who has moved into genuine daily use is already differentiated.

The career value here is real. When decisions get made about which AI tools the team adopts, which workflows get standardized, who gets invited into the pilot, or who helps onboard the rest of the team, the AI person is already in the room. That positioning compounds quickly.

## Why you don't need a technical background (and why non-technical is actually better)

I came to AI coaching from a career development background. No CS degree, no coding experience, no engineering team to lean on. And what I've found, after working with dozens of professionals on this transition, is that the non-technical background is often a feature, not a gap.

Here's why. The professionals who struggle to become the AI person on their team are usually the ones who get lost in tool comparisons, model specifications, and trying to understand the architecture behind what they're using. (You don't need to know how the engine works to be a good driver.) The people who break through are the ones who pick a real task, open Claude or ChatGPT, and start.

Non-technical professionals tend to be better at translating what AI can do into language their colleagues understand. They're better at making it feel accessible to the majority of the team, who are also non-technical. They're better at meeting skeptics where they're at, because they remember being in that spot.

The question isn't whether you're technical enough. The question is whether you're willing to practice publicly, which means being imperfect in front of other people, and that's a different kind of challenge entirely.

## The move that actually builds the reputation

The career positioning I've watched work, over and over, follows a simple pattern. It's not a strategy. It's a behavior.

You figure something out. You share it.

Not in a formal presentation. Not in a carefully crafted Slack post with a header and bullet points. Informally: "Hey, I've been using Claude for X and it's saved me about two hours a week. Want to see how it works?" That's it. The seeds of the reputation are planted in those small, visible moments of use.

The key word is visible. Silent AI use doesn't build a reputation. Using Claude to write better reports but never mentioning it, never showing the process, never sharing what you figured out, keeps you efficient but anonymous. The career advantage comes from the habit plus the willingness to show your work.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: a member describing a moment when showing someone a Claude workflow led to being seen as the AI person on their team]

What I've watched happen in MVP Club's Getting Unstuck Friday sessions reinforces this. Members come in with something they built, someone asks how they did it, and within the same hour they're teaching a peer. T Gaines built a GitHub portfolio site with Claude's help and was troubleshooting it publicly the same day. That kind of visibility, being the person who tried something and was willing to debug it in front of others, is exactly what builds the AI reputation at your organization.

## The thing most career advice on this gets wrong

A lot of content about "becoming an AI leader" front-loads the strategy. Learn all the tools. Get certified. Build a portfolio. Then position yourself.

That sequence has the causality backwards.

The reputation doesn't come before the practice. It comes from the practice. You don't need to be ready before you're visible. You need to be visible while you're getting ready.

This is the contrarian comfort I offer everyone I coach through this: you don't have to have it figured out to be the AI person. You just have to be the one who is visibly figuring it out. That's more compelling than expertise, because your colleagues can imagine themselves in your shoes. They can't imagine themselves in the shoes of someone who seems to have already arrived.

"There's something really interesting about telling a story that when somebody sees it, it's just compelling on its own," Matt Hastings has said in our community sessions. "It's like, oh, I need this person. I don't know their title or what specifically they're going to do, but I need them to do this for me."

The same logic applies to your internal reputation. You're building a story about how you work, not a credential about what you know.

## Four things to do (in order)

### 1. Build one real workflow before you talk to anyone

This is the foundation. You need something real, something that saved you actual time on actual work, before you can credibly show anything to a colleague.

Pick one recurring task. Something you do at least weekly, that has a clear output, that you've done enough times that you'd recognize if the AI version was better or worse than your own. Weekly status reports. Inbox triage. First drafts of a recurring document. Meeting prep.

Open Claude.ai (Claude Pro at $20/month is worth it for professional work; the free version is fine to start). Give it real context: who you are, what this document is for, who will read it, what the constraints are. Evaluate what comes back. Iterate until it's genuinely useful, not just technically done.

Document what you built, even informally. A saved prompt, a note in your phone, a short paragraph. That document is the start of something you can share.

If you need a place to start, [How to Build AI into Your Daily Workflow](/learn/ai-daily-workflow) has a concrete 30-minute daily structure that works for building the habit consistently.

### 2. Share before you feel ready

Once you have one workflow that genuinely works, tell someone. One person. Informally.

The timing matters here. Most people wait until they feel like an expert before mentioning something. With AI, that moment doesn't come, because the tools are changing constantly and there's always more to learn. Waiting for expertise is how people stay invisible.

Share early, with caveats. "I've been experimenting with using Claude for my weekly reports. It's not perfect but it's cut my prep time in half. Want me to show you?" That framing is honest and inviting, not a performance.

The colleague's response will tell you a lot. Some people will be curious. Some will nod politely. The curious ones are your early adopters. Spend your energy there first.

### 3. Help one person get started

Once someone shows genuine interest, offer to sit with them for 15 minutes and show them how you use it. Not a class. Not a guide. A screen share.

This is where the reputation really takes root. You're not explaining what Claude can do in theory. You're showing them the specific thing you built, in the context of work they recognize, with the real output you actually got. That's more persuasive than anything you could say, and it positions you immediately as the person who knows how to do this.

The dynamic is simple: most people don't push through the initial friction of a new tool. When someone shows them the specific step that gets past the friction, they remember who showed them. That's you.

### 4. Make yourself consistently findable on the topic

This part of the positioning work is easy to skip. But it matters.

Inside your team or organization, find small ways to stay present on the topic. Forward an article about how AI is being used in your industry. Share a prompt that worked in a Slack channel. Bring up what you've been trying in a team meeting, briefly and without making it the main event. Answer someone's AI question even if they didn't direct it at you.

None of this is showing off. It's signal. You're letting your colleagues build an accurate mental model of you as someone who is actively working with these tools. When AI comes up later, in a real decision context, they'll think of you.

## What to do when someone challenges you

The first time you share something and a skeptical colleague says "I tried that and it gave me garbage output," you'll be tempted to backpedal.

Don't. Agree with them.

"Yeah, it gives garbage output when you ask it vague questions. What were you trying to do? Let me show you how I've gotten it to work for that kind of thing."

This is the most valuable move in the whole playbook, and it's something I've coached dozens of people through. Skeptics become allies faster when you meet their resistance than when you argue with it. You're not defending AI. You're sharing what you've learned about how to get good results. That's a different conversation entirely, and it's the one that positions you as someone worth listening to.

If you're working on how to handle this more formally, [How to Handle AI Skeptics on Your Team](/learn/handling-ai-skeptics) goes deeper on the specific dynamics and what actually changes people's minds.

## The career angle: what this actually gets you

Let's be direct about why any of this matters for your career.

AI adoption decisions are being made at organizations right now. Which tools to buy. Which workflows to standardize. Which roles to redefine. Who to put in charge of onboarding the rest of the team. Who to include in a pilot.

The people who get included in those decisions are the people who are already doing the work. Not the people who are planning to get started when things settle down (they won't), or who are waiting until they feel prepared (they won't).

Being the AI person isn't about having a title. It's about being in the right conversations at the right time. That positioning is built before the conversations happen, through visible practice and willingness to share.

I've worked with professionals across industries who came to this with zero technical background, myself included, and what I can tell you is: the window for this particular kind of positioning is still open. Not forever. But right now, it's genuinely available to anyone who's willing to show up and do the work.

The career case for building AI skills that are demonstrable (not just theoretical) is growing stronger every quarter. [NEEDS CITATION: verified source on employer demand for demonstrated AI skills in non-technical roles, 2025-2026]

If you're also thinking about how to make your AI skills visible on your resume and LinkedIn, [The AI Skills That Actually Matter for Career Advancement](/learn/ai-skills-for-career) covers how to translate what you're building into language that lands with hiring managers and career sponsors.

## You don't have to figure this out alone

One thing I hear often in coaching is that the hardest part of becoming the AI person isn't the learning. It's the isolation. You're trying to build something that doesn't have a job description yet, in an organization that isn't sure what it wants yet, without a peer group who is navigating the same thing.

That's exactly the gap MVP Club exists to close. The professionals who build this role fastest are the ones who are doing it alongside other people, sharing what they're figuring out, watching each other experiment and iterate in real time. If you want a community of people who are in the same season of this work, come find us at [mvpclub.ai/community](/community).

The role doesn’t get posted on an internal job board. It goes to whoever showed up and started figuring it out.

Every organization has one right now (or is desperately looking for one). In most cases, no one planned it. You just started doing it, and now people ask you questions.

If you’re reading this, you’re probably already further along than you think.

What “the AI person” actually means

It’s worth separating this from some of the loaded language that floats around this topic. You’re not trying to become an “AI thought leader.” You’re not angling for a Chief AI Officer title (at least not yet). The role I’m talking about is simpler and more valuable in the short term: you’re the person on your team who has actually used these tools on real work, who others feel comfortable asking questions, and whose judgment people trust when AI comes up.

That’s it. The bar is lower than most people assume. Most teams are still in the “everyone has an account, nobody has a habit” phase. Anyone who has moved into genuine daily use is already differentiated.

The career value here is real. When decisions get made about which AI tools the team adopts, which workflows get standardized, who gets invited into the pilot, or who helps onboard the rest of the team, the AI person is already in the room. That positioning compounds quickly.

Why you don’t need a technical background (and why non-technical is actually better)

I came to AI coaching from a career development background. No CS degree, no coding experience, no engineering team to lean on. And what I’ve found, after working with dozens of professionals on this transition, is that the non-technical background is often a feature, not a gap.

Here’s why. The professionals who struggle to become the AI person on their team are usually the ones who get lost in tool comparisons, model specifications, and trying to understand the architecture behind what they’re using. (You don’t need to know how the engine works to be a good driver.) The people who break through are the ones who pick a real task, open Claude or ChatGPT, and start.

Non-technical professionals tend to be better at translating what AI can do into language their colleagues understand. They’re better at making it feel accessible to the majority of the team, who are also non-technical. They’re better at meeting skeptics where they’re at, because they remember being in that spot.

The question isn’t whether you’re technical enough. The question is whether you’re willing to practice publicly, which means being imperfect in front of other people, and that’s a different kind of challenge entirely.

The move that actually builds the reputation

The career positioning I’ve watched work, over and over, follows a simple pattern. It’s not a strategy. It’s a behavior.

You figure something out. You share it.

Not in a formal presentation. Not in a carefully crafted Slack post with a header and bullet points. Informally: “Hey, I’ve been using Claude for X and it’s saved me about two hours a week. Want to see how it works?” That’s it. The seeds of the reputation are planted in those small, visible moments of use.

The key word is visible. Silent AI use doesn’t build a reputation. Using Claude to write better reports but never mentioning it, never showing the process, never sharing what you figured out, keeps you efficient but anonymous. The career advantage comes from the habit plus the willingness to show your work.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: a member describing a moment when showing someone a Claude workflow led to being seen as the AI person on their team]

What I’ve watched happen in MVP Club’s Getting Unstuck Friday sessions reinforces this. Members come in with something they built, someone asks how they did it, and within the same hour they’re teaching a peer. T Gaines built a GitHub portfolio site with Claude’s help and was troubleshooting it publicly the same day. That kind of visibility, being the person who tried something and was willing to debug it in front of others, is exactly what builds the AI reputation at your organization.

The thing most career advice on this gets wrong

A lot of content about “becoming an AI leader” front-loads the strategy. Learn all the tools. Get certified. Build a portfolio. Then position yourself.

That sequence has the causality backwards.

The reputation doesn’t come before the practice. It comes from the practice. You don’t need to be ready before you’re visible. You need to be visible while you’re getting ready.

This is the contrarian comfort I offer everyone I coach through this: you don’t have to have it figured out to be the AI person. You just have to be the one who is visibly figuring it out. That’s more compelling than expertise, because your colleagues can imagine themselves in your shoes. They can’t imagine themselves in the shoes of someone who seems to have already arrived.

“There’s something really interesting about telling a story that when somebody sees it, it’s just compelling on its own,” Matt Hastings has said in our community sessions. “It’s like, oh, I need this person. I don’t know their title or what specifically they’re going to do, but I need them to do this for me.”

The same logic applies to your internal reputation. You’re building a story about how you work, not a credential about what you know.

Give it real context: who you are, what this document is for, who will read it, what the constraints are. Evaluate what comes back. Iterate until it’s genuinely useful, not just technically done.

Document what you built, even informally. A saved prompt, a note in your phone, a short paragraph. That document is the start of something you can share.

AI Workplace Policies: What You Need to Know Before Using AI at Your Job
What most workplace AI policies actually say, what they don't cover, and how to protect yourself while still getting the benefits of AI at work.

Your company's AI policy is probably one of three things: nonexistent, vague enough to be useless, or so restrictive it was clearly written by someone who has never actually used these tools. And yet people in your organization are using ChatGPT, Claude, and a half-dozen other AI tools every day regardless.

That's not a knock on your colleagues. It's just the reality of where most workplaces are right now. The tools moved faster than the policies.

So what do you actually need to know before you use AI at work?

## What most companies actually mean when they say "use AI carefully"

If your company has any AI guidance at all, it probably says something like: "Employees should exercise caution when using AI tools and avoid sharing confidential information." That sentence sounds reassuring. It doesn't tell you much.

Here's what it's actually pointing at. The free and personal-plan versions of tools like ChatGPT (free and Plus tiers) are designed for individual users. By default, your conversations on those plans can be reviewed and used to improve the model. That's the core risk your company is worried about: an employee pastes a client contract or an employee performance review into ChatGPT on their personal account, and that information potentially ends up somewhere it shouldn't be.

This is a real risk. Research cited by enterprise security firms estimates that 22% of data pasted into AI chatbots by employees includes personally identifiable information, payment data, or other sensitive content, and the majority of that comes from unmanaged personal accounts. The stakes feel abstract until something goes wrong. Then they feel very concrete very fast.

## The actual things you should never put into a personal AI account

Regardless of whether your company has a formal policy, this is the practical list. It applies whether you're using Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or any other general-purpose AI tool on a personal account.

**Don't paste:**
- Client names, contact details, or any personally identifiable information
- Internal financial data that isn't publicly disclosed
- Employee compensation, performance ratings, or HR records
- Unreleased product plans, roadmaps, or proprietary methods
- Legal documents with confidential terms or settlement details
- Passwords, API keys, or system credentials

The workaround is simpler than it sounds: anonymize before you paste. Replace "Acme Corp" with "Client A." Swap the real budget number for a ballpark figure. Describe the situation without naming names. You keep 90% of the value. You eliminate nearly all of the risk.

This isn't about being paranoid. It's the same judgment call you'd make about any external tool. You wouldn't email client data to your personal Gmail. Same logic applies here.

## The plan you're on changes everything

One thing most employees don't know: the version of an AI tool you're using matters enormously for data handling.

For ChatGPT, OpenAI states clearly that Business, Enterprise, and API accounts do not use your conversations to train models. Your data is encrypted at rest and in transit. For free and Plus accounts, you can turn off model training in your settings (Settings > Data Controls > "Improve the model for everyone"), but it's opt-out, not opt-in, which means most people never change it.

Claude has similar tiers. Anthropic's standard and Pro plans include conversations in data handling practices that are different from Claude for Enterprise or Claude Team accounts, where conversations are not used for training by default. [Anthropic's privacy policy](https://www.anthropic.com/privacy) spells out what applies to which plan.

The practical implication: if your company provides access to an enterprise tier of any AI tool, use that one for work. It exists precisely because the data protections are meaningfully different. If your company doesn't provide one, use personal accounts only for non-sensitive tasks, and consider upgrading to a paid tier where you can control the data settings yourself.

## Three common situations and what to do in each

**Situation 1: Your company has no policy at all.**

This is more common than you'd think. A lot of organizations are in a gray zone where people are clearly using AI, nobody's said it's approved, and nobody's said it's forbidden. The practical path here is not to wait for policy before building the skill. Use personal accounts for your own work, stay off company systems until there's clarity, and be intentional about what you paste. The skill compounds whether you're using an enterprise account or not.

The other thing to do: start the conversation. Ask your manager or IT team what the current guidance is. This move almost always backfires on people's concerns that they'll look suspicious. In practice, it reads as thoughtful and proactive.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An MVP Club member describing how they started the AI policy conversation at their company and what happened — ideally someone in a non-technical role]

**Situation 2: Your company has banned or blocked specific tools.**

Some organizations block ChatGPT entirely. If yours does, that decision is worth taking seriously. Using banned tools on company devices creates real exposure for you personally, separate from any data risk.

But a ban on one tool doesn't mean a ban on all of them. Many companies that have blocked public ChatGPT have simultaneously approved Microsoft Copilot (which runs through their existing Microsoft 365 agreement), or Google's Gemini features embedded in Workspace, or a vendor-specific AI tool built into software they already pay for. Check [what AI tools you already have access to](/learn/ai-tools-you-already-have) before assuming your options are limited.

**Situation 3: Your company has a formal policy.**

Read it once, carefully, for two things: what's approved, and what the consequence language says. Most corporate AI policies name approved tools and account tiers, define what counts as sensitive data, and describe what happens if you use AI in a way that causes a breach. Knowing those specifics lets you work confidently inside the policy rather than nervously guessing at the edges.

If something's unclear, ask HR or legal. Asking for clarification is never a bad look.

## How to protect yourself when using AI for real work

Once you understand the risk categories, protecting yourself is mostly common sense with a few practical specifics.

**Use the right account for the task.** Personal account on personal device for personal-use tasks and experimentation. Company-approved account for anything involving real work data, even if it feels low-stakes.

**Anonymize before you paste.** Build this into the habit. Before any paste, spend 30 seconds swapping out proper nouns and specific numbers. If the work is worth doing with AI, it's worth 30 seconds to do it safely.

**Read the output before you use it.** AI tools can misstate facts, fill in gaps with plausible-sounding fiction, or produce something that's accurate in general and wrong in your specific situation. Before sending anything AI helped you write, read it the way you'd read a contractor's first draft: as a starting point, not a finished product.

One member from our Getting Unstuck sessions, Brian Lightfoot, described this habit well: "When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I'm conscious: Does this sound like AI?" That question, asked every time before hitting send, is worth the three seconds.

**Keep a paper trail for significant outputs.** If you use AI to help draft something important (a proposal, a policy recommendation, a client-facing document), note that somewhere for your own records. This is less about liability and more about the ability to explain your process if anyone asks. The professionals who build this habit early have an easier time when their organizations formalize AI disclosure expectations later.

## What's changing: the policy landscape is moving fast

If you're in a state like California, Colorado, or Illinois, it's worth knowing that new AI laws are specifically affecting how employers can use AI in employment decisions. Several states now require employers to notify employees when AI is used in performance reviews, hiring, or scheduling decisions, and to maintain the ability for a human to override those decisions.

This matters for you as an employee: you may have more rights around AI-based decisions than you realize. [SHRM's AI Policy Framework](https://www.shrm.org/advocacy/executive-order-impact-zone/ai-policy-framework) has current guidance on what's in effect where.

What's also shifting is company policy. Organizations that had no guidance two years ago are starting to formalize things. If your workplace is drafting or updating an AI policy, this is an ideal moment to be part of that conversation. The people who help shape early AI policies tend to become informal resources for the rest of the organization. That's a career position worth having.

## The bigger picture: using AI well is part of the job now

The best professionals I've coached through this aren't waiting for perfect policy clarity before building the skill. They're learning the tools on tasks where the stakes are low, building their own intuition about what's safe to share, and staying curious about what their organizations are doing. By the time formal policy arrives, they already know how to work within it.

That's the mindset that tends to work. Not "I can't use AI until my company figures this out." More like: "Here's what I know is safe today. I'll keep building from there."

For a sense of how to build that habit from the ground up, [how to build AI into your daily workflow](/learn/ai-daily-workflow) is a good next read. And if you want a broader look at the tools you're working with, [how to use ChatGPT at work without getting in trouble with IT](/learn/chatgpt-at-work) covers the specific data-handling questions in more detail.

---

Figuring out workplace AI policy is faster and less stressful when you're doing it alongside people in similar situations. In MVP Club's weekly sessions, members across industries share what's working at their organizations, what's gotten complicated, and what they wish they'd known earlier. If that sounds useful, [come see what we're building together](/community).

What most companies actually mean when they say “use AI carefully”

If your company has any AI guidance at all, it probably says something like: “Employees should exercise caution when using AI tools and avoid sharing confidential information.” That sentence sounds reassuring. It doesn’t tell you much.

Here’s what it’s actually pointing at. The free and personal-plan versions of tools like ChatGPT (free and Plus tiers) are designed for individual users. By default, your conversations on those plans can be reviewed and used to improve the model. That’s the core risk your company is worried about: an employee pastes a client contract or an employee performance review into ChatGPT on their personal account, and that information potentially ends up somewhere it shouldn’t be.

This is a real risk. Research cited by enterprise security firms estimates that 22% of data pasted into AI chatbots by employees includes personally identifiable information, payment data, or other sensitive content, and the majority of that comes from unmanaged personal accounts. The stakes feel abstract until something goes wrong. Then they feel very concrete very fast.

The actual things you should never put into a personal AI account

Regardless of whether your company has a formal policy, this is the practical list. It applies whether you’re using Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or any other general-purpose AI tool on a personal account.

Don’t paste:

  • Client names, contact details, or any personally identifiable information
  • Internal financial data that isn’t publicly disclosed
  • Employee compensation, performance ratings, or HR records
  • Unreleased product plans, roadmaps, or proprietary methods
  • Legal documents with confidential terms or settlement details
  • Passwords, API keys, or system credentials

The workaround is simpler than it sounds: anonymize before you paste. Replace “Acme Corp” with “Client A.” Swap the real budget number for a ballpark figure. Describe the situation without naming names. You keep 90% of the value. You eliminate nearly all of the risk.

This isn’t about being paranoid. It’s the same judgment call you’d make about any external tool. You wouldn’t email client data to your personal Gmail. Same logic applies here.

The plan you’re on changes everything

One thing most employees don’t know: the version of an AI tool you’re using matters enormously for data handling.

For ChatGPT, OpenAI states clearly that Business, Enterprise, and API accounts do not use your conversations to train models. Your data is encrypted at rest and in transit. For free and Plus accounts, you can turn off model training in your settings (Settings > Data Controls > “Improve the model for everyone”), but it’s opt-out, not opt-in, which means most people never change it.

Claude has similar tiers. Anthropic’s standard and Pro plans include conversations in data handling practices that are different from Claude for Enterprise or Claude Team accounts, where conversations are not used for training by default. Anthropic’s privacy policy spells out what applies to which plan.

The practical implication: if your company provides access to an enterprise tier of any AI tool, use that one for work. It exists precisely because the data protections are meaningfully different. If your company doesn’t provide one, use personal accounts only for non-sensitive tasks, and consider upgrading to a paid tier where you can control the data settings yourself.

Three common situations and what to do in each

Situation 1: Your company has no policy at all.

This is more common than you’d think. A lot of organizations are in a gray zone where people are clearly using AI, nobody’s said it’s approved, and nobody’s said it’s forbidden. The practical path here is not to wait for policy before building the skill. Use personal accounts for your own work, stay off company systems until there’s clarity, and be intentional about what you paste. The skill compounds whether you’re using an enterprise account or not.

The other thing to do: start the conversation. Ask your manager or IT team what the current guidance is. This move almost always backfires on people’s concerns that they’ll look suspicious. In practice, it reads as thoughtful and proactive.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: An MVP Club member describing how they started the AI policy conversation at their company and what happened — ideally someone in a non-technical role]

Situation 2: Your company has banned or blocked specific tools.

Some organizations block ChatGPT entirely. If yours does, that decision is worth taking seriously. Using banned tools on company devices creates real exposure for you personally, separate from any data risk.

But a ban on one tool doesn’t mean a ban on all of them. Many companies that have blocked public ChatGPT have simultaneously approved Microsoft Copilot (which runs through their existing Microsoft 365 agreement), or Google’s Gemini features embedded in Workspace, or a vendor-specific AI tool built into software they already pay for. Check what AI tools you already have access to before assuming your options are limited.

Situation 3: Your company has a formal policy.

Read it once, carefully, for two things: what’s approved, and what the consequence language says. Most corporate AI policies name approved tools and account tiers, define what counts as sensitive data, and describe what happens if you use AI in a way that causes a breach. Knowing those specifics lets you work confidently inside the policy rather than nervously guessing at the edges.

If something’s unclear, ask HR or legal. Asking for clarification is never a bad look.

How to protect yourself when using AI for real work

Once you understand the risk categories, protecting yourself is mostly common sense with a few practical specifics.

Use the right account for the task. Personal account on personal device for personal-use tasks and experimentation. Company-approved account for anything involving real work data, even if it feels low-stakes.

Anonymize before you paste. Build this into the habit. Before any paste, spend 30 seconds swapping out proper nouns and specific numbers. If the work is worth doing with AI, it’s worth 30 seconds to do it safely.

Read the output before you use it. AI tools can misstate facts, fill in gaps with plausible-sounding fiction, or produce something that’s accurate in general and wrong in your specific situation. Before sending anything AI helped you write, read it the way you’d read a contractor’s first draft: as a starting point, not a finished product.

One member from our Getting Unstuck sessions, Brian Lightfoot, described this habit well: “When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I’m conscious: Does this sound like AI?” That question, asked every time before hitting send, is worth the three seconds.

Keep a paper trail for significant outputs. If you use AI to help draft something important (a proposal, a policy recommendation, a client-facing document), note that somewhere for your own records. This is less about liability and more about the ability to explain your process if anyone asks. The professionals who build this habit early have an easier time when their organizations formalize AI disclosure expectations later.

What’s changing: the policy landscape is moving fast

If you’re in a state like California, Colorado, or Illinois, it’s worth knowing that new AI laws are specifically affecting how employers can use AI in employment decisions. Several states now require employers to notify employees when AI is used in performance reviews, hiring, or scheduling decisions, and to maintain the ability for a human to override those decisions.

This matters for you as an employee: you may have more rights around AI-based decisions than you realize. SHRM’s AI Policy Framework has current guidance on what’s in effect where.

What’s also shifting is company policy. Organizations that had no guidance two years ago are starting to formalize things. If your workplace is drafting or updating an AI policy, this is an ideal moment to be part of that conversation. The people who help shape early AI policies tend to become informal resources for the rest of the organization. That’s a career position worth having.

The bigger picture: using AI well is part of the job now

The best professionals I’ve coached through this aren’t waiting for perfect policy clarity before building the skill. They’re learning the tools on tasks where the stakes are low, building their own intuition about what’s safe to share, and staying curious about what their organizations are doing. By the time formal policy arrives, they already know how to work within it.

That’s the mindset that tends to work. Not “I can’t use AI until my company figures this out.” More like: “Here’s what I know is safe today. I’ll keep building from there.”

Figuring out workplace AI policy is faster and less stressful when you’re doing it alongside people in similar situations. How to Get Better Results from AI (Beyond Basic Prompting)
Stop blaming the tool. Better AI results come from context, iteration, and knowing what to give Claude or ChatGPT before you ask.

When you ask Claude or ChatGPT to "write a summary of this project," you get something polished and completely generic. Because the model doesn't know your leadership team, your timeline, what matters to them, or what to avoid. More context, not a better prompt, is what actually changes the output.

## The real reason AI gives you generic output

Most professionals open a chat window and type something like: "Write a summary of this project for my leadership team." They get back something polished, professional, and completely generic. It reads like it was written for every leadership team at every company in every industry.

Because it was.

Claude and ChatGPT don't know your leadership team. They don't know whether your leaders want three bullets or three paragraphs, whether they're coming to this with context or starting cold, whether the project is controversial or celebrated internally. When you give a general request, you get a general response.

More context, not a better prompt, is what actually changes the output.

## What context actually means

Context is everything the model needs to know to do the job well. And it's what actually changes the output quality.

Anthropic's documentation on [prompt engineering for Claude](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/long-context-tips) makes this explicit: when you provide more background information up front, Claude performs measurably better. This is especially true for professional writing tasks, where generic instructions produce generic results.

Context has five components for professional work:

**Your role and situation.** Who are you, and what's the setting? "I'm a marketing manager preparing a client-facing report" gives Claude a completely different starting point than just "write a report."

**The audience.** Who will read this? What do they already know? What do they care about? Your CFO and your direct report need different things from the same project update.

**The goal.** What should this output accomplish? "Summarize the project" and "build confidence with the stakeholder before our renewal conversation" are very different briefs.

**The constraints.** Length, tone, format, things to avoid. "Keep it under 200 words, no jargon, don't mention the budget issue until the third paragraph" is clear direction. Leaving these out means Claude guesses.

**The raw material.** Paste in your notes, the email thread, the meeting transcript, the draft you already have. Give Claude your actual material. Claude pulls together information faster than it makes something new from nothing.

## Step-by-step: building a context-first prompt for real work

Here's what this looks like applied to one of the most common professional writing tasks: the status update.

**Without context:**
*"Write a project status update for my stakeholders."*

Output: a generic template.

**With context:**
*"I'm a project manager at a healthcare technology company. I'm writing a weekly status update for my VP of Operations and our executive sponsor, who are both coming to this with general awareness of the project but not the details. Our project (implementing a new scheduling system) is on track for timeline but slightly over budget on the integration phase. I want to communicate confidence without hiding the budget situation. We also just resolved a major vendor issue that had been worrying the team. Draft a three-paragraph status update: accomplishments, current status, and what we need from leadership next week. Conversational but professional. Avoid bullet lists in this particular email."*

Output: something you can actually send with light edits.

The prompt is longer. The output is better. The total time is still faster than writing from scratch.

## The iteration habit that changes everything

One prompt is rarely enough. This is where most people give up too soon.

When Claude gives you something that's 70% right, the instinct is to start over with a new prompt. Don't. Iterate on the same conversation thread instead. Claude has all your context in memory. You can build from what it gave you:

- "That's good, but the second paragraph sounds too formal. Rewrite just that paragraph in a more conversational tone."
- "Add a sentence acknowledging the vendor issue we resolved. Keep everything else the same."
- "The third paragraph is too long. Cut it to two sentences."

Each of these directives is faster than starting over, and Claude builds on what's already working. You gave it the requirements, it built something, and now you're managing the output through evaluation. That evaluation-and-direction loop is the skill.

[NEEDS VERIFIED QUOTE: A founder or community member on the importance of explicit direction when iterating with AI — specific enough to be attributed by name and session]

The lesson behind this: specificity is not optional. Even people who use these tools every day have to be direct and explicit about what they want. Claude's job is to respond to what you give it, not to intuit what you meant.

## What "context engineering" means for your workflow

You may have seen the term "context engineering" showing up in AI circles. It describes something practical: designing what goes into the model before you ask your question (everything Claude sees before you make your request), so the output is better from the start.

For most professionals, context engineering is less exotic than it sounds. It's really just three habits — and the rhythm matters as much as the rules:

**Habit 1: Keep a running notes document.** Before you open Claude or ChatGPT, spend two minutes jotting the key facts: the audience, the goal, the constraints, any relevant background. Paste that in before your request. This alone improves output quality significantly. It takes two minutes and saves twenty.

**Habit 2: Give Claude your actual material** — your meeting notes, email threads, existing drafts, the client's message. Claude pulls together information faster than it generates from nothing. Your actual inputs make the outputs specific instead of generic.

**Habit 3: Build on what's working.** Don't restart the conversation when you need changes. Claude remembers what came before in the same thread. Use that continuity.

The OpenAI prompt engineering guide makes a similar point: "Be clear about what you need. Specify role, audience, or format." This isn't advice about magic phrases. It's advice about giving the model enough to work with.

## Common mistakes (and what to do instead)

**Asking without context.** The most common issue. If you wouldn't send a new contractor to do a task with just a three-word instruction, don't do it with AI either.

**Giving up after one mediocre output.** The first response is a starting point. The good version usually comes on the second or third pass.

**Pasting raw material without a clear ask.** Dumping a 2,000-word document into the chat and typing "summarize this" works, but "summarize this into five bullets for a technical audience who already understand the product" works much better.

## What We See in MVP Club Sessions

In MVP Club coaching sessions, we consistently see the same pattern: the people getting the best AI results at work are not the ones who have found better prompts. They're the ones who've gotten into the habit of briefing the AI the way they'd brief a team member.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing their specific before/after on context vs. prompt quality. Ideal: what they used to type, what they changed, and how the output quality shifted. Concrete task example preferred. — Do not publish this section until filled with a verified member story.]

Want to try this today? Pick one task you regularly ask AI to help with. Write out the context you'd give a new colleague doing that task for the first time. Paste that context in before your usual request. Compare the output to what you normally get. Then do it again tomorrow with a different task. That repetition is what builds the habit — and the habit is what actually changes your output quality.
