Do First, Understand Later: The Only Way to Learn AI
You can't prepare your way into AI fluency. Understanding comes from doing. Here's why 'do first, understand later' is the approach that actually works.

Every person I've coached who's waiting to feel "ready" before they really try AI is making the same mistake: they think understanding comes first. It doesn't. Understanding comes from doing. And the sooner you accept that, the faster everything changes.

"Do first, understand later" is the only approach that actually works with AI.

## Why preparation doesn't work here

Most of us are good at preparing. We read the manual, take the course, figure out how something works before we use it. That's a perfectly sensible approach for deterministic tools: software that does the same thing every time you press the same button.

AI is different. It's probabilistic. The output shifts based on what you give it, how you phrase things, what context you bring. You cannot prepare your way into understanding that. You have to feel it. You have to run it, get something unexpected, poke at it, redirect it, and run it again. That feedback loop is the learning.

I've done this with over 100 people. The ones who read the most articles before trying didn't do better than the ones who just opened Claude and started. In fact, they often did worse, because they'd built up a mental model of how AI should work that got in the way of discovering how it actually works for them.

## What "doing first" actually looks like

It doesn't mean jumping in blind or treating this like you'd rip off a bandage. It means picking something real and specific from your actual work, not a test scenario, and asking Claude or ChatGPT to help you with it.

Something you do regularly. Something with a clear output. Something where you'd know immediately if the result was good or bad.

Not your most complex project. Not your highest-stakes deliverable. A routine email. A meeting summary. A first pass at a proposal you've written a dozen times before.

You give Claude the task. You see what comes back. And then you notice something important: how long it would have taken you to produce that draft from scratch, versus how long it took you to edit what Claude gave you. That gap is the first thing you actually understand about AI. You can't get that from reading.

> "All these companies are hyper competing right now... they all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?" — Matt Hastings, MVP Club Getting Started session

That's the right question to start with. Not "how does AI work?" but "what do I want AI to do for me?" The first question has a hundred-page answer. The second one you can answer in five minutes, by doing.

## The understanding that only comes from experience

Some things only become clear once you're using AI regularly:

**AI doesn't do the same thing twice.** The first time you realize this, it's disorienting. You asked Claude something yesterday and got a great answer. You ask nearly the same thing today and it goes sideways. You learn, quickly, that your job is to manage inputs and evaluate outputs, not to find a magic phrase and repeat it. That's the managerial relationship with AI, and you only understand it by experiencing the variability firsthand.

**Context matters more than the question.** People who read about prompting often focus on how to phrase the request. People who use AI every day focus on what they give it to work with. The phrasing matters less than the background, the goal, the constraints, the raw material. You learn this by asking the same question with and without context and seeing what happens. Books don't teach that. Your own conversations do.

**Iteration is the process, not the failure.** Almost everyone expects a correct answer on the first try. The first time Claude gives you something that's 70% right, you want to close the tab and try again from scratch. The second or third time, you learn to build on what's working. By the tenth time, you're not even thinking about it. You're just iterating. That shift in expectation comes from repetition, not from understanding it intellectually first.

Lesley Richardson, an MVP Club member who came into our Getting Started series planning to just observe, ended up building and deploying a working site in two sessions. Her reaction afterward: "I didn't think I'd get this far." She didn't think she'd get there because she hadn't imagined getting there was possible for her. Doing it proved it was.

[MATT REVIEW: Confirm Lesley's "I didn't think I'd get this far" quote and context. Transcript summary attributes this to GitHub setup — please verify whether the framing around the portfolio/deployed site is accurate before publishing.]

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing the specific moment when they stopped waiting to feel ready and just started using Claude or ChatGPT — what they did, what happened, and what shifted for them as a result.]

## The fear that keeps people in preparation mode

The most common reason people stay in preparation mode is that they're afraid of doing it wrong. They don't want to look stupid. They don't want to waste time. They don't want to figure out, two weeks in, that they've been using AI incorrectly this whole time and have to start over.

I understand that fear. It's reasonable. And it's also the wrong model for how this works.

With AI, there's no "using it wrong" in the way there is with traditional software. You don't break it. You don't corrupt your files. The downside of a bad session with Claude is that you got an output you didn't like, which took maybe five minutes. The downside of waiting to feel ready is weeks of not using a tool that would have made your work better.

The messy sessions are actually useful. The time you asked Claude something and got back gibberish taught you something. The time you gave it too little context and got something generic taught you something. The time you iterated through six drafts before landing on something good taught you something. All of that becomes part of how you work. None of it came from reading.

## How fast it actually happens

This is the thing that surprises people: you don't need very many sessions before AI starts to click.

Not because the tool is easy. Because doing something, even imperfectly, compresses the learning in a way that nothing else does. People I coach who use Claude every day for two weeks come back with opinions. They know which tasks it handles well. They know where they still have to do the work themselves. They know what to give it upfront. They didn't build that knowledge from articles or walkthroughs. They built it from forty imperfect conversations.

The [AI learning path](/learn/ai-learning-path) breaks this into three phases, but the common thread through all three is the same: you practice, you learn, you practice more. The phases exist to give you a structure for that practice. The practice itself is the thing.

Compare that to someone who spent those two weeks reading about AI, bookmarking articles, following every new tool announcement. They know more about AI in the abstract. They know much less about what AI can do for them specifically.

## One thing to do today

Open Claude or ChatGPT. Pick one task from your actual work this week, something you need to do anyway. Give it real context: who you are, what the task is, who it's for, what would make it good. Then read what comes back.

Don't judge it yet. Just notice: how close is it? What did it get right? What's off? What would you tell it to fix?

Then tell it that. And see what happens.

That loop, right there, is the skill. You can't understand it until you've run it. And once you've run it a few times, you won't need anyone to explain why doing first works. You'll already know.

For a deeper look at how to work with what Claude gives you once you're in that loop, [how to iterate with AI](/learn/when-ai-gives-bad-output) covers the specific moves that turn a 70% output into something you'd actually use. And if you're curious why AI is different from every other tool you've used, the explainer on [probabilistic vs. deterministic thinking](/blog/probabilistic-vs-deterministic-ai) makes sense of the variability you'll notice right away.

## Where this gets easier

Doing first is simpler when you're not doing it alone. In MVP Club's weekly sessions, people bring real tasks, try things live, and watch how others work through the same loop. You see that the messy sessions are normal. You pick up techniques by watching someone else stumble through something you've already figured out, and vice versa.

If you want that kind of practice community, [come see what's happening](/community).

How to Explain AI to Your Boss, Team, or Skeptical Colleague
Practical ways to talk about AI with people who aren't convinced yet. No hype, no lectures. Just approaches that actually work.

A colleague forwarded you an article about AI. You said, "Yeah, I've been using Claude for a few weeks now, it's actually really useful." And they looked at you like you had just told them you were training for a marathon in Antarctica.

You know the look.

The thing is, you probably can't convince them of anything in that moment. And trying to usually makes it worse.

## The first thing to understand: you're not their teacher

When someone is skeptical about AI, launching into an explanation of large language models or listing off use cases is rarely what they need. Most of the time, they don't have an information problem. They have a permission problem. They're wondering: *Is this worth my time? Am I already behind? Will this make me look foolish?*

Those are not questions that a list of features answers.

Jill's most useful insight from coaching professionals through this: the people who are quickest to adopt new tools aren't the ones who got the best explanation. They're the ones who felt safe enough to try something imperfect. Your job, when you're talking to a skeptic, is to make the idea feel lower-stakes, not to deliver more information.

That reframe changes everything about how you have the conversation.

## How to talk to your skeptical colleague

The classic mistake is starting with the technology. "AI can do X, Y, and Z" positions you as someone selling something, and nobody likes being sold to.

Start with a problem instead.

Think about something they complain about regularly. The weekly status report that takes forever. The mountain of emails before a big meeting. The presentation deck they're always scrambling to finish. Now tell them about a time you had that same problem and what you actually did. Specific beats general, every time.

Something like: "You know how I always dread writing the recap for Monday's sync? I pasted my notes into Claude last week and had a draft in four minutes. It wasn't perfect, but I spent another five minutes editing it and it was done. I don't know why I waited so long to try it."

Notice what that does. It doesn't tell them AI is great. It shows them what it did for a real task they recognize. It admits the output wasn't perfect (which actually builds trust). And it ends with a little self-deprecation, not a sales pitch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing the moment they finally convinced a skeptical colleague or team member by showing them something concrete instead of explaining it]

## How to talk to your boss

Talking to your boss about AI requires a different frame. The questions running through their head are usually: *What does this mean for my team? Am I supposed to be doing something about this? What if someone uses it wrong?*

They're less worried about whether it works and more worried about what it means organizationally. So your approach needs to speak to that.

A few things that tend to land well:

**Start with a win you can show, not tell.** If you've used Claude or ChatGPT to speed up a real work task, bring that to the conversation concretely. "I've been using Claude for the past three weeks. I used it to prep for the Anderson account review, and it took about half the time. Thought you'd want to know I'm experimenting with it." That's not asking for permission. It's sharing a result. Big difference.

**Don't suggest a team-wide initiative.** At least not yet. The moment you propose a "team rollout" (especially without a plan), you've just handed your boss a decision that requires IT, legal, and a committee. The [most common mistake organizations make with AI](/blog/4-well-intentioned-ai-approaches-that-miss-the-mark) is treating adoption as a technology decision instead of a behavior change problem. Start with your own practice. Let results speak.

**Answer the "what if someone misuses it" question before they ask it.** You can say: "I'm not suggesting we change anything formally. I just wanted to be transparent that I'm using it for my own work and it's genuinely helping. Happy to share what I've learned if it's useful." That turns a potential concern into an offer.

## How to talk to your team

If you manage people, the conversation is different again. Your team is watching how you engage with AI as a signal of what's expected of them.

If you show up skeptical or dismissive, they'll stay quiet about their own experimentation. If you show up as the enthusiastic evangelist who's insisting everyone "get on board," you'll create anxiety for people who don't know where to start.

The approach that works is somewhere in between: curious, low-pressure, and honest about your own learning curve.

Try running a short "what I'm trying" share in a team meeting. Not a formal presentation. Just two minutes: "I've been messing around with using Claude for writing first drafts of our weekly report. Here's what worked, here's what didn't." Then open it up. "Curious if anyone else has been trying anything."

You'll usually find that someone on your team has already been using AI quietly and just hasn't brought it up. [That's more common than people think.](/blog/what-to-actually-do-about-something-big-is-happening)

Once that person speaks up, everything shifts. The conversation becomes peer-to-peer instead of top-down.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A people manager describing how a "what I'm trying" share changed the dynamic with their team around AI use]

## The questions they'll ask (and honest answers to give)

**"Isn't AI just going to give me wrong information?"**

Yes, sometimes. Claude and ChatGPT can get things wrong, especially for anything that requires current facts or specific data. The right way to use them isn't to trust outputs blindly; it's to treat the output like a first draft from a capable colleague who might have made mistakes. You still review it. You still apply your own judgment. The tool speeds up the work; you're the quality check.

**"Isn't this just going to take people's jobs?"**

This is the question underneath most AI anxiety. An honest answer acknowledges the real uncertainty: no one can predict exactly how work will change. But the professionals who are figuring out how to work well *with* AI tend to be the ones who feel most secure, not least. Not because AI can't change roles, but because they're ahead of the shift instead of behind it. That's a real advantage.

**"I don't have time to learn a new tool."**

This one is actually worth taking seriously instead of dismissing. They're right that most tool adoptions require significant time investment. AI is genuinely different because you can start getting value in the first session. The bar to entry is knowing how to type and having a task. If you have 15 minutes and a real work task, that's enough to start. The [AI time paradox](/blog/ai-time-paradox): using AI is actually what creates more time.

**"I'm not very technical."**

Neither was Jill when she started. Neither were the majority of MVP Club members who now use Claude daily. The tools are built for people who type in plain English and describe what they need. You don't configure anything. You just talk to it.

## One thing worth saying out loud

If you're trying to bring someone along on this, the most useful thing you can do is invite them to try it next to you.

The demystification happens in the doing, not the explaining. That's something the MVP Club founding team learned from running dozens of webinars and coaching sessions with non-technical professionals. As Matt put it during one of our webinars: "The goal is to make one reusable process feel accessible. It's just a chat window doing stuff."

When someone types their first real work task into Claude and gets back something genuinely useful, no explanation was ever needed. The experience is its own argument.

## If you want to practice the conversation itself

Some of the most useful moments in our community sessions happen when people share how they talked about AI with someone who was skeptical. What they said, how it landed, what they'd do differently. Because this is a skill too, not just knowing how to use the tools, but knowing how to bring the people around you along.

If that sounds useful, it's exactly the kind of thing we work through together at [MVP Club](/community). The people who get good at this fastest aren't doing it in isolation.

You know the look.

The thing is, you probably can’t convince them of anything in that moment. And trying to usually makes it worse.

The first thing to understand: you’re not their teacher

When someone is skeptical about AI, launching into an explanation of large language models or listing off use cases is rarely what they need. Most of the time, they don’t have an information problem. They have a permission problem. They’re wondering: Is this worth my time? Am I already behind? Will this make me look foolish?

Those are not questions that a list of features answers.

Jill’s most useful insight from coaching professionals through this: the people who are quickest to adopt new tools aren’t the ones who got the best explanation. They’re the ones who felt safe enough to try something imperfect. Your job, when you’re talking to a skeptic, is to make the idea feel lower-stakes, not to deliver more information.

That reframe changes everything about how you have the conversation.

How to talk to your skeptical colleague

The classic mistake is starting with the technology. “AI can do X, Y, and Z” positions you as someone selling something, and nobody likes being sold to.

Start with a problem instead.

Think about something they complain about regularly. The weekly status report that takes forever. The mountain of emails before a big meeting. The presentation deck they’re always scrambling to finish. Now tell them about a time you had that same problem and what you actually did. Specific beats general, every time.

Something like: “You know how I always dread writing the recap for Monday’s sync? I pasted my notes into Claude last week and had a draft in four minutes. It wasn’t perfect, but I spent another five minutes editing it and it was done. I don’t know why I waited so long to try it.”

Notice what that does. It doesn’t tell them AI is great. It shows them what it did for a real task they recognize. It admits the output wasn’t perfect (which actually builds trust). And it ends with a little self-deprecation, not a sales pitch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing the moment they finally convinced a skeptical colleague or team member by showing them something concrete instead of explaining it]

How to talk to your boss

Talking to your boss about AI requires a different frame. The questions running through their head are usually: What does this mean for my team? Am I supposed to be doing something about this? What if someone uses it wrong?

They’re less worried about whether it works and more worried about what it means organizationally. So your approach needs to speak to that.

A few things that tend to land well:

Start with a win you can show, not tell. If you’ve used Claude or ChatGPT to speed up a real work task, bring that to the conversation concretely. “I’ve been using Claude for the past three weeks. I used it to prep for the Anderson account review, and it took about half the time. Thought you’d want to know I’m experimenting with it.” That’s not asking for permission. It’s sharing a result. Big difference.

Don’t suggest a team-wide initiative. At least not yet. The moment you propose a “team rollout” (especially without a plan), you’ve just handed your boss a decision that requires IT, legal, and a committee. The most common mistake organizations make with AI is treating adoption as a technology decision instead of a behavior change problem. Start with your own practice. Let results speak.

Answer the “what if someone misuses it” question before they ask it. You can say: “I’m not suggesting we change anything formally. I just wanted to be transparent that I’m using it for my own work and it’s genuinely helping. Happy to share what I’ve learned if it’s useful.” That turns a potential concern into an offer.

How to talk to your team

If you manage people, the conversation is different again. Your team is watching how you engage with AI as a signal of what’s expected of them.

If you show up skeptical or dismissive, they’ll stay quiet about their own experimentation. If you show up as the enthusiastic evangelist who’s insisting everyone “get on board,” you’ll create anxiety for people who don’t know where to start.

The approach that works is somewhere in between: curious, low-pressure, and honest about your own learning curve.

Try running a short “what I’m trying” share in a team meeting. Not a formal presentation. Just two minutes: “I’ve been messing around with using Claude for writing first drafts of our weekly report. Here’s what worked, here’s what didn’t.” Then open it up. “Curious if anyone else has been trying anything.”

You’ll usually find that someone on your team has already been using AI quietly and just hasn’t brought it up. That’s more common than people think.

Once that person speaks up, everything shifts. The conversation becomes peer-to-peer instead of top-down.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A people manager describing how a “what I’m trying” share changed the dynamic with their team around AI use]

The questions they’ll ask (and honest answers to give)

“Isn’t AI just going to give me wrong information?”

Yes, sometimes. Claude and ChatGPT can get things wrong, especially for anything that requires current facts or specific data. The right way to use them isn’t to trust outputs blindly; it’s to treat the output like a first draft from a capable colleague who might have made mistakes. You still review it. You still apply your own judgment. The tool speeds up the work; you’re the quality check.

“Isn’t this just going to take people’s jobs?”

This is the question underneath most AI anxiety. An honest answer acknowledges the real uncertainty: no one can predict exactly how work will change. But the professionals who are figuring out how to work well with AI tend to be the ones who feel most secure, not least. Not because AI can’t change roles, but because they’re ahead of the shift instead of behind it. That’s a real advantage.

“I don’t have time to learn a new tool.”

This one is actually worth taking seriously instead of dismissing. They’re right that most tool adoptions require significant time investment. AI is genuinely different because you can start getting value in the first session. The bar to entry is knowing how to type and having a task. If you have 15 minutes and a real work task, that’s enough to start. The AI time paradox: using AI is actually what creates more time.

“I’m not very technical.”

Neither was Jill when she started. Neither were the majority of MVP Club members who now use Claude daily. The tools are built for people who type in plain English and describe what they need. You don’t configure anything. You just talk to it.

One thing worth saying out loud

If you’re trying to bring someone along on this, the most useful thing you can do is invite them to try it next to you.

The demystification happens in the doing, not the explaining. That’s something the MVP Club founding team learned from running dozens of webinars and coaching sessions with non-technical professionals. As Matt put it during one of our webinars: “The goal is to make one reusable process feel accessible. It’s just a chat window doing stuff.”

When someone types their first real work task into Claude and gets back something genuinely useful, no explanation was ever needed. The experience is its own argument.

If you want to practice the conversation itself

Some of the most useful moments in our community sessions happen when people share how they talked about AI with someone who was skeptical. What they said, how it landed, what they’d do differently. Because this is a skill too, not just knowing how to use the tools, but knowing how to bring the people around you along.

If that sounds useful, it’s exactly the kind of thing we work through together at MVP Club. The people who get good at this fastest aren’t doing it in isolation.

How to Handle AI Skeptics on Your Team
A coaching-based approach to working with AI skeptics at work. Meet people where they are, skip the lectures, and focus on what actually moves them.

Your most skeptical team member isn't broken. They're probably the one who asks the hardest questions and has the clearest sense of what could go wrong. That's not a problem to fix. That's a resource to use.

The instinct when someone pushes back on AI is to explain it better. More context, more examples, a demo, a workshop. If they just understood what these tools can actually do, they'd get it. Right?

That's almost never how it works.

## Skepticism usually isn't about the technology

In a recent survey of 1,400 U.S.-based employees, [Harvard Business Review found](https://hbr.org/2025/11/leaders-assume-employees-are-excited-about-ai-theyre-wrong) that 76% of executives believed their employees were enthusiastic about AI adoption, but only 31% of individual contributors actually felt that way. That's not a knowledge gap. That's a trust gap, a safety gap, an identity gap.

The people I've coached through this at General Assembly, and now through MVP Club, consistently show the same pattern. The technical piece clicks relatively fast (sometimes embarrassingly fast, once people actually sit down with it). What takes longer is the emotional recalibration: Who am I if this tool can do a significant chunk of what I built my reputation on?

That question doesn't get answered by a better explanation of how Claude works.

[NEEDS CITATION: McKinsey 2025 survey on culture vs technology as AI barrier] Your skeptical team member is not an outlier. They're the norm. And the leaders who treat this as a psychological challenge, not a technical rollout, are the ones who actually move the needle.

## The three types of skeptics (and what they actually need)

Not all skepticism is the same. Knowing which type you're dealing with changes your approach.

### The Identity-Threatened Skeptic

This person has spent years building expertise. They're genuinely excellent at something. And they've heard enough about AI to believe it might make that excellence less valuable.

What they're thinking: "If AI can write the first draft, what's the point of the twenty years I spent learning to write the first draft?"

What they need: Proof that their judgment, context, and relationships are the actual product. The draft is cheap. Knowing what should be in it isn't. You're not losing ground here. Your expertise is what tells the AI what to produce and whether the output is any good.

### The Quality-Worried Skeptic

This person has seen AI get things wrong, maybe embarrassingly wrong. They're not being dramatic. AI tools do make mistakes. Their concern is legitimate.

What they're thinking: "I'm not going to stake my reputation on something I can't verify."

What they need: A workflow that keeps them in control. Show them how to use Claude or ChatGPT as a first-draft tool, not a final-answer machine. The person who reviews, edits, and approves AI output isn't abdicating responsibility. They're doing exactly what a good editor does.

### The "Not My Job" Skeptic

This person isn't opposed to AI. They're stretched thin and genuinely can't see where to fit one more thing into their week. Their resistance isn't philosophical, it's logistical. They're not saying no. They're saying "not right now, and maybe not ever if you keep making this sound like a project."

What they need is not a framework. It's one specific thing they can try in the next 30 minutes, with their actual work, today. Then you let them come to the conclusion themselves.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: operations manager describing skeptical direct report who shifted]

## What doesn't work (and why leaders keep doing it anyway)

The instinct to educate is strong. You've seen what these tools can do. You're genuinely excited. You want to share that. So you:

- Schedule a team workshop
- Forward articles and demos
- Suggest a shared AI challenge or experiment week
- Mandate adoption as part of a team initiative

These aren't wrong, exactly. But they tend to fail for the same reason: they treat AI adoption as an information problem, and it's not. Workshops and demos inform. They don't build the psychological safety that skeptics need to actually try something in public, imperfectly, and be okay with that.

The [4 Well-Intentioned AI Approaches That Miss the Mark](/blog/4-well-intentioned-ai-approaches-that-miss-the-mark) are worth reading here. Jill laid out in detail why the most sensible-seeming approaches (the all-hands, the tool evaluation committee, the company-wide mandate) so often fall flat. The short version: this is a behavior change problem. Behavior change requires practice and safety, not information.

## The approach that actually works: coaching, not convincing

The most effective thing you can do with a skeptic is ask more and tell less.

This is the coaching instinct. Instead of "Here's why you should try this," try "What would make this worth trying for you?" That question does several things at once. It treats them as someone with a legitimate perspective (because they are, even when they're wrong about the specifics). It surfaces the actual objection, which is often different from the stated one. And it gives you something real to work with.

A few questions worth keeping in your back pocket:

**"What's a task you regularly do that you find tedious?"**

This surfaces the low-stakes, high-repetition work that's ideal for AI-assist. Nobody's reputation lives or dies on the meeting recap or the agenda email. That's the right place to start.

**"What would you need to see to think this was worth your time?"**

This question gets specific. "I'd need to see it actually handle our terminology" is a real answer you can test. "I'd need to see it not be wrong" is too vague, but it opens a conversation about the edit-and-verify workflow.

**"What are you most worried about?"**

Say this with genuine curiosity, not as a setup to counter the answer. Skeptics often haven't been asked this directly. When someone feels heard, they're more willing to move.

## Show, don't tell (specifically)

If you're going to demonstrate AI, the demo matters a lot.

Generic demos backfire. "Let me show you what ChatGPT can do with a writing prompt" is not compelling to someone who's skeptical. It's abstract. It doesn't connect to anything they care about.

The demo that works uses their actual work. Ask if they'd be willing to pick something they're working on right now, open claude.ai, paste it in, and see what happens. You're not saying the output will be perfect. You're saying let's see what happens in five minutes.

That specificity changes the energy. When a skeptic sees Claude produce a reasonable draft of their quarterly review template, or a summary of a meeting they just sat through, the conversation changes. The tool is no longer theoretical.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific team session where someone tried AI with their actual work and what changed in their perception afterward]

## The identity threat is real, and it deserves a real response

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific coaching conversation where someone voiced an identity threat around AI replacing their expertise, and what shifted for them]

When someone says "AI is just going to take our jobs," the instinct is to reassure quickly. "Oh no, it's augmentation, not replacement. Your job is safe." That response is usually too fast and too easy. It can feel dismissive of a concern that's actually worth taking seriously.

Here's a more honest version: No one can predict exactly how these tools will reshape roles over the next five years (and anyone who tells you they do is selling something). What we do know is that professionals who are building experience with AI now tend to feel more secure, not less, because they're ahead of the shift rather than behind it. You don't have to pretend your field isn't shifting. You can acknowledge that it is, and also point out where the person's skills become more valuable, not less, as AI handles more of the commodity work.

The specialists, the relationship-holders, the people with deep organizational context: those are hard to replace. AI is better at the generic than the specific. Someone who knows your client history, your team's dynamics, your organization's actual priorities. That person's judgment is what makes the AI output worth anything.

This framing tends to land better than reassurance. It's honest. It's specific. And it actually addresses the question rather than talking past it.

## Practical things to try this week

If you're leading a team with skeptics, here are a few concrete starting points.

**Make it optional and visible.** Invite people to share what they're trying at the start of a team meeting. Even two minutes of "I used Claude for X this week, here's what happened" makes AI feel less like a mandate and more like a shared experiment. Once someone who was skeptical shares a small win, it tends to shift the group dynamic fast.

**Start with the people who are willing.** Spend your early energy on the team members who are curious, even cautiously so. Let them build the skill. Let them become the informal resource. You don't need everyone to adopt at once. You need a few people to go first visibly.

**Give skeptics a specific, low-stakes experiment.** Not "try AI this week." More like: "We have that vendor summary to draft on Thursday. Would you be willing to try pasting your notes into Claude first and see what it produces? We'll still edit it either way." Concrete, time-bound, low-stakes.

**Check what tools they already have.** A lot of the teams I've worked with don't realize they already have AI built into the tools they use every day. Microsoft 365 Copilot, Google Workspace, Notion AI. [AI tools your company already has](/learn/ai-tools-you-already-have) is worth sharing with your team before suggesting they sign up for anything new.

## Skeptics make adoption stronger

Here's something worth holding onto: the teams that adopt AI most thoughtfully usually have at least one or two serious skeptics in the room.

Skeptics ask the quality questions. They want to know what happens when the output is wrong. They want to know who's accountable. They want to know if the tool is actually saving time or creating new work. Those are not obstacles to good AI use. Those are the conditions for it.

The goal is to channel skepticism, not eliminate it. A skeptic who becomes a thoughtful practitioner is worth more to a team than an enthusiast who never thinks critically about what they're producing.

The [AI adoption research from Harvard Business Review](https://hbr.org/2026/02/why-ai-adoption-stalls-according-to-industry-data) [NEEDS VERIFIED LINK] makes this point clearly: adoption stalls when organizations push for speed and consensus over depth. The teams that build sustainable capability are the ones that make room for legitimate questions.

If you're trying to figure out how to [explain AI to your boss, team, or skeptical colleague](/learn/explain-ai-to-your-team), the underlying principle is the same: meet people where they are, lead with curiosity, and let the tool's usefulness speak for itself.

---

The professionals who are navigating this best aren't doing it alone. In MVP Club's weekly sessions, leaders share what's working with their teams, including what they tried with skeptics and what actually shifted. If you're building this skill in real time, [that's exactly the community for it](/community).

How AI Is Changing Your Job (And Where to Actually Start)
An honest look at how AI is changing work for mid-career professionals, what's actually shifting in your role, and the one move that makes the difference.

I talk to a lot of people who are quietly (or maybe not so quietly) freaking out about AI. Not in a dramatic "robots are coming for us" way. More like some deep "everyone seems to be moving on this and I don't know where to start and I'm not sure how behind I already am," energy. That feeling is real, but I also think it's sending people in the wrong direction.

So let me share what I actually see, working with professionals every day who are trying to figure this out.

## The Shift Is Already Happening, Whether You're Participating or Not

Here's the thing nobody's saying out loud at work: your colleagues are probably already using AI. Just quietly, on their own, to get stuff done faster. McKinsey's 2025 "Superagency in the Workplace" report found that employees are three times more likely than their leaders think to already be using generative AI for at least 30% of their daily work. Subtext: your boss probably has no idea how much this is already happening around them.

And while that gap between them and everyone else stays invisible for a while, it does eventually show up in output quality, who gets asked to lead things and who seems to always be one step ahead.

The World Economic Forum's 2025 Future of Jobs Report projects 170 million new roles will be created alongside the 92 million displaced. I know those numbers can feel alarming, but here's what I'd actually take from them: the labor market is in transformation, not freefall. The jobs that survive, and the new ones getting created, will look different because AI is handling some of what used to eat your time, freeing you up for the work that still requires a human.

What that means for your career specifically depends entirely on whether you're in motion or still watching from the sideline.

## What's Actually Changing in Your Day-to-Day Work

Here's the reframe I keep coming back to: your judgment is still the center of the work. What's shifting is where in the process you apply it.

Think about the parts of your job that involve assembling information. Stuff like:

- Writing up a meeting summary
- Drafting a first version of a proposal
- Researching a vendor
- Building a presentation from scratch
- Compiling a weekly status report

AI can produce a workable first pass on all of those in minutes. Not a finished product. A first pass.

Which means you spend less time on production and more time on evaluation. Deciding whether the draft is right. Knowing what's missing. Applying your specific knowledge of the client, the team, the history, the context that no AI has access to. Your judgment is still doing the work. You're just applying it later in the process, and more deliberately.

I'd argue that's actually a better use of your brain. But it does require a shift in how you think about your role.

The question I'd encourage you to sit with isn't "will AI take my job." It's "what do I actually want this to do for me?" Those are very different questions, and only one of them has an answer you can act on today.

## The Three Places AI Is Changing Your Work Right Now

In my experience, regardless of your role, the shift is showing up in three specific places:

**The writing layer.** If you produce written communication, reports, or documents (and most of us do), AI is changing how the first draft gets made. The question is whether you're using that or still producing everything from scratch.

**The research layer.** Gathering background information, competitive context, and precedent used to eat real chunks of time. Claude, ChatGPT, and similar tools can compress that work substantially. What remains is your interpretation of what that information means for the specific decision you're making. That part is still yours.

**The preparation layer.** Prepping for meetings, reviews, negotiations, or presentations involves assembling a lot of context quickly. That's something AI does really well. The professionals who show up to those conversations more prepared, more quickly, are the ones who've figured out the assembly step.

None of this makes your job easier in a relaxing sense. It changes what hard means. The creative, relational, strategic work doesn't get delegated to AI. The assembly work does. And honestly, good riddance to the assembly work.

## What's Not Changing

Your domain expertise is still your primary asset. Possibly more valuable than before, actually.

AI generates confident-sounding output. It doesn't know your organization's political dynamics, your client's actual concerns, your team's history with a particular approach, or what "good" looks like in your specific context. You do. That knowledge is what turns a generic AI output into something that actually works.

The people I work with who struggle most with AI are usually the ones who treat it like a magic box that should produce finished work. The ones who get the most out of it treat it more like a very fast, sometimes wrong, always-willing colleague who needs clear direction and honest feedback. Your expertise tells you when the output is trustworthy and when it needs to be pushed further. That's not a small thing. That's the job now.

## What Starting Actually Looks Like (Hint: It's Messier Than You Think)

I want to tell you about Tara, a member of our MVP Club community. She works at a university and came in excited to build a tool to help her department navigate a switch from quarters to semesters. She's non-technical. She dove in anyway.

And almost immediately, she hit a wall. The curricular complexity turned out to be way bigger than she'd anticipated, way faster than she'd anticipated. She had to step back from building to rethink her whole approach. Her words: "I'm using Claude to help me think through solutions, but I'm not building anything yet." She also mentioned she was eating chocolate and trying not to be demoralized.

I love this story. Not because it ends in a win (it isn't yet, she's still in it), but because it's real. She didn't wait until she had it figured out. She started, got stuck, used AI to think through the stuck part, and kept going. That's what the actual path forward looks like. It's not linear. It's not a highlight reel. It's someone working through something genuinely hard with a new tool, and staying in it anyway.

## The One Thing I'd Tell You to Do This Week

Find one hour. Open Claude or ChatGPT. Tell it what your job is, what's expected of you, and what's eating the most time or causing the most friction right now. Then just have a brainstorming conversation about what might be possible.

Not "teach me how to use AI." And definitely not watching demo after demo from an AI hype bro on YouTube. Just a conversation about your actual work.

I promise you'll learn more from that one hour than from any article (including this one!) or report about AI's impact on the workforce.

## The Career Positioning Piece

AI capability is already showing up as a differentiator in performance reviews and promotion conversations. I'm seeing it as I talk to people in the community and just friends in general who are feeling the pressure. I'm also hearing it firsthand — in a recent conversation with a Chief People Officer at a tech company in Philadelphia, she said something to the effect of "the time to drag your feet on this is over."

You don't need to be an AI expert. You just need to have a real practice. The distinction matters more than people realize. A practitioner knows how to apply AI to their actual work. Most organizations right now desperately need practitioners, and most people in your immediate peer group aren't there yet. That window won't stay open forever.

## You Don't Have to Figure This Out Alone

AI's impact on work is still being figured out in real time, and no one — and I mean NO ONE — knows the full answer to this. So I'm choosing to focus on what this means for my career versus the existential dread of "robots taking over."

The people I see doing it fastest aren't doing it alone. They're comparing notes, sharing what worked and what didn't, and practicing alongside other people who are navigating the same questions.

An idea for a path forward: organize a small group of peers at work and set aside an hour a week to share what's working. Create a Slack channel where you can share in real time — including where you are stuck. Community really matters with this stuff.

That's exactly what we're building at MVP Club. If you want to figure out what AI actually means for your work alongside a community of people who are in it with you, come find us at [mvpclub.ai/community](/community).

Your First Week with AI: A Day-by-Day Practice Guide
A day-by-day plan for getting started with AI at work. Five days, real tasks, no jargon. Build the habit before you overthink it.

The most useful thing I've seen in a hundred-plus coaching sessions is also the simplest: people who use AI every day get better at it. People who use it occasionally wonder why it never quite works for them.

That gap has nothing to do with technical ability. It's practice. And most first-week attempts fail not because AI is hard, but because there's no structure to follow. You open Claude or ChatGPT, stare at a blank chat window, and have no idea what to type.

This guide fixes that. Five days, one task per day, real work. By Friday, you'll have more clarity about where AI fits in your life than any tutorial could give you.

## What this week is actually about

This week, your goal is not to get amazing AI output. Your goal is to build a habit. Small, daily contact with a real task beats a two-hour AI binge once a week.

Think of it the way you'd think about learning any new skill: you wouldn't practice guitar for three hours on Sunday and expect to improve. You'd play for twenty minutes every day. AI works the same way. The understanding comes through doing, not preparation.

One more thing before we start: use the paid version of Claude or ChatGPT if you can. $20/month unlocks meaningfully better responses, longer conversations, and fewer frustrating limits. It's the best professional development money you'll spend this year. But if you're on the free tier, everything here still works. Just know the paid experience is different enough to matter.

## Day 1 (Monday): One real email

Find an email you need to write today. Not a test email. A real one, to a real person, about something that actually matters to you.

Open [Claude](https://claude.ai) or ChatGPT and tell it: who you're writing to, what the relationship is, what you want the email to accomplish, and the tone you're going for. Paste in any relevant context (a previous message thread, background on the project, whatever helps). Then ask it to draft the email.

Read what it gives you. It almost certainly won't sound exactly like you, and that's fine. The point of today isn't to send what the AI wrote. The point is to start with something rather than a blank screen. Edit the draft until it sounds like you, and notice how much faster that is than starting from nothing.

That editing process, where you push back on what the AI gave you and make it yours, is the core skill. You're not outsourcing your work. You're managing the inputs, goals, and outputs of a capable assistant who needs your direction.

**One thing to try:** Before you send the email, open a new Claude chat and paste your final version. Ask: "Is there anything in this email that could be misread or land badly?" That second pass often catches things you'd miss on your own.

## Day 2 (Tuesday): Prep for a meeting

Pick a meeting on your calendar this week where you could use a sharper perspective going in. It could be a team check-in with a tricky agenda, a client call you want to prepare for, or a conversation you've been half-avoiding.

Paste the meeting agenda, pre-read materials, or a brief description of the situation into Claude or ChatGPT. Then ask a specific question: "What are the three things most likely to come up in this meeting that I should be prepared for?" or "What's the strongest argument against my proposal?"

You're not outsourcing your thinking. You're giving yourself a head start. Spend five minutes reviewing what it gives you and sharpen your own talking points from there.

**The difference between useful and useless here:** Asking "summarize this agenda" gets you a summary. Asking "what should I be watching for in this conversation given that the VP has been skeptical of this project?" gets you something you can actually use. The more context you give, the better the output. That's not a quirk of AI. That's how it works.

## Day 3 (Wednesday): Tackle something that's been sitting on your to-do list

You know the task. The one that keeps getting pushed to tomorrow. The report nobody's asked for yet but you know you need to write. The proposal that needs to go out. The summary of a project that's wrapped up but never properly documented.

Today you're going to take one of those and get a working draft by end of day. Paste whatever context you have (notes, meeting transcripts, emails, bullet points) into Claude and ask it to turn the material into a first draft of whatever you need. Doesn't have to be perfect. It just needs to exist.

> "All these companies are hyper competing right now... they all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?" — Matt Hastings (from an MVP Club Getting Started session)

That question is exactly the right frame for today. Don't start with "what can AI do?" Start with "what do I actually need done?" Then ask AI to help with that specific thing.

**What to expect:** The first draft will probably need real editing. That's normal. A draft that's 60% of the way there is still ahead of a blank page, and you'll get faster at prompting as you build a sense of what context Claude actually needs.

## Day 4 (Thursday): Think through something you're stuck on

This is the one that surprises people most. You don't need a task for this. You need a problem.

Pick something you've been going around in circles on. A decision you keep postponing. A project issue you can't quite diagnose. A conversation you're dreading because you can't figure out what you actually want to say.

Describe the situation to Claude in detail. Not "help me decide" but "here's what I'm weighing, here's what I'm worried about, here's what I've already tried, help me see what I might be missing." Treat it like thinking out loud with a colleague who has no political agenda and a lot of time for you.

Push back on what it says. Ask follow-up questions. Tell it where it's wrong. This is where using AI stops feeling like a search engine and starts feeling like a working relationship.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing the moment using AI as a thinking partner clicked for them, specifically about a decision or stuck point at work]

**One observation from coaching:** The professionals who get the most out of this kind of session aren't the ones who accept Claude's first answer. They're the ones who say "okay, but what if you're wrong about X?" That back-and-forth is where the real thinking happens.

## Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and decide what sticks

Don't skip this one. It's the one most people skip, and it's the one that determines whether next week looks different from this one.

Open a Claude or ChatGPT chat and describe your week. What did you try? What worked? What felt clunky or forced? What would you want to do differently?

Then ask it to help you write a short paragraph capturing what you want to keep doing next week. Not a big productivity system. Just: what one or two habits are actually worth continuing?

That reflection is what separates people who build an AI practice from people who dabble. The ones who stick with it aren't the most technical. They're the ones who found, in week one, at least one thing that made their work genuinely easier, and they kept doing that thing.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing what their first week felt like, and specifically what moment made them decide to keep going]

## The common mistake to avoid this week

You're going to be tempted, at some point, to read about AI instead of using it. A newsletter comes in with ten prompting tricks. A LinkedIn post promises to "transform how you work in five minutes." You'll click it, read it, and suddenly twenty minutes have passed and you haven't actually opened Claude.

As I covered in [The AI Time Paradox](/blog/ai-time-paradox), this is the trap: the same pressure making you want to learn AI is the same pressure that eats the time you'd spend actually using it. Reading about AI is easier than using it. It also teaches you almost nothing.

Close the tab. Open Claude. Do the task.

## What "getting good at this" actually looks like

After week one, you'll probably have two or three use cases that clicked and three or four that felt like more effort than they were worth. That's exactly right. The goal isn't to use AI for everything. The goal is to find the places where it genuinely helps you and build those into your regular workflow.

The shift that happens around weeks three and four is that you stop consciously deciding to use AI. You start reaching for Claude or ChatGPT the way you'd reach for a calculator or a search bar: not because you're "doing an AI thing," but because it's just faster. The tool becomes part of how you think.

That shift doesn't come from a workshop or a tutorial series. It comes from [doing the thing every day until it becomes familiar](/blog/what-to-actually-do-about-something-big-is-happening). This week is the beginning of that.

## A few practical notes

**Which tool to use:** Claude and ChatGPT are both good starting points. Claude tends to produce writing that sounds more natural and handles nuanced professional situations well. ChatGPT is widely used and has a larger connects to more tools and services. Try both this week if you're curious. See which one's responses feel more useful for your kind of work.

**How much context to give:** More than you think. When Claude gives you something generic, that's almost always because you gave it generic input. Paste in the actual email thread. Describe the actual person you're dealing with. Tell it what you've already tried. The more real context you provide, the more real the output.

**When the output is bad:** That's information, not failure. Ask yourself: what context did I leave out? What did I tell it to do that I actually didn't mean? Then try again with a more specific prompt. Getting better at this is mostly just getting better at noticing what you forgot to say.

**Anything you do more than once:** Write down how you did it. As Matt describes in our community sessions: "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here's how I want you to do it. Just go." That's the beginning of a real AI workflow, not just occasional use.

## You don't have to figure this out alone

The professionals who get good at this fastest aren't the ones who go off and figure it out in private. They're the ones who practice alongside other people going through the same thing, see how others are using these tools, and pick up techniques they would never have found on their own.

That's what MVP Club is built for. If you want to practice alongside other mid-career professionals who are figuring this out in real time, come see what we're doing at [mvpclub.ai/community](/community).

The point is to start with something rather than a blank screen. Edit the draft until it sounds like you, and notice how much faster that is than starting from nothing.

That editing process, where you push back on what the AI gave you and make it yours, is the core skill. You’re not outsourcing your work. You’re managing the inputs, goals, and outputs of a capable assistant who needs your direction.

One thing to try: Before you send the email, open a new Claude chat and paste your final version. Ask: “Is there anything in this email that could be misread or land badly?” That second pass often catches things you’d miss on your own.

Day 2 (Tuesday): Prep for a meeting

Pick a meeting on your calendar this week where you could use a sharper perspective going in. It could be a team check-in with a tricky agenda, a client call you want to prepare for, or a conversation you’ve been half-avoiding.

Paste the meeting agenda, pre-read materials, or a brief description of the situation into Claude or ChatGPT. Then ask a specific question: “What are the three things most likely to come up in this meeting that I should be prepared for?” or “What’s the strongest argument against my proposal?”

You’re not outsourcing your thinking. You’re giving yourself a head start. Spend five minutes reviewing what it gives you and sharpen your own talking points from there.

The difference between useful and useless here: Asking “summarize this agenda” gets you a summary. Asking “what should I be watching for in this conversation given that the VP has been skeptical of this project?” gets you something you can actually use. The more context you give, the better the output. That’s not a quirk of AI. That’s how it works.

Day 3 (Wednesday): Tackle something that’s been sitting on your to-do list

You know the task. The one that keeps getting pushed to tomorrow. The report nobody’s asked for yet but you know you need to write. The proposal that needs to go out. The summary of a project that’s wrapped up but never properly documented.

Today you’re going to take one of those and get a working draft by end of day. Paste whatever context you have (notes, meeting transcripts, emails, bullet points) into Claude and ask it to turn the material into a first draft of whatever you need. Doesn’t have to be perfect. It just needs to exist.

“All these companies are hyper competing right now… they all want your attention and they want people hyping them up. But what do I want these tools to do for me?” — Matt Hastings (from an MVP Club Getting Started session)

That question is exactly the right frame for today. Don’t start with “what can AI do?” Start with “what do I actually need done?” Then ask AI to help with that specific thing.

What to expect: The first draft will probably need real editing. That’s normal. A draft that’s 60% of the way there is still ahead of a blank page, and you’ll get faster at prompting as you build a sense of what context Claude actually needs.

Day 4 (Thursday): Think through something you’re stuck on

This is the one that surprises people most. You don’t need a task for this. You need a problem.

Pick something you’ve been going around in circles on. A decision you keep postponing. A project issue you can’t quite diagnose. A conversation you’re dreading because you can’t figure out what you actually want to say.

Describe the situation to Claude in detail. Not “help me decide” but “here’s what I’m weighing, here’s what I’m worried about, here’s what I’ve already tried, help me see what I might be missing.” Treat it like thinking out loud with a colleague who has no political agenda and a lot of time for you.

Push back on what it says. Ask follow-up questions. Tell it where it’s wrong. This is where using AI stops feeling like a search engine and starts feeling like a working relationship.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing the moment using AI as a thinking partner clicked for them, specifically about a decision or stuck point at work]

One observation from coaching: The professionals who get the most out of this kind of session aren’t the ones who accept Claude’s first answer. They’re the ones who say “okay, but what if you’re wrong about X?” That back-and-forth is where the real thinking happens.

Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and decide what sticks

Don’t skip this one. It’s the one most people skip, and it’s the one that determines whether next week looks different from this one.

Open a Claude or ChatGPT chat and describe your week. What did you try? What worked? What felt clunky or forced? What would you want to do differently?

Then ask it to help you write a short paragraph capturing what you want to keep doing next week. Not a big productivity system. Just: what one or two habits are actually worth continuing?

That reflection is what separates people who build an AI practice from people who dabble. The ones who stick with it aren’t the most technical. They’re the ones who found, in week one, at least one thing that made their work genuinely easier, and they kept doing that thing.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing what their first week felt like, and specifically what moment made them decide to keep going]

The common mistake to avoid this week

You’re going to be tempted, at some point, to read about AI instead of using it. A newsletter comes in with ten prompting tricks. A LinkedIn post promises to “transform how you work in five minutes.” You’ll click it, read it, and suddenly twenty minutes have passed and you haven’t actually opened Claude.

As I covered in The AI Time Paradox, this is the trap: the same pressure making you want to learn AI is the same pressure that eats the time you’d spend actually using it. Reading about AI is easier than using it. It also teaches you almost nothing.

Close the tab. Open Claude. Do the task.

What “getting good at this” actually looks like

After week one, you’ll probably have two or three use cases that clicked and three or four that felt like more effort than they were worth. That’s exactly right. The goal isn’t to use AI for everything. The goal is to find the places where it genuinely helps you and build those into your regular workflow.

The shift that happens around weeks three and four is that you stop consciously deciding to use AI. You start reaching for Claude or ChatGPT the way you’d reach for a calculator or a search bar: not because you’re “doing an AI thing,” but because it’s just faster. The tool becomes part of how you think.

That shift doesn’t come from a workshop or a tutorial series. It comes from doing the thing every day until it becomes familiar. This week is the beginning of that.

A few practical notes

Which tool to use: Claude and ChatGPT are both good starting points. Claude tends to produce writing that sounds more natural and handles nuanced professional situations well. ChatGPT is widely used and has a larger connects to more tools and services. Try both this week if you’re curious. See which one’s responses feel more useful for your kind of work.

How much context to give: More than you think. When Claude gives you something generic, that’s almost always because you gave it generic input. Paste in the actual email thread. Describe the actual person you’re dealing with. Tell it what you’ve already tried. The more real context you provide, the more real the output.

When the output is bad: That’s information, not failure. Ask yourself: what context did I leave out? What did I tell it to do that I actually didn’t mean? Then try again with a more specific prompt. Getting better at this is mostly just getting better at noticing what you forgot to say.

Anything you do more than once: Write down how you did it. As Matt describes in our community sessions: “Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here’s how I want you to do it. Just go.” That’s the beginning of a real AI workflow, not just occasional use.

You don’t have to figure this out alone

The professionals who get good at this fastest aren’t the ones who go off and figure it out in private. They’re the ones who practice alongside other people going through the same thing, see how others are using these tools, and pick up techniques they would never have found on their own.

That’s what MVP Club is built for. If you want to practice alongside other mid-career professionals who are figuring this out in real time, come see what we’re doing at mvpclub.ai/community.

Day 4 (Thursday): Think through something you’re stuck on
Day 5 (Friday): Reflect and decide what sticks

Your First Vibe Coding Project in 60 Minutes
Build a working tool with AI in under an hour. A step-by-step guide for non-developers who want to start vibe coding today.

The first time I got a working web app running from scratch, it took me about 45 minutes. No prior knowledge of that stack. No tutorial. Just Claude, a clear idea of what I wanted, and a willingness to type "I don't understand what this error means, can you explain it?" without embarrassment.

That was only possible for the last couple of months, and I already consider it old school now. If you've been curious about vibe coding but haven't started yet, this is the walkthrough I wish I'd had.

## What You're Actually Going to Build

Pick something you'd actually use. That's the only rule.

The best first vibe coding project is something small, specific, and useful to you right now. Not an app to sell. Not a platform. A tool that solves one problem you have. Some examples from people in our community [NEEDS REAL COMMUNITY EXAMPLE: attribute with first name and role]:

- A simple tracker for daily habits (one page, a list, checkboxes)
- A form that formats client intake information the way you want it
- A calculator that applies your personal rate formula for client billing
- A static page that consolidates links you visit constantly

If you can describe your tool in one sentence, you're ready to build it.

## What You'll Need Before You Start

My recommendation: start with Claude Code desktop. Download the [Claude desktop app](https://claude.ai/download), which includes Claude Code built in. You'll need a Claude Pro subscription ($20/month). Lower friction, no terminal, and you're building within minutes. That's the right call for a first project.

The other path is Claude Code in the terminal with VS Code. Slightly more setup, better performance on longer sessions, and where I spend most of my time. The [Getting Started with Claude Code blog post](/blog/getting-started-with-claude-code-non-developer-guide) walks through that setup in detail. Worth doing eventually. Not worth blocking on today.

For your first project, the desktop app is fine. The goal is to ship something, not optimize your environment.

A note on cost: the Claude Pro subscription ($20/month) covers Claude Code desktop usage. If you go the terminal route with API keys instead, you'll pay per token. A typical first project session runs a few dollars. We were watching our API bills closely in the early days. The subscription is simpler to start.

You also need a folder on your computer where the files will live. Create a new empty folder somewhere easy to find, like your Desktop or Documents. That's your project folder. When you start Claude Code, point it at that folder.

## The 60-Minute Breakdown

This is how I'd actually structure your first session.

### Minutes 0-10: Write Your Brief

Before you open Claude, write down what you're building. Not in code. In plain English.

Write three things:

1. What this tool does (one sentence)
2. Who uses it and how (you, daily, on your laptop)
3. What it should look like at minimum (a page with a form, a checklist, a table)

This document becomes your context. You're going to paste it into Claude at the start of every session. Think of it as a briefing document. Claude doesn't remember previous conversations by default, so giving it this context every time prevents you from starting over from scratch.

### Minutes 10-20: The First Prompt

Open Claude Code and paste your brief. Then add this:

> "Create the simplest version of this that actually works. Use plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript so I can open it in a browser without running a server. Don't add anything I didn't ask for."

That last sentence matters. Claude often adds features to be helpful. On your first project, extras create confusion. Keep it minimal.

Hit enter. Watch it build.

Your job right now is not to understand every line it writes. Your job is to watch what appears and notice whether it matches what you asked for.

### Minutes 20-35: Open It and React

When Claude finishes, it will have created files in your project folder. Find the file called `index.html` (or whatever Claude names the main file), double-click it, and it will open in your browser.

Look at it. Does it do what you asked? Are there things missing? Does something look wrong?

Take notes in plain English. Don't try to fix things yourself. Go back to Claude and describe exactly what you see:

> "The form shows up but there's no submit button. Also the title text is very small. Can you add the button and make the title larger?"

This back-and-forth is the job. You're managing the outputs, refining, steering. [NEEDS VERIFIED QUOTE: Matt on vibe coding evaluation — something from a Getting Started session about this being the managerial step]

That's the whole skill. Not writing code. Evaluating outputs and giving direction.

### Minutes 35-50: Iterate

Keep going. Each round should address one or two specific things. Keep your feedback concrete:

- "The button works but nothing happens after I click it. It should show a confirmation message."
- "Move the section that shows results to the top of the page, above the form."
- "The colors are fine but the font is hard to read. Make it bigger."

If Claude does something unexpected or you get an error message, paste the error directly into the chat:

> "I'm getting this error: [paste the error text]. What does this mean and how do I fix it?"

You don't need to understand the error. Claude does. Your job is to copy it accurately and let Claude diagnose.

One thing worth knowing: if Claude starts going in circles on the same problem, or if the conversation gets very long and responses slow down, start a fresh chat. Paste your brief again, describe what you have so far, and continue. Long conversations degrade Claude's performance. Fresh context helps.

### Minutes 50-60: Declare Done

Your first project doesn't need to be perfect. It needs to exist.

At the end of your session, close the loop:

> "Summarize what this tool does, what's been built so far, and what still needs work. Write this as a short document I can use as context in our next session."

Save that summary. Next time you open Claude Code, paste it in first. This is how you build continuity without losing progress.

## What "Plan Mode" Is and When to Use It

If you're using Claude Code and you want to talk through an approach before Claude starts changing files, type `plan mode` at the start of your message. Claude will respond in conversation only without touching your code. This is useful when you're unsure what to ask for next, or when you want to think out loud with Claude before committing to a direction.

Once you're ready to build, ask normally and Claude will start executing.

## Common Mistakes on a First Project

**Asking for too much at once.** "Build me an app that tracks expenses, sends email reminders, and syncs to my calendar" is not a first project. Pick one function. Ship that. Then add the second.

**Not looking at the output before asking for changes.** Always open the file, test it, and form your own opinion before going back to Claude. You are the quality filter.

**Expecting it to be right on the first try.** Three or four rounds of feedback is normal. Ten rounds is normal for something more complex. Expecting perfection from the first output is how people conclude vibe coding doesn't work.

**Treating errors as failures.** An error message is information. Paste it into Claude. It's not a sign you did something wrong.

## The Point of Starting Small

"Why would I build something tiny when I could use an existing app for that?"

I get this one a lot. The answer is that the first project isn't really about the tool. It's about learning how to work with Claude in a building context. Every project after this one will go faster because you went through the confusion, the iteration loops, and the small wins here.

I've shipped five products as a solo builder now. Geddit Together is live in the App Store. Rendition Video is running. None of those would have happened if I'd waited until I felt ready. The first thing I built was a prototype that three people used and nobody asked for again. It was worth building anyway.

The cost of niching down to a specific user, solving a specific problem, for yourself or for a small team, is approaching zero. That's new. That's the actual opportunity here.

## Your Project Ideas If You're Stuck

If you're drawing a blank on what to build, here are some starting points that work well at the beginner level:

- **A personal dashboard**: Paste in your weekly priorities, get a clean visual layout
- **A rate calculator**: Input hours and project type, output your billable amount
- **A simple checklist app**: Custom categories for a workflow you repeat every week
- **A meeting prep template**: Answer a few questions, get formatted talking points
- **A contact log**: Track follow-ups with clients or colleagues without using a CRM

All of these are one-page HTML projects you can build in a single session.

---

When you've finished your first project, the question worth sitting with is: what problem do I have that I've been tolerating? That's the second project.

The community at [MVP Club](/community) is full of people who have gone through this exact process, many of them with no technical background at all. They share what they're building, troubleshoot together, and help each other figure out the next step. If you want to build alongside people who are figuring it out in real time, that's where they are.
