How to Use AI at Work: A Practical Guide for Busy Professionals
A step-by-step framework for integrating AI into your daily work. Start with 20 minutes a day and build from there.

Last Tuesday, a product manager in our community told me she'd been "meaning to start using AI" for eight months. She has a Claude Pro subscription. She's opened it maybe five times. Every time, she types something in, gets a result that feels... fine, closes the tab, and goes back to the way she's always worked.

She's not lazy. She's not behind. She just doesn't know where to start when "use AI at work" could mean a thousand different things.

If that sounds familiar, this guide is for you.

## Why "use AI at work" is harder than it sounds

Most advice about using AI at work boils down to "just start using it." That's like telling someone who wants to get in shape to "just go to the gym." Technically true. Practically useless.

The problem is that AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT are general-purpose. They can do almost anything, which means they give you no obvious place to begin. You open a blank chat window and think: "Now what?"

This is actually a new kind of challenge. Every other tool you've adopted at work had a specific function. Excel does spreadsheets. Slack does messaging. Zoom does meetings. You knew what the tool was for, so you knew how to practice.

AI doesn't work that way. It's more like hiring a new team member who's brilliant but has no idea what your job involves. You have to tell it what to do, give it context, evaluate its work, and push back when it misses the mark. That's what we mean when we say [using AI is a managerial relationship](/blog/probabilistic-vs-deterministic-ai): you're managing inputs, goals, and outputs. Not pushing buttons.

Once you see it that way, the question shifts from "how do I use AI?" to "what parts of my work would benefit from a capable assistant who needs clear direction?"

That second question is much more answerable.

## The four types of work AI can help with

Before you open Claude or ChatGPT, it helps to have a mental model for where AI actually fits into professional work. Not every task is a good fit. And the tasks that are good fits might not be the ones you'd expect.

Here's a framework we use in our coaching practice. Think of your work in four categories:

### 1. Drafting work (the blank page problem)

This is any task where you're starting from nothing and creating something: emails, reports, proposals, presentations, project briefs, status updates. The blank page is where most people waste the most time, not because the work is hard, but because getting started is hard.

AI is excellent at first drafts. Not because it writes better than you (it often doesn't), but because it gives you something to react to instead of starting from zero. Editing is always faster than creating.

**Where to start:** Open Claude or ChatGPT and paste in your last meeting notes. Ask it to draft a status update for your manager. See what comes out. Then edit it until it sounds like you. That editing process, where you push back on what the AI gave you and shape it into something real, that's the core skill.

### 2. Synthesizing work (the information overload problem)

You have a 40-page report to read before tomorrow's meeting. You have 200 emails to process. You have six competing proposals to compare. You have meeting transcripts piling up.

Synthesis is one of AI's genuine strengths. It can read a long document and pull out the three things that matter to you specifically, if you tell it what matters to you. The key word is "specifically." Asking Claude to "summarize this document" gives you a generic summary. Asking Claude to "read this proposal and tell me the three biggest risks to our Q3 timeline" gives you something you can actually use.

**Where to start:** Take a document you need to read this week. Paste it into Claude. Instead of asking for a summary, ask a specific question about it. "What are the cost implications?" or "What assumptions is this proposal making about our team's capacity?" You'll be surprised how much faster you can prepare for meetings this way.

### 3. Thinking-through work (the reasoning partner problem)

This one surprises people. AI is genuinely useful as a thinking partner, not because it has better ideas than you, but because it can help you [build scaffolds for complex thinking](/blog/ai-thinking-tool-build-scaffolds-expand-capability).

You're preparing for a difficult conversation with a team member. You're trying to figure out why a project keeps stalling. You're weighing two strategic options and can't see past your own assumptions. These are all places where talking it through with Claude or ChatGPT can surface angles you hadn't considered.

**Where to start:** Next time you're stuck on a decision, open Claude and describe the situation. Not "make this decision for me" but "here's what I'm weighing, here's what I'm worried about, help me think through the angles I might be missing." Treat it like talking to a thoughtful colleague who has no political agenda.

### 4. Process work (the repetitive task problem)

This is the category most people think of first, but it's actually the last place I'd recommend starting. Process work means tasks you do repeatedly in the same way: formatting data, generating weekly reports, filling out templates, converting information from one format to another.

AI can absolutely help with repetitive work, and the time savings can be significant. But it's the last place to start because it often requires more setup, more specific prompting, and more iteration to get right. Save this for after you've built some comfort with the first three categories.

**Where to start:** Identify one task you do every week that follows the same basic pattern. Write out the steps you take. Then ask Claude to help you build a reusable prompt that handles the routine parts. This is where 20 minutes of setup can save you hours over time.

## Your first week: 20 minutes a day

The biggest mistake people make is trying to "transform their workflow" all at once. That doesn't work. What works is small, consistent practice that builds on itself. Here's what I'd suggest for your first week.

### Monday: Pick one email (10 minutes)

Find an email you need to write today. Something real, not a test. Open Claude or ChatGPT and give it context: who you're writing to, what the situation is, what you want the email to accomplish, and your general tone.

Read what it gives you. It probably won't sound like you. That's fine. The point isn't to send what the AI wrote. The point is to have a starting point that you can edit. Most people find they can write a polished email in half the time when they start from a draft instead of a blank screen.

### Tuesday: Prep for a meeting (15 minutes)

Take the agenda or pre-read for an upcoming meeting. Paste it into Claude. Ask three specific questions about the material: "What are the three most important decisions we need to make in this meeting?" or "What's the strongest argument against this proposal?"

Use the answers to prepare your own talking points. You're not outsourcing your thinking. You're giving yourself a head start.

### Wednesday: Summarize your day (10 minutes)

At the end of the day, open Claude and describe what you worked on. Ask it to help you write a quick summary of your priorities for tomorrow, or draft a brief update for your team. This is a low-stakes way to practice giving AI context about your work.

### Thursday: Think through a challenge (20 minutes)

Pick something you're stuck on. A project issue, a team dynamic, a decision you're putting off. Describe the situation to Claude in detail and ask it to help you think through your options. Push back on its suggestions. Ask follow-up questions. This is where the "managerial relationship" starts to click: you're directing a conversation, not just asking for output.

### Friday: Reflect on the week (10 minutes)

Open Claude and tell it what you tried this week. What worked? What felt awkward? What would you want to try next week? This isn't busywork. The reflection helps you identify which use cases are actually saving you time versus which ones feel like novelty.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member sharing what their first week of daily AI practice felt like, the specific moment it clicked]

## Common mistakes (and what to do instead)

After coaching over a hundred professionals through their first weeks with AI, here are the patterns I see over and over.

### Mistake 1: Starting with the hardest task

People hear "AI can help with anything" and immediately try to automate their most complex, high-stakes work. That's like learning to drive on a highway. Start with low-stakes tasks where a mediocre result doesn't matter. Drafting an internal email is a much better starting point than writing a client proposal.

### Mistake 2: Giving no context

"Write me an email" is the most common first prompt I see. It's also the least effective. AI doesn't know who you are, who you're writing to, what your relationship is, what you're trying to accomplish, or what tone is appropriate. The more context you give, the better the output. Think of it this way: if you handed this task to a new hire on their first day, what would you need to tell them?

### Mistake 3: Accepting the first output

Most people type a prompt, read the response, and decide AI either "works" or "doesn't work" based on that single exchange. That's not how the tool is designed to be used. The first output is a starting point. The real value comes from the conversation: "That's close, but the tone is too formal. Make it more direct and cut the second paragraph."

AI is a do-first, understand-later tool. The understanding comes through iteration, not through getting it right on the first try.

### Mistake 4: Trying to learn AI "properly" before using it

I see this one constantly, especially with experienced professionals. They want to take a course, read a book, watch a tutorial series, and feel prepared before they start. But [AI is a practice skill, not a knowledge skill](/blog/ai-time-paradox). You will learn more in one week of daily use than in a month of reading about it. The understanding comes through doing, not preparation.

### Mistake 5: Going it alone

Using AI can feel isolating in a weird way. You're having these conversations with a tool, getting results nobody else sees, and you're not sure if what you're experiencing is normal. Am I using this right? Is this output good enough? Should this be taking me this long?

[Having a community of people figuring it out alongside you](/blog/ai-is-for-all-of-us-collective-knowledge) changes everything. You see approaches you wouldn't have thought of. You realize your struggles are normal. You pick up techniques from watching how other people work.

## From the practice: what we're seeing in coaching

Every week, we run community sessions where professionals share what they're building and where they're stuck. A few patterns keep showing up.

**The "aha" moment isn't about prompting.** People expect the breakthrough to be a clever prompt trick. It's almost never that. The breakthrough is usually the moment someone realizes they can paste their actual work into Claude (their real meeting notes, their real project brief, their real email thread) and get genuinely useful output. The tool becomes real when the context becomes real.

**The second week matters more than the first.** Week one is exciting and novel. Week two is where people either build a habit or drift back to their old patterns. The ones who stick with it are usually the ones who found one specific use case in week one that saved them real time, even if it was just 10 minutes.

**People underestimate how much context AI needs.** The single most common issue we see is people providing too little context and then being disappointed with the output. When we sit with someone and help them build a proper prompt (with background, audience, purpose, constraints, and tone), they're often shocked at how different the result is. The difference comes from you getting better at directing it, not from the AI improving.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A specific member describing a use case that saved them meaningful time, with concrete details about the task]

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after from a coaching session showing the difference between a low-context prompt and a high-context prompt with the resulting outputs]

## What happens after the first week

If you follow the daily practice above, you'll start noticing something by the end of week two or three. You'll start seeing opportunities without looking for them. A report lands in your inbox and you think, "I bet Claude could pull out the key risks in this." A colleague asks for feedback on a proposal and you think, "Let me run this through Claude first to sharpen my thinking."

That shift, from "I should try to use AI today" to "AI is just part of how I work," is what we're after. It doesn't happen through a workshop or a training session. It happens through consistent, daily practice with your actual work.

Here's what the progression typically looks like:

**Weeks 1-2:** You're consciously deciding to use AI. It feels like an extra step. Some tasks go faster, some feel clunky. This is normal. You're building a new habit, and new habits feel awkward at first.

**Weeks 3-4:** You start reaching for Claude or ChatGPT without thinking about it. You develop a few go-to use cases that reliably save you time. You get better at giving context, and the outputs get noticeably better as a result.

**Month 2 and beyond:** AI becomes part of your workflow the way email or your calendar is. You stop thinking about "using AI" as a separate activity. You also start noticing tasks where AI doesn't help, which is just as valuable as knowing where it does. You develop judgment about when to use the tool and when your own expertise is what the situation needs.

The professionals who are getting the most from these tools right now aren't the most technical people. They're the ones who show up every day, try something, learn from what works and what doesn't, and keep going. It's [the same principle behind everything we do](/blog/what-to-actually-do-about-something-big-is-happening): the skill develops through practice, not preparation.

## The $20 question

One practical note: the free versions of Claude and ChatGPT are fine for experimenting. But if you're serious about building AI into your work, the paid tier ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) is a different experience entirely. Faster responses, better reasoning, longer conversations, access to the latest models.

Think of it this way: $20/month is less than most people spend on coffee in a week. If it saves you even 30 minutes a week (and it will save you more than that), the math is obvious. This is the most affordable professional development investment you'll find right now.

And you don't have to figure it out on your own. This is going to be the most disruptive moment of our professional careers, and it is better to go through this moment together than alone.

## Start here

You don't need to overhaul your workflow. You don't need to become a prompt expert. You don't need to understand how large language models work under the hood.

You need 20 minutes, a real task, and a willingness to try.

If you want to practice alongside other professionals who are figuring this out in real time, that's what we built [MVP Club](/) for. We're not a course. We're a community of people who believe that AI belongs to all of us, and that getting good at it is more of a human challenge than a technical one.

Come see what we're building. Take the [AI readiness assessment](/assess) to see where you stand, or just show up to a session and see how it feels.

We'd love to have you.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific before/after from a coaching session showing the difference between a low-context prompt and a high-context prompt with the resulting outputs]

What happens after the first week

If you follow the daily practice above, you’ll start noticing something by the end of week two or three. You’ll start seeing opportunities without looking for them. A report lands in your inbox and you think, “I bet Claude could pull out the key risks in this.” A colleague asks for feedback on a proposal and you think, “Let me run this through Claude first to sharpen my thinking.”

That shift, from “I should try to use AI today” to “AI is just part of how I work,” is what we’re after. It doesn’t happen through a workshop or a training session. It happens through consistent, daily practice with your actual work.

Here’s what the progression typically looks like:

Weeks 1-2: You’re consciously deciding to use AI. It feels like an extra step. Some tasks go faster, some feel clunky. This is normal. You’re building a new habit, and new habits feel awkward at first.

Weeks 3-4: You start reaching for Claude or ChatGPT without thinking about it. You develop a few go-to use cases that reliably save you time. You get better at giving context, and the outputs get noticeably better as a result.

Month 2 and beyond: AI becomes part of your workflow the way email or your calendar is. You stop thinking about “using AI” as a separate activity. You also start noticing tasks where AI doesn’t help, which is just as valuable as knowing where it does. You develop judgment about when to use the tool and when your own expertise is what the situation needs.

The professionals who are getting the most from these tools right now aren’t the most technical people. They’re the ones who show up every day, try something, learn from what works and what doesn’t, and keep going. It’s the same principle behind everything we do: the skill develops through practice, not preparation.

The $20 question

One practical note: the free versions of Claude and ChatGPT are fine for experimenting. But if you’re serious about building AI into your work, the paid tier ($20/month for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus) is a different experience entirely. Faster responses, better reasoning, longer conversations, access to the latest models.

Think of it this way: $20/month is less than most people spend on coffee in a week. If it saves you even 30 minutes a week (and it will save you more than that), the math is obvious. This is the most affordable professional development investment you’ll find right now.

And you don’t have to figure it out on your own. This is going to be the most disruptive moment of our professional careers, and it is better to go through this moment together than alone.

Start here

You don’t need to overhaul your workflow. You don’t need to become a prompt expert. You don’t need to understand how large language models work under the hood.

You need 20 minutes, a real task, and a willingness to try.

If you want to practice alongside other professionals who are figuring this out in real time, that’s what we built MVP Club for. We’re not a course. We’re a community of people who believe that AI belongs to all of us, and that getting good at it is more of a human challenge than a technical one.

Come see what we’re building. Take the AI readiness assessment to see where you stand, or just show up to a session and see how it feels.

We’d love to have you.

How to Run an AI Workshop Your Team Will Actually Enjoy
A practical guide to running an AI workshop for your team: design, facilitation, and follow-through that drives real adoption, not just attendance.

Most AI workshops fail before they start. Not because the facilitator doesn't know enough about AI, but because the design is wrong. A slide deck about what AI can do doesn't make anyone go back to their desk and try it. What changes behavior is doing something real, in a room with other people, and seeing that it actually works.

If you're tasked with running an AI workshop for your team (or you've decided to organize one yourself), this guide covers what actually works: the design choices that create momentum, the facilitation moves that lower the fear, and the follow-through that turns a good session into lasting capability.

## Start with demystification, not instruction

The instinct for most first-time AI facilitators is to teach. To explain how large language models work, walk through a dozen use cases, and leave people with "homework." Resist this entirely.

The first AI workshop is about one thing: making the tool feel less foreign. [NEEDS QUOTE: Matt on demystification — the core design challenge in every session he runs, something like "the goal is to demystify, hey, this is just a chat window"] When that clicks for someone (when they realize the thing they've been avoiding is actually just a text box), everything else becomes possible.

Aim for one moment per person where they use the tool themselves and it works. That's the whole objective. Everything else is secondary.

## Before you run the workshop

### Pick the right scope

A two-hour "AI overview for the whole company" is almost always the wrong format. Too many skill levels in the room. Too much ground to cover. Too little time to actually try anything.

Instead, aim for smaller, more focused sessions: a team of 8-15, a single use case they all share, and 60-90 minutes of hands-on work. You can run more workshops. You can't undo the damage of a session where half the room tuned out because the content was pitched wrong.

The sweet spot is a team that shares a real problem. HR teams running this with performance review season approaching, for example, or a marketing team prepping for a campaign sprint. The more specific the problem, the more engaged the room. Vague prompts get vague outputs; the same is true of workshops.

### Choose one tool and one task

Ask participants to install Claude on their laptops before the session (the free version works). Claude's conversational interface is the lowest-friction starting point for most non-technical professionals. It doesn't require setup, accounts, or technical context to start producing useful output.

Then pick one task the whole team does regularly. Meeting notes. First drafts of weekly updates. Summarizing a long document they've already read. The task should be something participants have actual material for, because the magic happens when they use their own real content, not a hypothetical example.

### Set the right room expectation

Send a one-paragraph message before the session. Something like: "This isn't a presentation. We're going to try something together and see what happens. Bring a work task you've been avoiding, or something you'd like to do faster. No preparation required."

That framing does two things. It removes the test anxiety that comes with "learning." And it signals that confusion and imperfection are expected. Both matter enormously for a room that includes skeptics.

## How to facilitate the session

### Open with honesty, not hype

Don't start by telling people AI is going to change everything. That's a conversation that puts people on the defensive immediately. Instead, start with: "I'm going to show you something I've found genuinely useful. I'd love for you to try it. Let's see what happens."

The energy you want in the room is curiosity, not pressure. Your job as the facilitator isn't to convince anyone of anything. It's to create enough safety for people to experiment.

Ask people to share their current experience level in the first five minutes. A quick round-the-room: "On a scale of 1 to 5, how much have you used AI tools for work?" This does two things. It levels the playing field (nobody feels alone in being a beginner). And it gives you a read on the room before you decide how much to explain versus how much to jump in.

### Run a "prompt along," not a demo

The most common facilitation mistake is showing the tool while participants watch. Watching Claude respond to someone else's prompt is about as engaging as watching someone else eat. What shifts the room is having everyone try the same thing at the same time, together.

A "prompt along" format works like this: put a starting prompt on screen and explain what you're trying to do, then give everyone 3-4 minutes to try it with their own content. Then ask 2-3 people to share what they got. The variation in outputs, across the same team, is usually the best moment of the session. People realize there's no single right answer, and they start to see what steering the tool looks like.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific prompt-along moment from an MVP Club webinar or workshop, showing a before/after of participant outputs]

One thing to plan for: people who get stuck immediately. Have a simple fallback prompt ready. Something like: "Summarize this paragraph in three bullet points" with a sample paragraph pre-loaded. The goal is that nobody sits with a blank screen for more than 30 seconds. Momentum matters more than any individual output.

### Address the skeptic in the room directly

Every workshop has at least one. The person who is visibly unimpressed, or who asks pointed questions about accuracy or job displacement. Don't deflect these. Engage them.

The most effective response to skepticism is honest agreement. "You're right, it gets things wrong. Let me show you what that looks like." Then pull up a bad output, ask the room what's off about it, and walk through how you'd revise the prompt. That moment reframes the whole workshop: the workshop is about learning to steer AI, not blind trust.

Jill Ozovek's framework for meeting skeptics applies here directly: the issue is rarely the tool. It's that people derive real meaning from expertise they spent years building, and AI feels like it's questioning the value of all that. Acknowledge that directly, and the resistance often softens.

### End with a commitment, not homework

At the end of the session, don't hand out a reading list or a list of "things to try this week." Those get ignored. Instead, ask each person to name one task they're going to use Claude for before the next team meeting. Out loud, to the group. That social commitment is more powerful than any follow-up email.

Give them 5 minutes at the end to do it. Right there, before the session ends. Have them open Claude, start the task, and then close their laptop. The point is to make the first real try feel normal, not to finish the task.

## Common mistakes to avoid

**Covering too many tools.** Pick one. Claude, or ChatGPT, or whatever your company uses. The moment you introduce multiple interfaces is the moment you've made this about tool comparison instead of practice.

**Skipping the hands-on component.** [NEEDS VERIFIED STAT: percentage of employees who feel confused about how to use AI at work — remove or replace with verified source] Explanation rarely resolves confusion. Doing does.

**Running a one-and-done event.** A single workshop builds awareness. Capability comes from repetition. The most durable outcomes happen when the session is followed by regular, low-stakes practice: a 30-minute team check-in two weeks later, a Slack channel where people share what's working, a follow-up session for the people who got curious.

**Making it about you.** If you're the person who organized this, there's a temptation to demonstrate expertise. Resist it. The more you frame yourself as someone also figuring it out, the more permission others feel to be imperfect. "I tried this last week and got a mediocre first draft, and here's how I iterated" is always more useful than a polished demo.

## What to do after the workshop

The 72 hours after a workshop are where most of the follow-through happens. Or doesn't. Send a short message the next day. Not a summary of everything covered. Just a single prompt: "What's one thing you tried since yesterday?"

Two weeks out, schedule a 30-minute follow-up. Ask what's actually working. Share one new technique. Give people space to report that they haven't used it at all, without shame. The "I tried it and stopped" conversation is just as useful as the success story, because it reveals where the friction actually lives.

Over time, look for the two or three people who've gotten genuinely curious. Those are the people who can keep the momentum going without you needing to run every session yourself. Give them space and recognition, and they'll do most of the work.

> **From the community:** [NEEDS VERIFICATION: Jill Ozovek quote on friction and first-time participants — something to the effect of "the average person is not going to push through friction, they just won't."] The design implication is that every moment of confusion, every step that requires googling something, is a place where people give up. Build your workshop to eliminate all of that before anyone arrives.

## On being the person who runs this

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: Matt personal anecdote about running a session that got messy — a moment where something didn't work as expected and how staying curious about it changed the room's energy]

There's a particular courage in volunteering to run an AI workshop when you're not an expert. You're going to get questions you can't answer. You're going to have outputs that don't work as expected. That's actually the most useful thing you can show.

When you say "I don't know, let me try this" and then iterate in front of the room, you model the exact skill the team needs to develop. The ability to stay curious when the output is wrong. To try again without embarrassment. To treat AI as a collaborator rather than an oracle.

That's what running this workshop is. A managerial relationship. You manage the inputs, the context, and the direction. Claude brings scale and speed. The combination is what makes it work.

The professionals building this muscle fastest aren't doing it alone. They're doing it in community, with peers who are in the same learning curve, practicing together and sharing what's working. If you want to keep developing as the person who leads AI adoption on your team, [MVP Club's community](/community) is where that happens.

New AI Tools Worth Trying This Month (April 2026)
Four AI tools with major April 2026 updates worth your time: Claude Cowork, Perplexity Custom Skills, NotebookLM video, and Gemini 3.1 for Workspace.

Anthropic shipped something big for office workers in late January, Perplexity started letting you build custom automations in March, and Google's NotebookLM can now turn your documents into narrated video. It's been a fast few weeks. This is our monthly roundup of what's new, what's actually useful, and what you can skip.

We focus on tools built for working professionals, not developers. If you need a terminal to use it, we'll say so upfront.

## What we're covering this month

Four tools had updates in March-April 2026 that are worth your attention:

1. **Claude Cowork** (Anthropic): autonomous task completion for knowledge workers
2. **Perplexity Custom Skills**: research that also creates deliverables
3. **NotebookLM Cinematic Video Overviews** (Google): turning documents into video
4. **Gemini 3.1 in Google Workspace**: reasoning upgrades inside Docs and Drive

We've been testing these in our weekly sessions and with community members. Here's the honest take.

## Quick verdict

If you only have time to try one thing: **Claude Cowork**, if you're on a Pro or Team plan. It does more than Claude Chat and is easier to get started with than Claude Code.

If you're already a heavy Claude user, **Perplexity's Custom Skills** are the other one worth half an hour.

The other two are worth knowing about, especially NotebookLM if you work with a lot of documents. Gemini 3.1 in Workspace is legitimately improved, but only if your organization already runs on Google.

---

## Claude Cowork: autonomous work for non-technical users

Anthropic launched Cowork in research preview for Pro subscribers in January and has been adding features through March. The short version: it brings Claude Code-level capabilities to people who have never opened a terminal.

With Claude Chat, you type something, Claude responds, and you copy-paste the output somewhere else. Cowork works at a different level: give it a goal, and it makes a plan, works through it, and delivers a finished result. It can open files on your computer, work with your documents, schedule recurring tasks, and connect to tools like Google Drive, Gmail, and Docusign.

The key thing that sets it apart from the standalone chat interface: it has a sense of context over time. You can point it at a folder and it remembers the project. You can schedule it to run a report every Monday morning.

### What our community is doing with it

In our New AI Tools session this spring, Carmella Thompson shared how she built a portfolio site using the Claude Code desktop app: "I built a GitHub portfolio site for myself, and I did it all in Claude Code. I didn't need to look at the folder structure."

Cowork is designed for people who had that same reaction to the terminal that Angela Frances Peat described in the same session: "The desktop is probably what I prefer, versus the terminal looks a little bit scary."

The permission model is also worth understanding. Cowork is intentionally constrained, closer to giving someone intern-level access than root access to your machine. That's a feature, not a limitation. For most office workflows, you want something that can do the work without having the keys to your entire system.

### Worth trying if:

- You're on Claude Pro, Team, or Enterprise
- You have a repeating task you'd like automated (weekly reports, summarizing documents, research roundups)
- You've been curious about AI that can take multi-step actions on its own (sometimes called agentic AI) but didn't want to deal with setup

### Skip for now if:

- You're on the free plan (not available yet)
- Your organization has strict IT policies around AI agents accessing local files (check with your IT team first)

**Getting started:** Download the Claude desktop app (not the browser version, which is more limited). Open the Cowork section, create a project, point it at a folder. Give it a task and watch what it does.

---

## Perplexity Custom Skills and Deep Research deliverables

Perplexity has been the steadiest improver in this space over the past year. In March, it shipped two updates that work well together.

**Custom Skills** let you define a repeating task once and invoke it with a single command. Anything you find yourself asking Perplexity to do more than once, whether it's monitoring a topic, reformatting research, or running the same analysis on new inputs, can become a skill. Think of it as your own personal workflow template inside the tool.

**Deep Research deliverables** are the other update worth noting. Previously, Perplexity Deep Research would return a long report. Now it can turn that research into a presentation, a spreadsheet, a structured summary, or a website. Research and creation in one place.

Perplexity built its reputation on "answers with sources," which has always been its edge for research work. These two features extend that into actual work output rather than just research output. The combination means less switching between tools.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific case from a community member using Custom Skills or Deep Research deliverables in their workflow]

The Comet iOS app (Perplexity's mobile agent) also hit broad release in late March. It's Perplexity's attempt at a full mobile AI agent. Worth downloading if you use Perplexity heavily on your phone.

### What works well

Perplexity's source citations have always been its edge over Claude and ChatGPT for research tasks. When you need to write something with references, or when accuracy matters and you want to verify where a claim came from, Perplexity handles this more naturally than any of the alternatives right now.

Custom Skills add useful repeatability to that. Matt noted in our community session: "Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here's how I want you to do it. Just go." The same logic applies in Perplexity now.

### Worth trying if:

- You do regular research work (competitive analysis, market monitoring, client briefings)
- You use Perplexity already and want to reduce repetitive setup
- You're on Perplexity Pro or Max (some features are limited to paid plans)

**Getting started:** Go to Perplexity, look for the Skills section in your profile settings. Define one task you do regularly and build your first skill. Five minutes to set up.

---

## NotebookLM Cinematic Video Overviews

Google rolled out Cinematic Video Overviews in NotebookLM in early March. The existing Video Overviews feature could already turn your documents into a narrated video explanation. The cinematic update adds fluid animations and visual storytelling on top of that.

The use case: you upload a document or set of sources, and NotebookLM generates a short video that explains the content. It uses the same AI-generated audio overview format that made NotebookLM's podcast feature popular, but now with visuals.

Document-to-video has real applications: onboarding new team members, presenting research findings to non-technical stakeholders, or creating explainer content without spending time on slides. It's a workflow that didn't exist six months ago.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific case from a community member using NotebookLM video for a work deliverable]

### Honest caveats

The video quality is still in an earlier-generation feel compared to something like a polished explainer video. Don't expect it to replace a professional video production. Think of it as a useful first draft, or a tool for internal use where polish matters less than speed.

Also: this works best when your source documents are well-structured. Messy notes produce generic videos with vague narration. A clean, section-by-section document gives the model enough structure to produce a coherent narrative with accurate chapter-level coverage.

### Worth trying if:

- You already use NotebookLM for research synthesis
- You need to explain a complex topic to stakeholders who won't read the full document
- You're creating internal explainers or onboarding materials

**Getting started:** Go to [notebooklm.google.com](https://notebooklm.google.com), upload a document or paste in some sources, and look for the Video Overview button. Free to use.

---

## Gemini 3.1 in Google Workspace

Gemini 3.1 Pro launched in February 2026, and Google has been rolling out its improvements through the Workspace integration since. If you use Docs, Sheets, Slides, or Drive at work, there are upgrades worth knowing about.

The model-level improvement that matters most for everyday use: hallucination reduction. Google reports that Gemini 3.1 Pro makes fewer factual errors than the previous version [NEEDS CITATION: verify Google's published benchmarks]. When you're using AI to help research or draft documents, getting fewer things wrong changes how much you can trust the output without checking everything manually.

The Workspace integration improvements go alongside this. Gemini can now search across your Drive files and Gmail threads to answer questions with context from your actual documents. You can ask "what did we agree on in the Q4 planning doc?" and it will find the answer rather than making something up.

Cross-platform chat import (for users switching from ChatGPT or Claude to Gemini) and personal memory improvements are also live for Workspace users.

### What we use at MVP Club

Our community members are split on this one. People who live in Google Workspace already have a clear reason to try the Gemini integrations. People who rely on Claude or ChatGPT for their daily writing work have less reason to switch. Gemini 3.1 Pro leads on benchmarks in some categories, but benchmarks and daily feel don't always match. The practical difference on professional writing tasks between Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro is not dramatic enough to justify switching your main tool.

The reason to try it: if you're already inside Docs or Drive for most of your day, having Gemini search your files means fewer trips to a separate chat window.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific case from a community member using Gemini in Workspace to surface past documents or emails]

### Worth trying if:

- Your organization uses Google Workspace
- You regularly need to reference past documents or emails while writing
- You want AI that's already connected to your file system without additional setup
- You've been looking for a reason to cut down on switching between your email, Drive, and a separate AI chat window

**Getting started:** Look for the Gemini button inside Google Docs or Google Drive. If your organization has a Workspace account with Gemini enabled, it should already be there.

---

## Comparison by use case

| Use case | Best pick this month |
|---|---|
| Repeating automated tasks | Claude Cowork |
| Research with citations | Perplexity Deep Research + Custom Skills |
| Document-to-video explainers | NotebookLM |
| Search across work files in Google | Gemini in Workspace |
| Professional writing, first drafts | Claude (still our workhorse) |
| Model comparison curiosity | Perplexity Model Council runs multiple models in parallel |

## What we're keeping an eye on

The biggest story we're watching is Claude Cowork's enterprise rollout. As of now it's a research preview. If you're thinking about how AI agents might work in your organization, Cowork is worth understanding now, before it becomes a procurement question.

Also: Perplexity's MCP integration is potentially significant for teams. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard that lets AI tools connect directly to external apps and data sources. Perplexity now has 400+ prebuilt connectors, meaning you can hook Perplexity into your CRM, project management tool, or internal knowledge base and run research without leaving those systems. ## Update log

*First published April 2, 2026. This column covers AI tool updates on a monthly basis.*

---

The one thing worth doing this week: pick one tool from this list and give it 30 minutes. Not to master it. Just to see what it does with a real task from your actual job.

Claude Cowork is the best use of your time if you're already on Pro. Perplexity Custom Skills if you already use Perplexity. Either way, you'll know more on Friday than you do now, and that's how this works.

In our weekly sessions, we go through new tools together so you don't have to figure it out alone. [Join us at MVP Club](/community) when you're ready for the next round.

Anthropic shipped something big for office workers in late January, Perplexity started letting you build custom automations in March, and Google’s NotebookLM can now turn your documents into narrated video. It’s been a fast few weeks. This is our monthly roundup of what’s new, what’s actually useful, and what you can skip.

We focus on tools built for working professionals, not developers. If you need a terminal to use it, we’ll say so upfront.

What we’re covering this month

Four tools had updates in March-April 2026 that are worth your attention:

  1. Claude Cowork (Anthropic): autonomous task completion for knowledge workers
  2. Perplexity Custom Skills: research that also creates deliverables
  3. NotebookLM Cinematic Video Overviews (Google): turning documents into video
  4. Gemini 3.1 in Google Workspace: reasoning upgrades inside Docs and Drive

We’ve been testing these in our weekly sessions and with community members. Here’s the honest take.

Quick verdict

If you only have time to try one thing: Claude Cowork, if you’re on a Pro or Team plan. It does more than Claude Chat and is easier to get started with than Claude Code.

If you’re already a heavy Claude user, Perplexity’s Custom Skills are the other one worth half an hour.

The other two are worth knowing about, especially NotebookLM if you work with a lot of documents. Gemini 3.1 in Workspace is legitimately improved, but only if your organization already runs on Google.

Claude Cowork: autonomous work for non-technical users

Anthropic launched Cowork in research preview for Pro subscribers in January and has been adding features through March. The short version: it brings Claude Code-level capabilities to people who have never opened a terminal.

With Claude Chat, you type something, Claude responds, and you copy-paste the output somewhere else. Cowork works at a different level: give it a goal, and it makes a plan, works through it, and delivers a finished result. It can open files on your computer, work with your documents, schedule recurring tasks, and connect to tools like Google Drive, Gmail, and Docusign.

The key thing that sets it apart from the standalone chat interface: it has a sense of context over time. You can point it at a folder and it remembers the project. You can schedule it to run a report every Monday morning.

What our community is doing with it

In our New AI Tools session this spring, Carmella Thompson shared how she built a portfolio site using the Claude Code desktop app: “I built a GitHub portfolio site for myself, and I did it all in Claude Code. I didn’t need to look at the folder structure.”

Cowork is designed for people who had that same reaction to the terminal that Angela Frances Peat described in the same session: “The desktop is probably what I prefer, versus the terminal looks a little bit scary.”

The permission model is also worth understanding. Cowork is intentionally constrained, closer to giving someone intern-level access than root access to your machine. That’s a feature, not a limitation. For most office workflows, you want something that can do the work without having the keys to your entire system.

Worth trying if:

  • You’re on Claude Pro, Team, or Enterprise
  • You have a repeating task you’d like automated (weekly reports, summarizing documents, research roundups)
  • You’ve been curious about AI that can take multi-step actions on its own (sometimes called agentic AI) but didn’t want to deal with setup

Skip for now if:

  • You’re on the free plan (not available yet)
  • Your organization has strict IT policies around AI agents accessing local files (check with your IT team first)

Getting started: Download the Claude desktop app (not the browser version, which is more limited). Open the Cowork section, create a project, point it at a folder. Give it a task and watch what it does.

Perplexity Custom Skills and Deep Research deliverables

Perplexity has been the steadiest improver in this space over the past year. In March, it shipped two updates that work well together.

Custom Skills let you define a repeating task once and invoke it with a single command. Anything you find yourself asking Perplexity to do more than once, whether it’s monitoring a topic, reformatting research, or running the same analysis on new inputs, can become a skill. Think of it as your own personal workflow template inside the tool.

Deep Research deliverables are the other update worth noting. Previously, Perplexity Deep Research would return a long report. Now it can turn that research into a presentation, a spreadsheet, a structured summary, or a website. Research and creation in one place.

Perplexity built its reputation on “answers with sources,” which has always been its edge for research work. These two features extend that into actual work output rather than just research output. The combination means less switching between tools.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific case from a community member using Custom Skills or Deep Research deliverables in their workflow]

The Comet iOS app (Perplexity’s mobile agent) also hit broad release in late March. It’s Perplexity’s attempt at a full mobile AI agent. Worth downloading if you use Perplexity heavily on your phone.

What works well

Perplexity’s source citations have always been its edge over Claude and ChatGPT for research tasks. When you need to write something with references, or when accuracy matters and you want to verify where a claim came from, Perplexity handles this more naturally than any of the alternatives right now.

Custom Skills add useful repeatability to that. Matt noted in our community session: “Anything you do more than once, write down how to do it, and then tell Claude, here’s how I want you to do it. Just go.” The same logic applies in Perplexity now.

Worth trying if:

  • You do regular research work (competitive analysis, market monitoring, client briefings)
  • You use Perplexity already and want to reduce repetitive setup
  • You’re on Perplexity Pro or Max (some features are limited to paid plans)

Getting started: Go to Perplexity, look for the Skills section in your profile settings. Define one task you do regularly and build your first skill. Five minutes to set up.

NotebookLM Cinematic Video Overviews

Google rolled out Cinematic Video Overviews in NotebookLM in early March. The existing Video Overviews feature could already turn your documents into a narrated video explanation. The cinematic update adds fluid animations and visual storytelling on top of that.

The use case: you upload a document or set of sources, and NotebookLM generates a short video that explains the content. It uses the same AI-generated audio overview format that made NotebookLM’s podcast feature popular, but now with visuals.

Document-to-video has real applications: onboarding new team members, presenting research findings to non-technical stakeholders, or creating explainer content without spending time on slides. It’s a workflow that didn’t exist six months ago.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific case from a community member using NotebookLM video for a work deliverable]

Honest caveats

The video quality is still in an earlier-generation feel compared to something like a polished explainer video. Don’t expect it to replace a professional video production. Think of it as a useful first draft, or a tool for internal use where polish matters less than speed.

Also: this works best when your source documents are well-structured. Messy notes produce generic videos with vague narration. A clean, section-by-section document gives the model enough structure to produce a coherent narrative with accurate chapter-level coverage.

Worth trying if:

  • You already use NotebookLM for research synthesis
  • You need to explain a complex topic to stakeholders who won’t read the full document
  • You’re creating internal explainers or onboarding materials

Getting started: Go to notebooklm.google.com, upload a document or paste in some sources, and look for the Video Overview button. Free to use.

Navigating AI Anxiety: What to Do When You Feel Behind
AI anxiety is real, common, and not a sign you're failing. Here's how to recognize it, work through it, and start moving again.

The feeling usually arrives around the same moment. Someone mentions a tool you've never heard of. Your company sends another "AI strategy" memo. A colleague casually mentions they built something with Claude last week. And something tightens in your chest.

You're behind. Everyone else figured this out. You haven't.

Here's what I want to tell you from 15 years of career coaching: that feeling is not information about your capabilities. It's your nervous system reacting to an environment that keeps moving faster than feels manageable. And it's happening to almost everyone.

## AI anxiety has a pattern

In my coaching work, I see the same cluster of feelings come up again and again. Overwhelm at the pace of change. Guilt about not having done more already. Imposter syndrome about whether you even belong in the AI conversation. A low-grade dread about what it all means for your job.

These feelings tend to arrive together, and they feed each other. The overwhelm makes you avoid the tools. The avoidance creates more guilt. The guilt makes the whole thing feel bigger and scarier than it is. You end up frozen.

What makes this particularly tough is that the AI world is genuinely designed to make you feel this way. Every tool announcement is louder than the last. Every LinkedIn post is some version of "I just automated my entire job in a weekend." The comparison pressure is relentless.

But comparison is not reality. Most of the people posting breathlessly about AI are at the loud edge of a much quieter majority who are figuring this out slowly, imperfectly, and on the side.

## The biggest barrier is psychological, not technical

I came to this work from a non-technical background. That actually helps me see something that purely technical practitioners often miss: the people who struggle most with AI adoption aren't struggling because of technical complexity. They're struggling because of what the technology represents to them.

For a lot of mid-career professionals, AI feels like a direct challenge to expertise they spent years building. If you're good at your job because of your knowledge, your judgment, your relationships, your institutional memory, and now a tool can apparently do some of that in seconds, the question it raises isn't just "how do I use this?" The question underneath is: "Am I still valuable?"

That's an identity question. And you can't answer it with a tutorial.

What I've seen work instead is starting small enough that the stakes feel low. Not "learn AI." Not "build an AI strategy." One task. With one tool. Today.

> "Most people's biggest AI barrier isn't technical: it's psychological."

When you do a small thing and it works, your nervous system gets a different signal. Not "AI is overwhelming and I'm behind," but "I did a thing, it helped, I can do it again." That's the loop that breaks the anxiety. Not understanding. Doing.

## What "behind" actually means

There is no finish line you missed. No certification that proves competency. No point at which the people in front of you stopped learning and locked in their lead.

The people who feel most confident with AI right now are mostly people who started doing small things six months earlier than you. That's it. They weren't smarter or more technical. They just had a lower threshold for starting.

The gap closes fast. A few weeks of regular use with Claude or ChatGPT is genuinely enough to shift from "I don't know where to begin" to "I have a few things I do every week that actually help me." That's not a hype claim. That's what I watch happen in coaching sessions, repeatedly.

I know this because I lived a version of it. I stepped away from all of this for about three months after a family loss. When I came back, I was convinced I'd missed too much, the tools had changed, and it was going to take forever to catch up. It didn't. The practice came back faster than I expected. The anxiety about being behind was much louder than the actual gap.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: community member who returned to AI after an extended break and found the reentry faster than expected — get permission before using name]

## Three things to do when you feel stuck

These aren't hacks. They're the moves that consistently work in coaching when someone is frozen by AI anxiety.

### Name what you're actually afraid of

The anxiety often has a specific fear underneath it. Not "AI is scary" but something more precise, like:
- "I'm afraid my company will expect me to know things I don't know yet"
- "I'm afraid that what I'm good at won't matter anymore"
- "I'm afraid I'll try something and look foolish"

Writing that fear down makes it smaller. It also tells you what you actually need: reassurance about your role, a low-stakes place to try things, or just permission to not know everything yet.

### Do one thing, not a plan

The urge when you're anxious is to get ready to start. Research the tools. Watch some videos. Make a plan for how you'll approach this. All of that keeps you busy and feeling productive while maintaining a safe distance from actually doing anything.

Skip the plan. Open Claude. Paste in a document you've been meaning to summarize, an email you need to draft, or a question you've been stuck on at work. See what comes back. That's it.

You learn nothing about what AI can do for your specific work until you show it your specific work. Five minutes of actually using it is worth more than an hour of reading about it.

### Stop comparing your process to other people's outputs

The posts you see on LinkedIn are finished things. Someone's polished win. You're seeing the output, not the fifteen attempts that preceded it, not the prompts that failed, not the things the AI got completely wrong, not the time they spent editing before sharing.

Your messy, uncertain, first-attempt experience is just what learning looks like from the inside.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member describing the dissonance between feeling behind and the actual experience of starting — something that captures the "it was less scary than I thought" realization]

## What happens after you start

The anxiety doesn't disappear once you start using these tools. But it changes character. It stops being the paralytic kind and becomes the normal, productive discomfort of learning something new.

I've coached a lot of people through this. The ones who get unstuck share a consistent trait: they stopped waiting until they felt ready and just made the first move small enough that they couldn't justify avoiding it. One task. One tool. One week.

After a few weeks of that, something shifts. The AI conversation at work stops feeling like a threat and starts feeling like a space you belong in. You start having opinions about what works. You start noticing what your colleagues are still doing manually that you could show them a faster way to do. You stop feeling like someone who hasn't figured this out yet.

That's just what happens when you give yourself permission to start imperfectly.

If you want to understand what's actually happening in your specific role as AI evolves, [this piece on how AI is changing your job](/learn/ai-learning-path) is a good next read. And if you're navigating this alongside a team where some people are excited and some are not, the coaching approach in [how to handle AI skeptics](/learn/handling-ai-skeptics) applies to your own internal skeptic, too.

## You don't have to do this alone

The professionals who move through AI anxiety fastest aren't the most technical ones. They're the ones doing it alongside other people who are figuring it out at the same time.

That's what [MVP Club's weekly practice sessions](/community) are for. Not to teach you AI, but to give you a place to try things with real work, with peers who are navigating the same questions. If you've been waiting until you felt ready, that's the place where "ready enough to try" becomes the standard.

When I came back, I was convinced I’d missed too much, the tools had changed, and it was going to take forever to catch up. It didn’t. The practice came back faster than I expected. The anxiety about being behind was much louder than the actual gap.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: community member who returned to AI after an extended break and found the reentry faster than expected — get permission before using name]

Three things to do when you feel stuck

These aren’t hacks. They’re the moves that consistently work in coaching when someone is frozen by AI anxiety.

Name what you’re actually afraid of

The anxiety often has a specific fear underneath it. Not “AI is scary” but something more precise, like:

  • “I’m afraid my company will expect me to know things I don’t know yet”
  • “I’m afraid that what I’m good at won’t matter anymore”
  • “I’m afraid I’ll try something and look foolish”

Writing that fear down makes it smaller. It also tells you what you actually need: reassurance about your role, a low-stakes place to try things, or just permission to not know everything yet.

Do one thing, not a plan

The urge when you’re anxious is to get ready to start. Research the tools. Watch some videos. Make a plan for how you’ll approach this. All of that keeps you busy and feeling productive while maintaining a safe distance from actually doing anything.

Skip the plan. Open Claude. Paste in a document you’ve been meaning to summarize, an email you need to draft, or a question you’ve been stuck on at work. See what comes back. That’s it.

You learn nothing about what AI can do for your specific work until you show it your specific work. Five minutes of actually using it is worth more than an hour of reading about it.

Stop comparing your process to other people’s outputs

The posts you see on LinkedIn are finished things. Someone’s polished win. You’re seeing the output, not the fifteen attempts that preceded it, not the prompts that failed, not the things the AI got completely wrong, not the time they spent editing before sharing.

Your messy, uncertain, first-attempt experience is just what learning looks like from the inside.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member describing the dissonance between feeling behind and the actual experience of starting — something that captures the “it was less scary than I thought” realization]

What happens after you start

The anxiety doesn’t disappear once you start using these tools. But it changes character. It stops being the paralytic kind and becomes the normal, productive discomfort of learning something new.

I’ve coached a lot of people through this. The ones who get unstuck share a consistent trait: they stopped waiting until they felt ready and just made the first move small enough that they couldn’t justify avoiding it. One task. One tool. One week.

After a few weeks of that, something shifts. The AI conversation at work stops feeling like a threat and starts feeling like a space you belong in. You start having opinions about what works. You start noticing what your colleagues are still doing manually that you could show them a faster way to do. You stop feeling like someone who hasn’t figured this out yet.

That’s just what happens when you give yourself permission to start imperfectly.

If you want to understand what’s actually happening in your specific role as AI evolves, this piece on how AI is changing your job is a good next read. And if you’re navigating this alongside a team where some people are excited and some are not, the coaching approach in how to handle AI skeptics applies to your own internal skeptic, too.

You don’t have to do this alone

The professionals who move through AI anxiety fastest aren’t the most technical ones. They’re the ones doing it alongside other people who are figuring it out at the same time.

That’s what MVP Club’s weekly practice sessions are for. Not to teach you AI, but to give you a place to try things with real work, with peers who are navigating the same questions. If you’ve been waiting until you felt ready, that’s the place where “ready enough to try” becomes the standard.
The biggest barrier is psychological, not technical

How to Pitch an AI Pilot to Leadership
A practical guide to building the business case for an AI pilot at your organization, without needing a technical background or executive title.

AI pilots stall because the pitch landed wrong, not because the budget wasn't there.

Someone who's been quietly using Claude every day for three months finally gets a meeting with their manager to propose a team-wide experiment. They come in talking about large language models, automation potential, and productivity multipliers. The manager nods politely, says "that sounds interesting, let me think about it," and nothing happens.

The tool wasn't the problem. The framing was.

Before we get into how to fix that: pitching something you care about to people with power over your work is genuinely nerve-wracking. You don't have to feel confident to do this well. You just have to be prepared.

## What leadership actually needs to hear

When you pitch an AI pilot to a leader, they're hearing it through a particular filter: what's the cost, what's the risk, and what's the upside I can explain to my own stakeholders? They're not deciding whether AI is a good idea in the abstract. They're deciding whether *this proposal*, from *this person*, is worth putting time and political capital behind.

Most pitches lose before they start because they answer a different question. They make the case for AI as a category (interesting, growing, important) rather than the case for a specific thing your specific team can try in the next 90 days to produce a specific outcome. Those are very different conversations.

A large share of organizations are still stuck in the gap between individual experimentation and anything that counts as organizational alignment [NEEDS CITATION: verify directly from McKinsey 2025 State of AI report]. Your job in the pitch is to propose a small, scoped, low-risk experiment that answers a question your leadership already cares about.

## Start with the problem, not the solution

The most effective pitch structure is counterintuitive: don't lead with AI at all.

Start with a problem your organization is already paying attention to. Something your manager talks about in team meetings. A process that everyone agrees takes too long or produces inconsistent results. A bottleneck that's showing up in retrospectives or quarterly reviews.

That problem is your opening. "You know how we've been talking about how long our status report cycle takes, and how half of them get ignored anyway? I've been experimenting with a different approach, and I'd love to show you what it looks like."

Now you're in a conversation they already wanted to have. The AI is the mechanism, not the lead.

This matters because of how organizational attention works. Leaders have a shortlist of active problems they're trying to solve. If your pitch connects to something already on that list, it gets real consideration. If it's positioned as something new they should add to the list, it almost never makes it.

## The anatomy of a pitch that gets a yes

Once you've grounded the conversation in a real problem, the pitch itself needs four things. Not a deck. Not a business plan. Four clear answers to questions your leadership will have, in the order they'll have them.

**1. What exactly are we trying?**

Name the specific task or workflow. Not "AI for marketing." Something like: "I want to run a four-week experiment where three people on the team use Claude to draft our weekly client summary emails, and we measure whether we can cut draft time in half and improve client response rates."

Specificity reduces perceived risk. A well-scoped experiment feels fundable. "AI for marketing" feels like a commitment they can't evaluate.

**2. What does success look like?**

Name a number, a behavior, or a milestone. Time saved per person per week. Response rate on a particular type of communication. Number of deliverables completed in a sprint. If you can't name a metric, you haven't scoped it tightly enough yet.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member who successfully pitched an AI pilot describing what success metric they used and how leadership responded]

**3. What's the actual cost and risk?**

For most AI pilots with Claude or ChatGPT, the cost is almost offensively small: a $20/month subscription per person, or a few hundred dollars for an API integration if you want more capability. That number is important to say out loud because leadership often imagines AI experiments as expensive. The moment you name the real figure, the risk calculus changes.

The other risk question is about data: will employees be sharing anything sensitive? This is worth having a clear answer to. For a Claude Pro account, individual conversations aren't used for training by default on paid plans. For anything involving confidential company data, route that question through IT or legal first, and know that this is exactly what [AI workplace policies](/learn/ai-workplace-policies) cover.

**4. What's the timeline to a decision?**

Ask for 30 to 90 days, not indefinitely. "If we run this from May through July, we'll have enough data to know whether it's worth scaling." A defined endpoint makes a pilot feel safe. Leadership can approve a contained experiment they can't approve an open-ended program.

## The one slide that actually moves things

If you do need to show something visual, keep it to one page. More than one page usually means the idea isn't tight yet. That's fine to acknowledge, but tighten it before the meeting.

The one slide has three sections. Current state (what the problem costs you now, in time or money or quality). Proposed pilot (what you want to try, who's involved, for how long). Expected outcome (what success looks like, and what you'll do with the data).

That's it. Everything else is appendix, and appendix material usually means you're not sure your core case is strong enough.

The best proposals I've seen from professionals coaching through this process have one thing in common: they treat leadership like adults who are making a resource allocation decision, not audiences who need to be persuaded that AI is good. The difference in how those conversations land is significant.

## How to handle the most common objections

**"I'm not sure our team is ready for this."**

This objection usually means: I'm worried people won't adopt it, and I don't want to sponsor a thing that fails. The answer is to make adoption part of the pilot design. "That's exactly why I want to start small. Three people, four weeks, structured check-ins. We learn what works for this team before we expand from there."

The framing matters. Keeping it small and scoped signals that you're not asking for a big commitment, just permission to find out.

**"How do we know this is secure?"**

Have the data handling answer ready before they ask. Paid Claude plans (Pro, Team, and Enterprise) don't use your conversations to train models by default. If your organization uses specific tools through enterprise agreements, those have additional protections. If you genuinely don't know the answer for a specific tool, say so and offer to find out before the pilot starts. That answer, delivered calmly, lands better than a stumble.

**"What's the ROI?"**

This is the question most people fear, and it's also the most honest one. The truthful answer is: "We don't know yet, which is why we want to pilot." But you can give a directional estimate. If the pilot saves three people three hours per week for four weeks, that's 36 hours of recovered capacity. At whatever fully-loaded cost your manager uses for that estimate, that's a number you can put in the room. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific time-saved estimate from an MVP Club member's pilot that became a real business case number]

The ROI frame works when it's tied to a problem leadership already cares about. Which brings us back to where this started.

## What to do before you're in the room

The pitch is actually the last step. Before you get there, three things will make it significantly easier.

**Build your own evidence first.** The most credible pitches come from people who've already been using the tool for themselves. If you've been using Claude to draft communications and it's cut your time in half, say that. Your personal experience is evidence. Use it.

I came from a non-technical background and I was skeptical. Once I started using Claude for my own client work, I stopped wondering whether this was worth pitching. I knew.

**Find your allies.** Who else in your organization is already using AI? In MVP Club sessions, we hear this constantly: once one person starts talking about what's working, two or three others come out of the woodwork who've been doing the same thing quietly. Those people are your coalition. A pilot proposal from three people in different functions carries more weight than one from a lone enthusiast.

**Talk to IT early.** Not to get permission before the pitch, but to know what's already approved and in place. What tools are approved? What questions will legal or security ask? Having those answers in advance makes you look more prepared and makes the pilot design more realistic. It also surfaces any deal-breakers before you're in front of leadership.

## After the pitch

If the answer is yes: run the pilot with as much rigor as you promised. Document what you're measuring. Check in weekly. Be honest if something isn't working. The worst thing that can happen to a future AI initiative at your organization is a pilot that nobody followed up on.

If the answer is "let's revisit this later": ask what it would take to move forward. Sometimes "later" means "show me a stronger business case." Sometimes it means "get IT's sign-off first." Sometimes it means "I'm not the right person, try this person." All of those are navigable. "Revisit later" is rarely a hard no.

If the answer is no: that's information too. Some organizations aren't ready to run a formal pilot, and trying to force it rarely helps. The more durable path is to keep building your own skills, keep connecting with peers who are doing the same, and let your personal results make the case over time. The professionals who become the AI leads at their organizations are usually not the ones who got a formal pilot approved in their first meeting.

If you want a broader look at what this looks like from the individual contributor level, [how to become the AI person on your team](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) is a useful companion read.

---

The professionals I've watched make the most progress on this are the ones who showed up to the pitch meeting with real evidence from their own use (not borrowed confidence from a McKinsey stat), a scoped experiment with a clear endpoint, and at least one person in the room who already knew it was worth trying. That combination is buildable. It just takes a few weeks of doing the thing before you ask for permission to do the thing together. In MVP Club, members share what actually worked in their pitch meetings, what they wish they'd said differently, and what they're preparing to try next. If that sounds like a room worth being in, [come find us](/community).
