The Silent AI Users: Why People Aren't Talking About How They Use AI at Work
Nearly half of workers hide their AI use from colleagues and managers. Here's why that's happening, what it costs you, and what to do instead.

You finish a first draft of a report in twenty minutes using Claude. It took your colleague three hours to do the same thing last week. You save the file. You don't say a word.

That's not unusual. [NEEDS CITATION: verify Anthropic published this study] [Nearly half of employees actively hide their AI use from their employers](https://www.securitymagazine.com/articles/101834-49-of-americans-hide-ai-use-from-employers), according to Security Magazine, and more than a third say it's because they worry others will think they're cutting corners.

There's a whole shadow economy of AI use happening in offices right now. Efficiently and completely out of sight.

## Why the silence makes total sense

The fear driving this isn't irrational. Research from Duke's Fuqua School of Business found that people who disclosed their AI use were consistently rated by observers as lazier, less competent, and less diligent than those who received help from human colleagues. The perception penalty is real. You can understand why someone would do the mental math and decide: "I'll just keep this to myself."

And then there's the deeper fear. If you tell your manager you used ChatGPT to write that analysis, are you showing them which parts of your job could be automated? That worry isn't paranoia. The [Microsoft Work Trend Index](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/ai-at-work-is-here-now-comes-the-hard-part) found that 52% of AI users worry that using it on important tasks makes them look replaceable.

The silence has two very different causes, and they require different responses.

The first cause is the policy gap. Most companies haven't told people clearly what's allowed. When rules are vague, people default to private. If you're unsure whether you're allowed to use Claude for client-facing work, keeping quiet feels like the safer bet. (For more on navigating this, see [AI Workplace Policies: What You Need to Know Before Using AI at Your Job](/learn/ai-workplace-policies).)

The second cause is identity. A lot of people have spent years, sometimes decades, building expertise in something. AI feels like it's questioning whether that expertise still counts. When using a tool feels like an admission, of course you don't talk about it out loud.

## What keeping quiet actually costs you

The silence feels protective. It's also more expensive than it looks.

When you work alone with AI tools and don't share what's working, you slow down everyone around you. Your colleague spends three hours on a task you did in twenty minutes. You have the solution to their problem and you don't share it. That's not a small thing.

There's also a career cost. The professionals who are going to be recognized as AI-capable over the next few years aren't just the people who use these tools. They're the people who are visible using these tools. The ones who say "here's what I tried, here's what I got, here's how I used my judgment to make it better." That visibility builds a reputation. The silent AI user builds nothing publicly, even if they're more efficient privately.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member who was a silent user and what they experienced before they started sharing]

And there's a personal cost too. Figuring out these tools alone is slower and more frustrating than figuring them out alongside other people. When you keep your practice private, you miss the questions other people are already asking, the shortcuts they've already found, the mistakes they've already made. You do that work again yourself, without the benefit of anyone else's experience.

## The thing most people get wrong about disclosure

Sharing that you use AI at work doesn't mean announcing it constantly or turning every conversation into a lesson. It's much simpler than that.

It looks like this: "I used Claude to generate a first draft and then rewrote the structure." Or: "I ran this data through ChatGPT to find patterns, then checked the output against our actuals." That's it. You're not explaining AI. You're just being transparent about your process the way you would be if you said "I used a template" or "I ran it by Sarah first."

The part people skip is that framing. They either say nothing, or they feel like they need to justify themselves. Neither is right. Disclosure doesn't have to be a speech. It can just be a sentence.

[NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: a specific moment where someone mentioned they used Claude and it opened up a conversation rather than causing judgment]

## What to do if your organization has no clarity

A lot of companies still haven't given clear guidance on which AI tools are approved. If yours is one of them, here's a practical path.

Start with your own low-stakes work. Use Claude or ChatGPT on tasks that don't involve confidential data, client information, or sensitive strategy. Get good at it. Build a track record of output you're proud of.

Then bring one concrete example to a manager or peer you trust. "I used Claude to draft this template. It saved me two hours. Here's what I changed and why." You're not asking permission retroactively. You're creating a visible data point.

Over time, this is how AI adoption actually spreads in organizations. Not through mandates, not through workshops, but through one person showing another what they did and how it worked. The quiet users who stay quiet don't create that effect. The ones who share, even casually, do.

If you want to understand where your company is on the policy side before you say anything, [AI Workplace Policies: What You Need to Know Before Using AI at Your Job](/learn/ai-workplace-policies) is worth reading first.

## The career read on this moment

The silent AI users aren't doing anything wrong. They've made a reasonable bet given the social dynamics of their workplaces. But reasonable bets can still be expensive over time.

The people who benefit most from AI aren't just the ones who use it most. They're the ones who make their use legible to the people around them. Who say "here's what worked" and "here's what didn't" and "you should try this." That kind of transparency is how you become the person others come to when they have an AI question. Which is, right now, one of the most valuable professional positions you can hold.

You don't need to wait for your organization to figure this out before you start. You can start with one honest conversation. With a colleague. With your manager. Even with yourself about why you've been keeping it private.

You're not cutting corners. You're doing your job well and using the best tools available. That's worth saying out loud.

For more on building your AI practice at work, see [How to Use AI at Work: A Practical Guide for Busy Professionals](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) and [How to Build AI into Your Daily Workflow](/learn/ai-daily-workflow).

## You don't have to figure this out alone

A lot of the silence comes from not having peers to practice with. When you're learning in private, everything feels higher stakes. When you're working alongside other professionals who are doing the same things and asking the same questions, the stakes come down fast.

That's what happens in MVP Club every week: real professionals sharing what they tried, what landed, and what they'd do differently. If you want to move from silent to visible, that's a good place to start. [Come see how the community works.](/community)

You finish a first draft of a report in twenty minutes using Claude. It took your colleague three hours to do the same thing last week. You save the file. You don’t say a word.

That’s not unusual. [NEEDS CITATION: verify Anthropic published this study] Nearly half of employees actively hide their AI use from their employers, according to Security Magazine, and more than a third say it’s because they worry others will think they’re cutting corners.

There’s a whole shadow economy of AI use happening in offices right now. Efficiently and completely out of sight.

Why the silence makes total sense

The fear driving this isn’t irrational. Research from Duke’s Fuqua School of Business found that people who disclosed their AI use were consistently rated by observers as lazier, less competent, and less diligent than those who received help from human colleagues. The perception penalty is real. You can understand why someone would do the mental math and decide: “I’ll just keep this to myself.”

And then there’s the deeper fear. If you tell your manager you used ChatGPT to write that analysis, are you showing them which parts of your job could be automated? That worry isn’t paranoia. The Microsoft Work Trend Index found that 52% of AI users worry that using it on important tasks makes them look replaceable.

The silence has two very different causes, and they require different responses.

The first cause is the policy gap. Most companies haven’t told people clearly what’s allowed. When rules are vague, people default to private. If you’re unsure whether you’re allowed to use Claude for client-facing work, keeping quiet feels like the safer bet. (For more on navigating this, see AI Workplace Policies: What You Need to Know Before Using AI at Your Job.)

The second cause is identity. A lot of people have spent years, sometimes decades, building expertise in something. AI feels like it’s questioning whether that expertise still counts. When using a tool feels like an admission, of course you don’t talk about it out loud.

What keeping quiet actually costs you

The silence feels protective. It’s also more expensive than it looks.

When you work alone with AI tools and don’t share what’s working, you slow down everyone around you. Your colleague spends three hours on a task you did in twenty minutes. You have the solution to their problem and you don’t share it. That’s not a small thing.

There’s also a career cost. The professionals who are going to be recognized as AI-capable over the next few years aren’t just the people who use these tools. They’re the people who are visible using these tools. The ones who say “here’s what I tried, here’s what I got, here’s how I used my judgment to make it better.” That visibility builds a reputation. The silent AI user builds nothing publicly, even if they’re more efficient privately.

You do that work again yourself, without the benefit of anyone else’s experience.

What Is Vibe Coding? A Plain-English Explanation
Vibe coding lets you build real software by describing what you want in plain English. Here's what it is, how it works, and why it matters.

A year ago, our co-founder Matt built a browser-based game. He has a PhD in Educational Foundations. He has never written a for-loop. And he built a browser-based game. That's vibe coding.

The term was coined by Andrej Karpathy, a co-founder of OpenAI, in February 2025. His description: a new kind of coding where you "fully give in to the vibes, embrace exponentials, and forget that the code even exists." Collins English Dictionary named it their Word of the Year for 2025. Merriam-Webster added it to their slang and trending list.

Big moment for a term that describes something pretty simple: you tell the AI what you want to build. The AI builds it. You look at what it made, tell it what to change, and keep going until you have the thing you wanted.

## What vibe coding actually is

Vibe coding is a conversational approach to software development. Instead of writing code line by line in a programming language, you describe your goal in plain English, review what the AI produces, give feedback, and iterate.

You're not writing code. You're directing it. The mental model is closer to managing a project than writing a program. You bring the goal, the context, and the judgment. Claude, or whatever AI you're using, brings the technical execution.

![Vibe coding shifts you from executing the work to shaping it. On the left, the old path of writing code line by line. On the right, directing the work in plain English.](/images/learn/what-is-vibe-coding-1-directing-code.svg)

## Vibe coding is a strategic, managerial skill

Even before AI, many very intelligent people have brought software to market without writing a line of code. Product managers were the closest thing to vibe coders in the pre-AI era, they just had a team of engineers rather than an LLM to work with. But the skill and practice are the same: be clear about what your team is building, be judgmental in what you choose to build, listen to your users when it comes to feature development...all very important and valuable skills!

At the end of the day, vibe coding is a practiced skill like any other, and it still follows the same fundamental principle of all business processes: garbage in, garbage out. You have to take the time to provide solid input to get a good result.

The AI doesn't read your mind. Vague instructions produce vague results, and the professionals who get the most out of vibe coding are the ones who learn to describe what they want clearly and evaluate what they get honestly. Steering the conversation when things go sideways is part of the job.

Our non-technical community members use vibe coding to build internal dashboards, automate reporting workflows, create client-facing tools, and replace spreadsheets they've been maintaining manually for years. When it comes to internal tools, especially the growing practice of "software for one," vibe coding can produce effective tools at lightning speed.

## Why this is different from "just using AI"

When you use Claude or ChatGPT to write an email or summarize a document, the output lives in the chat window. You copy it out, paste it somewhere, done.

Vibe coding is different because the output is software. It runs. Other people can use it. You can deploy it to the internet (meaning publish it so anyone can access it from a browser). The thing you made has a life outside the chat window.

That shift matters. It means non-technical professionals can now build tools they previously had to request from engineering teams, buy off the shelf (often imperfectly), or do without entirely.

Once you can share your vibe-coded tool with your team and they say "let's start using this," that's the turning point that makes vibe coding feel meaningful beyond hobby projects. That moment when someone else is using the thing you made changes how you think about what you're capable of.

## What tools do people use?

The most common setup in MVP Club is Claude Code, Anthropic's coding-focused AI. You can run it as a desktop app or through a terminal (a text-based command window, like a chat interface for your computer). The conversation works the same either way: you describe what you want, Claude asks clarifying questions, builds the files, and shows you the result.

Don't let the name "Claude Code" fool you: Claude is the one doing the coding, and you're the manager telling it what to create. 

A few other tools people use:

- Lovable or Base44: browser-based, good for beginners who want to see visual results quickly
- ChatGPT Codex: popular with people building more complex projects, requires more tenacity and curiosity to interact with your project directly
- Claude Code desktop app: a good middle ground if the terminal feels intimidating

The right tool depends on what you're building and how you like to work. Most people in our community start with Claude Code because it's what we use in our weekly building sessions and there's always someone around to help when you get stuck.

There's sometimes a mysterious layer of vibe coding projects, where you as the creator might not be able to articulate "what" the app is. You just see it running in your browser when Claude tells you the app is running. 

It's definitely worth taking some time conversing with Claude about "what" you actually built and how it can be used or shared by others. Yes, you built an app: what are the pieces of the tech stack? It's worth learning the high-level components and a bit of the jargon, even if you're not the one writing the code. "How do I deploy an express app to google cloud" is a much clearer and actionable next step question than "How do I get this out there?"

## The skill that actually matters

When I coach people through their first vibe coding project, the thing that trips them up almost never involves code. It involves description and planning.

"Make it look nicer" is not actionable feedback for Claude. "The button colors feel too bold, and the section headings are harder to read than I'd like" gives Claude something to work with. The more specific you are about what you want and what you see, the faster you get there.

A few things that help in practice:

- **Start with a clear goal, not a list of features.** "I want a tool that lets me log client check-ins without opening a spreadsheet" is better than "I want a database with a form."
- **Tell the LLM to ask you clarifying questions** to refine your goal into a workable plan, often a PRD (Product Requirements Document).
- **Review what it plans and make adjustments.** Vibe coding works in iterations. Approve what's working, fix what isn't, then move forward.
- **Keep a project notes file.** An LLM doesn't remember previous conversations. A short document summarizing what you've built and what's next keeps things coherent across sessions.

## Who should try this

The short answer: anyone who has ever thought "I wish there was a tool that did X" and assumed they couldn't build it. Even if you're not the one who ultimately builds a thing, a vibe coded demo is much farther along as a prototype than a few sketches and an ideas document!

You don't need a technical background. The barrier to starting is now lower than the barrier to learning Excel was fifteen years ago. What used to require years of training or a hefty engineering contract now requires a $20/month Claude subscription and an afternoon.

What you need are a clear goal, an ability to evaluate what's working and articulate what isn't, and the tenacity to stick with the continuous cycle of refinement until you've gotten what you want.

## The real reason this matters

Most professionals have been constrained by the software that exists. You work inside tools someone else built, with features someone else decided you needed, within workflows someone else designed. If you needed something different, you either adapted or requested it and waited.

Vibe coding changes that constraint. The question stops being "does a tool exist for this?" and starts being "what do I want to build?"

That's a genuinely new question for most people. It takes some getting used to. But once you've experienced the moment where something you described in a conversation becomes a working thing that other people can use, it's hard to go back to assuming you couldn't.

---

If you want to see what this looks like in practice alongside other professionals who are figuring it out in real time, that's exactly what happens in MVP Club's weekly building sessions. [Come join us.](/community)

What AI Can and Can't Do Right Now (An Honest Rundown)
An honest look at what AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT can and can't do for busy professionals, before you spend time figuring it out the hard way.

One of the most common things I hear from professionals starting out with AI is some version of: "I tried it, it didn't work, and now I'm not sure what it's actually good for." That confusion is reasonable. The hype around these tools is enormous, and the gap between what AI companies promise and what most people actually experience in their first few uses is real.

So here's a clear-eyed look at what AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT can genuinely do for you right now, and where they'll let you down if you lean on them wrong.

## What AI is actually good at

### First drafts of almost anything

The blank page is where most professionals lose the most time. AI is exceptionally good at giving you something to react to. You describe what you need, give it some context about your audience and goal, and it produces a draft you can edit.

The draft won't be perfect. It probably won't sound like you. But editing a 400-word draft takes 15 minutes. Starting from zero takes 45. That difference adds up fast across a week of writing.

This applies to more than emails. Status reports, meeting summaries, project briefs, talking points for a difficult conversation, LinkedIn posts, proposal outlines. Anywhere you'd typically face a blank screen, AI shortens the runway.

### Reading and summarizing long documents

You have a 50-page report that's relevant to tomorrow's meeting. With Claude or ChatGPT, you can paste the whole thing in and ask specific questions: "What are the three biggest risks in this proposal?" or "Summarize the financial assumptions in section four."

This works better than a generic summary request because the tool is reading to answer your question, not just compressing the text. You can follow up. You can ask for clarification. You can tell it what you already know and ask what's new.

This alone, for professionals who deal with information overload, can save hours a week.

### Thinking out loud about a problem

This one surprises people. Claude and ChatGPT are useful as thinking partners when you're stuck. Not because they have the answer, but because describing a problem clearly enough for the tool to respond forces you to clarify your own thinking.

Try it with something you're actually wrestling with. A team conflict you're not sure how to address. A project that keeps stalling for reasons you can't name. A decision where you can see two reasonable paths and can't get past the uncertainty. Explain the situation in plain language and ask the tool to help you think through what you might be missing.

It'll surface angles you hadn't considered. Some of them won't apply. A few of them will. That's not magic, that's useful.

### Repeatable tasks with a clear pattern

If you do the same kind of work every week (weekly status updates, meeting recaps, reviewing a standard document type), AI can dramatically speed up the routine parts once you invest a little time building a good prompt.

A product manager in our community described this: she spent 20 minutes building a prompt template for her weekly status updates. Now the first draft takes her about 5 minutes instead of 45, and she spends the rest of the time editing rather than writing from scratch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing a specific repeatable task they use AI for regularly, with time savings details]

---

## Where AI falls short

This is the part that matters as much as the list above. Using AI well means knowing its real limits, not the theoretical ones.

### It makes things up, confidently

This is the most important thing to understand about these tools. AI models like Claude and ChatGPT do not have a "truth detector." They generate plausible-sounding text based on patterns. When they don't know something, they often produce something that sounds credible but is simply wrong.

This is called hallucination, and it happens most often with specific facts: names, dates, statistics, citations, product details, legal or medical specifics. If you ask Claude "what were Apple's Q3 revenues in 2024?" you might get a number. That number might be correct. It might also be confidently fabricated. You need to verify it.

Treat AI output the way you'd treat a smart colleague's off-the-cuff recollection: useful as a starting point, worth double-checking before you stake anything on it.

### It doesn't know your context

AI tools have no idea who you are, what your organization does, who your manager is, or what happened in last week's meeting. Every conversation starts from scratch. The output you get before you give it context will be generic. The output you get after giving it real context, your actual situation, your actual audience, your actual goal, will be substantially better.

This is why so many people try AI once, get a mediocre result, and conclude it doesn't work. They gave it a vague prompt with no context and compared the output to what they could produce themselves after years of institutional knowledge.

"All these companies want your attention," I said in one of our Getting Started sessions. "But what do I want these tools to do for me?" That reframe matters. Start with your real problem. Give the tool your real context. Then evaluate.

### It has no taste (for real)

Visual work is genuinely hard for AI right now. Claude can verify that a button exists on a webpage, but it can't tell you whether the design feels right. It doesn't have aesthetic judgment.

A member of our community built a product specifically because of this problem. He put it simply: "Claude and LLMs have no taste, no visual opinion to render." That's an honest summary of a real constraint. If you need visual judgment, design intuition, or aesthetic direction, AI tools are limited partners right now.

### It can't take real-world actions (mostly)

Standard AI chat tools (Claude.ai, ChatGPT) are conversation-only. They can't send emails, update your CRM, schedule meetings, or do anything in the systems you actually use for work unless you're using specific integrations or agentic tools that wire them into those systems.

This matters because people often imagine AI as a more capable version of what it is. It's a powerful thinking and drafting tool. Getting it to actually *do* things in your existing systems takes additional setup that's outside the scope of most basic use cases.

### It doesn't remember between conversations

Each conversation with Claude or ChatGPT is a fresh start. It doesn't remember what you discussed last Tuesday. If continuity matters, you need to bring context into every session yourself: a brief summary of where things stand, relevant background documents, key decisions already made.

This is less limiting than it sounds once you build a habit around it. A short "here's my context" paragraph at the start of each session takes 30 seconds and dramatically improves the quality of what comes back.

---

## The category that depends entirely on you

There's one area where AI capability and your own skill are deeply intertwined: iteration.

The first output from Claude or ChatGPT is almost never the final output. The people who get the most value from these tools are the ones who treat that first response as a starting point, push back on what doesn't work, add detail, redirect, and refine. [That iterative relationship is what we mean by a managerial approach to AI](/blog/probabilistic-vs-deterministic-ai): you're managing inputs, goals, and outputs.

"This is the really important step of vibe coding," I said in a recent session. "You gave it the requirements, it built it, and now you're managing the output through evaluation." That applies equally to writing, analysis, and any other AI-assisted work. The value is in the back-and-forth, not the first result.

People who accept the first output and decide AI "doesn't work" are skipping the most important part of the process.

---

## A few honest calibrations

**AI is faster for most drafting work, but not always faster for high-stakes work.** When you're writing something where the specific details and your specific voice really matter, editing AI output can take longer than writing from scratch. Know when to use it and when to just write.

**It gets better as you get better at directing it.** The tool's capabilities haven't changed; what's changed is your ability to give it useful context and clear goals. [That's a practice skill, not a technical one](/blog/ai-time-paradox), and it builds with use.

**Free and paid are genuinely different.** Claude Pro and ChatGPT Plus ($20/month each) offer faster responses, more capable models, and longer conversations. If you're doing serious daily work with these tools, the paid tier is worth it. If you're experimenting, free is fine to start.

**The gap between what AI can do and what you get depends on your prompts.** "Be very specific," is advice I give constantly in our sessions. The more precise your context, audience, and goal, the better the output. Vague asks produce vague answers.

---

## Where to go from here

The honest summary: AI is genuinely good at drafting, synthesizing, thinking things through, and handling repeatable work. It's unreliable for specific facts, unaware of your context by default, weak on visual judgment, and not connected to your work systems without additional setup.

If you want to build a practical sense of where AI fits into your actual work, [the best starting point is picking one real task and trying it this week](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work). Not a test. Something you actually have to do.

The professionals who figure out where AI works for them aren't reading more about it. They're using it on real problems with real stakes, making note of where it helps and where it doesn't, and building judgment over time. If you want to do that alongside people who are in the same process, that's what happens every week in [MVP Club's community sessions](/community).

One of the most common things I hear from professionals starting out with AI is some version of: “I tried it, it didn’t work, and now I’m not sure what it’s actually good for.” That confusion is reasonable. The hype around these tools is enormous, and the gap between what AI companies promise and what most people actually experience in their first few uses is real.

So here’s a clear-eyed look at what AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT can genuinely do for you right now, and where they’ll let you down if you lean on them wrong.

What AI is actually good at

First drafts of almost anything

The blank page is where most professionals lose the most time. AI is exceptionally good at giving you something to react to. You describe what you need, give it some context about your audience and goal, and it produces a draft you can edit.

The draft won’t be perfect. It probably won’t sound like you. But editing a 400-word draft takes 15 minutes. Starting from zero takes 45. That difference adds up fast across a week of writing.

This applies to more than emails. Status reports, meeting summaries, project briefs, talking points for a difficult conversation, LinkedIn posts, proposal outlines. Anywhere you’d typically face a blank screen, AI shortens the runway.

Reading and summarizing long documents

You have a 50-page report that’s relevant to tomorrow’s meeting. With Claude or ChatGPT, you can paste the whole thing in and ask specific questions: “What are the three biggest risks in this proposal?” or “Summarize the financial assumptions in section four.”

This works better than a generic summary request because the tool is reading to answer your question, not just compressing the text. You can follow up. You can ask for clarification. You can tell it what you already know and ask what’s new.

This alone, for professionals who deal with information overload, can save hours a week.

Thinking out loud about a problem

This one surprises people. Claude and ChatGPT are useful as thinking partners when you’re stuck. Not because they have the answer, but because describing a problem clearly enough for the tool to respond forces you to clarify your own thinking.

Try it with something you’re actually wrestling with. A team conflict you’re not sure how to address. A project that keeps stalling for reasons you can’t name. A decision where you can see two reasonable paths and can’t get past the uncertainty. Explain the situation in plain language and ask the tool to help you think through what you might be missing.

It’ll surface angles you hadn’t considered. Some of them won’t apply. A few of them will. That’s not magic, that’s useful.

Repeatable tasks with a clear pattern

If you do the same kind of work every week (weekly status updates, meeting recaps, reviewing a standard document type), AI can dramatically speed up the routine parts once you invest a little time building a good prompt.

A product manager in our community described this: she spent 20 minutes building a prompt template for her weekly status updates. Now the first draft takes her about 5 minutes instead of 45, and she spends the rest of the time editing rather than writing from scratch.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A member describing a specific repeatable task they use AI for regularly, with time savings details]

Where AI falls short

This is the part that matters as much as the list above. Using AI well means knowing its real limits, not the theoretical ones.

It makes things up, confidently

This is the most important thing to understand about these tools. AI models like Claude and ChatGPT do not have a “truth detector.” They generate plausible-sounding text based on patterns. When they don’t know something, they often produce something that sounds credible but is simply wrong.

This is called hallucination, and it happens most often with specific facts: names, dates, statistics, citations, product details, legal or medical specifics. If you ask Claude “what were Apple’s Q3 revenues in 2024?” you might get a number. That number might be correct. It might also be confidently fabricated. You need to verify it.

Treat AI output the way you’d treat a smart colleague’s off-the-cuff recollection: useful as a starting point, worth double-checking before you stake anything on it.

It doesn’t know your context

AI tools have no idea who you are, what your organization does, who your manager is, or what happened in last week’s meeting. Every conversation starts from scratch. The output you get before you give it context will be generic. The output you get after giving it real context, your actual situation, your actual audience, your actual goal, will be substantially better.

This is why so many people try AI once, get a mediocre result, and conclude it doesn’t work. They gave it a vague prompt with no context and compared the output to what they could produce themselves after years of institutional knowledge.

“All these companies want your attention,” I said in one of our Getting Started sessions. “But what do I want these tools to do for me?” That reframe matters. Start with your real problem. Give the tool your real context. Then evaluate.

It has no taste (for real)

Visual work is genuinely hard for AI right now. Claude can verify that a button exists on a webpage, but it can’t tell you whether the design feels right. It doesn’t have aesthetic judgment.

A member of our community built a product specifically because of this problem. He put it simply: “Claude and LLMs have no taste, no visual opinion to render.” That’s an honest summary of a real constraint. If you need visual judgment, design intuition, or aesthetic direction, AI tools are limited partners right now.

It can’t take real-world actions (mostly)

Standard AI chat tools (Claude.ai, ChatGPT) are conversation-only. They can’t send emails, update your CRM, schedule meetings, or do anything in the systems you actually use for work unless you’re using specific integrations or agentic tools that wire them into those systems.

This matters because people often imagine AI as a more capable version of what it is. It’s a powerful thinking and drafting tool. Getting it to actually do things in your existing systems takes additional setup that’s outside the scope of most basic use cases.

It doesn’t remember between conversations

Each conversation with Claude or ChatGPT is a fresh start. It doesn’t remember what you discussed last Tuesday. If continuity matters, you need to bring context into every session yourself: a brief summary of where things stand, relevant background documents, key decisions already made.

This is less limiting than it sounds once you build a habit around it. A short “here’s my context” paragraph at the start of each session takes 30 seconds and dramatically improves the quality of what comes back.

The category that depends entirely on you

There’s one area where AI capability and your own skill are deeply intertwined: iteration.

The first output from Claude or ChatGPT is almost never the final output. The people who get the most value from these tools are the ones who treat that first response as a starting point, push back on what doesn’t work, add detail, redirect, and refine. That iterative relationship is what we mean by a managerial approach to AI: you’re managing inputs, goals, and outputs.

“This is the really important step of vibe coding,” I said in a recent session. “You gave it the requirements, it built it, and now you’re managing the output through evaluation.” That applies equally to writing, analysis, and any other AI-assisted work. The value is in the back-and-forth, not the first result.

People who accept the first output and decide AI “doesn’t work” are skipping the most important part of the process.

A few honest calibrations

AI is faster for most drafting work, but not always faster for high-stakes work. When you’re writing something where the specific details and your specific voice really matter, editing AI output can take longer than writing from scratch. Know when to use it and when to just write.

It gets better as you get better at directing it. The tool’s capabilities haven’t changed; what’s changed is your ability to give it useful context and clear goals. That’s a practice skill, not a technical one, and it builds with use.

Free and paid are genuinely different. Claude Pro and ChatGPT Plus ($20/month each) offer faster responses, more capable models, and longer conversations. If you’re doing serious daily work with these tools, the paid tier is worth it. If you’re experimenting, free is fine to start.

The gap between what AI can do and what you get depends on your prompts. “Be very specific,” is advice I give constantly in our sessions. The more precise your context, audience, and goal, the better the output. Vague asks produce vague answers.

Where to go from here

The honest summary: AI is genuinely good at drafting, synthesizing, thinking things through, and handling repeatable work. It’s unreliable for specific facts, unaware of your context by default, weak on visual judgment, and not connected to your work systems without additional setup.

If you want to build a practical sense of where AI fits into your actual work, the best starting point is picking one real task and trying it this week. Not a test. Something you actually have to do.

The professionals who figure out where AI works for them aren’t reading more about it. They’re using it on real problems with real stakes, making note of where it helps and where it doesn’t, and building judgment over time. If you want to do that alongside people who are in the same process, that’s what happens every week in MVP Club’s community sessions.

What to Do When AI Gives You Bad Output (The Iteration Skill)
AI rarely nails it on the first try. Here's how to diagnose bad output and iterate your way to results worth using.

You paste in a task, Claude spits something back, and it's... not what you wanted. Too formal. Missing the point. Weirdly generic. You stare at it, wonder if you're using AI wrong, and close the tab.

That moment of frustration is where most people stop. And it's exactly where the skill begins.

Getting bad output from Claude or ChatGPT is the first data point in an iterative process, not a failure signal. The professionals who get real results from AI aren't the ones who write perfect prompts on the first try. They're the ones who learned to diagnose what went wrong and course-correct fast.

## Why AI output goes sideways (and what you can actually fix)

Bad output almost always comes from one of four places. Knowing which one you're dealing with tells you exactly what to change.

**1. The goal was unclear.** You asked for "an email to my team," but the AI doesn't know if that's a weekly update, a difficult conversation, a celebration, or a request for information. Vague goal, vague output.

**2. The context was missing.** AI doesn't know who you are, what your company does, what your relationship with the recipient is, or what you've already tried. It's filling in blanks with reasonable guesses that might not match your situation.

**3. The format wasn't specified.** You wanted three bullet points. You got four paragraphs. You wanted a formal memo. You got a conversational draft. If you didn't say what you wanted the output to look like, the AI made a call on your behalf.

**4. The output was close but not right.** This is actually the most useful case. The AI understood what you wanted, got 70% of the way there, and needs targeted feedback to close the gap.

Each of these has a different fix. Let's go through them.

## The iteration loop: four steps that actually work

Think of this as a conversation, not a vending machine. You wouldn't hand a new colleague a task with zero context and expect perfection on the first try. You'd explain, they'd draft, you'd give feedback, they'd revise. AI works exactly the same way.

Anthropic's [prompt engineering documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview) describes this as an iterative cycle: generate a draft, identify where it falls short, then give specific feedback in a follow-up. Most people skip the second step (the diagnosis) and just hit regenerate, which rarely helps.

### Step 1: Name what's wrong before you try again

Before you send another message, spend 30 seconds being specific. "The output was bad" doesn't help. "The tone is too formal for a casual team update" helps. "The bullet points are too long and the third one is off-topic" helps.

The more precisely you can identify the gap, the more targeted your follow-up can be.

### Step 2: Add the context that was missing

If the output missed your situation entirely, the fix is context, not a new prompt. Tell Claude what the previous response got wrong, and add the background it needed. Something like:

> "This isn't quite right. My team is a small creative group that communicates informally, and this reads like a corporate announcement. Here's more context: [brief background]. Can you revise with that in mind?"

You don't need to start a new chat. In fact, keeping the conversation going is usually better because the model retains the full context of what you were trying to do.

### Step 3: Give specific, directive feedback

Vague feedback produces vague results. Compare:

- "This isn't what I wanted. Try again." (No information for the AI to use)
- "The intro paragraph is too long and it buries the main point. Shorten it to two sentences and lead with the ask, not the background." (Actionable)

The second version gives Claude something to work with. This is what Matt Hastings, one of MVP Club's founders, calls "managing the output through evaluation." After the AI generates a draft, your job shifts from requester to reviewer. You're reading like an editor now, not a user.

> "This is the really important step. You gave it the requirements, it built it, and now you're managing the output through evaluation." — Matt Hastings, during an MVP Club Getting Started session

### Step 4: Confirm what worked before changing what didn't

If parts of the output are good, say so explicitly. "The first two sections are exactly right. The third section is too generic. Here's what I actually wanted there: [specifics]."

Anchoring what worked keeps the AI from changing what already landed. And noticing what's worth keeping trains you to read output more carefully, which makes you faster at iteration over time.

## When to start fresh vs. when to keep going

Staying in the same conversation is usually the right call for the first 2-3 rounds of iteration. But there are times when starting a new chat is worth it:

- The AI has gone so far off track that correcting it would take more work than starting over
- You've added so many caveats and constraints that the conversation is getting unwieldy
- You realized midway through that you actually want something different from what you originally described

When you start fresh, take 2 minutes to write a better initial prompt based on what you learned from the failed conversation. Every "bad" conversation is research into what the AI needs from you.

## Three common iteration mistakes

**Regenerating without changing anything.** Clicking "try again" on the same input produces the same distribution of outputs. If the first response missed, the second probably will too. You need to change the inputs, not just roll the dice again.

**Over-prompting.** Some people respond to bad output by writing a 500-word prompt full of every constraint they can think of. This can actually make things worse. AI performs better with clear, focused instructions than with a wall of text that includes contradictions. If you've over-specified, try a simpler version.

**Giving up at round one.** Brian, a consultant who went through an MVP Club Website Building session, put it well: he'd asked Claude to give him "two versions that were radically different," but got back "two versions that were pretty much the same. One was darker, just different colors." Rather than giving up, he realized he needed to be explicit about structure, not just aesthetics. He added a reference folder with screenshots and got the variety he was looking for on the next try.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: member experience iterating on a work deliverable, first got generic output then got it right after specific feedback]

## The deeper shift: from one-shot to iterative

The mental model change here is bigger than any single technique. Most tools we use at work are deterministic: type a formula in Excel and you get the same result every time. AI is probabilistic. As the [probabilistic vs. deterministic AI](/blog/probabilistic-vs-deterministic-ai) article explains, the output varies because AI is generating a range of possible responses and you're influencing which ones become most likely.

Once you accept that, the expectation of "perfect on the first try" goes away. Iteration becomes the process, not the failure. You're not doing AI wrong if you're on round three. You're doing it right.

This is also why the skill matters more than the prompt. You can find a great prompt template online and still get bad results if you don't know how to work with what comes back. The iteration skill is the one that compounds.

## A practical starting point for your next session

Next time you get output you don't like, try this sequence before giving up:

1. Write down (in plain language) exactly what's wrong with what you got. Be specific.
2. In your next message, quote the problem, explain why it doesn't work, and add any context the AI was missing.
3. If it improves but still isn't right, name what improved and what still needs work.
4. Repeat until it's good enough to use, or until you've learned enough to start a better conversation.

For tasks you repeat often (weekly updates, performance review feedback, client emails), keep notes on what context works best. Over time, you'll build a set of starting conditions that gets you to good output faster. That's what [building AI into your daily workflow](/learn/how-to-use-ai-at-work) looks like in practice.

Claude's own documentation on [prompt engineering best practices](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/claude-4-best-practices) is worth bookmarking if you want to go deeper on the mechanics of how instructions get interpreted.

## Where people who iterate well end up

Professionals who get comfortable with iteration tend to describe a turning point: the moment they stopped expecting a magic prompt and started treating AI like a capable colleague who needs good direction. The tool didn't change. Their relationship with it did.

That shift doesn't happen from reading about it. It happens from doing it. Most people need 10 to 20 bad sessions before it clicks, and that's completely normal.

If you want to run through these techniques alongside other professionals who are figuring it out in real time, that's exactly what happens in MVP Club's weekly sessions. You get to watch iteration in action, share what's working, and skip some of the trial-and-error solo. [Come see what the community is working on.](/community)

The deeper shift: from one-shot to iterative

The mental model change here is bigger than any single technique. Most tools we use at work are deterministic: type a formula in Excel and you get the same result every time. AI is probabilistic. As the probabilistic vs. deterministic AI article explains, the output varies because AI is generating a range of possible responses and you’re influencing which ones become most likely.

Once you accept that, the expectation of “perfect on the first try” goes away. Iteration becomes the process, not the failure. You’re not doing AI wrong if you’re on round three. You’re doing it right.

This is also why the skill matters more than the prompt. You can find a great prompt template online and still get bad results if you don’t know how to work with what comes back. The iteration skill is the one that compounds.

A practical starting point for your next session

Next time you get output you don’t like, try this sequence before giving up:

  1. Write down (in plain language) exactly what’s wrong with what you got. Be specific.
  2. In your next message, quote the problem, explain why it doesn’t work, and add any context the AI was missing.
  3. If it improves but still isn’t right, name what improved and what still needs work.
  4. Repeat until it’s good enough to use, or until you’ve learned enough to start a better conversation.

For tasks you repeat often (weekly updates, performance review feedback, client emails), keep notes on what context works best. Over time, you’ll build a set of starting conditions that gets you to good output faster. That’s what building AI into your daily workflow looks like in practice.

Claude’s own documentation on prompt engineering best practices is worth bookmarking if you want to go deeper on the mechanics of how instructions get interpreted.

Where people who iterate well end up

Professionals who get comfortable with iteration tend to describe a turning point: the moment they stopped expecting a magic prompt and started treating AI like a capable colleague who needs good direction. The tool didn’t change. Their relationship with it did.

That shift doesn’t happen from reading about it. It happens from doing it. Most people need 10 to 20 bad sessions before it clicks, and that’s completely normal.

If you want to run through these techniques alongside other professionals who are figuring it out in real time, that’s exactly what happens in MVP Club’s weekly sessions. You get to watch iteration in action, share what’s working, and skip some of the trial-and-error solo. Come see what the community is working on.

When AI Gets It Wrong: The Human Judgment Layer
AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT make mistakes confidently. Here's how to stay in the driver's seat and catch errors before they cost you.

Claude told Lesley the wrong year on her own bio. Pulled it from the web, stated it with total confidence, and moved on. She caught it. But only because she was paying attention.

That moment captures something important about working with AI tools. They are genuinely useful, and they are genuinely fallible, often at the same time. The output looks correct. It reads well. And some piece of it is just wrong.

None of this is a reason to stop using Claude or ChatGPT. But it is worth understanding your role more clearly. You are not a passive recipient of AI output. You are the person responsible for it.

## Why AI gets things wrong (and why it doesn't know when it does)

AI tools generate responses by predicting what text should come next, based on patterns in enormous amounts of training data. They are not looking facts up in a database. They are not checking their work. When a model produces something confidently wrong, it is not lying. It genuinely cannot distinguish between "a fact I learned from reliable sources" and "a plausible-sounding thing I generated." That distinction lives in your judgment, not the model's.

This matters for professional work because the outputs look polished. A hallucinated statistic, a wrong date, a fabricated citation: these do not announce themselves. They sit in the middle of otherwise solid paragraphs and wait for someone to notice. According to [PwC's overview of AI hallucinations](https://www.pwc.com/us/en/tech-effect/ai-analytics/ai-hallucinations.html), confident but false statements are among the hardest errors to catch precisely because the surrounding context is accurate.

The types of errors that catch professionals off guard tend to cluster around a few categories. Factual specifics (dates, numbers, names, citations). Anything about your organization or your own background, which the model may have learned nothing about or learned outdated information about. Interpretive leaps, where the model fills a gap in your instructions with a reasonable-sounding inference that does not match your intent. And domain-specific judgment calls, where the right answer depends on context the model does not have.

## The human judgment layer in practice

Here is something I said in a recent MVP Club Getting Started session, when a community member found inaccurate information Claude had pulled into her portfolio site:

> "This is the really important step. You gave it the requirements, it built it, and now you're managing the output through evaluation. You're evaluating the output, you're like: this is wrong. So how do you correct it?" — from a Getting Started session

The word "managing" is doing real work in that sentence. You are not just using a tool. You are in a managerial relationship with it. You specify the goal, you review the output, you catch the gaps, and you give feedback. That cycle repeats until the output is something you'd put your name on.

This shift changes what "using AI" actually means. The skill is not writing a perfect prompt. The skill is evaluating what comes back. And that evaluation is yours to do, every time.

## Three categories of output that need closer review

Not every piece of AI output carries the same risk. A brainstormed list of meeting agenda items is low-stakes. A compliance policy draft is not. Knowing which category you are in helps you calibrate how carefully to read.

**Anything you would forward, publish, or submit.** Before any AI output leaves your hands, it needs a real read. Not a skim. Especially for professional documents, client-facing content, or anything with your name on it. You are the author. The model is the first drafter.

**Specific facts, figures, and citations.** This is where AI errors are most common and most damaging. If Claude gives you a statistic, look it up. If it generates a citation, check that it exists. Lawyers have learned this the hard way: fabricated case citations have appeared in real filings, with real consequences. [NEEDS REAL EXAMPLE: A specific community member describing a moment they caught a hallucinated detail in a professional document]

**Anything about your own organization.** Claude and ChatGPT know very little about your company specifically. Internal processes, org structure, recent decisions, your specific team's norms: none of this is in the model's training data. When you ask for something that requires organizational context and you have not provided it, the model will make reasonable inferences that may or may not match reality. You are the only one who can close that gap.

## The "does this sound like me?" check

One category of error is subtler than a wrong fact. It is a right fact delivered in a wrong way: wrong for your voice, wrong for your relationship with the reader, wrong for your organization's culture.

Brian, who participated in a Getting Started session on website building, named it well:

> "When I use these tools, some things just sound a little bit more like AI. So I'm conscious: Does this sound like AI?" — Brian Lightfoot, MVP Club community member

That question is worth internalizing. AI-generated text has patterns: it hedges consistently, it transitions smoothly between paragraphs, it uses certain sentence structures over and over. When you are the author, you notice when something does not sound like you. Readers notice too, even if they cannot name why.

A real editorial pass, one where you read aloud or at minimum read slowly, catches these moments. You are not looking for factual errors. You are asking whether this is something you would actually say to this person.

## How to build verification into your workflow (without slowing everything down)

The goal is not to spend as much time reviewing AI output as you would have spent writing from scratch. That defeats the purpose. The goal is a quick, deliberate check that catches the errors most likely to matter.

A few patterns that work well:

**Read for claims.** As you scan a draft, mentally flag every specific claim: names, numbers, dates, external references. Give each one five seconds of attention. Does it seem right? If you are not sure, verify. Most claims you will know on sight. The ones you pause on are worth a quick check.

**Paste facts back in and ask Claude to verify them.** This sounds circular, but it is surprisingly effective: take the specific claims from a draft and ask Claude in a separate message to confirm them and flag anything it is not confident about. You are essentially asking the model to do a fact-check pass on its own output. It will not catch everything, but it catches some things, and the process forces you to look at claims individually rather than embedded in prose.

**Add organizational context before you generate.** Many errors happen because the model fills context gaps with guesses. Giving Claude a paragraph of background about your team, your audience, or your organization's specific situation before asking for a draft reduces how much it needs to infer.

Human judgment on output quality is the critical layer. This came up repeatedly in our Getting Started series: AI speeds up the workflow, but humans still evaluate, refine, edit, and decide what to keep. That is not a limitation of the tools. It is how they are designed to work.

## What this means for your career

There is an interesting flip side to AI error that does not get discussed enough. The professionals who develop good verification habits are building something valuable: judgment that compounds.

When you review AI output carefully enough to catch what is wrong, you are also building pattern recognition for what good looks like. You start to notice how the model handles uncertainty (often: by sounding certain). You learn where it tends to slip in your domain. You develop a feel for when a draft needs a close read versus when it is close enough to pass through quickly.

That judgment is yours. The model does not learn between conversations. But you do.

A recent [HR Dive report](https://www.hrdive.com/news/workplace-ai-not-reliable-human-oversight/812949/) noted that only 17% of U.S. adults consider workplace AI reliable without human oversight. The professionals who matter to their organizations right now are not the ones who generate the most AI output. They are the ones who generate AI output their organization can trust.

## Working on this with other people speeds it up

One of the most useful things you can do to develop the verification instinct is watch how someone else catches errors in AI output, and have someone else watch you. The moments where a colleague says "wait, that number doesn't seem right" build the instinct faster than anything you will do solo.

[NEEDS COMMUNITY QUOTE: A community member describing a specific moment in a session where they or someone else caught an AI error and what they learned from it]

This is one of the things that happens regularly in MVP Club's weekly sessions. People share what they are building, show the output, and the group spots things the individual missed. If you want to build a sharper eye for AI output alongside other professionals doing the same, come see [what the community is working on](/community).
